| **Gradient Descent** | Local | Simple, fast convergence near optimum | Requires differentiable metric, local minima |
| **Powell's Method** | Local (derivative-free) | No gradients needed, robust | Slower than gradient-based |
| **Levenberg–Marquardt** | Local | Fast for SSD-type metrics | Needs Jacobian |
| **Exhaustive Search** | Global | Finds global optimum | Computationally infeasible for fine search |
| **Multi-Resolution (Pyramid)** | Hierarchical | Avoids local minima, fast | Implementation complexity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Practice:** KTU-evaluated algorithms almost always use a **multi-resolution (coarse-to-fine) pyramid** strategy combined with **gradient descent** on MI or NCC. This is the de facto standard in clinical packages like ITK, Elastix, and 3D Slicer.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit | Notes |
|--------|---------|------|-------|
| $\theta$ | Rotation angle | radians (or degrees) | Range: $[0, 2\pi)$ or $(-\pi, \pi]$ |
| $s$ | Uniform scale factor | dimensionless | $s > 0$; $s = 1$ means no scaling |
| $t_x, t_y$ | Translation vector components | mm (or pixels) | Centered or origin-based |
| $R(\theta)$ | $2 \times 2$ rotation matrix | — | Orthonormal: $R^T R = I$ |
| $T$ | Full $3 \times 3$ homogeneous matrix | — | 4 DOF in 2D |
| $\mathcal{S}$ | Similarity metric (cost function) | — | SSD → minimize; NCC/MI → maximize |
| $H(X)$ | Shannon entropy of image $X$ | bits (or nats) | $H(X) = -\sum p \log p$ |
| TRE | Target Registration Error | mm | Gold-standard accuracy metric |
| FLE | Fiducial Localization Error | mm | Error in landmark picking |
| FRE | Fiducial Registration Error | mm | RMS error over fiducials |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Global similarity registration is the **workhorse** of clinical image fusion:

- **Neurosurgery (BrainLab, StealthStation):** Pre-op MRI fused to intra-op CT for cranial navigation.
- **Radiation Therapy Planning (Eclipse, Pinnacle):** CT-MRI fusion for tumor delineation.
- **Cardiac Imaging:** Aligning cine-MRI frames across the cardiac cycle.
- **Dental/Orthopedic Imaging:** Pre-op planning fused with intra-op fluoroscopy.

The 4-DOF constraint is not a limitation — it is a **regularizer** that exploits the known rigidity of bones and the brain to achieve sub-millimeter accuracy robustly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 2D Similarity Transformation Matrix

**Starting Point:** We seek a transformation $T: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ that preserves shape (angles and length ratios). Mathematically, this means the linear part of $T$ is a **scalar multiple of an orthogonal matrix** (by definition of similarity transformations).

**Step 1 — Express the transformation as a composition:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T(\mathbf{x}) &= T_{\text{trans}} \circ T_{\text{rot}} \circ T_{\text{scale}}(\mathbf{x})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Scale by factor $s$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{scale}}(\mathbf{x}) &= s \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Rotate by angle $\theta$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{rot}}(\mathbf{x}) &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Translate by $(t_x, t_y)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{trans}}(\mathbf{x}) &= \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} t_x \\ t_y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compose (apply scale first, then rotation, then translation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}' &= T_{\text{trans}}\big(T_{\text{rot}}\big(T_{\text{scale}}(\mathbf{x})\big)\big) \\
&= T_{\text{trans}}\left( s \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} \right) \\
&= s \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} t_x \\ t_y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Lift to homogeneous coordinates** by appending a row $[0 \; 0 \; 1]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{homog}} &= \begin{bmatrix} s\cos\theta & -s\sin\theta & t_x \\ s\sin\theta & s\cos\theta & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **complete 2D global similarity transformation** with 4 parameters.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of NCC (Normalized Cross-Correlation)

**Step 1 — Define the mean intensities:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{I}_1 &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} I_1(\mathbf{x}_i) \\
\bar{I}_2 &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} I_2(T(\mathbf{x}_i))
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Define zero-mean versions:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tilde{I}_1(\mathbf{x}_i) &= I_1(\mathbf{x}_i) - \bar{I}_1 \\
\tilde{I}_2(T(\mathbf{x}_i)) &= I_2(T(\mathbf{x}_i)) - \bar{I}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Form the numerator (covariance-like term):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cov term} &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{I}_1(\mathbf{x}_i) \cdot \tilde{I}_2(T(\mathbf{x}_i))
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Form the denominator (variance-like normalization):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Var term} &= \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{I}_1(\mathbf{x}_i)^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{I}_2(T(\mathbf{x}_i))^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{NCC}(\mathbf{p}) &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{I}_1(\mathbf{x}_i) \cdot \tilde{I}_2(T(\mathbf{x}_i))}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{I}_1(\mathbf{x}_i)^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \tilde{I}_2(T(\mathbf{x}_i))^2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Key property:** $\text{NCC} \in [-1, +1]$, with $+1$ indicating perfect positive linear correspondence at the current transformation $\mathbf{p}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Mutual Information (MI)

**Step 1 — Compute marginal probability distributions** of intensities (via histogram with $B$ bins):

$$\begin{aligned}
p_I(a) &= \frac{\text{count of pixels with intensity } a}{N}, \quad a \in \{0, 1, \ldots, B-1\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute joint histogram** of $(I_1(\mathbf{x}_i), I_2(T(\mathbf{x}_i)))$ pairs, normalized to give joint probability $p_{I_1, I_2}(a, b)$.

**Step 3 — Marginal entropies:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(I_1) &= -\sum_{a} p_{I_1}(a) \log p_{I_1}(a) \\
H(I_2) &= -\sum_{b} p_{I_2}(b) \log p_{I_2}(b)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Joint entropy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H(I_1, I_2) &= -\sum_{a, b} p_{I_1, I_2}(a, b) \log p_{I_1, I_2}(a, b)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Mutual Information (in bits if $\log_2$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MI}(I_1, I_2) &= H(I_1) + H(I_2) - H(I_1, I_2)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalence property:** $\text{MI}(I_1, I_2) = H(I_1) - H(I_1 \mid I_2) = H(I_2) - H(I_2 \mid I_1)$. When images are perfectly registered, knowledge of one intensity completely predicts the other, making conditional entropies zero and MI maximal.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Estimating 2D Similarity Parameters

**Given:** Two corresponding landmark pairs:
- $(x_1, y_1) = (0, 0) \leftrightarrow (x_1', y_1') = (2, 3)$
- $(x_2, y_2) = (1, 0) \leftrightarrow (x_2', y_2') = (3.5, 4.33)$

**Step 1 — Estimate scale $s$ from distance ratios:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{source}} &= \sqrt{(1-0)^2 + (0-0)^2} = 1 \\
d_{\text{target}} &= \sqrt{(3.5-2)^2 + (4.33-3)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 + 1.7689} = \sqrt{4.0189} \approx 2.005 \\
s &= \frac{d_{\text{target}}}{d_{\text{source}}} = 2.005
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate rotation $\theta$ from angle of displacement vector:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{source}} &= \arctan(0/1) = 0 \text{ rad} \\
\theta_{\text{target}} &= \arctan(1.33/1.5) = \arctan(0.8867) \approx 0.726 \text{ rad} \; (\approx 41.6^\circ) \\
\theta &= 0.726 - 0 = 0.726 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Estimate translation using the first landmark (origin) after transformation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T(0, 0) &= s \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} t_x \\ t_y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} t_x \\ t_y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

Equating to $(2, 3)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_x &= 2, \quad t_y = 3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verify on the second landmark:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T(1, 0) &= s \begin{bmatrix} \cos 0.726 & -\sin 0.726 \\ \sin 0.726 & \cos 0.726 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= 2.005 \begin{bmatrix} 0.7487 \\ 0.6629 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 1.501 \\ 1.329 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.501 \\ 4.329 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the target $(3.5, 4.33)$ within numerical precision. **Registration successful.**

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Transformation Models

| Feature | Rigid (3 DOF) | Similarity (4 DOF) | Affine (6 DOF) | Deformable (>>6 DOF) |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Translation | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Rotation | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Uniform Scale | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Non-uniform Scale | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Shear | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Local warping | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Anatomical realism | Skull, bone | Same modality, mild FOV change | Cross-FOV, oblique slices | Soft tissue, organ motion |
| Computational cost | Lowest | Low | Medium | High |
| Robustness | Highest | High | Medium | Lowest (overfitting) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Global Similarity Registration Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fixed Image I_fixed] --> C[Multi-Resolution Pyramid Builder]
    B[Moving Image I_moving] --> C
    C --> D[Initialize Transformation Parameters p = t_x, t_y, theta, s]
    D --> E[Apply Transformation T_p to Moving Image]
    E --> F[Resample / Interpolate at Transformed Coordinates]
    F --> G[Compute Similarity Metric: SSD or NCC or MI]
    G --> H{Optimizer: Gradient Descent or Powell}
    H -->|Update p| D
    H -->|Converged| I[Refine at Next Pyramid Level]
    I --> J{All Levels Done?}
    J -->|No| D
    J -->|Yes| K[Final Transformation p_star]
    K --> L[Warped Moving Image Aligned to Fixed Image]
```

**Pipeline interpretation:**
- The fixed and moving images are progressively downsampled to form a Gaussian pyramid (coarse → fine).
- At the coarsest level, the optimizer searches a wide parameter range to escape local minima.
- At each finer level, the previous optimum is used as initialization, achieving sub-pixel accuracy.

### 4.2 Transformation Group Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[General Deformable B-Spline FFD] --> B[Affine 6 DOF]
    B --> C[Similarity 4 DOF]
    C --> D[Rigid 3 DOF]
    D --> E[Translation 2 DOF]
    D --> F[Rotation Only 1 DOF]
    E --> G[Identity 0 DOF]
```

**Key insight:** As we move right, the **degrees of freedom decrease** and the **regularization increases**, giving stronger guarantees of physical plausibility at the cost of modeling flexibility.

### 4.3 Block Diagram of the Cost Function Evaluation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Current Parameters p] --> T[Apply T_p]
    Q[Image Grid Coordinates] --> T
    T --> S[Resampler - Bilinear or B-spline interpolation]
    F[Fixed Image] --> M[Metric Evaluator]
    S --> M
    M --> V[Metric Value S_p]
    V --> OPT{Optimizer}
    OPT -->|Compute gradient or Brent step| P
    OPT -->|Converged| OUT[Output: p_star]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define image registration. What is a global similarity transformation? List its parameters in 2D.*

**Model Answer:**
Image registration is the process of spatially aligning two or more images of the same scene or anatomy so that corresponding structures overlap precisely. A **global similarity transformation** is a geometric transformation that preserves the shape of objects (no shear, no non-uniform scaling) and applies uniformly across the entire image. In 2D, it has **4 parameters**: two translations $(t_x, t_y)$, one rotation angle $\theta$, and one uniform scale factor $s$. In matrix form:
$$T = \begin{bmatrix} s\cos\theta & -s\sin\theta & t_x \\ s\sin\theta & s\cos\theta & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between Sum of Squared Differences (SSD) and Mutual Information (MI) as similarity metrics. When is MI preferred over SSD?*

**Model Answer:**
SSD measures the sum of squared intensity differences between corresponding pixels and assumes a **linear** relationship between image intensities. It is minimized when images are aligned. MI measures the statistical dependence between two images via entropies, requiring **no intensity linearity** assumption, and is maximized at alignment. **MI is preferred for multi-modal registration** (e.g., CT–MRI fusion) where intensity scales and contrasts differ fundamentally. SSD is restricted to **mono-modal** registration (e.g., two MRI scans of the same patient).

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the 2D global similarity transformation matrix. Explain the role of each parameter and the canonical order of composition.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the four parameters:** $t_x, t_y$ (translation), $\theta$ (rotation), $s$ (uniform scale).

**Step 2 — Build the elementary transformations:**

- Scale: $S = s \cdot I_{2 \times 2} = \begin{bmatrix} s & 0 \\ 0 & s \end{bmatrix}$
- Rotation: $R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$
- Translation in homogeneous: $T_t = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$

**Step 3 — Compose in canonical order (scale → rotate → translate):**

$$T_{\text{homog}} = T_t \cdot R_s$$

where $R_s$ is the scaled rotation embedded in $3 \times 3$ form:

$$R_s = \begin{bmatrix} s\cos\theta & -s\sin\theta & 0 \\ s\sin\theta & s\cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$T_{\text{homog}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & t_x \\ 0 & 1 & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s\cos\theta & -s\sin\theta & 0 \\ s\sin\theta & s\cos\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} s\cos\theta & -s\sin\theta & t_x \\ s\sin\theta & s\cos\theta & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Apply to point $(x, y, 1)^T$:**

$$\begin{aligned} x' &= s(x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta) + t_x \\ y' &= s(x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta) + t_y \end{aligned}$$

**Roles:**
- $s$: uniform magnification factor (compensates FOV differences)
- $\theta$: orientation correction (compensates patient rotation)
- $t_x, t_y$: positional correction (compensates translation)

**Canonical order:** Scale is applied first (innermost), then rotation, then translation. This is the physically correct order for "growing, turning, and moving" an object.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of multi-resolution (coarse-to-fine) strategy in global similarity registration. Why is a Gaussian image pyramid used, and what problem does it solve?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Problem statement:** Direct optimization of similarity metrics on full-resolution images has a very small **capture range**. The optimizer converges to the nearest local minimum, which may be far from the true global optimum if the initial misalignment is large.

**Step 2 — Capture range definition:** The capture range is the maximum initial parameter error for which the optimizer still converges to the correct solution. For NCC/SSD, this is typically only a few pixels.

**Step 3 — Pyramid construction:** A **Gaussian pyramid** is built by iteratively smoothing the image with a Gaussian kernel and downsampling by a factor of 2:

$$I_{l+1}(\mathbf{x}) = (G_\sigma * \downarrow_2 I_l)(\mathbf{x})$$

where $G_\sigma$ is a Gaussian smoothing kernel (typically $\sigma = 1.0$ to satisfy the Nyquist criterion) and $\downarrow_2$ denotes 2× downsampling.

**Step 4 — Coarse-to-fine search:** Registration begins at the coarsest pyramid level $L$ (e.g., 1/16 resolution), where the metric landscape is smooth and the capture range is effectively amplified by the downsampling factor. The optimal parameters found at level $l$ are **upsampled and used as initialization** for level $l-1$.

**Step 5 — Refinement:** At each finer level, the optimizer refines the parameters to sub-pixel accuracy, ultimately yielding a high-precision final transformation at the original resolution.

**Why Gaussian smoothing?** Subsampling without low-pass filtering causes **aliasing** of high-frequency content, producing spurious local minima in the metric. Gaussian smoothing acts as an anti-aliasing filter and progressively removes high-frequency details, leaving only the broad intensity structure that drives coarse alignment.

**Engineering benefits:**
- 10–100× reduction in computation
- Robustness to local minima
- Larger capture range (16× at 1/16 resolution)
- Sub-pixel final accuracy preserved

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With relevant equations, derive the Mutual Information (MI) similarity metric. Explain why MI is the metric of choice for multi-modal medical image registration.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define image intensities:** Let $A$ and $B$ be the two images with intensity random variables $I_A$ and $I_B$.

**Step 2 — Marginal probability distributions** from intensity histograms (with $N_b$ bins):

$$p_A(a) = \frac{|\{i : I_A(\mathbf{x}_i) = a\}|}{N}, \quad a \in \{1, \ldots, N_b\}$$

$$p_B(b) = \frac{|\{i : I_B(\mathbf{x}_i) = b\}|}{N}, \quad b \in \{1, \ldots, N_b\}$$

**Step 3 — Joint probability distribution** from the 2D joint histogram of paired intensities $(I_A(\mathbf{x}_i), I_B(T(\mathbf{x}_i)))$:

$$p_{A,B}(a, b) = \frac{|\{i : I_A = a \text{ and } I_B = b\}|}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Shannon entropies (in bits):**

$$H(A) = -\sum_a p_A(a) \log_2 p_A(a)$$

$$H(B) = -\sum_b p_B(b) \log_2 p_B(b)$$

$$H(A, B) = -\sum_{a, b} p_{A,B}(a, b) \log_2 p_{A,B}(a, b)$$

**Step 5 — Define MI:**

$$\boxed{\text{MI}(A, B) = H(A) + H(B) - H(A, B)}$$

**Step 6 — Alternative form using KL divergence:**

$$\text{MI}(A, B) = \sum_{a,b} p_{A,B}(a,b) \log_2 \frac{p_{A,B}(a,b)}{p_A(a) \, p_B(b)}$$

**Why MI for multi-modal registration:**

1. **No intensity linearity assumption:** SSD assumes $I_B = a \cdot I_A + b$. This fails for CT–MRI (bone is bright in CT, dark in MRI T1; air is dark in both but with different contrast mechanisms).

2. **Captures statistical dependence:** MI measures *all* forms of dependence, not just linear. Even nonlinear intensity mappings (e.g., PET tracer uptake vs. MRI T2) are handled.

3. **Maximized at geometric alignment:** When images are aligned, the joint histogram becomes **concentrated along a low-dimensional manifold** (1D curve), reducing joint entropy and increasing MI. When misaligned, the joint histogram spreads out as a 2D blob, increasing joint entropy and decreasing MI.

4. **Validated by Viola & Wells (1997) and Maes et al. (1997):** MI-based registration is the **clinical standard** for CT–MRI, PET–MRI, and US–MRI fusion.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Fiducial Registration Error (FRE) and Target Registration Error (TRE). How are they related, and what is the Fiducial Localization Error (FLE)?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — FLE definition:** The **Fiducial Localization Error (FLE)** is the error in identifying the 3D (or 2D) coordinates of a fiducial landmark in each image independently. It arises from manual picking uncertainty, image resolution, and partial volume effects:

$$\text{FLE}_i^{\text{image}} = \| \mathbf{x}_i^{\text{true}} - \mathbf{x}_i^{\text{measured}} \|$$

**Step 2 — FRE definition:** The **Fiducial Registration Error (FRE)** is the RMS distance between corresponding fiducial pairs *after* registration has been performed. If $N_f$ fiducial pairs are used:

$$\text{FRE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N_f} \sum_{i=1}^{N_f} \left\| T(\mathbf{x}_i^{\text{fixed}}) - \mathbf{y}_i^{\text{moving}} \right\|^2}$$

where $T$ is the estimated transformation. FRE is the **fitting error** of the registration on the landmarks used to compute it.

**Step 3 — TRE definition:** The **Target Registration Error (TRE)** is the actual clinically relevant error — the distance between corresponding *non-fiducial* anatomical points after registration:

$$\text{TRE}(\mathbf{x}) = \| T(\mathbf{x}^{\text{fixed}}) - \mathbf{x}^{\text{moving}} \|$$

TRE is what the surgeon actually cares about: how accurate is the alignment at the point of clinical interest (e.g., tumor centroid)?

**Step 4 — Relationship:** TRE is **unrelated to FRE** in a direct proportional sense but is statistically coupled. The classic **Fitzpatrick & West relationship** (1998) for a rigid transformation in 3D with $N_f$ fiducials is:

$$\langle \text{TRE}^2 \rangle \approx \langle \text{FLE}^2 \rangle \left( \frac{1}{N_f} + \frac{d_i^2}{\sum_{j=1}^{N_f} d_j^2} \right)$$

where $d_i$ is the distance of the $i$-th target point from the centroid of the fiducial configuration.

**Step 5 — Key insight:** FRE is *not* a reliable predictor of TRE. A small FRE does NOT guarantee small TRE — they can even be anti-correlated if the target is far from the fiducial centroid. **TRE is the gold standard** for clinical validation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Order of composition matters!** Many students write the transformation as "translate → rotate → scale" — this is **wrong** for the canonical similarity form. The correct order (when reading right-to-left on point $\mathbf{x}$) is **scale → rotate → translate**, which becomes **translate → rotate → scale** when multiplying matrices left-to-right.
> 2. **SSD vs MI confusion:** SSD requires *mono-modal* images. Using SSD on CT–MRI will give incorrect results because intensities are not linearly related. Always state the metric's validity scope.
> 3. **TRE ≠ FRE:** Examiners specifically test this. Saying "TRE measures how well the registration fits the landmarks" is wrong — that is FRE. TRE measures accuracy at *unseen* target points.
> 4. **DOF count:** Rigid = 3 (2D), Similarity = 4 (2D), Affine = 6 (2D). Do not confuse with 3D versions (6, 7, 12).
> 5. **Mutual Information maximization, not minimization.** The optimizer is set to **maximize** MI, not minimize it. Sign errors are a common source of lost marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Image Registration** = finding spatial correspondence between two images of the same anatomy.
- **Global Similarity Transformation** = translation + rotation + **uniform** scale (no shear, no local warping).
- **2D DOF = 4:** $t_x, t_y, \theta, s$.
- **Homogeneous matrix form** (canonical):
  $$\begin{bmatrix} s\cos\theta & -s\sin\theta & t_x \\ s\sin\theta & s\cos\theta & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
- **Metric selection rule of thumb:**
  - Same modality, similar intensities → **SSD** or **NCC**
  - Different modalities (CT–MRI, PET–CT) → **Mutual Information (MI)**
  - Illumination/bias changes → **NCC**
- **Optimization best practice:** Multi-resolution (Gaussian pyramid) + gradient descent or Powell's method.
- **Capture range** is small; pyramid strategy expands it dramatically.
- **NCC range:** $[-1, +1]$; **+1 = perfect alignment**.
- **MI range:** $[0, H(\min(H_A, H_B))]$; **maximum at alignment**.
- **Accuracy benchmarks:** FRE and TRE measured in **mm**; clinical target is TRE < 2 mm for neurosurgery.
- **Group hierarchy:** Translation ⊂ Rigid ⊂ Similarity ⊂ Affine ⊂ Deformable.
- **Key property:** Similarity preserves angles and length ratios — this is its defining geometric property.
- **Canonical composition order:** Scale → Rotate → Translate (applied to point).
- **Resampling:** Bilinear interpolation is the standard choice for similarity registration; B-spline for deformable.
- **Clinical examples to cite:** Brain MRI–CT fusion, radiation therapy planning, intraoperative neuronavigation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transform Identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transform Identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transform Identification** in medical image processing is the systematic process of determining, classifying, and applying an appropriate mathematical transform (such as Fourier, Wavelet, Cosine, Hough, or Radon) to a given medical image dataset in order to reveal hidden structural, frequency, or geometric information that is not directly observable in the spatial domain.

In the context of **KTU PEBMT755 Module 1**, *Transform Identification* is the gateway operation that allows biomedical engineers to:

1. Convert a spatial-domain image $f(x,y)$ into a transform-domain representation $F(u,v)$.
2. Match the *signature* of the transform coefficients to a specific anatomical or pathological feature.
3. Choose between orthogonal, non-orthogonal, separable, or non-separable transforms based on the medical imaging application (denoising, compression, segmentation, registration, or feature extraction).

The mathematical statement of any 2-D image transform takes the canonical form:

$$
F(u,v) \;=\; \sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y)\,K(x,y,u,v)
$$

where $K(x,y,u,v)$ is the **kernel** (or basis function) of the transform, and the inverse is:

$$
f(x,y) \;=\; \sum_{u=0}^{M-1}\sum_{v=0}^{N-1} F(u,v)\,I(x,y,u,v)
$$

The *identification* part of the topic concerns **how to select $K$** that best represents the underlying signal structure of the image.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Right Pair of Glasses"**
> Imagine you are looking at a stained-glass church window from very far away. All you see is a blur of colour. Now put on different pairs of glasses:
> - **Clear-glass glasses** → you see the window as it is (this is the *spatial domain*).
> - **Red-tinted glasses** → only the red parts of the window stand out.
> - **High-pass glasses** → only the sharp edges (window leading) become visible.
> - **Low-pass glasses** → only the broad colour washes remain.
>
> Each pair of glasses is a **transform**. *Identifying the right transform* is exactly like choosing the right pair of glasses so that the feature you care about (a tumour edge, a bone fracture, a slow-changing tissue region) becomes the *most prominent* thing in the resulting "image."

The **transform coefficients $F(u,v)$** are essentially a *new image* in which the dimensions $(u,v)$ no longer mean *horizontal pixel* and *vertical pixel* but instead mean **frequency, scale, orientation, or projection-angle** — whatever the transform's basis functions naturally describe.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in Transform Identification

> [!TIP]
> The following metrics are the **gold standard** KTU examiners look for when grading transform-based answers:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Spatial resolution | **pixels / mm** | Determines sampling requirement before transform |
| Frequency resolution | **cycles / mm** | Output of Fourier-type transforms |
| Energy compaction ratio | **ECR** | How well a transform packs energy into few coefficients (DCT ≈ Wavelet > DFT) |
| Transform separability | Boolean | Whether kernel $K(x,y,u,v) = K_1(x,u)\cdot K_2(y,v)$ |
| Orthogonality | Boolean | Required for lossless reconstruction |
| Computational complexity | **$O(N^2)$ or $O(N^2 \log N)$** | Decides real-time viability |

---

## 1.4 Why "Identification" — Not Just "Application"

Most introductory texts teach *applying* a transform. The KTU Module-1 emphasis on **identification** demands that the engineer demonstrate:

- **What is the dominant feature** in the image? (Edge, texture, periodic pattern, point source, line, ellipse)
- **Which transform's basis functions best match** that feature?
- **What is the noise model** (Gaussian, Poisson, salt-and-pepper)?
- **Is real-time performance needed** (e.g., fluoroscopy) or is offline analysis acceptable (e.g., MRI post-processing)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students are expected to compare at least **five** major transforms (2-D DFT, DCT, Hadamard, Wavelet, Hough/Radon) and justify their selection for a given medical image processing task.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Transform Identification

The process of identifying the right transform proceeds through six logical stages:

### Stage 1 — Feature Inventory
Catalogue the *target features* in the medical image:
- **Edges** (organ boundaries, tumour margins)
- **Textures** (parenchyma patterns, micro-calcifications)
- **Periodic structures** (ribs, vascular tree, ECG-gated artefacts)
- **Point sources** (micro-calcifications in mammography)
- **Lines / curves** (vessels, bronchi)
- **Projections** (sinogram data in CT)

### Stage 2 — Noise Audit
Identify the dominant noise:
- **Gaussian** (thermal noise in MRI, US speckle) → Fourier denoising effective
- **Poisson** (X-ray, PET photon counting) → Wavelet/Anscombe transform preferred
- **Salt-and-pepper** (rare in medical; more in transmission) → Median filter beats transform

### Stage 3 — Constraint Check
- Real-time? → **Haar / Walsh / Short-length DCT**
- Lossless compression required? → **Integer Wavelet (S-transform, JPEG2000)**
- Reconstruction from projections? → **Radon / Filtered Back-Projection**

### Stage 4 — Basis-Function Matching
Match the geometric shape of the basis to the geometric shape of the feature:

| Feature Shape | Best-Fit Basis |
|---|---|
| Sinusoid | Complex exponential (DFT) |
| Cosine wave (real signal) | Cosine (DCT) |
| Step / discontinuous edge | Haar (Wavelet) |
| Smooth curve | Daubechies / Symlet (Wavelet) |
| Line in $(x,y)$ | Point in Hough $(\rho,\theta)$ |
| Projection at angle $\theta$ | Sinusoid in Radon space |

### Stage 5 — Energy Compaction & Sparsity Test
A transform "identifies" itself as appropriate if the **energy compaction ratio (ECR)** is high:

$$
\text{ECR} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{(u,v) \in S_{k}} \vert F(u,v)\vert^{2}}{\sum_{u=0}^{M-1}\sum_{v=0}^{N-1} \vert F(u,v)\vert^{2}}
$$

where $S_{k}$ is the set of the top-$k\%$ largest magnitude coefficients. Higher ECR ⇒ sparser representation ⇒ better for compression and denoising.

### Stage 6 — Computational Cost Validation
DFT: $O(N^{2}\log N)$ via FFT. DCT: $O(N^{2}\log N)$. Wavelet: $O(N)$. Hough: $O(N_{\theta} \cdot N_{\rho})$ over parameter space. Radon: $O(N^{3})$ for direct, faster with FFT-based projection-slice.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-grade reference** for transform identification. Memorise the kernels, separability, and orthogonality flags.

| Transform | Forward Kernel $K(x,y,u,v)$ | Separable | Orthogonal | Typical Medical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2-D DFT | $\exp\!\left[-j2\pi\!\left(\dfrac{ux}{M}+\dfrac{vy}{N}\right)\right]$ | Yes | Yes (Unitary) | MRI k-space, deblurring, periodic-noise removal |
| 2-D DCT | $\alpha(u)\alpha(v)\cos\!\left[\dfrac{\pi(2x+1)u}{2M}\right]\cos\!\left[\dfrac{\pi(2y+1)v}{2N}\right]$ | Yes | Yes | JPEG / JPEG2000 compression, DICOM storage |
| 2-D WHT (Walsh–Hadamard) | $\prod_{i}\prod_{j}(-1)^{b_{i}(x)b_{j}(u)}$ | Yes | Yes | Fast real-time edge detection, low-cost implants |
| 2-D DWT (Haar) | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{MN}}\,h_{a}(x)\,h_{b}(y)$ | Yes | Yes | Edge detection, denoising (US, MRI) |
| 2-D Continuous Wavelet | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a_{x}a_{y}}}\,\psi\!\left(\dfrac{x-x_{0}}{a_{x}},\dfrac{y-y_{0}}{a_{y}}\right)$ | No | Depends on $\psi$ | Multi-scale texture analysis, micro-calcification |
| Hough | $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ | N/A | N/A | Line / circle / ellipse detection in mammography |
| Radon | $R(\rho,\theta) = \iint f(x,y)\delta(\rho - x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta)\,dx\,dy$ | N/A | N/A | CT reconstruction, sinogram analysis |

Where:
- $\alpha(u) = \sqrt{1/M}$ for $u=0$ and $\sqrt{2/M}$ otherwise
- $b_i(\cdot)$ extracts the $i$-th bit
- $h_a, h_b$ are low-pass / high-pass filters

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Medical Field | Transform of Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **MRI Reconstruction** | 2-D DFT | Data natively acquired in k-space (Fourier domain) |
| **CT Reconstruction** | Radon + Filtered Back-Projection | Physics of X-ray attenuation = line integrals |
| **DICOM Image Compression** | DCT / Integer DWT | High ECR, industry standard (JPEG, JPEG2000) |
| **Mammography CAD** | Hough + DWT | Detect micro-calcifications (points) and spiculated masses (curves) |
| **Ultrasound De-noising** | Wavelet thresholding | Non-Gaussian speckle is well-separated in wavelet scales |
| **PET / SPECT Denoising** | Wavelet / Curvelet | Poisson noise → Anscombe → Wavelet shrinkage |
| **Retinal Vessel Detection** | Hough / Matched filter | Tubular structures in fundus images |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The 2-D Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)

The 2-D DFT is the cornerstone of medical image transforms. We derive it from the continuous 2-D Fourier transform by **sampling** in both spatial and frequency domains.

### Step 1 — Start with Continuous 2-D Fourier Transform

$$
F(\omega_{x},\omega_{y}) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y)\,e^{-j(\omega_{x}x + \omega_{y}y)}\,dx\,dy
$$

This is the *identification* of the image in the **continuous frequency domain**. Continuous variables $(\omega_x,\omega_y)$ have units of **radians per mm**.

### Step 2 — Sample the Continuous Image

Sample $f(x,y)$ on a uniform grid with $M$ columns and $N$ rows, spacing $\Delta x, \Delta y$:

$$
x \;=\; m\,\Delta x,\qquad y \;=\; n\,\Delta y,\qquad m=0,\ldots,M-1,\;\;n=0,\ldots,N-1
$$

Substitute into the integral and replace $dx\,dy$ with the cell area $\Delta x \Delta y$. The integral collapses to a double sum because the image is zero outside the grid:

$$
F(\omega_{x},\omega_{y}) \;\approx\; \Delta x\,\Delta y \sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m,n)\,e^{-j(\omega_{x}m\Delta x + \omega_{y}n\Delta y)}
$$

### Step 3 — Sample the Frequency Domain

To obtain a *discrete* output, sample the frequency plane at the same number of points:

$$
\omega_{x} \;=\; 2\pi u\,\Delta\omega_{x} \;=\; \frac{2\pi u}{M\,\Delta x},\qquad
\omega_{y} \;=\; 2\pi v\,\Delta\omega_{y} \;=\; \frac{2\pi v}{N\,\Delta y}
$$

with $u=0,\ldots,M-1$ and $v=0,\ldots,N-1$. Substitute:

$$
F(u,v) \;\approx\; \Delta x\,\Delta y \sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m,n)\,e^{-j\,2\pi\left(\dfrac{um}{M} + \dfrac{vn}{N}\right)}
$$

### Step 4 — Drop the Scaling Constant and Re-label

For digital implementation, the constant $\Delta x \Delta y$ is dropped (it merely scales the spectrum):

$$
F(u,v) \;=\; \sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m,n)\,e^{-j\,2\pi\left(\dfrac{um}{M} + \dfrac{vn}{N}\right)}
$$

### Step 5 — Exploit Separability

Note the kernel factors as a product of two 1-D DFTs:

$$
F(u,v) \;=\; \sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\left[\,e^{-j2\pi u m / M} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m,n)\,e^{-j2\pi v n / N}\,\right]
$$

This means a 2-D DFT can be computed as a **row-DFT followed by a column-DFT** (or vice versa), giving the famous complexity reduction:

$$
O(N^{2}\log N)\quad\text{instead of}\quad O(N^{4})
$$

### Step 6 — Inverse 2-D DFT

$$
f(m,n) \;=\; \frac{1}{MN}\sum_{u=0}^{M-1}\sum_{v=0}^{N-1} F(u,v)\,e^{+j\,2\pi\left(\dfrac{um}{M} + \dfrac{vn}{N}\right)}
$$

The conjugate symmetry property $F(M-u,N-v)=F^{*}(u,v)$ for real images halves the storage requirement — a key *identification property* exploited in MRI.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — The 2-D Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT)

The DCT is the **real-valued cousin** of the DFT and is identified whenever the image is real and even-extended. We derive the DCT-I variant (used in JPEG).

### Step 1 — Even-Extend the Image

Mirror the image $f(m,n)$ of size $M \times N$ to size $2M \times 2N$ to create an even-symmetric function $\tilde f$:

$$
\tilde f(m,n) \;=\; f\!\left(\vert m \mod 2M - M\vert,\;\vert n \mod 2N - N\vert\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Take the 2-D DFT of the Even-Extended Image

Because $\tilde f$ is real and even, its DFT is also real and even. A standard algebraic reduction (using the identity $e^{-j2\pi u m/2M} + e^{j2\pi u m/2M} = 2\cos(2\pi u m/2M)$) yields:

$$
F_{\text{DCT}}(u,v) \;=\; \alpha(u)\alpha(v)\sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m,n)\cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2m+1)u}{2M}\right]\cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)v}{2N}\right]
$$

with normalisation coefficients:

$$
\alpha(u) \;=\; \begin{cases} \sqrt{1/M}, & u=0 \\ \sqrt{2/M}, & u=1,2,\ldots,M-1 \end{cases},\qquad
\alpha(v) \;=\; \begin{cases} \sqrt{1/N}, & v=0 \\ \sqrt{2/N}, & v=1,2,\ldots,N-1 \end{cases}
$$

### Step 3 — Why This is a Good "Identification"

Because the cosine is the real part of the complex exponential, DCT coefficients are **always real**, halving storage vs. complex DFT. The energy compaction for natural (and most medical) images is **higher than DFT** because natural images have most information at low spatial frequencies, and the DCT basis functions have no discontinuities at the image boundaries — eliminating the **Gibbs phenomenon** (a major DFT pitfall).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identification Decision Tree (Algebraic)

> [!NOTE]
> This analytical matrix is what KTU examiners reward. It links **measurable image features** to **transform families** through mathematically stated criteria.

Let $H(\cdot)$ denote the 2-D histogram, $\mu$ the mean, $\sigma$ the standard deviation. Define the following measurable *identifiers*:

$$
I_{1} \;=\; \text{FFT}\{f\}\;\text{peak at origin} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{low-frequency dominant}
$$

$$
I_{2} \;=\; \frac{\text{Var}\!\left[F_{\text{DCT}}(u>u_{c},v>v_{c})\right]}{\text{Var}\!\left[F_{\text{DCT}}\right]} \;<\; 0.01 \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{DCT-suitable}
$$

$$
I_{3} \;=\; \text{Number of zero-crossings of } \psi_{a,b}*f \;>\; 0.3\,N \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Wavelet-suitable}
$$

$$
I_{4} \;=\; \max_{(\rho,\theta)} R(\rho,\theta) \;>\; 0.5\,\mu \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Radon-suitable (sinusoidal projection present)}
$$

$$
I_{5} \;=\; \text{Vote-count peak in } (\rho,\theta) \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Hough-suitable (line present)}
$$

### Decision Logic (Algebraic Form)

$$
\text{Transform}^{*} \;=\; \arg\max_{T \in \mathcal{T}}\; w_{1}\,I_{1}(T) \;+\; w_{2}\,I_{2}(T) \;+\; w_{3}\,I_{3}(T) \;+\; w_{4}\,I_{4}(T) \;+\; w_{5}\,I_{5}(T)
$$

where the weights $w_i$ reflect the medical task (e.g., $w_4$ is large in CT, $w_5$ is large in mammography CAD).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Transform Identification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Medical Image f of x,y] --> B[Stage 1 Feature Inventory]
    B --> B1[Edges Boundaries]
    B --> B2[Textures Parenchyma]
    B --> B3[Periodic Ribs Vessels]
    B --> B4[Point Microcalcifications]
    B --> B5[Lines Vessels]
    B --> B6[Projections Sinogram]

    B1 --> C[Stage 2 Noise Audit]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    B6 --> C

    C --> D[Stage 3 Constraint Check]
    D --> D1[Real Time Required]
    D --> D2[Lossless Compression]
    D --> D3[Reconstruction from Projections]

    D1 --> E[Stage 4 Basis Function Matching]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E

    E --> F1[DFT MRI kspace]
    E --> F2[DCT JPEG DICOM]
    E --> F3[WHT Walsh Hadamard]
    E --> F4[DWT Wavelet Denoising]
    E --> F5[CWT Multi Scale Texture]
    E --> F6[Hough Line Circle]
    E --> F7[Radon CT Reconstruction]

    F1 --> G[Stage 5 ECR Energy Compaction Test]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    F7 --> G

    G --> H[Stage 6 Computational Cost Validation]
    H --> I[Selected Transform Output F of u,v]
    I --> J[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Transform Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputStage[Input Stage]
        IMG[Medical Image f x y] --> PADD[Zero Padding to Power of 2]
    end

    subgraph TransformStage[Transform Stage]
        PADD --> KER[Apply Kernel K x y u v]
        KER --> COEFF[Compute Coefficients F u v]
    end

    subgraph IdentificationStage[Identification Stage]
        COEFF --> MET1[Energy Compaction Ratio ECR]
        COEFF --> MET2[Sparsity Metric L0 L1 Norm]
        COEFF --> MET3[Basis Feature Match Score]
        MET1 --> DEC{Decision Matrix}
        MET2 --> DEC
        MET3 --> DEC
    end

    subgraph OutputStage[Output Stage]
        DEC --> SEL[Selected Transform Tag]
        DEC --> RECON[Inverse Transform f hat]
        RECON --> EVAL[PSNR SSIM Evaluation]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Transform Selection Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Doc as Doctor
    participant Sys as Imaging System
    participant Tr as Transform Identifier
    participant Out as DICOM Archive

    Doc->>Sys: Order CT or MRI or US Scan
    Sys->>Tr: Acquire raw image f x y
    Tr->>Tr: Stage 1 to Stage 6 analysis
    Tr->>Tr: Compute ECR sparsity cost
    Tr->>Tr: Match basis to feature
    Tr->>Out: Apply chosen transform
    Out->>Sys: Store F u v plus metadata
    Sys->>Doc: Reconstructed diagnostic image
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define "transform identification" in the context of medical image processing. List the four key stages involved.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Transform identification is the systematic process of selecting and applying an appropriate mathematical transform (Fourier, Wavelet, DCT, Hough, or Radon) to a medical image so that diagnostically relevant features become most prominent in the transform domain.
>
> **Four Key Stages (2 marks):**
> 1. **Feature Inventory** — identify dominant structures (edges, textures, points, lines, projections)
> 2. **Noise Audit** — characterise noise statistics (Gaussian, Poisson, speckle)
> 3. **Basis-Function Matching** — match image feature geometry to transform kernel
> 4. **Energy Compaction Test** — measure sparseness using the energy compaction ratio (ECR)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why is DCT preferred over DFT for JPEG / DICOM compression of medical images?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **Real-valued coefficients (1 mark):** DCT produces only real values, halving storage vs. complex DFT.
> 2. **Boundary continuity (1 mark):** DCT's implicit even-extension eliminates Gibbs phenomenon (boundary artefacts) seen in DFT.
> 3. **Higher energy compaction (1 mark):** For natural medical images, DCT packs ≈ 90% of energy into the top 10% of coefficients, outperforming DFT.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> - Students frequently **forget the separability property** of 2-D DFT and DCT, which costs 2–3 marks.
> - Many write the kernel of the 2-D DFT with **wrong sign in the exponent** — both $+j$ and $-j$ are acceptable as long as forward and inverse are consistently opposite.
> - The **energy compaction ratio (ECR)** must always be quoted *with the percentage of coefficients used*. Writing "ECR = 0.92" without context is incomplete.
> - For Hough transform answers, students often **omit the polar-to-Cartesian back-mapping formula** $y = -\cot\theta \cdot x + \rho/\sin\theta$ — losing 2 marks.
> - Skipping the **inverse transform** when the question asks for "identification and reconstruction" costs a full 4-mark sub-part.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the 2-D Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) of a digital image $f(m,n)$ of size $M \times N$. Show all steps clearly and explicitly state the separability property. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $512 \times 512$ chest X-ray image $f(m,n)$ is suspected to contain a periodic grid artefact at spatial frequency 8 cycles/cm in the horizontal direction. Outline, with full justification, the **transform identification** procedure you would follow to confirm the artefact and to remove it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Continuous 2-D Fourier Transform (1 mark):**

$$
F(\omega_{x},\omega_{y}) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y)\,e^{-j(\omega_{x}x + \omega_{y}y)}\,dx\,dy
$$

**Step 2 — Spatial Sampling (1 mark):** Substitute $x = m\Delta x,\; y = n\Delta y$:

$$
F(\omega_{x},\omega_{y}) \;\approx\; \Delta x\,\Delta y \sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m,n)\,e^{-j(\omega_{x}m\Delta x + \omega_{y}n\Delta y)}
$$

**Step 3 — Frequency Sampling (1 mark):** Choose $\omega_{x} = 2\pi u / (M\Delta x),\; \omega_{y} = 2\pi v / (N\Delta y)$:

$$
F(u,v) \;\approx\; \Delta x\,\Delta y \sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m,n)\,e^{-j\,2\pi\left(\dfrac{um}{M} + \dfrac{vn}{N}\right)}
$$

**Step 4 — Drop Scaling (1 mark):** $F(u,v) = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} f(m,n)\,e^{-j2\pi(um/M + vn/N)}$

**Step 5 — Separability (2 marks):** Re-write as product of 1-D transforms:

$$
F(u,v) \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\left[\,e^{-j2\pi um/M} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m,n)\,e^{-j2\pi vn/N}\,\right]}_{\text{row-wise 1-D DFTs}} \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{m=0}^{M-1} e^{-j2\pi um/M}\,F_{\text{row}}(m,v)}_{\text{column-wise 1-D DFTs}}
$$

**Step 6 — Inverse (1 mark):**

$$
f(m,n) \;=\; \frac{1}{MN}\sum_{u=0}^{M-1}\sum_{v=0}^{N-1} F(u,v)\,e^{+j\,2\pi\left(\dfrac{um}{M} + \dfrac{vn}{N}\right)}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Identify the dominant feature (1 mark):** The artefact is *periodic* and *directional* (horizontal). This signature is the textbook domain of the **2-D DFT** (sinusoidal basis functions).

**Step 2 — Choose the transform (1 mark):** Compute $F(u,v) = \mathcal{F}\{f(m,n)\}$ using FFT. The period of 8 cycles/cm at a sampling pitch of 0.125 mm (4 cycles/pixel) corresponds to $u \approx M/8 \approx 64$ on the $u$-axis.

**Step 3 — Identify the spectral signature (2 marks):** In $|F(u,v)|$ we expect **two bright symmetric peaks** at $(u, v) = (\pm 64, 0)$ for a purely horizontal periodic artefact, with their complex conjugates ensuring real-valued reconstruction. The energy compaction ratio (ECR) over these 2 coefficients gives a clear identification metric.

**Step 4 — Design the notch reject filter (1 mark):** Construct a Butterworth notch reject filter:

$$
H_{\text{nr}}(u,v) \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + \left[\dfrac{D_{0}^{2}}{D_{1}(u,v)\,D_{2}(u,v)}\right]^{n}}
$$

where $D_{1}, D_{2}$ are distances from $(64,0)$ and $(-64,0)$ respectively.

**Step 5 — Remove artefact and reconstruct (1 mark):** Compute $G(u,v) = H_{\text{nr}}(u,v)\,F(u,v)$ and apply the inverse 2-D DFT to obtain the cleaned image $\hat f(m,n)$.

**Step 6 — Validate (1 mark):** Compute PSNR and SSIM between $\hat f$ and the original; check that diagnostically relevant features (ribs, mediastinum, lung fields) are preserved.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With reference to medical imaging, derive the 2-D Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) of an image $f(m,n)$. Explain why DCT gives **better energy compaction** than DFT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Hough transform** and the **Radon transform** for identifying linear and curvilinear structures in medical images. State one diagnostic application of each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Even-extension construction (2 marks):** Mirror $f$ to obtain $\tilde f$ of size $2M \times 2N$:

$$
\tilde f(m,n) \;=\; f(\vert m \bmod 2M - M \vert,\; \vert n \bmod 2N - N \vert)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply 2-D DFT to $\tilde f$ (2 marks):** Use $e^{-j\theta}+e^{+j\theta}=2\cos\theta$ to convert complex exponentials to real cosines:

$$
F_{\text{DCT}}(u,v) \;=\; \alpha(u)\alpha(v) \sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m,n) \cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2m+1)u}{2M}\right]\cos\!\left[\frac{\pi(2n+1)v}{2N}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — State normalisation (1 mark):** $\alpha(0)=\sqrt{1/M}$, $\alpha(u\neq 0)=\sqrt{2/M}$.

**Step 4 — Separability (1 mark):** Cosine factors as product of 1-D DCTs — row then column.

**Step 5 — Energy compaction justification (1 mark):** For natural medical images, the **autocorrelation decays smoothly** with lag. The cosine basis aligns with this smoothness better than the discontinuous complex-exponential wrap-around of the DFT, so coefficients decay faster away from $(0,0)$. Empirical ECR(10%) ≈ 0.92 for DCT vs 0.85 for DFT.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

| Aspect (Marks) | Hough Transform | Radon Transform |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition (1)** | Maps each image point to a sinusoid in $(\rho,\theta)$ accumulator; peak = line | Integrates image intensity along a line at angle $\theta$ and offset $\rho$: $R(\rho,\theta) = \iint f(x,y)\,\delta(\rho - x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta)\,dx\,dy$ |
| **Output space (1)** | Accumulator array (votes) | Sinogram $R(\rho,\theta)$ |
| **Detects (1)** | Lines, circles, ellipses (after parameterisation) | All line integrals through object |
| **Inverse (1)** | Back-map peak $(\rho,\theta)$ to line $y = -\cot\theta \cdot x + \rho/\sin\theta$ | Filtered back-projection (FBP) reconstructs $f(x,y)$ |
| **Computational cost (1)** | $O(N_\theta \cdot N_\rho \cdot N_{\text{edges}})$ | $O(N^{3})$ direct; $O(N^{2}\log N)$ via FFT (Central Slice Theorem) |
| **Diagnostic application (1)** | Mammographic micro-calcification cluster detection, retinal vessel segmentation | **CT reconstruction** from X-ray projections |
| **Invertibility for image reconstruction (1)** | Not used for full reconstruction | Yes — basis of CT scanners |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Transform Identification:**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Transform identification = choosing the right mathematical kernel to expose hidden image features.
- ⭐ **Six-stage pipeline:** Feature → Noise → Constraint → Basis-Match → ECR → Cost.
- ⭐ **DFT kernel:** $e^{-j2\pi(um/M + vn/N)}$ — complex, periodic, separable.
- ⭐ **DCT kernel:** $\alpha(u)\alpha(v)\cos[\pi(2m+1)u/(2M)]\cos[\pi(2n+1)v/(2N)]$ — real, boundary-clean, higher ECR.
- ⭐ **DFT separability** ⇒ row-then-column 1-D FFTs ⇒ $O(N^{2}\log N)$.
- ⭐ **Hough:** point in image ↔ sinusoid in $(\rho,\theta)$; **Radon:** image ↔ sinogram.
- ⭐ **Wavelet advantage:** Multi-resolution, $O(N)$ complexity, ideal for non-Gaussian medical noise (Poisson, speckle).
- ⭐ **ECR formula:** $\text{ECR} = \sum_{(u,v) \in S_k} \vert F(u,v)\vert^{2} \,/\, \sum_{u,v} \vert F(u,v)\vert^{2}$ — always state the % of coefficients used.
- ⭐ **MRI is naturally DFT-based** (k-space); **CT is naturally Radon-based** (projection-slice theorem).
- ⭐ **DCT is the JPEG / DICOM standard** because of real-valued, high-ECR, separable kernel.
- ⭐ **Gibbs phenomenon** is a DFT-specific pitfall; DCT avoids it via even-extension.
- ⭐ **Inverse transform** must be mentioned whenever the question mentions *reconstruction* — losing 4 marks otherwise.
- ⭐ **Energy Compaction:** DCT > DWT > DFT for natural medical images; Walsh–Hadamard is fastest but lowest ECR.
- ⭐ **Walsh–Hadamard** uses $\pm 1$ entries only — fastest transform for hardware-implant / point-of-care devices.
- ⭐ **Hough vs Radon:** Hough is for *detecting geometric primitives*; Radon is for *inverting projections to reconstruct* the image.
- ⭐ **Real-time implants / wearables:** prefer Walsh–Hadamard or short-length Haar DWT.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Registration Evaluation and Approval
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Registration Evaluation and Approval

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Medical Image Registration?

**Medical Image Registration** is the process of spatially aligning two or more medical images of the same anatomical region acquired at different times, from different modalities (CT, MRI, PET, USG), or from different viewpoints, into a single unified coordinate system so that corresponding anatomical structures overlap with sub-voxel accuracy.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, registration evaluation refers to the **quantitative and qualitative assessment** of the alignment quality, while **approval** refers to the regulatory and clinical validation pathway that allows a registration algorithm to be used in diagnostic or therapeutic workflows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> Registration is the estimation of a geometric transformation $T$ that maps points in a *floating* (moving) image $I_F(x,y,z)$ to corresponding points in a *reference* (fixed) image $I_R(x,y,z)$ such that $I_R(x) \approx I_F(T(x))$ for all spatial coordinates $x$ in the image domain.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Registration Pipeline

1. **Registration** — The computational alignment algorithm itself.
2. **Evaluation** — Quantitative metrics (TRE, Dice, MI, SSD) and qualitative visual checks.
3. **Approval** — Regulatory clearance (FDA 510(k), CE Mark, CDSCO) and clinical acceptance.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Two-Map Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine you have two paper maps of Kerala drawn by different surveyors in 1850 and 2024. The rivers are in slightly different positions, the scale is different, and one map is rotated by 3°. **Registration** is the process of finding the rotation, scaling, and translation that best overlays the 1850 map onto the 2024 map. **Evaluation** is measuring how well the rivers now overlap (are they within 2 mm of each other?). **Approval** is the Survey of India officially certifying that the merged map is fit for navigation.

In medical imaging, instead of paper maps, the "rivers" are tumors, blood vessels, or bone structures, and the "surveyors" are CT, MRI, PET, or ultrasound machines.

## 1.4 Why Registration Matters in Clinical Workflow

| Clinical Task | Registration Use |
|---|---|
| **Image-Guided Surgery (IGS)** | Pre-op MRI fused with intra-op CT to guide neuronavigation |
| **Radiotherapy Planning** | CT-MRI fusion for accurate dose delivery to tumor while sparing organs-at-risk |
| **Disease Monitoring** | Longitudinal MRI registration to track tumor growth across months |
| **Multi-modal Diagnosis** | PET-MRI fusion to overlay metabolic activity on anatomical detail |
| **Atlas-Based Segmentation** | Patient image registered to a labeled anatomical atlas |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard:** The clinical benchmark for rigid registration accuracy in image-guided neurosurgery is **Target Registration Error (TRE) ≤ 2 mm**, as defined by the **American Society of Neurological Surgeons (AANS)** guidelines.

---

## 1.5 The Registration Output

A successful registration produces:

- A **Transformation Matrix** $T$ (4×4 homogeneous matrix in 3D, 3×3 in 2D)
- A **Resampled Image** $I_{resampled}(x) = I_F(T^{-1}(x))$
- An **Evaluation Score** $S$ indicating alignment quality

---

## 1.6 Evaluation vs Approval — The Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students often confuse "evaluation" with "approval". Evaluation is a **technical/engineering** measure of registration accuracy (numbers and metrics). Approval is a **regulatory/governance** decision by bodies like the **FDA, CE, or ICMR/CDSCO** that certifies the software/hardware for clinical use.

---

## 1.7 Registration Workflow Overview

The standard KTU-recommended workflow consists of **four sequential stages**:

1. **Feature Detection** — Identify landmarks, surfaces, or voxel intensities.
2. **Feature Matching** — Establish correspondences between features in the two images.
3. **Transformation Model Estimation** — Compute the mapping function $T$.
4. **Image Resampling & Transformation** — Apply $T$ to the moving image and interpolate.

> [!NOTE]
> Evaluation metrics are computed *after* Stage 4, while approval is sought *before* the algorithm is deployed clinically (validation phase) and *after* (post-market surveillance).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Framework of Registration

Given a reference image $I_R$ and a floating image $I_F$, registration finds a transformation $T: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^n$ that minimizes a dissimilarity function $\mathcal{C}$:

$$T^* = \arg\min_T \, \mathcal{C}(I_R, I_F \circ T)$$

where $I_F \circ T$ denotes the transformed floating image, and $T^*$ is the optimal transformation.

## 2.2 The Four Building Blocks of Registration

### Block 1: Transformation Model $T$

| Model Type | DOF (2D / 3D) | Use Case | Equation Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rigid** | 3 / 6 | Bone, skull | $T(x) = R x + t$ |
| **Similarity** | 4 / 7 | Pre/post-op comparison | $T(x) = s R x + t$ |
| **Affine** | 6 / 12 | Compensation for scaling/shear | $T(x) = A x + t$ |
| **Projective** | 8 / 15 | 2D X-ray to 3D | $T(x) = (A x + t) / (w^T x + 1)$ |
| **Deformable (B-spline, Demons)** | $> 12$ | Soft tissue, organ warping | Non-parametric field $\phi(x)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** The **rigid transformation in 3D** is the most-tested concept, having **6 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**: 3 translations $(t_x, t_y, t_z)$ and 3 rotations $(\theta_x, \theta_y, \theta_z)$.

### Block 2: Similarity / Cost Function $\mathcal{C}$

The cost function quantifies "how badly" the images are aligned. Lower $\mathcal{C}$ ⇒ better alignment.

| Metric | Formula | Best Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Mean Squared Difference (MSD/SSD)** | $\mathcal{C}_{MSD} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i (I_R(x_i) - I_F(T(x_i)))^2$ | Mono-modal, same patient |
| **Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC)** | $\mathcal{C}_{NCC} = \frac{\sum_i (I_R - \bar{I}_R)(I_F - \bar{I}_F)}{\sqrt{\sum (I_R-\bar{I}_R)^2 \sum (I_F-\bar{I}_F)^2}}$ | Mono-modal, intensity scaling |
| **Mutual Information (MI)** | $\mathcal{C}_{MI} = H(I_R) + H(I_F) - H(I_R, I_F)$ | Multi-modal (CT-MRI) |
| **Normalized MI (NMI)** | $\mathcal{C}_{NMI} = \frac{H(I_R) + H(I_F)}{H(I_R, I_F)}$ | Robust multi-modal |
| **Sum of Squared Differences (SSD)** | Same as MSD but unnormalized | Mono-modal baseline |

### Block 3: Optimization Strategy

| Optimizer | Type | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **Gradient Descent** | Local | Smooth cost functions |
| **Powell's Method** | Local, derivative-free | Initial alignment |
| **Levenberg-Marquardt** | Local, second-order | Landmark-based |
| **Simplex (Nelder-Mead)** | Local, derivative-free | MI maximization |
| **Genetic Algorithm** | Global | Deformable registration |

### Block 4: Interpolation Method

| Method | Order | Speed | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nearest Neighbor** | 0 | Fastest | Lowest |
| **Bilinear / Trilinear** | 1 | Fast | Moderate |
| **B-spline (Cubic)** | 3 | Slow | High (clinical standard) |
| **Sinc Interpolation** | ∞ | Slowest | Highest |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Trilinear interpolation is the clinical standard in most FDA-approved systems because it offers the best trade-off between computational speed and accuracy (typical sub-voxel registration precision of **0.1–0.3 mm**).

## 2.3 Evaluation Metrics — The Quantitative Side

### 2.3.1 Target Registration Error (TRE)

The gold standard for measuring registration accuracy. Given $N$ target landmarks with known ground-truth positions $\{x_i^{GT}\}$, and the registration-estimated positions $\{x_i^{est}\} = T(x_i^{measured})$:

$$\text{TRE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left\vert x_i^{GT} - x_i^{est} \right\vert^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units:** TRE is in **millimeters (mm)**. For neurosurgical IGS, the FDA-cleared benchmark is **TRE ≤ 2 mm** at 95% confidence.

### 2.3.2 Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)

For evaluating registration when a segmentation of an anatomical structure is available:

$$\text{DSC} = \frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$$

where $A$ is the ground-truth segmentation and $B$ is the warped segmentation. DSC ranges from 0 (no overlap) to 1 (perfect overlap).

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Threshold:** A DSC ≥ **0.70** is generally accepted as "good" overlap in radiotherapy contouring studies.

### 2.3.3 Mutual Information (MI) — Detailed

The MI between $I_R$ and $I_F$ is:

$$MI(I_R, I_F) = \sum_{a \in I_R} \sum_{b \in I_F} p(a, b) \log_2 \frac{p(a, b)}{p(a) \cdot p(b)}$$

where $p(a)$ and $p(b)$ are marginal histograms, and $p(a,b)$ is the joint histogram of the overlapping intensity pairs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** MI measures the **information shared** between the two images. Higher MI = better alignment. MI is **maximized** (not minimized) during registration when used as the cost function.

## 2.4 The Approval Pathway

### 2.4.1 Regulatory Bodies

| Region | Authority | Pathway |
|---|---|---|
| **USA** | FDA (CDRH) | 510(k) clearance for Class II |
| **Europe** | CE Mark (MDR 2017/745) | Class IIa / IIb |
| **India** | CDSCO (under ICMR) | MD-14 licensing |
| **Global** | IEC 62304 | Software lifecycle standard |

### 2.4.2 The V-Model for Software Approval

The IEC 62304 standard requires medical device software to follow a V-model:

1. **Software Requirements Specification (SRS)**
2. **Software Architecture & Detailed Design**
3. **Unit Implementation**
4. **Unit Verification**
5. **Integration Testing**
6. **System Testing**
7. **Clinical Evaluation (CER)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The "Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)" is the document that ties registration algorithm performance (e.g., TRE values) to clinical outcomes (e.g., surgical margin accuracy).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rigid 3D Transformation | $T(x) = R_{3\times 3} \cdot x + t_{3\times 1}$ | 6 DOF |
| 2 | Affine Transformation | $T(x) = A_{3\times 3} \cdot x + t$ | 12 DOF in 3D |
| 3 | MSD Cost Function | $\frac{1}{N}\sum (I_R - I_F \circ T)^2$ | Unitless |
| 4 | NCC Cost Function | $\frac{\sum (I_R-\bar{I}_R)(I_F-\bar{I}_F)}{\sqrt{\sum(I_R-\bar{I}_R)^2 \sum(I_F-\bar{I}_F)^2}}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$ |
| 5 | MI Cost Function | $\sum p(a,b) \log_2 \frac{p(a,b)}{p(a)p(b)}$ | Bits |
| 6 | Target Registration Error (TRE) | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum \left\vert x^{GT} - x^{est} \right\vert^2}$ | mm |
| 7 | Dice Similarity Coefficient | $\frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Range $[0, 1]$ |
| 8 | Hausdorff Distance | $\max(\max_{a\in A} \min_{b\in B} d(a,b), \max_{b\in B} \min_{a\in A} d(b,a))$ | mm |
| 9 | Jacobian Determinant | $\det(J_\phi(x))$ | Volume change measure |
| 10 | Fiducial Localization Error (FLE) | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2}$ | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Remember:** Dice, MI, NCC are all *similarity* measures (higher = better). MSD, SSD, RMSE are *dissimilarity* measures (lower = better). Students often mix this up in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The 3D Rigid Transformation Matrix

### 3.1.1 Starting Point

A rigid transformation in 3D combines a **rotation** $R$ (3×3 orthonormal, $\det(R) = 1$) and a **translation** $t$ (3×1 vector). In homogeneous coordinates:

$$T_{4\times 4} = \begin{bmatrix} R_{3\times 3} & t_{3\times 1} \\ 0_{1\times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.1.2 Building the Rotation Matrix

We construct $R$ as a product of three elementary rotations about the $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ axes:

**Step 1: Rotation about X-axis by angle $\theta_x$**

$$R_x(\theta_x) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\theta_x & -\sin\theta_x \\ 0 & \sin\theta_x & \cos\theta_x \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Rotation about Y-axis by angle $\theta_y$**

$$R_y(\theta_y) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_y & 0 & \sin\theta_y \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta_y & 0 & \cos\theta_y \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Rotation about Z-axis by angle $\theta_z$**

$$R_z(\theta_z) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_z & -\sin\theta_z & 0 \\ \sin\theta_z & \cos\theta_z & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Combined Rotation (ZYX Tait-Bryan convention)**

The full rotation is the matrix product:

$$R = R_z(\theta_z) \cdot R_y(\theta_y) \cdot R_x(\theta_x)$$

**Step 5: Apply to a generic 3D point $p = (x, y, z)^T$**

$$p' = R \cdot p + t$$

This can be written in homogeneous form as:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ z' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R_{3\times 3} & t_{3\times 1} \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.1.3 Verification: Number of DOF

The 3×3 rotation $R$ has **9 elements**, but constrained by orthonormality gives **3 DOF** ($\theta_x, \theta_y, \theta_z$). The translation $t$ has **3 DOF** ($t_x, t_y, t_z$). Total rigid DOF = **3 + 3 = 6 DOF**. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean Squared Difference Cost Function

### 3.2.1 Setup

We have $N$ corresponding voxel pairs between reference and transformed floating images.

**Step 1: Define the residual for the $i$-th voxel**

$$r_i = I_R(x_i) - I_F(T(x_i))$$

**Step 2: Square the residual (to remove sign)**

$$r_i^2 = (I_R(x_i) - I_F(T(x_i)))^2$$

**Step 3: Sum over all $N$ voxels**

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (I_R(x_i) - I_F(T(x_i)))^2$$

**Step 4: Normalize by $N$ to get average error per voxel**

$$\text{MSD}(T) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (I_R(x_i) - I_F(T(x_i)))^2$$

**Step 5: Registration is the optimization problem**

$$T^* = \arg\min_T \, \text{MSD}(T)$$

**Step 6: Take square root to express in original intensity units (RMSE)**

$$\text{RMSE}(T) = \sqrt{\text{MSD}(T)} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (I_R(x_i) - I_F(T(x_i)))^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MSD is mono-modal only:** MSD assumes that $I_R(x) \approx I_F(T(x))$ with similar intensity values — true only for same-modality pairs (e.g., MRI-MRI). For CT-MRI pairs, MI must be used instead.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Target Registration Error (TRE) from FLE and FRE

### 3.3.1 Definitions

- **FLE (Fiducial Localization Error)**: Error in locating a single fiducial marker in either image. Each localization has zero-mean Gaussian error with variance $\sigma^2$.
- **FRE (Fiducial Registration Error)**: RMS distance between corresponding fiducials *after* registration.
- **TRE (Target Registration Error)**: RMS distance at an *anatomical target* (not a fiducial).

### 3.3.2 Theoretical Relationship (Fitzpatrick, 1998)

For $N$ fiducials, the expected squared TRE at a point $r$ is:

$$\langle \text{TRE}^2(r) \rangle \approx \frac{\sigma^2}{N} \left( 1 + \frac{1}{3} \sum_{k=1}^{3} \frac{d_k^2}{f_k^2} \right)$$

where:
- $d_k$ = distance from target $r$ to the $k$-th principal axis of the fiducial configuration
- $f_k$ = RMS distance of fiducials from the $k$-th principal axis
- $N$ = number of fiducials

**Step-by-step interpretation:**

1. $1/N$ factor: More fiducials ⇒ lower TRE.
2. $1/3$ coefficient: From statistical averaging over 3 spatial dimensions.
3. $d_k^2 / f_k^2$ ratio: TRE grows when the target is *far* from the centroid of fiducials relative to their *spread*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Place fiducials as **far apart as possible** (maximize $f_k$) and as **close to the target** as feasible (minimize $d_k$). This is why bone-anchored screws in skull neurosurgery outperform skin-adhesive markers.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Registration Methods at a Glance

| Method | Feature Type | Transformation | Speed | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Intensity-based MI** | All voxels | Rigid / Affine | Slow | High (multimodal) |
| **Landmark-based** | Anatomical points | Rigid / Affine | Fast | Depends on landmark count |
| **Surface-based (ICP)** | Mesh vertices | Rigid | Moderate | High (bone) |
| **Demons (deformable)** | All voxels | Deformable | Slow | High (soft tissue) |
| **B-spline FFD** | Control points | Deformable | Slowest | Highest |
| **Deep Learning (VoxelMorph)** | Learned | Deformable | Very fast (inference) | High |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** Two 2D CT slices of the same patient are registered using 4 fiducial markers. The post-registration residual distances at the markers are 0.8 mm, 1.2 mm, 0.5 mm, and 0.7 mm. Compute the FRE and comment on clinical acceptability.

**Step 1: Identify the residual distances**
$$d_1 = 0.8, \quad d_2 = 1.2, \quad d_3 = 0.5, \quad d_4 = 0.7 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Square each residual**

$$d_1^2 = 0.64, \quad d_2^2 = 1.44, \quad d_3^2 = 0.25, \quad d_4^2 = 0.49 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Sum the squares**

$$S = 0.64 + 1.44 + 0.25 + 0.49 = 2.82 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Divide by $N = 4$**

$$\frac{S}{N} = \frac{2.82}{4} = 0.705 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Take square root**

$$\text{FRE} = \sqrt{0.705} \approx 0.84 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Clinical interpretation**

Since FRE = 0.84 mm, and using the rule of thumb **TRE ≈ (2/3)·FRE** for well-spread fiducials:

$$\text{TRE} \approx \frac{2}{3} \times 0.84 \approx 0.56 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verdict:** TRE = 0.56 mm is **well below the 2 mm** neurosurgical benchmark. The registration is **clinically acceptable** for IGS.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Registration-Evaluation-Approval Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Image Acquisition\nCT / MRI / PET / USG] --> B[Preprocessing\nDenoising, Bias Correction, Skull Stripping]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction\nLandmarks / Surfaces / Voxels]
    C --> D[Initial Alignment\nCentroid + Principal Axis Coarse Registration]
    D --> E[Transformation Model Selection\nRigid / Affine / Deformable]
    E --> F[Cost Function Computation\nMSD / NCC / MI / NMI]
    F --> G[Optimizer\nGradient Descent / Powell / Levenberg Marquardt]
    G --> H{Converged?}
    H -- No --> F
    H -- Yes --> I[Image Resampling\nTrilinear / B-spline Interpolation]
    I --> J[Evaluation Metrics\nTRE, Dice, MI, Hausdorff Distance]
    J --> K{Accuracy Acceptable?\nTRE less than 2 mm}
    K -- No --> L[Algorithm Refinement\nRetune Parameters]
    L --> E
    K -- Yes --> M[Clinical Validation\nRetrospective and Prospective Study]
    M --> N[Regulatory Documentation\nIEC 62304, ISO 13485, CER]
    N --> O[Submission to FDA 510k\nor CE Mark or CDSCO]
    O --> P{Approval Granted?}
    P -- No --> Q[Address Deficiencies\nfrom Regulator]
    Q --> N
    P -- Yes --> R[Clinical Deployment\nImage Guided Surgery PACS Integration]
    R --> S[Post Market Surveillance\nPeriodic Performance Audit]
    S --> T[Feedback Loop to Improve Algorithm]
    T --> E
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: The Registration Algorithm Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["Input Stage"]
        IR[Reference Image\nI sub R]
        IF[Floating Image\nI sub F]
    end
    subgraph Init["Initialization"]
        T0[Initial Transform\nT0 Identity]
    end
    subgraph Loop["Iterative Optimization Loop"]
        W[Apply Current Transform T\nto Floating Image]
        S[Compute Cost Function\nC equals MSD or MI]
        O[Compute Gradient\ndelta C by delta T]
        U[Update Transform\nT new equals T old minus alpha delta C]
    end
    subgraph Output["Output Stage"]
        TRES[Transformed Image\nI sub F composed with T]
        TEVAL[Evaluation Metrics\nTRE Dice MI]
    end
    IR --> S
    IF --> W
    T0 --> W
    W --> S
    S --> O
    O --> U
    U --> W
    S --> TRES
    U --> TEVAL
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Approval Pathway Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Registration Algorithm\nor Device] --> B{Intended Use}
    B -- Diagnostic --> C[FDA Class II\n510k Pathway]
    B -- Therapeutic\nImage Guided Surgery --> D[FDA Class II\nPremarket Notification 510k]
    B -- Radiotherapy Dose Planning --> E[FDA Class II\nor Class III PMA]
    C --> F[Clinical Performance Testing\nTRE and Dice Benchmarks]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[TRE less than 2 mm\nDSC greater than 0.80\non Validation Set]
    G -- Pass --> H[Submit to Regulatory Body\nwith CER Clinical Evaluation Report]
    G -- Fail --> I[Algorithm Refinement]
    I --> F
    H --> J{FDA or CE or CDSCO Review}
    J -- Approved --> K[Market Launch]
    J -- Rejected --> L[Address Deficiency Letter]
    L --> H
    K --> M[Post Market Surveillance\nAERS Adverse Event Reporting]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above visualize the **three-layer architecture** of the topic: (1) the technical registration-iteration loop, (2) the clinical evaluation gate, and (3) the regulatory approval pipeline. Together, they form the complete KTU 2024 Module 1 framework for "Registration Evaluation and Approval".

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Medical Image Registration and List Its Three Major Clinical Applications. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Medical image registration is the process of spatially aligning two or more medical images (often from different modalities such as CT and MRI, or acquired at different times) into a single coordinate system such that corresponding anatomical structures overlap with sub-voxel accuracy.

**Three clinical applications:**
1. **Image-Guided Surgery (IGS):** Pre-operative MRI registered with intra-operative CT to guide neurosurgical resection.
2. **Radiotherapy Treatment Planning:** CT-MRI fusion for accurate tumor targeting and organ-at-risk sparing.
3. **Longitudinal Disease Monitoring:** Sequential MRI scans registered to track tumor growth or shrinkage over time.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate Between FLE, FRE, and TRE in Image Registration. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Full Form | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **FLE** | Fiducial Localization Error | Error in detecting the 3D position of a single fiducial marker in either image |
| **FRE** | Fiducial Registration Error | RMS distance between corresponding fiducial points *after* registration |
| **TRE** | Target Registration Error | RMS distance between corresponding points at the actual *anatomical target* (not fiducial) |

> **Key relation:** TRE depends on FLE, number of fiducials $N$, and the geometric configuration. The clinical benchmark is **TRE ≤ 2 mm** for image-guided neurosurgery.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain the mathematical framework of medical image registration with the help of a cost function. Derive the Mean Squared Difference (MSD) cost function and state the conditions under which it is optimal. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** With neat block diagrams, describe the step-by-step workflow of a rigid intensity-based registration algorithm. Discuss the role of the optimizer and the interpolation method. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Mathematical Framework and MSD Derivation (7 marks):**

The registration problem is formulated as an optimization:

$$T^* = \arg\min_T \, \mathcal{C}(I_R, I_F \circ T)$$

**Step 1: Define the residual at the $i$-th voxel** (1 mark)

$$r_i = I_R(x_i) - I_F(T(x_i))$$

**Step 2: Square to remove sign** (1 mark)

$$r_i^2 = (I_R(x_i) - I_F(T(x_i)))^2$$

**Step 3: Sum over all voxels** (1 mark)

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} r_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (I_R(x_i) - I_F(T(x_i)))^2$$

**Step 4: Normalize by $N$ to obtain MSD** (1 mark)

$$\text{MSD}(T) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (I_R(x_i) - I_F(T(x_i)))^2$$

**Step 5: Optimality conditions** (3 marks)

MSD is optimal under the following conditions:
1. **Mono-modal imaging** — both images have similar intensity scales (e.g., MRI-MRI, CT-CT).
2. **Linear intensity relationship** — $I_R = a \cdot I_F + b$ for constants $a, b$.
3. **Gaussian noise assumption** — voxel intensities are corrupted by zero-mean i.i.d. Gaussian noise.
4. **No outliers** — no foreign objects, surgical clips, or contrast agent present.

When these are violated (e.g., CT-MRI), use **Mutual Information** instead.

---

**(b) Rigid Intensity-Based Registration Workflow (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Input images** (1 mark) — Reference $I_R$ and Floating $I_F$.

**Step 2: Initial alignment** (1 mark) — Apply centroid alignment and principal-axis matching to bring images into coarse overlap.

**Step 3: Transformation model** (1 mark) — Choose rigid 3D model with 6 DOF:

$$T(x) = R \cdot x + t$$

**Step 4: Cost function evaluation** (1 mark) — Compute MSD or MI for current $T$.

**Step 5: Optimization** (2 marks) — Iteratively update $T$ using Powell's or Gradient Descent method to minimize cost. The optimizer explores the 6-DOF parameter space $(\theta_x, \theta_y, \theta_z, t_x, t_y, t_z)$ until convergence.

**Step 6: Resampling and interpolation** (1 mark) — Apply final $T$ to $I_F$ using **trilinear interpolation** to obtain the resampled image.

**Role of interpolation:** Since $T(x)$ often lands between voxel centers, interpolation is required to estimate intensity. Trilinear interpolation uses the 8 surrounding voxels (in 3D) with linear weights.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**
> 
> **(a)** What is Target Registration Error (TRE)? Derive its relationship with FLE and the number of fiducials $N$. Why is TRE considered the clinical gold standard over FRE? **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Discuss the regulatory approval pathway for a medical image registration software in India (CDSCO) and the United States (FDA). Mention the relevant ISO/IEC standards that govern the software lifecycle. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) TRE Definition and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Definition** (1 mark)

TRE is the RMS distance between the *true anatomical target position* $r_k$ and the position predicted by the registration $T(r_k)$:

$$\text{TRE}^2(r) = \frac{1}{N_t} \sum_{k=1}^{N_t} \left\vert r_k - T(r_k) \right\vert^2$$

**Step 2: Statistical model of FLE** (1 mark) — Each fiducial localization has zero-mean Gaussian error with covariance $\sigma^2 I$.

**Step 3: Fitzpatrick derivation** (2 marks) — Expected squared TRE for a target point $r$:

$$\langle \text{TRE}^2(r) \rangle \approx \frac{\sigma^2}{N} \left( 1 + \frac{1}{3} \sum_{k=1}^{3} \frac{d_k^2}{f_k^2} \right)$$

where $d_k$ is the distance from target to $k$-th principal axis of fiducials, and $f_k$ is the RMS spread of fiducials along axis $k$.

**Step 4: Interpretation** (2 marks)
- TRE $\propto 1/\sqrt{N}$ → more fiducials reduce error.
- TRE increases when target is far from fiducial centroid.
- FRE is **not** TRE — FRE measures alignment at fiducials themselves; TRE measures accuracy at the actual clinical target.

**Step 5: Why TRE is the gold standard** (1 mark) — Because surgeons care about accuracy at the tumor or anatomical target, not at the fiducial screws. FRE can be deceptively low while TRE remains clinically unacceptable.

---

**(b) Regulatory Approval Pathway (7 marks):**

**India (CDSCO)** (3 marks)
