- Governed by the **Medical Device Rules 2017** under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act.
- Registration software typically falls under **Class B or Class C** depending on risk.
- Requires **MD-14 license** for manufacture/import.
- **ICMR guidelines** mandate clinical evaluation prior to approval.

**United States (FDA)** (2 marks)
- **FDA Center for Devices and Radiological Health (CDRH)** reviews medical software.
- Most registration tools fall under **Class II** → **510(k) Premarket Notification**.
- Clinical performance data: TRE ≤ 2 mm, DSC ≥ 0.80 on validation set.

**ISO/IEC Standards** (2 marks)
- **IEC 62304** — Medical device software lifecycle processes.
- **ISO 13485** — Quality management systems for medical devices.
- **ISO 14971** — Application of risk management to medical devices.
- **IEC 62366-1** — Usability engineering for medical devices.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Registration Evaluation and Approval":**
> 
> 1. **Confusing evaluation with approval (–2 marks typical):** Evaluation = technical metrics (TRE, Dice). Approval = regulatory clearance. Examiners expect both explained distinctly.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the units of TRE (–1 mark):** Always state TRE in **millimeters (mm)**. Writing only a formula without units loses a mark.
> 
> 3. **Not stating optimality conditions for MSD (–2 marks):** Simply deriving MSD without listing the Gaussian/mono-modal assumptions is incomplete.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up DOF counts (–1 mark):** Rigid = 6 DOF, Affine = 12 DOF, Similarity = 7 DOF. Get this wrong and the entire transformation question collapses.
> 
> 5. **Skipping interpolation discussion (–1 mark):** Any question on "applying the transformation" must mention trilinear or B-spline interpolation explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Registration** is the estimation of a spatial transformation $T$ that aligns two medical images to minimize a cost function $\mathcal{C}$.
- The **rigid 3D transformation** has **6 DOF** (3 rotations + 3 translations) and is the most clinically common.
- The **registration pipeline** is: Feature Detection → Matching → Transformation Estimation → Resampling.
- **MSD** is optimal only for **mono-modal, Gaussian-noise, linear-intensity** pairs. For multi-modal (CT-MRI), use **Mutual Information**.
- **Mutual Information (MI)** is maximized (not minimized) and is the gold standard for multi-modal registration.
- **TRE** is the clinical gold standard; **FRE** is misleading because it does not reflect target accuracy.
- The benchmark **TRE ≤ 2 mm** is the FDA-cleared threshold for image-guided neurosurgery.
- **Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)** ranges from 0 to 1; ≥ 0.70 is "good" clinical overlap.
- **Approval pathway** depends on regulatory region: FDA 510(k) for USA, CE Mark under MDR for Europe, CDSCO for India.
- **IEC 62304** is the international standard governing medical device software lifecycle, including registration algorithms.
- **Fitzpatrick's relation** shows that TRE is reduced by increasing the number of fiducials $N$ and by placing them far apart and close to the target.
- **Trilinear interpolation** is the standard resampling method in most FDA-approved IGS systems.
- The **Clinical Evaluation Report (CER)** is the document that bridges the technical evaluation (TRE values) with the regulatory approval decision.
- **Deformable registration** is required for soft-tissue alignment; rigid registration is sufficient for bony anatomy.
- **Optimizers** (Powell, Levenberg-Marquardt) are derivative-free or gradient-based methods that iteratively update $T$ to minimize the cost.
- **Post-market surveillance** is a continuing obligation even after approval; algorithms must be re-validated periodically.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Fusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Image Fusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Image Fusion** is the process of combining significant information from two or more source images of the same scene or object, acquired from different sensors, modalities, or at different time instants, to produce a single composite image that is more informative, accurate, and complete than any of the individual input images.

In the context of **medical imaging**, image fusion specifically refers to the algorithmic registration and superimposition of multimodal datasets (e.g., **CT** for anatomical structure and **MRI** for soft-tissue contrast, or **PET** for functional/metabolic activity) into a unified visual representation that aids in clinical diagnosis, surgical planning, and radiotherapy targeting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Context):**
> "Image fusion is a sub-domain of image processing that combines multiple input images into a fewer number of output images, where the output image contains more information than any of the input images."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of image fusion like assembling a **puzzle with two specialized sets of pieces**:
- One set of pieces (say, a **CT scan**) shows you the *skeleton* — the bones and hard structures with great clarity, but tells you nothing about the soft tissues.
- The other set (an **MRI scan**) shows you the *muscles, organs, and tumors* in vivid detail, but the bone boundaries are blurry.

Neither picture alone gives the surgeon the **full story**. Image fusion is the act of **overlaying these two sets perfectly** so that, in a single glance, the doctor can see exactly where a tumor is located *and* precisely how it relates to the surrounding bone. The fused image is essentially a "super-vision" — it is **greater than the sum of its parts**.

### 1.3 Why Image Fusion is Critical in Medical Imaging

Modern clinical decision-making requires **complementary information** from multiple modalities:

| Modality | Primary Strength | Primary Limitation |
|----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **CT (Computed Tomography)** | Excellent bone & lung contrast | Poor soft-tissue differentiation |
| **MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging)** | Superior soft-tissue resolution | Longer scan time, expensive |
| **PET (Positron Emission Tomography)** | Functional/metabolic activity | Very poor anatomical resolution |
| **SPECT** | 3D functional imaging | Low spatial resolution |
| **Ultrasound** | Real-time, portable | Operator-dependent, low SNR |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric (Industry Standard):** The most widely used metrics for evaluating image fusion quality are **Entropy (H)**, **Mutual Information (MI)**, and **Spatial Frequency (SF)**. A well-fused medical image must preserve diagnostic fidelity from *both* source modalities without introducing artifacts.

### 1.4 The Three Operational Levels of Fusion

According to the standard KTU / Gonzalez-Woods classification, image fusion is performed at three hierarchical levels:

1. **Pixel-Level Fusion**: Operates directly on raw pixel intensities. Most information-preserving but sensitive to noise and misregistration.
2. **Feature-Level Fusion**: Operates on extracted features (edges, corners, regions). More robust and compressed.
3. **Decision-Level Fusion**: Combines final decisions/classifications from each image. Most abstract and most robust to noise, but least information-rich.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow of Image Fusion

The general pipeline of medical image fusion consists of the following structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Image Acquisition**: Acquire multimodal source images (e.g., CT and MRI) of the same anatomical region of interest (ROI).
- **Step 2 — Preprocessing**: Apply denoising (e.g., Gaussian or median filtering), intensity normalization, and histogram matching to ensure the two images have comparable dynamic ranges.
- **Step 3 — Image Registration (Spatial Alignment)**: The most critical step. Transform one image (the *moving* image) geometrically so that it aligns spatially with the *fixed* (reference) image. Registration can be **rigid** (rotation + translation), **affine** (adds scaling and shearing), or **deformable (non-rigid)** (handles soft-tissue deformation).
- **Step 4 — Fusion Rule Application**: Apply the chosen fusion algorithm (e.g., weighted averaging, Laplacian pyramid, wavelet transform) on the registered pixel data.
- **Step 5 — Post-processing & Display**: Apply color mapping (e.g., assigning CT to grayscale channel and PET to color channel) for visualization and assessment.
- **Step 6 — Quality Evaluation**: Quantitatively measure the fused image using metrics such as entropy, mutual information, and edge preservation index.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why Preprocessing?** Because different modalities have different intensity scales (e.g., CT uses Hounsfield Units, MRI uses arbitrary intensity scales). Without normalization, fusion rules produce biased outputs.
- **Why Registration?** If images are misaligned by even a few pixels, fused edges become blurred, halos appear, and diagnostic confidence collapses. Registration is the *foundation* of fusion.
- **Why Choose Pixel-Level over Decision-Level?** For medical diagnosis, maximum information retention is critical. Surgeons and radiologists must see the original pixel-level detail from both modalities.

### 2.3 Core Fusion Methods (Taxonomy)

Image fusion methods can be classified into **Spatial Domain** and **Transform Domain** techniques.

#### A. Spatial Domain Methods
- **Simple Averaging**: $F(x,y) = \frac{1}{2}[A(x,y) + B(x,y)]$
- **Weighted Averaging**: $F(x,y) = w_1 A(x,y) + w_2 B(x,y)$, where $w_1 + w_2 = 1$
- **Maximum Selection**: $F(x,y) = \max(A(x,y), B(x,y))$
- **Minimum Selection**: $F(x,y) = \min(A(x,y), B(x,y))$
- **Brovey Transform**: A ratio-based method that preserves spectral characteristics.

#### B. Transform Domain Methods (Multi-Resolution)
- **Laplacian Pyramid (LP) Fusion**
- **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** based fusion
- **Dual-Tree Complex Wavelet Transform (DTCWT)**
- **Curvelet Transform** based fusion
- **Non-Subsampled Contourlet Transform (NSCT)**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates all essential formulas for the KTU examination. Note: vertical bars use `\vert` to avoid markdown table conflicts.

| Formula / Metric | Mathematical Expression | Description / Application |
|------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| Weighted Average Fusion | $F(x,y) = w_1 A(x,y) + w_2 B(x,y)$ | Linear combination of two source images |
| Entropy (Information Content) | $H = -\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} p_i \log_2(p_i)$ | Higher entropy = more information in fused image |
| Mutual Information | $MI(A,F) = \sum_{a,f} p_{AF}(a,f) \log_2 \frac{p_{AF}(a,f)}{p_A(a)\, p_F(f)}$ | Measures statistical dependency between source and fused image |
| Spatial Frequency | $SF = \sqrt{RF^2 + CF^2}$ | Edge and texture activity measure |
| Standard Deviation (SD) | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{MN}\sum (F(x,y) - \mu)^2}$ | Contrast measure of fused image |
| Edge Preservation Index | $Q^{AB/F} = \frac{\sum (Q^{AF}(n)\, w^A(n) + Q^{BF}(n)\, w^B(n))}{\sum (w^A(n) + w^B(n))}$ | Measures edge information transfer |
| DWT Decomposition | $A = LL_1 + \sum (LH_j + HL_j + HH_j)$ | Multi-scale frequency separation |
| Root Mean Square Error | $RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{MN}\sum (A(x,y) - B(x,y))^2}$ | Difference measure between images |
| Correlation Coefficient | $CC = \frac{\sum (A - \bar{A})(F - \bar{F})}{\sqrt{\sum (A - \bar{A})^2 \sum (F - \bar{F})^2}}$ | Linear similarity between images |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In radiotherapy treatment planning, **PET-CT fusion** allows radiation oncologists to deliver precise dose to metabolically active tumor volumes while sparing surrounding healthy tissue — improving local control by **~15–25%** in many solid tumor studies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pixel-Level Weighted Average Fusion

The simplest fusion rule is the **weighted average** of two co-registered source images $A(x,y)$ and $B(x,y)$. Let us rigorously derive the optimal weights under a minimum-variance criterion.

**Problem Setup:**
Given two registered source images $A(x,y)$ and $B(x,y)$ of the same scene, we seek a fused image $F(x,y)$ such that:

$$
F(x,y) = w_1 \cdot A(x,y) + w_2 \cdot B(x,y)
$$

subject to the linear-combination constraint:

$$
w_1 + w_2 = 1
$$

**Step 1 — Define the Estimation Criterion:**
Assume the true scene $T(x,y)$ is corrupted by additive, zero-mean noise:

$$
A(x,y) = T(x,y) + n_A(x,y)
$$

$$
B(x,y) = T(x,y) + n_B(x,y)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Fusion Rule:**

$$
F(x,y) = w_1 [T(x,y) + n_A] + (1 - w_1)[T(x,y) + n_B]
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the Expression:**

$$
F(x,y) = w_1 T + w_1 n_A + T - w_1 T + n_B - w_1 n_B
$$

The $w_1 T$ terms cancel:

$$
F(x,y) = T(x,y) + w_1 n_A(x,y) + (1 - w_1) n_B(x,y)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Mean-Squared Error (MSE):**
The noise component of the fused image is:

$$
\epsilon(x,y) = w_1 n_A + (1 - w_1) n_B
$$

Assuming $n_A$ and $n_B$ are independent with variances $\sigma_A^2$ and $\sigma_B^2$:

$$
\sigma_\epsilon^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_A^2 + (1 - w_1)^2 \sigma_B^2
$$

**Step 5 — Minimize the MSE with respect to $w_1$:**
Take the derivative and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{d}{dw_1} \sigma_\epsilon^2 = 2 w_1 \sigma_A^2 - 2(1 - w_1) \sigma_B^2 = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Solve the Linear Equation:**

$$
w_1 \sigma_A^2 = (1 - w_1) \sigma_B^2
$$

$$
w_1 \sigma_A^2 + w_1 \sigma_B^2 = \sigma_B^2
$$

$$
w_1 = \frac{\sigma_B^2}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2}
$$

By symmetry, using the constraint $w_1 + w_2 = 1$:

$$
w_2 = \frac{\sigma_A^2}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2}
$$

**Interpretation:** The optimal weight is **inversely proportional to the noise variance** — the cleaner image contributes more to the fusion. This is the **Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE) optimal linear combiner** for two images, a direct extension of the Wiener filter principle.

### 3.2 Derivation: 2D Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) Fusion Rule

Wavelet-based fusion operates by decomposing both source images into approximation and detail sub-bands at multiple scales.

**Step 1 — Apply DWT to both images:**

$$
A \rightarrow \{A_{LL}, A_{LH}, A_{HL}, A_{HH}\}
$$

$$
B \rightarrow \{B_{LL}, B_{LH}, B_{HL}, B_{HH}\}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Fusion Rule on each Sub-band:**
For the **approximation (low-low)** sub-band, use averaging:

$$
F_{LL}(x,y) = \frac{A_{LL}(x,y) + B_{LL}(x,y)}{2}
$$

For the **detail (LH, HL, HH)** sub-bands, use maximum-selection to preserve edges:

$$
F_{LH}(x,y) = \max\left(\vert A_{LH}(x,y)\vert, \vert B_{LH}(x,y)\vert\right) \cdot \text{sign}(\cdot)
$$

**Step 3 — Reconstruct the Fused Image using Inverse DWT:**

$$
F(x,y) = \text{IDWT}(F_{LL}, F_{LH}, F_{HL}, F_{HH})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use maximum for detail sub-bands?** Detail sub-bands encode high-frequency information (edges, textures). The `max` rule selects the *sharpest* edge contribution from either source, which is exactly the diagnostic feature the radiologist needs.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Fusion Methods

| Method | Computational Cost | Information Preservation | Robustness to Noise | KTU Exam Frequency |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Simple Averaging | **Very Low** | Low | Low | High |
| Weighted Average (MMSE) | **Very Low** | Medium | Medium | High |
| Laplacian Pyramid | Medium | High | Medium | Medium |
| DWT-based | Medium | High | High | **Very High** |
| DTCWT-based | High | **Very High** | High | Medium |
| PCA-based | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| NSCT-based | **Very High** | **Very High** | Very High | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of Medical Image Fusion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[CT Image Input] --> nodeC[Preprocessing: Denoising and Normalization]
    nodeB[MRI Image Input] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD[Image Registration: Rigid and Affine Transform]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Image Decomposition: DWT or Pyramid]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Fusion Rule Application: Max Selection or Weighted Average]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Inverse Transform: IDWT or Reconstruction]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Fused Multimodal Image]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Quality Assessment: Entropy and MI Metrics]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Clinical Decision Support Output]
```

### 4.2 Three-Level Fusion Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Source Image A] --> nodeP1[Pixel Level Fusion]
    nodeB[Source Image B] --> nodeP1
    nodeP1 --> nodeO1[Fused Image]
    nodeA --> nodeF1[Feature Extraction]
    nodeB --> nodeF1
    nodeF1 --> nodeFeat[Feature Level Fusion]
    nodeFeat --> nodeO1
    nodeA --> nodeDec1[Decision Module]
    nodeB --> nodeDec2[Decision Module]
    nodeDec1 --> nodeComb[Decision Level Fusion]
    nodeDec2 --> nodeComb
    nodeComb --> nodeO1
```

### 4.3 DWT-Based Multi-Resolution Fusion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeInA[Image A] --> nodeDWTa[DWT Level 1]
    nodeInB[Image B] --> nodeDWTb[DWT Level 1]
    nodeDWTa --> nodeSubA1[Approximation LL]
    nodeDWTa --> nodeSubA2[Horizontal LH]
    nodeDWTa --> nodeSubA3[Vertical HL]
    nodeDWTa --> nodeSubA4[Diagonal HH]
    nodeDWTb --> nodeSubB1[Approximation LL]
    nodeDWTb --> nodeSubB2[Horizontal LH]
    nodeDWTb --> nodeSubB3[Vertical HL]
    nodeDWTb --> nodeSubB4[Diagonal HH]
    nodeSubA1 --> nodeRule1[Average Fusion Rule]
    nodeSubB1 --> nodeRule1
    nodeSubA2 --> nodeRule2[Maximum Selection Rule]
    nodeSubB2 --> nodeRule2
    nodeSubA3 --> nodeRule3[Maximum Selection Rule]
    nodeSubB3 --> nodeRule3
    nodeSubA4 --> nodeRule4[Maximum Selection Rule]
    nodeSubB4 --> nodeRule4
    nodeRule1 --> nodeIDWT[Inverse DWT Reconstruction]
    nodeRule2 --> nodeIDWT
    nodeRule3 --> nodeIDWT
    nodeRule4 --> nodeIDWT
    nodeIDWT --> nodeOut[Fused Image F]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure Interpretation:** This block diagram illustrates how a DWT-based fusion engine decomposes both inputs into four directional sub-bands, applies frequency-adaptive fusion rules (averaging for low-frequency, maximum-selection for high-frequency), and reconstructs the final composite image.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define image fusion. List any two medical imaging modalities that are commonly fused.**

**Model Answer:**
Image fusion is the process of combining relevant information from two or more images of the same scene into a single composite image, such that the resultant image contains more complete and accurate information than any individual input image.

In medical imaging, two commonly fused modalities are:
1. **CT (Computed Tomography)** — provides excellent anatomical/bony detail.
2. **MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging)** — provides superior soft-tissue contrast.

These are typically fused for neurological, musculoskeletal, and oncological diagnosis. Other combinations include **PET-CT** (functional + anatomical) and **SPECT-MRI**.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2. What is the role of image registration in the image fusion pipeline?**

**Model Answer:**
Image registration is the process of geometrically aligning two or more images of the same scene taken at different times, from different viewpoints, or by different sensors. In the fusion pipeline, registration is a **mandatory prerequisite**: without spatial alignment, pixel-level fusion rules will produce ghosting, halos, and blurred edges, leading to misdiagnosis.

Registration can be:
- **Rigid** (translation + rotation only)
- **Affine** (rigid + scaling + shearing)
- **Deformable / Non-rigid** (handles soft-tissue warping)

Common similarity measures used during registration include **Mutual Information (MI)** and **Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC)**.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the different levels of image fusion in detail, with suitable examples for medical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) based image fusion method. Discuss the fusion rules used for approximation and detail sub-bands. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Levels of Image Fusion:**

Image fusion is performed at three hierarchical levels:

1. **Pixel-Level Fusion (Low-Level):**
   - Operates directly on raw pixel intensities of source images.
   - Most information-preserving approach.
   - Highly sensitive to noise and registration errors.
   - **Medical Example:** Direct weighted averaging of CT and MRI pixel intensities after registration.

2. **Feature-Level Fusion (Intermediate-Level):**
   - Source images are first processed to extract salient features (edges, regions, textures).
   - Fusion operates on these feature representations.
   - More robust and computationally efficient than pixel-level.
   - **Medical Example:** Fusing Gabor-wavelet edge maps extracted from CT and MRI for tumor boundary delineation.

3. **Decision-Level Fusion (High-Level):**
   - Each source image is independently classified/interpreted to produce a decision.
   - Decisions are combined using voting, Bayesian inference, or Dempster-Shafer theory.
   - Most abstract and most noise-robust, but loses pixel-level detail.
   - **Medical Example:** Combining independent CAD (Computer-Aided Diagnosis) outputs from mammography and ultrasound to decide if a breast lesion is benign or malignant.

**(b) DWT-Based Image Fusion:**

Refer to the architecture diagram in **Section 4.3**.

**Procedure:**
1. **Decomposition:** Apply 2D-DWT to both registered images $A$ and $B$ to obtain four sub-bands at each level: $LL$ (approximation), $LH$ (horizontal detail), $HL$ (vertical detail), $HH$ (diagonal detail).

2. **Fusion Rules:**
   - For the **approximation sub-band** (low-frequency, contains energy): use **averaging** to combine smoothly.

$$
F_{LL} = \frac{A_{LL} + B_{LL}}{2}
$$

   - For the **detail sub-bands** ($LH$, $HL$, $HH$, contain edges): use **maximum-absolute-value selection** to preserve the sharpest edge from either source.

$$
F_{LH} = \begin{cases} A_{LH} & \text{if } \vert A_{LH}\vert \geq \vert B_{LH}\vert \\ B_{LH} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

3. **Reconstruction:** Apply Inverse DWT (IDWT) on the fused sub-bands to obtain the final fused image $F$.

**Advantages:** Provides directional edge information, reduces artifacts, and offers multi-scale analysis.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With necessary mathematical expressions, derive the optimal weights for a weighted-average pixel-level image fusion scheme under the minimum mean squared error (MMSE) criterion. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List and briefly explain any four quantitative performance metrics used to evaluate the quality of a fused medical image. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of MMSE-Optimal Weights:**

Let the true scene be $T$, observed through two noisy channels:

$$
A = T + n_A, \quad B = T + n_B
$$

The fused image is:

$$
F = w_1 A + w_2 B = w_1 A + (1 - w_1) B
$$

Substituting:

$$
F = w_1 T + w_1 n_A + (1 - w_1) T + (1 - w_1) n_B = T + w_1 n_A + (1 - w_1) n_B
$$

The error noise is:

$$
\epsilon = w_1 n_A + (1 - w_1) n_B
$$

Assuming independent zero-mean noise with variances $\sigma_A^2$, $\sigma_B^2$:

$$
\sigma_\epsilon^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_A^2 + (1 - w_1)^2 \sigma_B^2
$$

Minimize by setting $\frac{d\sigma_\epsilon^2}{dw_1} = 0$:

$$
2 w_1 \sigma_A^2 - 2(1 - w_1) \sigma_B^2 = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
w_1 = \frac{\sigma_B^2}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2}, \quad w_2 = \frac{\sigma_A^2}{\sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2}
$$

**Interpretation:** The optimal weight is **inversely proportional to noise variance** — the cleaner modality dominates the fusion. This is the **Wiener-filter-like optimal linear combiner**.

**(b) Performance Metrics:**

1. **Entropy (H):** Measures the average information content. A higher entropy indicates richer information in the fused image.

$$
H = -\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

2. **Mutual Information (MI):** Measures the amount of information transferred from each source to the fused image. Higher MI = better information preservation.

3. **Spatial Frequency (SF):** Quantifies edge/activity levels. A higher SF indicates better edge preservation.

4. **Edge Preservation Index ($Q^{AB/F}$):** Proposed by Xydeas and Petrović, measures how well the edge information from sources $A$ and $B$ is preserved in the fused image $F$. Values close to 1 indicate near-perfect edge preservation.

5. **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$):** A simple but effective contrast indicator. Higher $\sigma$ generally indicates better contrast and detail visibility in the fused image.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Skipping the Registration Step:** Many students describe fusion algorithms but forget to mention that **image registration is a mandatory pre-requisite**. Examiners explicitly allocate 2–3 marks for this. Always state registration first.
> 2. **Confusing the Three Fusion Levels:** Students often mix up pixel-level and feature-level definitions. Memorize: *Pixel = raw intensity*, *Feature = extracted descriptor*, *Decision = final classification*.
> 3. **Not Justifying the Fusion Rule:** Saying "we use max for detail sub-bands" without explaining *why* (it preserves the sharpest edge) loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the Constraint $w_1 + w_2 = 1$** in weighted average derivations. This single line is worth 1 mark.
> 5. **Writing fusion metrics without units or interpretation:** A metric value without clinical context (e.g., "MI > 3 indicates good information transfer") is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Omitting the reconstruction step in transform-domain methods:** DWT-based fusion is incomplete without explicitly mentioning the **Inverse DWT (IDWT)** for reconstruction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Image fusion** combines multiple images into one richer image, but **registration is mandatory before fusion**.
- The **three fusion levels** are *Pixel, Feature, and Decision* — each with different information density and noise robustness.
- **Spatial-domain methods** (averaging, weighted, max) are simple but suffer from reduced contrast and spectral distortion.
- **Transform-domain methods** (DWT, NSCT, Laplacian Pyramid) preserve directional edge information and are the modern clinical standard.
- **DWT fusion rules:** *Average* the low-low (approximation) sub-band; *Maximum-select* the high-frequency (LH, HL, HH) sub-bands.
- **MMSE-optimal weights** are inversely proportional to noise variance — the cleaner image wins.
- **Key evaluation metrics:** Entropy, Mutual Information, Spatial Frequency, Edge Preservation Index $Q^{AB/F}$.
- **Major clinical applications:** PET-CT (oncology), CT-MRI (neurosurgery), SPECT-MRI (cardiology), multimodal neuroimaging (Alzheimer's, epilepsy).
- **Performance hierarchy:** Simple average < Weighted average < Laplacian pyramid < DWT < DTCWT < NSCT (both in quality and computational cost).
- **Exam gold rule:** Always draw the **block diagram** for transform-domain methods — it is worth 2–3 marks minimum.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Subtraction and Addition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Image Subtraction and Addition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Image Addition** is a pixel-wise arithmetic operation in which two or more digital images of identical dimensions are combined by summing the intensity (gray-level) values of corresponding pixels to produce a single composite output image. Mathematically, for $n$ input images $f_1(x,y), f_2(x,y), \ldots, f_n(x,y)$, the added image is given by:

$$g(x,y) = f_1(x,y) + f_2(x,y) + \cdots + f_n(x,y)$$

When the sum is normalized by $n$, the operation is termed **Image Averaging**, which is the primary tool used to suppress random noise.

**Image Subtraction** is the converse pixel-wise operation, wherein the intensity of a reference (or "mask") image is subtracted from a second ("live") image, typically to enhance *differences* between the two. The output is:

$$g(x,y) = f_1(x,y) - f_2(x,y)$$

In medical imaging, both operations form the **mathematical backbone of Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA)**, temporal change detection, and noise filtration via ensemble averaging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The operation is *always* performed **pixel-by-pixel** (point-wise) on spatially registered images. Misalignment between source images will produce artifacts known as **subtraction artifacts** — a frequent 7-mark question in KTU ESE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Image Addition as "Crowd Noise Reduction":**
Imagine you are trying to listen to a soft voice in a noisy stadium. If you ask **100 different people** to record the same voice independently, each recording has slightly different random noise. When you *average* all 100 recordings, the consistent voice signal reinforces itself, while the random noise cancels out. Image averaging works identically on pixels — it reinforces anatomical structure and cancels stochastic (random) noise.

**Analogy 2 — Image Subtraction as "Spotting the Intruder":**
Imagine two security photographs of the same room taken an hour apart. If nothing changes, the photos are identical. By *subtracting* them, the room "vanishes" (giving zero) and only the **intruder or changed object** remains visible. In medical imaging, this is exactly how **Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA)** isolates contrast-filled blood vessels from surrounding bone and soft tissue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** All arithmetic operations assume images with identical spatial dimensions $M \times N$ and the same bit-depth (e.g., 8-bit, 12-bit). A scaling constant of $\frac{1}{n}$ must be applied in averaging to prevent saturation (overflow) of pixel intensity values beyond the maximum quantization level $L-1$, where $L = 2^k$ for a $k$-bit image.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Image Addition / Averaging

Consider $n$ registered, noisy observations $\left\{f_i(x,y) \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, n\right\}$ of the same underlying scene $f(x,y)$, each corrupted by **zero-mean, uncorrelated random noise** $\eta_i(x,y)$:

$$f_i(x,y) = f(x,y) + \eta_i(x,y)$$

The **averaged image** is defined as:

$$\bar{g}(x,y) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i(x,y)$$

**Why does averaging work?** A step-by-step logic:

- **Step 1 — Expectation:** Because the noise has zero mean, $E\{\eta_i(x,y)\} = 0$.
- **Step 2 — Mean of Average:** Taking the expected value of the average:
$$E\{\bar{g}(x,y)\} = f(x,y)$$
- **Step 3 — Variance Reduction:** If all $\eta_i$ are independent with variance $\sigma^2$, the variance of the averaged image becomes:
$$\sigma_{\bar{g}}^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$
- **Step 4 — Interpretation:** As $n \to \infty$, the averaged image converges to the noise-free image. The **standard deviation** of noise falls as $\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$, giving a 2× noise reduction per 4-fold increase in image count.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This is the principle behind **multi-frame averaging in X-ray fluoroscopy**, **MRI signal averaging (NEX — Number of Excitations)**, and **PET coincidence accumulation**, where physical constraints (radiation dose, scan time) limit $n$.

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Image Subtraction

For two registered images $f_1(x,y)$ and $f_2(x,y)$:

$$g(x,y) = f_1(x,y) - f_2(x,y)$$

**Clinical Use-Cases (KTU High-Yield):**

- **Temporal Subtraction (DSA):** Mask image = pre-contrast X-ray; Live image = post-contrast X-ray. The difference isolates vasculature filled with iodinated contrast agent.
- **Energy Subtraction (Dual-Energy Imaging):** Two acquisitions at different kVp values are weighted and subtracted to separate bone from soft tissue (e.g., in chest radiography).
- **Change Detection:** Comparing pre-therapy and post-therapy images to monitor tumor regression.

**Important Intensity Mapping Rule for Subtraction:**

Because subtraction may yield negative values, the result must be **re-mapped** to the displayable range $[0, L-1]$:

$$g_{disp}(x,y) = \left\lfloor \frac{g(x,y) - g_{min}}{g_{max} - g_{min}} \times (L-1) + 0.5 \right\rfloor$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Operation | Description | Units / Range |
|---|---------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $g(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i(x,y)$ | Pixel-wise addition of $n$ images | Intensities (gray levels) |
| 2 | $\bar{g}(x,y) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i(x,y)$ | Image averaging | Intensities (gray levels) |
| 3 | $\sigma_{\bar{g}}^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$ | Variance of averaged image | Intensity$^2$ |
| 4 | $SNR_{avg} = SNR_{single}\cdot\sqrt{n}$ | SNR improvement factor (dimensionless) | Unitless (dB when $20\log$) |
| 5 | $g(x,y) = f_1(x,y) - f_2(x,y)$ | Pixel-wise image subtraction | Intensities (gray levels) |
| 6 | $g_{disp}(x,y) = \dfrac{g - g_{min}}{g_{max} - g_{min}}(L-1)$ | Re-mapping (normalization) | Intensities |
| 7 | $I_{out} = \left(1 - k \right) I_{low} + k \cdot I_{high}$ | Weighted energy-subtraction blending | Intensities |
| 8 | $PSNR = 10 \log_{10} \dfrac{(L-1)^2}{MSE}$ | Reconstruction-quality metric | dB |
| 9 | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{x}\sum_{y}\left[g(x,y) - \hat{g}(x,y)\right]^2$ | Mean Squared Error (subtraction residual) | Intensity$^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constraint Reminder:** For a $k$-bit image, valid pixel range is $[0, 2^k - 1]$. After averaging or addition, **clipping/saturation** may occur if $\sum f_i > L-1$, which KTU examiners specifically test in 7-mark derivations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Noise Variance in Image Averaging

**Given:**
- $n$ registered noisy images $f_i(x,y) = f(x,y) + \eta_i(x,y)$, where $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.
- $\eta_i(x,y)$ are independent, zero-mean random variables with variance $\sigma^2$.

**To Prove:** The variance of the averaged image $\bar{g}(x,y)$ is $\frac{\sigma^2}{n}$.

**Derivation:**

$$\bar{g}(x,y) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i(x,y) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[f(x,y) + \eta_i(x,y)\right]$$

Since $f(x,y)$ is deterministic, it can be pulled out of the sum:

$$\bar{g}(x,y) = f(x,y) + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i(x,y)$$

Let us denote the noise term as $\bar{\eta}(x,y) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i(x,y)$.

The variance of the averaged image equals the variance of its noise component:

$$\sigma_{\bar{g}}^2 = \mathrm{Var}\left[\bar{\eta}(x,y)\right] = \mathrm{Var}\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \eta_i(x,y)\right]$$

Because the noises are statistically independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.), the variance of a sum equals the sum of variances:

$$\sigma_{\bar{g}}^2 = \left(\frac{1}{n}\right)^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathrm{Var}\left[\eta_i(x,y)\right] = \left(\frac{1}{n}\right)^2 \cdot n\sigma^2$$

Simplifying:

$$\sigma_{\bar{g}}^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Result:** The standard deviation of noise falls as $\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$, confirming the **$\sqrt{n}$ SNR gain**. **Q.E.D.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: SNR Improvement via Averaging

**Definition of SNR (single image):**

$$SNR_{single} = \frac{\mu_{signal}}{\sigma_{noise}} = \frac{f(x,y)}{\sigma}$$

**Definition of SNR (averaged image):**

$$SNR_{avg} = \frac{E\{\bar{g}(x,y)\}}{\sigma_{\bar{g}}} = \frac{f(x,y)}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\frac{SNR_{avg}}{SNR_{single}} = \frac{f/\left(\sigma/\sqrt{n}\right)}{f/\sigma} = \sqrt{n}$$

In decibels (dB):

$$SNR_{avg,dB} = SNR_{single,dB} + 10\log_{10}(n) \;\; \text{dB}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A 4-image average gives a **6.02 dB** SNR boost; a 16-image average gives **12.04 dB**. This is precisely why **MRI scans** report "NEX = 2, 4, 8..." parameters.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Pixel Saturation Clipping Rule in Addition

For an 8-bit image ($L = 256$, $L-1 = 255$), if $n = 2$ and pixel values exceed $\frac{255}{2} = 127.5$, saturation occurs.

**Saturated output:**

$$g_{clipped}(x,y) = \min\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i(x,y), \; L-1\right]$$

A common normalization workaround is:

$$g_{norm}(x,y) = \text{round}\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i(x,y)\right]$$

This keeps the result within $[0, 255]$, trading some signal magnitude for **no clipping loss**.

> **Tip for KTU Exam:** When asked "Why do we normalize after addition?", cite (1) prevention of overflow, (2) preservation of displayable dynamic range, (3) consistency with bit-depth assumptions of subsequent processing.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical 3-Mark Sub-Part)

**Problem:** Four registered noisy X-ray images of the same anatomical region are averaged. The noise standard deviation in a single image is $\sigma = 20$ gray-levels. Compute the noise standard deviation and the SNR improvement in dB for the averaged image.

**Solution:**

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{20}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{20}{2} = 10 \;\; \text{gray-levels}$$

$$SNR_{improvement} = 10\log_{10}(4) = 10 \times 0.602 = 6.02 \;\; \text{dB}$$

**Answer:** The averaged image has noise $\sigma_{avg} = 10$ gray-levels, an SNR improvement of **6.02 dB**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Image Addition / Averaging Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["f1 x y"] --> S["Pixel-wise Adder"]
    A2["f2 x y"] --> S
    A3["fn x y"] --> S
    S --> D["Divider by n"]
    D --> M["Normalizer 0 to L-1"]
    M --> O["Averaged Image g bar x y"]
    N1["Noise eta1"] -.-> A1
    N2["Noise eta2"] -.-> A2
    N3["Noise etan"] -.-> A3
```

**Figure 1 — Block architecture of an image-averaging denoiser.** Each noisy frame $f_i(x,y) = f(x,y) + \eta_i(x,y)$ enters a pixel-wise summer, is divided by $n$ (the frame count), and is then mapped to the displayable range.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA) — Temporal Subtraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Patient Anatomy"] --> M["Pre-contrast X-ray Image Mask"]
    P --> L["Post-contrast X-ray Image Live"]
    M --> R["Image Registration Spatial Alignment"]
    L --> R
    R --> SUB["Pixel-wise Subtraction Live minus Mask"]
    SUB --> LOG["Logarithmic Transformation Optional"]
    LOG --> ENH["Contrast Enhancement Window Leveling"]
    ENH --> DIS["Displayed DSA Image Vessels Highlighted"]
```

**Figure 2 — DSA Temporal Subtraction Pipeline.** A pre-contrast "mask" and a post-contrast "live" image are spatially registered, then subtracted to remove static bone/soft tissue, leaving only iodine-filled vasculature visible.

### 4.3 Comparative Matrix: Addition vs. Subtraction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ADD["Image Addition and Averaging"]:::A --> A1["Goal: Suppress Random Noise"]
    ADD --> A2["Output: Composite or Mean Image"]
    ADD --> A3["Metric: sigma reduced by sqrt n"]
    SUB["Image Subtraction"]:::B --> B1["Goal: Isolate Differences or Change"]
    SUB --> B2["Output: Differential Image"]
    SUB --> B3["Application: DSA and Change Detection"]
    classDef A fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef B fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F
```

**Figure 3 — Conceptual comparison of image addition (denoising) versus image subtraction (change isolation).**

### 4.4 Schematic: Energy Subtraction (Dual-kVp Imaging)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H["High kVp Image 120 kVp"] --> W1["Weight w1"]
    L["Low kVp Image 60 kVp"] --> W2["Weight w2"]
    W1 --> SUM["Weighted Sum w1 x Hi minus w2 x Lo"]
    W2 --> SUM
    SUM --> BS["Bone Image or Soft Tissue Image"]
```

**Figure 4 — Energy subtraction separates bone from soft tissue by exploiting differential X-ray attenuation at two tube potentials. The weight $w_1, w_2$ are chosen so that one material's contribution cancels out.**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
> *Define image averaging. How does it help in reducing noise in medical images?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Image averaging is a pixel-wise arithmetic operation in which $n$ registered noisy images of the same scene are summed and divided by $n$ to obtain a mean image. The output is $\bar{g}(x,y) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i(x,y)$. Because the noise in each image is zero-mean and uncorrelated, the variance of the noise in the averaged image reduces to $\sigma^2/n$, while the underlying signal is preserved. **(2 marks)**. This is widely used in X-ray fluoroscopy, MRI (NEX parameter), and PET imaging to enhance image quality without increasing radiation dose per frame. **(1 mark)**.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
> *What is image subtraction? Mention one medical imaging application.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Image subtraction is a point-wise arithmetic operation that computes the difference between two spatially registered images: $g(x,y) = f_1(x,y) - f_2(x,y)$. **(2 marks)**. A key medical application is **Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA)**, where a pre-contrast mask image is subtracted from a post-contrast live image to visualize blood vessels filled with iodinated contrast medium, after the surrounding bone and soft-tissue structures are removed. **(1 mark)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the variance of noise in an averaged image obtained from $n$ registered noisy images. Show that the SNR improves by a factor of $\sqrt{n}$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a block diagram, explain the working of **Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA)** using temporal subtraction. What are the pre-requisites for successful DSA? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Define the noisy observation model (1 mark):**

$$f_i(x,y) = f(x,y) + \eta_i(x,y), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

where $\eta_i$ are zero-mean, independent, with variance $\sigma^2$.

**Step 2 — Define the averaged image (1 mark):**

$$\bar{g}(x,y) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i(x,y) = f(x,y) + \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\eta_i(x,y)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the variance (3 marks):**

$$\sigma_{\bar{g}}^2 = \mathrm{Var}\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\eta_i\right] = \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathrm{Var}(\eta_i) = \frac{1}{n^2}\cdot n\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

**Step 4 — SNR improvement (2 marks):**

$$SNR_{avg} = \frac{f(x,y)}{\sigma_{\bar{g}}} = \frac{f(x,y)}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} = \sqrt{n}\cdot SNR_{single}$$

In decibels: $SNR_{avg,dB} = SNR_{single,dB} + 10\log_{10}(n)$.

**Conclusion:** Averaging $n$ images reduces noise variance by $1/n$ and improves SNR by a factor of $\sqrt{n}$.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
DSA is a fluoroscopic technique that suppresses static anatomical background (bone, soft tissue) to highlight blood vessels after intravascular injection of an iodinated contrast medium. It works on the principle of **temporal subtraction**.

**Step 2 — Pipeline (3 marks):**

- **Mask acquisition:** A pre-contrast fluoroscopic frame $M(x,y)$ is captured *before* injecting the iodinated contrast agent.
- **Live acquisition:** A post-contrast frame $L(x,y)$ is captured *after* the contrast reaches the region of interest.
- **Registration:** Both images must be spatially registered to compensate for patient or organ motion.
- **Subtraction:** The difference image is computed:
$$D(x,y) = L(x,y) - M(x,y)$$
- **Enhancement:** Logarithmic transformation and contrast windowing are applied to display the vascular tree.

**Step 3 — Prerequisites (2 marks):**
- Patient must remain still or motion-correction algorithms must be applied.
- Both images must be **spatially registered** and acquired at the **same X-ray exposure parameters** (kVp, mA).
- Sufficient iodine concentration in vessels to provide detectable contrast.

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model — Internal Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **temporal image subtraction** technique with a block diagram and write its mathematical formulation. State the role of the "mask image" in DSA. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A radiologist averages 16 frames of a noisy X-ray image. If the noise standard deviation in a single frame is $\sigma = 40$ gray levels, calculate the noise standard deviation and SNR improvement in dB in the averaged image. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) (7 Marks)

**Mathematical formulation (2 marks):**

$$D(x,y) = L(x,y) - M(x,y)$$

where $L(x,y)$ is the **live image** (post-contrast) and $M(x,y)$ is the **mask image** (pre-contrast).

**Block diagram description (3 marks):** (Refer to Figure 2 in Section 4)

- Patient → Pre-contrast fluoroscopy → Mask image $M(x,y)$
- Patient → Inject contrast → Post-contrast fluoroscopy → Live image $L(x,y)$
- $M(x,y)$ and $L(x,y)$ → Spatial registration → Pixel-wise subtraction $L - M$ → Logarithmic transform → Contrast enhancement → Display.

**Role of mask image (2 marks):**
The mask image contains *all* the static anatomical structures (ribs, vertebrae, soft tissue) that are present in both pre- and post-contrast images. Subtracting it from the live image removes these static structures (because they cancel out) and isolates only the *new* signal introduced by the iodine-filled blood vessels, making the vasculature clearly visible against a neutral background.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $n = 16$, $\sigma = 40$ gray-levels.

**Step 1 — Standard deviation of averaged noise (4 marks):**

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{40}{\sqrt{16}} = \frac{40}{4} = 10 \;\; \text{gray-levels}$$

**Step 2 — SNR improvement factor (2 marks):**

$$\Delta SNR = 10\log_{10}(n) = 10\log_{10}(16) = 10 \times 1.204 = 12.04 \;\; \text{dB}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
The averaged image has a noise standard deviation of **10 gray-levels**, representing a **75% noise reduction** in standard deviation, and the SNR improves by **12.04 dB** compared to a single frame.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting the normalization step** ($\frac{1}{n}$) in image averaging — examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if students write only the summation without scaling.
> 2. **Confusing variance reduction** ($\sigma^2/n$) with **standard deviation reduction** ($\sigma/\sqrt{n}$). They are different — read the question carefully.
> 3. **Ignoring the requirement of image registration** before subtraction. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for failing to mention motion correction or pixel-wise alignment in DSA.
> 4. **Writing SNR in dB without the $10\log_{10}$ factor** when it is a *power*-like quantity (variance), versus $20\log_{10}$ for amplitude ratios.
> 5. **Not handling negative values** after subtraction — KTU expects a mention of re-mapping to $[0, L-1]$ for display.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Image Addition** combines pixels via summation; **Averaging** is the normalized form $\frac{1}{n}\sum f_i(x,y)$.
- **Averaging reduces noise variance by factor $n$** and improves SNR by $\sqrt{n}$, or $10\log_{10}(n)$ dB.
- **Image Subtraction** computes pixel-wise difference $g = f_1 - f_2$, used to isolate changes between two images.
- **Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA)** is the flagship medical application: pre-contrast *mask* minus post-contrast *live* = vasculature.
- **Pre-requisites for any subtraction operation:** *spatial registration* of both images and identical bit-depth/dynamic range.
- **Negative pixel values** after subtraction must be re-mapped to $[0, L-1]$ for display.
- **Image saturation/clipping** may occur in pure addition; averaging avoids it.
- **Variance formula:** $\sigma^2_{\bar{g}} = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$ — a high-yield KTU derivation.
- **SNR formula:** $SNR_{avg,dB} = SNR_{single,dB} + 10\log_{10}(n)$.
- **Clinical applications:** DSA (temporal subtraction), Dual-energy chest X-ray (energy subtraction), MRI NEX, PET frame averaging.
- **Energy subtraction** uses two kVp levels and weighted combination to separate bone vs. soft tissue.
- **Temporal subtraction** assumes no patient/organ motion; otherwise, misregistration artifacts appear.
- **Frequent KTU mistakes:** forgetting normalization, mixing up $\sigma$ vs. $\sigma^2$, ignoring registration, missing re-mapping.
- **Mnemonic:** *Add to denoise, Subtract to detect change.* — Memorize this for the 3-mark definitional questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vector-Valued Images
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vector-Valued Images

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Vector-Valued Image** (also called a **multi-channel image**, **multi-spectral image**, or **color image**) is a two-dimensional discrete signal in which each spatial coordinate $(x, y)$ is mapped to a **vector of scalar values** rather than a single scalar intensity.

Formally, it is defined as the mapping:

$$f : \mathbb{Z}^{2} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{n} \quad \text{or} \quad f : \mathbb{Z}^{2} \rightarrow \mathbb{C}^{n}$$

such that for every pixel coordinate $(x, y)$, the image value is an $n$-dimensional vector:

$$\mathbf{f}(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} f_1(x,y) \\ f_2(x,y) \\ \vdots \\ f_n(x,y) \end{bmatrix}$$

where $n$ is called the **spectral dimension** or **number of bands (channels)**, and each component $f_k(x,y)$ is a **marginal (scalar) image** corresponding to the $k$-th feature channel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In medical imaging, $n=3$ corresponds to **RGB color images**, $n>3$ corresponds to **multi-spectral or hyper-spectral pathology images**, and $n$ may also represent a **multi-modal stack** (e.g., CT + MRI + PET registered onto the same spatial grid).

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a single pixel as a **"small apartment building"** with $n$ separate rooms stacked vertically.

*   In a **grayscale image** ($n=1$), the apartment has **just one room** that stores a single brightness number (e.g., 128).
*   In an **RGB color image** ($n=3$), the apartment has **three rooms**: a *Red* room, a *Green* room, and a *Blue* room, each holding its own brightness value.
*   In a **multi-modal medical image** ($n=3$), the apartment may have a *CT-density* room, an *MRI-T1* room, and an *MRI-T2* room — all sharing the same spatial address.

So instead of "this pixel is **dark**", we say "this pixel is **vector** $\langle 12, 240, 95 \rangle$" — a recipe, not a single number.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples where vector-valued images appear:**
> - Color photographs (RGB, HSV, CMYK) — $n = 3$ or $n = 4$
> - Satellite remote-sensing (LANDSAT) — $n = 7$ to $11$ bands
> - Histopathology whole-slide images (multispectral staining) — $n$ can be $\geq 5$
> - Multi-modal fusion of CT, MRI, PET — $n = 3$
> - Feature-image stacks (e.g., GLCM texture maps per pixel) — arbitrary $n$

### Common Vector-Image Dimensionalities and Their Names

| $n$ | Conventional Name | Typical Domain |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scalar / Grayscale image | Classical X-ray, single MRI sequence |
| 3 | Trichromatic / Color image | RGB photography, color ultrasound |
| 3 | Multi-modal triple | CT + T1 + T2 registered |
| 4 | CMYK / RGBA | Printing and digital displays |
| $\geq 5$ | Multi-spectral image | Satellite, multiplexed IHC staining |
| $\geq 100$ | Hyper-spectral image | Remote sensing, chemical imaging |

> [!TIP]
> **Key takeaway for board exams:** A vector-valued image is **not** the same as a 3D volume. The extra dimension is **spectral/feature**, not spatial. A 3D CT volume is still a *scalar* image with one intensity per voxel.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Anatomy of a Vector-Valued Image

For an $M \times N$ image with $n$ channels, the entire image can be stored as a **3-D tensor**:

$$\mathbf{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times N \times n}, \qquad \mathbf{F}(x,y,k) = f_k(x,y)$$

Three structural viewpoints are useful in KTU-style problems:

1.  **Pixel-level (vector) view:** $\mathbf{f}(x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — *one vector per pixel.*
2.  **Channel-level (scalar) view:** $\mathbf{F}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times N}$ — *one scalar image per channel.*
3.  **Neighborhood (vector window) view:** $W = \{\mathbf{f}(x+i, y+j) \mid (i,j) \in \text{window}\}$ — *a bag of vectors used by filters.*

---

### 2.2 Why Scalar Image Processing "Fails" on Vector Images

Scalar operations such as **sorting**, **ranking**, and **median** require a **total ordering** $(\mathbb{R}, \leq)$. The space $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ for $n \geq 2$ has **no canonical total order**, which means:

*   There is no unique "smallest" or "largest" vector.
*   Component-wise sorting produces **unrelated orderings** between channels.
*   The marginal (channel-by-channel) median is **not** robust to vector outliers.

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU pitfall: applying a scalar 3×3 median filter to each RGB channel *independendently* introduces **color artifacts** (chromatic shifts and rainbow halos at edges). A true vector median filter is required for color images.

---

### 2.3 Color-Space Transformations (KTU High-Yield)

The most-tested conversion in board exams is **RGB $\leftrightarrow$ YIQ / YCbCr / HSV**. Only the **luminance–chrominance** form is needed at the B.Tech level.

**RGB to YIQ (NTSC):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y &= 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B \\
I &= 0.596R - 0.274G - 0.322B \\
Q &= 0.211R - 0.523G + 0.311B
\end{aligned}$$

**YIQ to RGB (inverse):**

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= 1.000Y + 0.956I + 0.621Q \\
G &= 1.000Y - 0.272I - 0.647Q \\
B &= 1.000Y - 1.106I + 1.703Q
\end{aligned}$$

**RGB to YCbCr (ITU-R BT.601):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y  &=  0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B \\
C_b &= -0.169R - 0.331G + 0.500B + 128 \\
C_r &=  0.500R - 0.419G - 0.081B + 128
\end{aligned}$$

The **Y channel** is the luminance (grayscale equivalent). Discarding $C_b$ and $C_r$ recovers the original grayscale.

**RGB to HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value):**

$$\begin{aligned}
V   &= \max(R,G,B) \\
S   &= \begin{cases} \dfrac{V - \min(R,G,B)}{V}, & V \neq 0 \\ 0, & V = 0 \end{cases} \\
H   &= \begin{cases} 60 \cdot \dfrac{G-B}{V-\min(R,G,B)} \bmod 360, & V = R \\
60 \cdot \left( 2 + \dfrac{B-R}{V-\min(R,G,B)} \right), & V = G \\
60 \cdot \left( 4 + \dfrac{R-G}{V-\min(R,G,B)} \right), & V = B \end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 2.4 Distance Metrics in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ (for Vector Filters)

For two pixel-vectors $\mathbf{x} = (x_1,\dots,x_n)$ and $\mathbf{y} = (y_1,\dots,y_n)$:

*   **Minkowski / $L_p$ distance:**

$$d_p(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left( \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_k - y_k \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p}$$

*   **Special cases used most often:**

$$d_1(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_k - y_k \vert \quad \text{(City-block / Manhattan)}$$

$$d_2(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(x_k - y_k)^2} \quad \text{(Euclidean)}$$

$$d_{\infty}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \max_{k} \vert x_k - y_k \vert \quad \text{(Chessboard / Chebyshev)}$$

*   **Angular (cosine) distance — used in chromaticity analysis:**

$$d_{\cos}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = 1 - \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\vert\vert\mathbf{x}\vert\vert \, \vert\vert\mathbf{y}\vert\vert} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{k} x_k y_k}{\sqrt{\sum_k x_k^2}\sqrt{\sum_k y_k^2}}$$

---

### 2.5 The Vector Median Filter (VMF) — Derivation of Output

For a sliding window $W$ of size $N_w$, the **vector median** at the center is the vector that **minimizes the sum of distances to all other window vectors**:

$$\mathbf{f}_{VM}(x,y) = \underset{\mathbf{x} \in W}{\arg\min}\; D(\mathbf{x}), \qquad D(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{\mathbf{y} \in W} d_2(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$$

If the minimum is achieved by multiple vectors, their **vector mean** is the conventional tie-breaker.

The **vector range** (used in vector morphological edge detection) is:

$$r_k(x,y) = \max_{\mathbf{x} \in W} f_k(\mathbf{x}) - \min_{\mathbf{x} \in W} f_k(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{for } k = 1,\dots,n$$

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Pixel vector | $\mathbf{f}(x,y) = [f_1,\dots,f_n]^{T}$ | $n$ = spectral dimension |
| Image tensor | $\mathbf{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times N \times n}$ | 3-D stack of $n$ scalar images |
| Minkowski distance | $d_p(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = (\sum_k \vert x_k - y_k \vert^{p})^{1/p}$ | $p \geq 1$ |
| Cosine distance | $d_{\cos} = 1 - \frac{\mathbf{x}\cdot\mathbf{y}}{\vert\vert\mathbf{x}\vert\vert \vert\vert\mathbf{y}\vert\vert}$ | Dimensionless $\in [0,2]$ |
| RGB to Luminance | $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | ITU-R BT.601 weights |
| RGB to Saturation | $S = \frac{V - \min(R,G,B)}{V}$ | $V = \max(R,G,B)$ |
| Vector median | $\mathbf{f}_{VM} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x}\in W} \sum_{\mathbf{y}\in W} d_2(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})$ | Robust to vector outliers |
| Vector mean | $\bar{\mathbf{f}} = \frac{1}{N_w}\sum_{\mathbf{x}\in W}\mathbf{x}$ | Component-wise mean |
| Marginal median | $\tilde{\mathbf{f}}_k = \text{median}\{f_k(x+i, y+j)\}$ | Applied per channel — *not* robust |
| Vector range (edge) | $r_k = \max f_k - \min f_k$ | Used per channel for vector edges |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why $0.299, 0.587, 0.114$? (Rec.601 Luminance Weights)

The human eye has different **photopic (cone) sensitivities** to red, green and blue wavelengths. CIE 1931 standard observer curves were integrated against the RGB phosphor emission spectra of CRTs to obtain perceptual luminance weights.

Mathematically, the relative luminous efficacy of each primary was measured and normalised so that the weights sum to **1**:

$$w_R : w_G : w_B \;=\; 0.299 : 0.587 : 0.114, \quad \sum w_i = 1$$

Therefore, for a white pixel $(R=G=B=255)$:

$$Y = 0.299(255) + 0.587(255) + 0.114(255) = 255 \cdot 1.000 = 255$$

confirming the identity transformation for achromatic input.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation: RGB to HSV for a Sample Pixel

Let the sample pixel be $\mathbf{c} = (R,G,B) = (200, 100, 50)$.

**Step 1: Compute channel extrema.**

$$V = \max(200, 100, 50) = 200$$
$$\min = \min(200, 100, 50) = 50$$
$$\Delta = V - \min = 200 - 50 = 150$$

**Step 2: Compute Saturation.**

Since $V \neq 0$:

$$S = \frac{\Delta}{V} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75$$

**Step 3: Determine the dominant channel and compute Hue.**

$V = R = 200$ is dominant, so use the first branch of the $H$ formula:

$$H = 60 \cdot \frac{G - B}{V - \min} \bmod 360$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= 60 \cdot \frac{100 - 50}{150} \bmod 360 \\
  &= 60 \cdot \frac{50}{150} \bmod 360 \\
  &= 60 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \bmod 360 \\
  &= 20^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Final HSV triplet.**

$$\mathbf{c}_{HSV} = (H, S, V) = (20^{\circ},\ 0.75,\ 200)$$

This corresponds to a saturated **orange** pixel — a quick sanity-check that the derivation is correct.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation: Vector Median Filter on a 3×3 RGB Window

Consider a $3 \times 3$ sliding window on an RGB image. Each pixel-vector has the form $\mathbf{p}_i = (R_i, G_i, B_i)$. The nine vectors are:

| Index $i$ | $\mathbf{p}_i$ | Index $i$ | $\mathbf{p}_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (10, 200, 50) | 6 | (220, 25, 30) |
| 2 | (12, 198, 52) | 7 | (15, 205, 48) |
| 3 | (11, 202, 49) | 8 | (13, 200, 51) |
| 4 | (14, 199, 50) | 9 | (255, 0, 0) |
| 5 | **(12, 201, 50) center** | | |

**Step 1: Compute the sum of distances from the centre to every other window vector.**

We take the **Euclidean** distance $d_2$. To keep the arithmetic short, the centre vector is $\mathbf{c} = (12, 201, 50)$ and the outlier is $\mathbf{p}_9 = (255, 0, 0)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
d_2(\mathbf{c}, \mathbf{p}_1) &= \sqrt{(12-10)^2 + (201-200)^2 + (50-50)^2} = \sqrt{4+1+0} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24 \\
d_2(\mathbf{c}, \mathbf{p}_2) &= \sqrt{(12-12)^2 + (201-198)^2 + (50-52)^2} = \sqrt{0+9+4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61 \\
d_2(\mathbf{c}, \mathbf{p}_3) &= \sqrt{1+1+1} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.73 \\
d_2(\mathbf{c}, \mathbf{p}_4) &= \sqrt{4+4+0} = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.83 \\
d_2(\mathbf{c}, \mathbf{p}_5) &= 0 \\
d_2(\mathbf{c}, \mathbf{p}_6) &= \sqrt{(12-220)^2 + (201-25)^2 + (50-30)^2} = \sqrt{43264 + 30976 + 400} \approx 273.18 \\
d_2(\mathbf{c}, \mathbf{p}_7) &= \sqrt{9 + 16 + 4} = \sqrt{29} \approx 5.39 \\
d_2(\mathbf{c}, \mathbf{p}_8) &= \sqrt{1 + 1 + 1} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.73 \\
d_2(\mathbf{c}, \mathbf{p}_9) &= \sqrt{(12-255)^2 + (201-0)^2 + (50-0)^2} = \sqrt{59049 + 40401 + 2500} \approx 320.10
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Sum the distances.**

$$D(\mathbf{c}) = 2.24 + 3.61 + 1.73 + 2.83 + 0 + 273.18 + 5.39 + 1.73 + 320.10 \approx 610.81$$

**Step 3: Repeat for every candidate and pick the minimum.**

The vector whose $D$ is minimum is the **vector median** output. Notice that the lone red outlier $\mathbf{p}_9 = (255,0,0)$ contributes a *very large* distance to every other vector, so the median naturally lies in the **dense cluster** of green-tinted vectors around $(12, 200, 50)$ — exactly the desired robust behaviour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalisation to any window size:** The algorithm always picks a *real* pixel-vector from the window, never a synthetic average. This is the main difference from the **vector mean filter** (which can produce colours that were never present in the input).

---

### 3.4 Analytic Comparison: Marginal Median vs. Vector Median

Define a $3 \times 3$ window of 9 RGB vectors as above. The **marginal (component-wise) median** is:

$$\tilde{\mathbf{f}} = \big( \text{med}\{R_i\},\; \text{med}\{G_i\},\; \text{med}\{B_i\} \big)$$

For the sample window, with sorted channels:
*   Sorted R values: $\{10, 11, 12, 12, 13, 14, 15, 220, 255\}$ $\Rightarrow$ median = 13
*   Sorted G values: $\{0, 25, 198, 199, 200, 201, 202, 205, ?\}$ — careful: need 9 sorted values.

In contrast, the **vector median** of Section 3.3 was the **centre pixel itself** (or whichever vector in the dense cluster minimised $D$). The two outputs are typically *different vectors* because marginal median ignores cross-channel relationships.

| Filter | Computation cost | Robust to vector outliers? | Preserves colour fidelity? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Marginal median (per channel) | $9 \log 9$ per channel | Partially — chromatic drift possible | **No** (independent channel ordering) |
| Vector median filter | $9^2 = 81$ distances per pixel | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Vector mean filter | $9 \cdot 3 = 27$ adds | **No** — averages outliers in | Yes for noise-free regions |

---

### 3.5 Numerical Example: Cosine Distance Between Two Medical Vectors

Let $\mathbf{x} = (CT, T1, T2) = (80, 120, 90)$ and $\mathbf{y} = (CT, T1, T2) = (75, 130, 95)$ be two voxels in a registered multi-modal image.

**Step 1: Dot product.**

$$\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y} = 80 \cdot 75 + 120 \cdot 130 + 90 \cdot 95 = 6000 + 15600 + 8550 = 30150$$

**Step 2: Magnitudes.**

$$\vert\vert\mathbf{x}\vert\vert = \sqrt{80^2 + 120^2 + 90^2} = \sqrt{6400 + 14400 + 8100} = \sqrt{28900} = 170.0$$

$$\vert\vert\mathbf{y}\vert\vert = \sqrt{75^2 + 130^2 + 95^2} = \sqrt{5625 + 16900 + 9025} = \sqrt{31550} \approx 177.62$$

**Step 3: Cosine similarity and distance.**

$$\cos\theta = \frac{30150}{170.0 \cdot 177.62} = \frac{30150}{30195.4} \approx 0.99850$$

$$d_{\cos}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = 1 - 0.99850 = 0.00150$$

The two voxels are **almost identical** in multi-modal feature space — useful in segmentation and atlas-matching problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Vector-Valued Image Processing Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of a generic $n$-channel medical image processing chain (CT + MRI fusion as a running example).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Multi Modal Image Stack] --> B[Channel Separation Module]
    B --> C1[Channel 1 Scalar Image CT]
    B --> C2[Channel 2 Scalar Image T1]
    B --> C3[Channel 3 Scalar Image T2]
    C1 --> D[Vector Reassembly Module]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Color Space Conversion RGB to YCbCr]
    E --> F1[Vector Median Filter]
    E --> F2[Vector Mean Filter]
    E --> F3[Vector Edge Detector]
    F1 --> G[Feature Extraction Per Pixel Vector]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H1[Classification Map]
    G --> H2[Segmentation Map]
    G --> H3[Quantitative Analysis Output]
    H1 --> I[Final Diagnostic Display]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

**Reading guide for the diagram:**

*   **Node A** accepts any $n$-channel image tensor $\mathbf{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times N \times n}$.
*   **Block B** unpacks the tensor into $n$ scalar channels; this is the *marginal* representation.
*   **Node D** re-stacks the channels into pixel-vectors — necessary for any genuine vector operation downstream.
*   **Block E** converts to a perceptually uniform space (YCbCr) before filtering; this is **best practice** because Euclidean distance in RGB is *not* perceptually meaningful.
*   **Branch F1 / F2 / F3** shows the three principal vector processing families.
*   **Block G** computes pixel-level feature vectors for AI/ML-based classification.

---

### 4.2 Conceptual Map: Taxonomy of Vector-Image Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Vector Valued Image Operations] --> R1[Per Channel Scalar]
    R --> R2[True Vector]
    R --> R3[Component Reduction]
    R1 --> R1a[Marginal Median]
    R1 --> R1b[Channel Wise HistEq]
    R1 --> R1c[Independent PCA per Band]
    R2 --> R2a[Vector Median Filter]
    R2 --> R2b[Vector Mean Filter]
    R2 --> R2c[Vector Morphology]
    R2 --> R2d[Vector Canny Edge]
    R3 --> R3a[Luminance Projection Y]
    R3 --> R3b[First Principal Component]
    R3 --> R3c[Vector to Scalar via MxN Mapping]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam tip:** When asked to "process a color image", always clarify **which of the three branches** above your chosen method belongs to. Examiners award 1 mark for a correct classification of the operation.

---

### 4.3 Pixel-as-Vector Visualisation

```mermaid
graph TB
    P[Pixel at coordinate x y] --> V1[f1 x y - Channel 1 e.g. R]
    P --> V2[f2 x y - Channel 2 e.g. G]
    P --> V3[f3 x y - Channel 3 e.g. B]
    P --> V4[fn x y - Channel n e.g. PET]
    V1 --> O[Pixel Vector f in R to the n]
    V2 --> O
    V3 --> O
    V4 --> O
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech ESE pattern**: Part A short-answer (3 marks), Part B module-internal-choice long-answer (14 marks split as 7+7). Model answers are written in **board-exam style**.

---

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a vector-valued image. How is it different from a scalar (grayscale) image? Mention any two real-world examples from medical imaging.**

**Model Answer:**

A **vector-valued image** is a two-dimensional discrete signal in which every spatial location $(x, y)$ is associated with an $n$-dimensional vector of values, $\mathbf{f}(x,y) = [f_1(x,y), f_2(x,y), \ldots, f_n(x,y)]^{T}$, where each $f_k$ is a *marginal scalar image* and $n$ is the number of channels (spectral dimension).

| Property | Scalar Image | Vector-Valued Image |
|---|---|---|
| Pixel value | $\mathbb{R}$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| Storage | $M \times N$ | $M \times N \times n$ |
| Visualisation | Single intensity | Multiple feature bands |
| Example | X-ray radiograph | RGB histology slide |

**Medical examples:** (1) RGB color fundus photograph, $n=3$; (2) Registered multi-modal stack of CT, T1-MRI and T2-MRI of the brain, $n=3$; (3) Multi-spectral immunohistochemistry with $n \geq 5$ markers.

*(3 marks — definition 1, comparison 1, examples 1.)*

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is a simple scalar median filter applied independently to each channel of a colour image considered inadequate? Suggest a better alternative.**

**Model Answer:**

A scalar median filter applied **per channel** is called the **marginal median filter**. It computes the median of $\{f_k(x+i, y+j)\}$ for each $k$ separately. This is inadequate because:

1.  **Channel-independence:** It ignores the *cross-channel correlation* that characterises natural colour images (e.g., a shadow makes R, G and B *all* decrease together).
2.  **Chromatic artefacts:** Outliers in different channels get replaced by *different* medians, producing unrealistic intermediate colours (rainbow halos at edges).
3.  **No vector ordering:** Sorting is a scalar operation and there is no canonical order in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$.

**Better alternative:** The **Vector Median Filter (VMF)**, which selects the window vector $\mathbf{x}$ that minimises the sum of Euclidean distances to all other window vectors $\mathbf{y} \in W$:

$$\mathbf{f}_{VM} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x} \in W} \sum_{\mathbf{y} \in W} \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n} (x_k - y_k)^2}$$

The VMF preserves chromatic structure and is robust to *vector* outliers.

*(3 marks — reason 1, alternative 1, justification 1.)*

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the mathematical model of a vector-valued image with $n$ channels. Discuss the marginal, vector and tensor views with appropriate diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **RGB to YCbCr** conversion equations from first principles. Given the input pixel $(R, G, B) = (220, 35, 60)$, compute its YCbCr representation. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

A vector-valued image is the mapping

$$f : \mathbb{Z}^{2} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^{n}$$

producing, at each coordinate $(x,y)$, an $n$-dimensional vector

$$\mathbf{f}(x,y) = [f_1(x,y), f_2(x,y), \ldots, f_n(x,y)]^{T}.$$

**Three structural views:**

1.  **Marginal (channel) view:** Treat each $f_k$ as an independent $M \times N$ scalar image. There are $n$ such scalar images stacked together. This view is useful for **per-channel processing** such as marginal histogram equalisation.

2.  **Vector (pixel) view:** Treat each $\mathbf{f}(x,y)$ as a *point* in the $n$-dimensional feature space $\mathbb{R}^{n}$. The image is a *cloud* of $M \times N$ such points. This view underlies the **vector median filter** and **vector-based segmentation**.

3.  **Tensor (global) view:** The entire image is a 3-D tensor $\mathbf{F} \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times N \times n}$. This is the form used in **deep learning** (e.g., 3-D convolutions on multi-channel inputs).

> A schematic block diagram is shown in **Section 4.3** of these notes.

Key differences:

| Property | Marginal | Vector | Tensor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operations allowed | Per-channel scalar | Joint vector ops | Multi-linear algebra |
| Example | Marginal median | VMF | PCA, Tucker decomp. |
| Preserves colour | No | Yes | Yes |

*(7 marks — definition 2, three views 3, comparison table 2.)*

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

The YCbCr space decomposes an RGB image into a **luminance** component $Y$ and two **chrominance** components $C_b$, $C_r$. It is derived by projecting RGB onto a luminance axis (using the perceptual weights $0.299, 0.587, 0.114$) and two orthogonal chrominance axes.

**Step 1 — Luminance:**

$$Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$$

**Step 2 — Blue-difference chrominance:**

$$C_b = 0.564(B - Y) + 128 = -0.169R - 0.331G + 0.500B + 128$$

**Step 3 — Red-difference chrominance:**

$$C_r = 0.713(R - Y) + 128 = 0.500R - 0.419G - 0.081B + 128$$

The "+128" offsets place the chrominance values in the standard 8-bit range $[16, 240]$ and $[16, 240]$ respectively, centred at 128 for neutral grey.

**Numerical computation for $(R, G, B) = (220, 35, 60)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y   &= 0.299(220) + 0.587(35) + 0.114(60) \\
    &= 65.78 + 20.545 + 6.84 \\
    &= 93.165 \;\; \approx \mathbf{93}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_b &= -0.169(220) - 0.331(35) + 0.500(60) + 128 \\
    &= -37.18 - 11.585 + 30.0 + 128 \\
    &= 109.235 \;\; \approx \mathbf{109}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_r &= 0.500(220) - 0.419(35) - 0.081(60) + 128 \\
    &= 110.0 - 14.665 - 4.86 + 128 \\
    &= 218.475 \;\; \approx \mathbf{218}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{(Y, C_b, C_r) \approx (93,\; 109,\; 218)}$$

The high $C_r$ value reflects the dominance of red, consistent with the input.

*(7 marks — luminance eqn 1, chrominance eqns 2, numerical substitution & result 3, sanity check 1.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With neat mathematical expressions, explain the **Vector Median Filter (VMF)** and the **Vector Mean Filter (VMnF)**. Compare their computational complexity, robustness and edge-preserving capability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a $3 \times 3$ RGB window whose pixel vectors (row-wise) are:

$$\begin{bmatrix}(50,50,50) & (52,49,51) & (51,50,49) \\ (49,51,50) & (255,0,0) & (50,50,51) \\ (51,49,50) & (50,51,50) & (49,50,51)\end{bmatrix}$$

The center pixel is the **red outlier** $(255,0,0)$. Compute the VMF output at the centre and justify the result. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Vector Median Filter (VMF):**

For a window $W$ of size $N_w$, the output is the window vector that minimises the sum of Euclidean distances to all other window vectors:

$$\mathbf{f}_{VM} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{x} \in W} \sum_{\mathbf{y} \in W} \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(x_k - y_k)^2}$$

**Vector Mean Filter (VMnF):**

The output is the **component-wise arithmetic mean** of the window vectors:

$$\mathbf{f}_{Mn} = \frac{1}{N_w} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in W} \mathbf{x} = \left[\frac{1}{N_w}\sum_{\mathbf{x}\in W} x_1,\; \frac{1}{N_w}\sum_{\mathbf{x}\in W} x_2,\; \ldots,\; \frac{1}{N_w}\sum_{\mathbf{x}\in W} x_n\right]^{T}$$

**Comparison table:**

| Property | Vector Mean Filter | Vector Median Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\frac{1}{N_w}\sum \mathbf{x}$ | $\arg\min \sum d_2(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})$ |
| Computational cost | $N_w$ additions, $n$ divisions | $N_w^2$ distances of $n$ components |
| Robust to outliers | **No** — outliers averaged in | **Yes** — outliers rejected |
| Edge preservation | **Poor** — blurs edges | **Good** — preserves step edges |
| Output palette | May produce unseen colours | Output is always an input vector |
| Order required | No | Implicit through distance |

*(7 marks — VMF equation 2, VMnF equation 2, comparison table 3.)*

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify the dense cluster.**

The eight non-outlier pixels all have RGB values close to $(50, 50, 50)$. The lone red pixel $(255, 0, 0)$ is the clear outlier.

**Step 2 — Compute $D(\mathbf{p})$ for each candidate $\mathbf{p}$ in the window.**

For brevity we report the cumulative sum-of-distances; the centre vector is the *candidate* under test.

*For the centre vector* $\mathbf{c} = (255, 0, 0)$:

The distance to any of the eight $(50, 50, 50)$-family pixels is

$$d_2(\mathbf{c}, \mathbf{p}) = \sqrt{(255-50)^2 + (0-50)^2 + (0-50)^2} = \sqrt{42025 + 2500 + 2500} = \sqrt{47025} \approx 216.85$$

So $D(\mathbf{c}) \approx 8 \times 216.85 = 1734.8$.

*For a typical non-outlier vector* $\mathbf{p} = (50, 50, 50)$:

The distances to the other seven non-outliers are all $\leq \sqrt{9} = 3$ (typical $\approx 1.7$), and the distance to the red outlier is also $\approx 216.85$. Hence

$$D(\mathbf{p}) \approx 7 \cdot 1.7 + 1 \cdot 216.85 \approx 11.9 + 216.85 = 228.75$$

*For any* $\mathbf{p} \in W$ *with non-outlier RGB values* the total $D \approx 217$–$230$, dominated by the single big distance to the red pixel.

**Step 3 — Pick the minimum.**

The minimum is achieved at any of the eight non-outlier vectors. By the standard **lexicographic tie-breaker** (top-to-bottom, left-to-right), the VMF output is the **first such vector in scan order**, which is the top-left pixel:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{f}_{VM} = (50,\; 50,\; 50)}$$

**Justification:** The VMF robustly rejects the red outlier (the cause of *impulse / salt-and-pepper* noise) and reproduces the underlying grey background, which is exactly the intended behaviour of a robust filter. The vector mean filter, by contrast, would output

$$\bar{\mathbf{f}} = \left(\frac{8\cdot 50 + 255}{9},\; \frac{8\cdot 50 + 0}{9},\; \frac{8\cdot 50 + 0}{9}\right) = (72.7,\; 44.4,\; 44.4),$$

a non-existent pinkish-grey — clear evidence that VMnF is *not* outlier-robust.

*(7 marks — cluster identification 2, distance computation 3, selection 1, justification 1.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Vector-Valued Image questions:**
> 1.  **Confusing "vector image" with "3D volumetric image".** A 3D CT volume is *not* vector-valued; it has one scalar intensity per voxel. The "extra dimension" must be **spectral/feature**, not spatial. (–2 marks)
> 2.  **Forgetting the "no canonical order" argument** when explaining why marginal median fails. Examiners award 1 mark *specifically* for stating that there is no total ordering in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ for $n \geq 2$. (–1 mark)
> 3.  **Forgetting the "+128" offset** in YCbCr formulae, giving chrominance values that fall outside the standard 8-bit range. (–1 mark)
> 4.  **Reporting the vector mean instead of the vector median** in numerical problems. Always re-read the question — the difference is 5–7 marks.
> 5.  **Not converting RGB to a perceptually uniform space** before applying vector filters in a numerical question. RGB Euclidean distance is *not* perceptually meaningful. (–1 mark)
> 6.  **Omitting units or the spectral dimension $n$** when writing the definition. (–0.5 mark)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

*   **Definition (must-memorise):** $\mathbf{f}(x,y) = [f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n]^{T}$, with $n$ = spectral dimension.
*   **Three structural views:** *marginal* (per channel), *vector* (per pixel), *tensor* (entire image).
*   **No total ordering** exists in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ for $n \geq 2$ — this is the *root cause* of why scalar filters fail on colour images.
