> - **Forgetting normalization:** Computing features directly on the raw co-occurrence counts $P_d$ instead of probabilities $p$ will yield incorrect values and lose **2–3 marks**.
> - **Confusing marginal means with overall mean:** $\mu_x$ is the mean of $p_x(i)$, **not** the mean of all $i$ values in the image.
> - **Off-by-one in pixel pairs:** Always remember that with offset $\mathbf{d} = (0, 1)$ on an $M \times N$ image, only $(N-1)$ pairs per row are valid. Forgetting this changes the divisor $R$.
> - **Skipping the $(0 \log 0 = 0)$ convention** in entropy: undefined terms may appear if the GLCM has zero entries; these must be omitted (not set to $\infty$).
> - **Single-direction GLCM only:** KTU expects you to mention that, in practice, features are averaged over 4 directions to achieve rotation invariance.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**
> - GLCM = **second-order** statistical texture descriptor (joint distribution of neighboring pixel pairs).
> - GLCM size = $N_g \times N_g$, where $N_g$ is the number of quantized gray levels (typically 8, 16, or 32 for medical images).
> - Displacement vector $\mathbf{d} = (d_x, d_y)$ defines neighbor relation; **4 canonical directions** (0°, 45°, 90°, 135°) are used in practice.
> - **Symmetric GLCM:** $P_{\text{sym}}(i,j) = P_d(i,j) + P_d(j,i)$.
> - **Normalized GLCM:** $p(i,j) = P_d(i,j) / R$, where $R$ = total pair count.
> - **Contrast** = $\sum \sum (i-j)^2 p(i,j)$ → local intensity variation.
> - **Homogeneity (IDM)** = $\sum \sum \frac{p(i,j)}{1 + (i-j)^2}$ → diagonal concentration.
> - **Energy (ASM)** = $\sum \sum [p(i,j)]^2$ → uniformity.
> - **Entropy** = $-\sum \sum p(i,j) \log_2 p(i,j)$ → randomness.
> - **Correlation** = $\frac{\sum \sum (i-\mu_x)(j-\mu_y) p(i,j)}{\sigma_x \sigma_y}$ → linear gray-level dependency.
> - **Haralick's 14 features** (1973) are the standard set; clinically used in **radiomics** for tumor grading, prognosis, and CAD systems.
> - Always **normalize** before feature extraction; always **average** features over 4 directions for rotation invariance.
> - KTU favorite 14-mark question pattern: *"Construct GLCM for a given image + compute 2–3 features"* (always paired with definition + Haralick discussion).
> - KTU favorite 3-mark question: *"Define GLCM / List features / Explain displacement vector."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generalized Co-occurrence Matrices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Generalized Co-occurrence Matrices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalized Co-occurrence Matrix (GCM)**: A second-order statistical descriptor of spatial texture in digital images that generalizes the classical Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) by relaxing the constraint of a single displacement vector $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ to an *arbitrary set* of displacement vectors $\mathcal{S}$. The GCM tabulates the joint probability $P(i,j;\mathcal{S})$ of encountering pixel intensity $i$ at a reference location and intensity $j$ at any location displaced by a vector contained in $\mathcal{S}$.

Mathematically, the GCM is expressed as:

$$
P(i,j;\mathcal{S}) = \frac{1}{Z}\sum_{\mathbf{r}}\sum_{\mathbf{d}\in\mathcal{S}} \delta\!\left(I(\mathbf{r})-i\right)\,\delta\!\left(I(\mathbf{r}+\mathbf{d})-j\right)
$$

where $I(\mathbf{r})$ is the image intensity at spatial coordinate $\mathbf{r}$, $\delta(\cdot)$ is the Kronecker delta, $\mathcal{S}$ is the set of admissible displacement vectors, and $Z$ is the normalization constant (total co-occurrence count).

> [!NOTE]
> The classical GLCM (Haralick, 1973) is a **special case** of the GCM in which $\mathcal{S}=\{(\Delta x,\Delta y)\}$ for a single fixed distance and angle. The "generalization" comes from permitting $\mathcal{S}$ to be a multi-vector, multi-scale, or neighborhood-shaped set.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a textile inspector examining a piece of fabric under a magnifying glass. The classical GLCM is like asking: *"In a single direction (say, horizontal) at one fixed step size, how often does a light thread follow a dark thread?"* The Generalized Co-occurrence Matrix, however, is like a much **smarter inspector** who simultaneously inspects:

- **Multiple directions** (horizontal, vertical, diagonal, even the 12 o'clock direction).
- **Multiple step sizes** (1 thread apart, 2 threads apart, 5 threads apart).
- **Multiple neighborhood shapes** (linear, L-shaped, circular, square, 3-D spherical).

In the medical-imaging world, the GCM acts as that "smart inspector" for **tissue texture** — quantifying how gray-levels of neighboring voxels in a CT, MRI, or ultrasound image relate across the full spatial neighborhood. This is crucial for differentiating, for instance, a benign cyst from a malignant tumor, where the underlying tissue micro-architecture has a distinctive spatial signature.

### 1.3 Key Parameters of a GCM

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|------------------|
| Number of gray levels | $N_g$ | **8 – 256** (often **64** in medical imaging) | Quantization depth of the input image |
| Displacement set | $\mathcal{S}$ | Subset of $\mathbb{Z}^2$ or $\mathbb{Z}^3$ | Defines spatial relationship under study |
| Distance | $d = \vert \mathbf{d}\vert$ | **1 – 10 pixels/voxels** | Step size of co-occurrence |
| Angle | $\theta$ | $0°, 45°, 90°, 135°$ (2-D); 13 directions in 3-D | Orientation of displacement |
| Symmetry flag | — | `True` / `False` | Whether $P(i,j)=P(j,i)$ is enforced |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric**: For medical image analysis, the image is typically quantized to **$N_g = 64$** gray levels before computing the GCM. This balances statistical robustness (more counts) against quantization noise (coarser resolution). According to Haralick's original prescription, $N_g$ should lie in the range **32 – 128**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of GCM Construction

The GCM is constructed by traversing **every pixel $\mathbf{r}$** in the image and, for each displacement $\mathbf{d}\in\mathcal{S}$, incrementing the matrix entry corresponding to the intensity pair. The construction proceeds in the following structured steps:

1. **Quantization**: Reduce the original image from $B$ bits/pixel to $N_g$ gray levels via uniform or histogram-equalized quantization.
2. **Displacement-Set Definition**: Choose $\mathcal{S} = \{\mathbf{d}_1, \mathbf{d}_2, \dots, \mathbf{d}_K\}$. Common choices include:
   - **Single offset**: $\mathcal{S}=\{(1,0)\}$ (classical GLCM, $0°$).
   - **Four directions**: $\mathcal{S}=\{(1,0),(0,1),(1,1),(1,-1)\}$ (classical 2-D GLCM).
   - **Multi-scale**: $\mathcal{S}=\{(d\cos\theta_d,\,d\sin\theta_d) : d\in\{1,2,3\},\ \theta_d\in\{0°,\dots,135°\}\}$.
   - **Neighborhood (circular)**: $\mathcal{S}=\{\mathbf{d} : d\le d_{\max}\}$.
3. **Pair Counting**: For each $\mathbf{r}$ and each $\mathbf{d}\in\mathcal{S}$ such that $\mathbf{r}+\mathbf{d}$ lies inside the image, increment $C\bigl(I(\mathbf{r}), I(\mathbf{r}+\mathbf{d})\bigr)$.
4. **Symmetrization (optional)**: Replace $C$ with $C + C^T$ to make the matrix symmetric. This is the *un-normalized* form.
5. **Normalization**: Divide every entry by the total count $Z$ to obtain the joint-probability matrix $P(i,j) = C(i,j)/Z$.
6. **Feature Extraction**: Compute Haralick's 14 statistical descriptors (or a subset) from $P$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works for medical images**: Biological tissues exhibit characteristic spatial arrangements of intensities. Malignant tissue tends to have *higher contrast* and *lower homogeneity* than normal tissue because of heterogeneous cellularity, necrosis, and micro-calcifications. The GCM captures this heterogeneity through second-order statistics.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive reference** for KTU examinations. Master every entry — they are the most-tested formulas in Module 2.

Let $P(i,j)$ be the normalized GCM, $i,j \in \{1,\dots,N_g\}$, and let $P_x(i)=\sum_j P(i,j)$, $P_y(j)=\sum_i P(i,j)$ be the marginal probabilities. Define the means $\mu_x = \sum_i i\,P_x(i)$ and $\mu_y = \sum_j j\,P_y(j)$, and standard deviations $\sigma_x, \sigma_y$.

| # | Feature | Formula | Range / Behavior |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | **Angular Second Moment (Energy)** | $\displaystyle f_1 = \sum_i\sum_j P(i,j)^2$ | $0 < f_1 \le 1$; $\uparrow$ for uniform images |
| 2 | **Contrast** | $\displaystyle f_2 = \sum_i\sum_j (i-j)^2 P(i,j)$ | $f_2 \ge 0$; $\uparrow$ for high local intensity variation |
| 3 | **Correlation** | $\displaystyle f_3 = \frac{\sum_i\sum_j (i-\mu_x)(j-\mu_y) P(i,j)}{\sigma_x \sigma_y}$ | $-1 \le f_3 \le 1$ |
| 4 | **Sum of Squares (Variance)** | $\displaystyle f_4 = \sum_i\sum_j (i-\mu)^2 P(i,j)$ | $f_4 \ge 0$ |
| 5 | **Inverse Difference Moment (Homogeneity)** | $\displaystyle f_5 = \sum_i\sum_j \frac{P(i,j)}{1+(i-j)^2}$ | $0 < f_5 \le 1$; $\uparrow$ for homogeneous texture |
| 6 | **Sum Average** | $\displaystyle f_6 = \sum_{k=2}^{2N_g} k\,P_{x+y}(k)$ | $2 \le f_6 \le 2N_g$ |
| 7 | **Sum Entropy** | $\displaystyle f_7 = -\sum_{k=2}^{2N_g} P_{x+y}(k)\,\log P_{x+y}(k)$ | $f_7 \ge 0$ |
| 8 | **Entropy** | $\displaystyle f_8 = -\sum_i\sum_j P(i,j)\,\log P(i,j)$ | $f_8 \ge 0$; $\uparrow$ for busy textures |
| 9 | **Difference Variance** | $\displaystyle f_9 = \sum_{k=0}^{N_g-1} (k-\mu_{x-y})^2 P_{x-y}(k)$ | $f_9 \ge 0$ |
| 10 | **Difference Entropy** | $\displaystyle f_{10} = -\sum_{k=0}^{N_g-1} P_{x-y}(k)\log P_{x-y}(k)$ | $f_{10} \ge 0$ |
| 11 | **Maximal Correlation Coefficient** | $\displaystyle f_{11} = \sqrt{\text{second largest eigenvalue of } Q}$ | $0 \le f_{11} \le 1$ |

where the auxiliary distributions are:
$$
P_{x+y}(k) = \sum_{i+j=k} P(i,j), \qquad P_{x-y}(k) = \sum_{\vert i-j\vert = k} P(i,j)
$$
$$
\mu_{x-y} = \sum_{k=0}^{N_g-1} k\,P_{x-y}(k), \qquad Q(i,j) = \sum_k \frac{P(i,k) P(j,k)}{P_x(k) P_y(k)}
$$

### 2.3 The "Generalized" Aspect — Why It Matters

| Classical GLCM | Generalized GCM (GCM) |
|----------------|----------------------|
| Single $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ | Arbitrary set $\mathcal{S}$ of vectors |
| Single scale | **Multi-scale** aggregation |
| One angle per matrix | **Multi-directional** aggregation in one matrix |
| 2-D only | **2-D and 3-D** (volumetric) support |
| Intensity only | Can be **adapted to gradient, Laplacian, or vector-valued** features |

> [!NOTE]
> **3-D Extension for Volumetric Medical Data**: For a 3-D image $I(x,y,z)$, define $\mathcal{S}_{3D}$ with 13 standard displacement vectors along the face-centers and corners of a cube (e.g., $(1,0,0), (1,1,0), (1,1,1)$, etc.). This is the standard for **CT/MRI tumor-texture analysis** as recommended in the IBSI (Image Biomarker Standardisation Initiative) guidelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Constructing a GCM

**Problem Setup**: Consider a $4\times 4$ grayscale image (already quantized to 3 gray levels: 1, 2, 3):

$$
I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 2 & 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

We construct a **symmetric Generalized GCM** using the displacement set:
$$
\mathcal{S} = \{(1,0),\ (0,1),\ (1,1),\ (1,-1)\}
$$

#### Step 1 — Horizontal Pairs $\mathbf{d}=(1,0)$ (12 pairs)

Scan each row; for every $(i,j)$ where $j$ is one column to the right of $i$:

- Row 0: $(1,1)\to C_{11}\!+\!+$; $(1,2)\to C_{12}\!+\!+$; $(2,3)\to C_{23}\!+\!+$
- Row 1: $(1,2)\to C_{12}\!+\!+$; $(2,2)\to C_{22}\!+\!+$; $(2,1)\to C_{21}\!+\!+$
- Row 2: $(3,2)\to C_{32}\!+\!+$; $(2,1)\to C_{21}\!+\!+$; $(1,2)\to C_{12}\!+\!+$
- Row 3: $(2,1)\to C_{21}\!+\!+$; $(1,3)\to C_{13}\!+\!+$; $(3,2)\to C_{32}\!+\!+$

**Tally for $\mathbf{d}=(1,0)$:**

$$
C^{(h)} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

#### Step 2 — Vertical Pairs $\mathbf{d}=(0,1)$ (12 pairs)

By analogous scanning of columns we obtain:

$$
C^{(v)} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 3 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

> **Textual explanation**: We swap the row and column iteration order so that $C^{(v)}(i,j)$ counts co-occurrences of $i$ at row $r$ and $j$ at row $r\!+\!1$ in the same column.

#### Step 3 — Diagonal Pairs $\mathbf{d}=(1,1)$ and $\mathbf{d}=(1,-1)$ (9 pairs each)

$$
C^{(d_+)} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix},\qquad C^{(d_-)} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

#### Step 4 — Sum the Directional Matrices (Aggregation Step of the GCM)

$$
C_{\text{raw}} = C^{(h)} + C^{(v)} + C^{(d_+)} + C^{(d_-)} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 6 & 2 \\ 5 & 3 & 5 \\ 0 & 4 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

> **Textual explanation**: This aggregation is the *defining step* of the **Generalized** Co-occurrence Matrix — the four classical 2-D directions are merged into a single descriptor.

#### Step 5 — Symmetrization

$$
C_{\text{sym}} = C_{\text{raw}} + C_{\text{raw}}^{T} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 & 11 & 2 \\ 11 & 6 & 9 \\ 2 & 9 & 4 \end{pmatrix}
$$

#### Step 6 — Normalization (Total = 60)

$$
P = \frac{1}{60}\,C_{\text{sym}} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.100 & 0.183 & 0.033 \\ 0.183 & 0.100 & 0.150 \\ 0.033 & 0.150 & 0.067 \end{pmatrix}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Three Haralick Features on the Example

#### Feature 1: Angular Second Moment (Energy)

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 &= \sum_i\sum_j P(i,j)^2 \\[4pt]
    &= (0.100)^2 + (0.183)^2 + (0.033)^2 + (0.183)^2 + (0.100)^2 + (0.150)^2 \\
    &\quad + (0.033)^2 + (0.150)^2 + (0.067)^2 \\[4pt]
    &= 0.0100 + 0.0336 + 0.0011 + 0.0336 + 0.0100 + 0.0225 \\
    &\quad + 0.0011 + 0.0225 + 0.0045 \\[4pt]
    &= 0.1389
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual explanation**: A value of $f_1 \approx 0.139$ indicates the image is **not highly uniform** (energy is low), consistent with a mixed-texture medical phantom.

#### Feature 2: Contrast

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_2 &= \sum_i\sum_j (i-j)^2\,P(i,j) \\[4pt]
    &= 0\cdot0.100 + 1\cdot0.183 + 4\cdot0.033 + 1\cdot0.183 + 0\cdot0.100 + 1\cdot0.150 \\
    &\quad + 4\cdot0.033 + 1\cdot0.150 + 0\cdot0.067 \\[4pt]
    &= 0 + 0.183 + 0.133 + 0.183 + 0 + 0.150 + 0.133 + 0.150 + 0 \\[4pt]
    &= 0.933
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual explanation**: The contrast value $f_2 \approx 0.933$ is moderate, indicating that the image contains a mix of similar and dissimilar neighboring pixels — typical of a textured biological region.

#### Feature 5: Inverse Difference Moment (Homogeneity)

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_5 &= \sum_i\sum_j \frac{P(i,j)}{1+(i-j)^2} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{0.100}{1} + \frac{0.183}{2} + \frac{0.033}{5} + \frac{0.183}{2} + \frac{0.100}{1} + \frac{0.150}{2} \\
    &\quad + \frac{0.033}{5} + \frac{0.150}{2} + \frac{0.067}{1} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.100 + 0.0917 + 0.0067 + 0.0917 + 0.100 + 0.0750 + 0.0067 + 0.0750 + 0.067 \\[4pt]
    &= 0.6138
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual explanation**: $f_5 \approx 0.614$ means that 61% of pixel pairs in the image are locally homogeneous — a useful indicator of tissue uniformity in clinical texture analysis.

### 3.3 Generalization Beyond Standard 2-D GLCM

The full *generalized* form for an arbitrary displacement set $\mathcal{S}$ is:

$$
P(i,j;\mathcal{S}) = \frac{1}{Z(\mathcal{S})}\sum_{\mathbf{r}}\sum_{\mathbf{d}\in\mathcal{S}}\,\delta\!\bigl(I(\mathbf{r})-i\bigr)\,\delta\!\bigl(I(\mathbf{r}+\mathbf{d})-j\bigr),\qquad Z(\mathcal{S}) = \sum_{\mathbf{d}\in\mathcal{S}}N(\mathbf{d})
$$

where $N(\mathbf{d})$ is the number of valid $\mathbf{r}$ for displacement $\mathbf{d}$. The extension to 3-D volumetric data proceeds identically by replacing $\mathbf{r}\in\mathbb{Z}^2$ with $\mathbf{r}\in\mathbb{Z}^3$ and adopting the 13-direction cube neighborhood of the IBSI standard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: The GCM forms the mathematical foundation of *radiomics* — the high-throughput extraction of quantitative features from medical images for predictive modelling, treatment-response assessment, and computer-aided diagnosis (CADx) of cancers, Alzheimer's disease, and cardiovascular pathologies.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of GCM-Based Texture Analysis

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the full pipeline from a raw medical image to a Haralick-feature vector, emphasizing the **generalized** displacement-set aggregation stage that distinguishes the GCM from the classical GLCM.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Medical Image CT or MRI Slice] --> B[Intensity Normalization 0 to 1]
    B --> C[Quantization to Ng Gray Levels typically 64]
    C --> D[Define Generalized Displacement Set S]
    D --> D1[Direction 0 deg]
    D --> D2[Direction 45 deg]
    D --> D3[Direction 90 deg]
    D --> D4[Direction 135 deg]
    D --> D5[Optional Multi Scale distances 1 to 5]
    D1 --> E[Co occurrence Pair Counting Loop]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Aggregate Directional Matrices]
    F --> G[Symmetrization C plus C transpose]
    G --> H[Normalization to Joint Probability P]
    H --> I[Haralick Feature Computation]
    I --> I1[Energy ASM]
    I --> I2[Contrast]
    I --> I3[Correlation]
    I --> I4[Homogeneity IDM]
    I --> I5[Entropy]
    I --> I6[Sum and Difference Statistics]
    I1 --> J[Feature Vector for Radiomics]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    I5 --> J
    I6 --> J
    J --> K[Classifier SVM or Random Forest]
    K --> L[Clinical Decision Benign vs Malignant]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of the Displacement-Set Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Classical GLCM - Single Offset"]
        O1[Offset d=1 theta=0 deg]
    end
    subgraph S2["Generalized GCM - Multi Direction Multi Scale"]
        V1[Offset 1 0]
        V2[Offset 0 1]
        V3[Offset 1 1]
        V4[Offset 1 -1]
        V5[Offset 2 0 scale 2]
        V6[Offset 0 2 scale 2]
    end
    subgraph S3["3D GCM - 13 Cube Neighbors"]
        W1[6 face neighbors]
        W2[8 corner neighbors]
    end
    S1 --> A[Single Matrix]
    S2 --> B[Aggregated Matrix]
    S3 --> C[Volumetric Matrix]
```

### 4.3 Pixel-by-Pixel Pair Counting Illustration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pixel at location r intensity i] --> Q1{Is r plus d in bounds?}
    Q1 -- yes --> R1[Read intensity j of neighbor]
    R1 --> S1[Increment C i j by 1]
    S1 --> T1[Move to next pixel]
    T1 --> Q1
    Q1 -- no --> T1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation**: The counting loop runs $|I|\times|\mathcal{S}|$ times. For a $256\times 256$ image with $|\mathcal{S}|=4$, this corresponds to $262144$ pair-evaluations per direction. The resulting matrix is $N_g\times N_g = 64\times 64$ in the IBSI-recommended configuration, yielding $4096$ entries that are then compressed into 14 (or more) Haralick descriptors.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
Define the **Generalized Co-occurrence Matrix (GCM)**. How does it differ from the classical Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) introduced by Haralick?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A Generalized Co-occurrence Matrix (GCM) is a second-order statistical texture descriptor that tabulates the joint probability $P(i,j;\mathcal{S})$ of a pixel of intensity $i$ occurring at a location $\mathbf{r}$ and a pixel of intensity $j$ occurring at a location displaced by **any vector** $\mathbf{d}$ belonging to a user-defined set $\mathcal{S}$.
>
> **Differences from the classical GLCM:**
> 1. The GLCM uses a **single, fixed displacement vector** $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ — typically one of the four canonical 2-D directions at distance $d=1$.
> 2. The GCM uses an **arbitrary set** $\mathcal{S}$ of displacement vectors, which may combine multiple directions, multiple scales, and arbitrary neighborhood shapes (linear, L-shaped, circular, spherical, etc.).
> 3. The GCM can be extended naturally to **3-D volumetric medical images** (CT/MRI volumes) using the 13-direction cube neighborhood, whereas the classical GLCM is fundamentally 2-D.
>
> The classical GLCM is therefore a **special case** of the GCM in which $\mathcal{S}$ contains exactly one element. *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for differences.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
List any **four Haralick texture features** computed from a GCM and state, for each, whether its value **increases** or **decreases** for a highly heterogeneous tissue region.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Behavior for Heterogeneous Tissue |
|---------|----------------------------------|
| 1. Contrast ($f_2$) | **Increases** (large intensity differences) |
| 2. Homogeneity / IDM ($f_5$) | **Decreases** (less local uniformity) |
| 3. Energy / ASM ($f_1$) | **Decreases** (matrix entries spread out) |
| 4. Entropy ($f_8$) | **Increases** (more disorder) |

> *(1 mark for listing the four features correctly, 1 mark for the trend of each, 1 mark for overall presentation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #1**: Forgetting to **symmetrize** the matrix (i.e., adding $C$ and $C^T$) before normalization. This causes the GCM to become direction-dependent and the Haralick features lose their clinical validity. **Penalty: 2 marks**.
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #2**: Using a **raw 12-bit or 16-bit** image (e.g., $4096$ or $65536$ gray levels). The GCM becomes sparse and noisy. Always quantize to $N_g \in [32, 128]$ first. **Penalty: 1 mark**.
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #3**: Forgetting the **normalization constant $Z$** in the joint-probability formula, leading to Haralick features that are off by an order of magnitude. **Penalty: 2 marks**.
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #4**: Confusing the **displacement set** $\mathcal{S}$ with the **image neighborhood**. These are not the same — the neighborhood defines which pixels are *counted*, while $\mathcal{S}$ defines which *vector offsets* are evaluated. **Penalty: 1 mark**.
> - **MOST COMMON MISTAKE #5**: Computing features from a **non-symmetric** GCM, leading to asymmetric Haralick outputs (e.g., $P_{x+y}\neq P_{y+x}$). **Penalty: 1 mark**.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **block-level pipeline** for computing Haralick texture features from a medical image using a Generalized Co-occurrence Matrix. Mention the role of the displacement set $\mathcal{S}$ in this pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the $4\times 4$ image given below, construct the **symmetric Generalized Co-occurrence Matrix** using the displacement set $\mathcal{S}=\{(1,0), (0,1), (1,1), (1,-1)\}$ and compute the **Energy** and **Homogeneity** features. Quantize to $N_g=3$ levels (values 1, 2, 3). **(7 marks)**

$$
I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 2 & 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block-level pipeline (7 marks):**

| Stage | Description | Marks |
|-------|-------------|-------|
| 1. Pre-processing | Intensity normalization to $[0,1]$ and quantization to $N_g$ levels | 1 |
| 2. Displacement-set definition | Choose $\mathcal{S}$; describe multi-directional / multi-scale concept | 2 |
| 3. Pair counting | Iterate over $\mathbf{r}\in I$ and $\mathbf{d}\in\mathcal{S}$ | 1 |
| 4. Aggregation + symmetrization | Sum directional matrices, add transpose | 1 |
| 5. Normalization | Divide by $Z$ to obtain $P(i,j)$ | 1 |
| 6. Feature extraction | Compute 14 Haralick descriptors | 1 |

**Role of $\mathcal{S}$:** It is the **defining parameter** that generalizes the GCM. By enlarging $\mathcal{S}$ to include multiple directions, scales, and 3-D vectors, the GCM becomes a richer, rotation-invariant, multi-scale texture descriptor than the classical GLCM.

*(Block diagram: see Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete Mermaid flowchart.)*

**(b) Construction and feature computation (7 marks):**

From Section 3.1, the aggregated raw matrix is:

$$
C_{\text{raw}} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 6 & 2 \\ 5 & 3 & 5 \\ 0 & 4 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

After symmetrization:

$$
C_{\text{sym}} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 & 11 & 2 \\ 11 & 6 & 9 \\ 2 & 9 & 4 \end{pmatrix},\quad \text{Total } Z = 60
$$

Normalized matrix:

$$
P = \frac{1}{60}\begin{pmatrix} 6 & 11 & 2 \\ 11 & 6 & 9 \\ 2 & 9 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.100 & 0.183 & 0.033 \\ 0.183 & 0.100 & 0.150 \\ 0.033 & 0.150 & 0.067 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Energy ($f_1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 &= \sum_i\sum_j P(i,j)^2 \\
&= (0.100)^2 + (0.183)^2 + (0.033)^2 + (0.183)^2 + (0.100)^2 \\
&\quad + (0.150)^2 + (0.033)^2 + (0.150)^2 + (0.067)^2 \\
&= 0.1389
\end{aligned}
$$

**Homogeneity ($f_5$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_5 &= \sum_i\sum_j \frac{P(i,j)}{1+(i-j)^2} \\
&= \frac{0.100}{1} + \frac{0.183}{2} + \frac{0.033}{5} + \frac{0.183}{2} + \frac{0.100}{1} \\
&\quad + \frac{0.150}{2} + \frac{0.033}{5} + \frac{0.150}{2} + \frac{0.067}{1} \\
&= 0.6138
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $f_1 = 0.139$ (low energy → heterogeneous), $f_5 = 0.614$ (moderate homogeneity).

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks matrix aggregation, 2 marks symmetrization+normalization, 1.5 marks energy, 1.5 marks homogeneity.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive the formula for **Contrast** ($f_2$) and **Correlation** ($f_3$) Haralick features starting from the normalized GCM $P(i,j)$. Explain the physical meaning of each feature in the context of medical-image texture analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss, with suitable examples, **three distinct ways** in which the Generalized Co-occurrence Matrix extends beyond the classical GLCM. For each, explain its clinical significance in radiomics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivations and physical meaning (7 marks):**

**Contrast derivation:**

The contrast is defined as the second moment of the intensity-difference distribution weighted by $P$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_2 &= \sum_{i=1}^{N_g}\sum_{j=1}^{N_g}(i-j)^2\,P(i,j) \\
    &= \sum_{k=0}^{N_g-1} k^2\,P_{x-y}(k),\qquad P_{x-y}(k)=\sum_{\vert i-j\vert=k}P(i,j)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Derivation step**: Substitute $k=\vert i-j\vert$. For every off-diagonal "shell" of the GCM, weight the joint probability by the squared distance from the diagonal. Diagonal entries ($i=j$) contribute zero, while entries far from the diagonal contribute heavily. *(2 marks for derivation)*

**Physical meaning**: $f_2$ is large when neighboring pixels have *very different* intensities. In a CT scan, malignant tumors exhibit high contrast because of necrotic cores, hypervascular rims, and heterogeneous cellularity. In a mammogram, micro-calcifications within a lesion cause localized contrast spikes. *(1 mark)*

**Correlation derivation:**

Define row and column means $\mu_x,\mu_y$ and standard deviations $\sigma_x,\sigma_y$:

$$
\mu_x = \sum_i i\,P_x(i),\quad P_x(i)=\sum_j P(i,j)
$$

$$
\sigma_x^2 = \sum_i (i-\mu_x)^2 P_x(i)
$$

The correlation coefficient is the normalized cross-moment:

$$
f_3 = \frac{\sum_i\sum_j (i-\mu_x)(j-\mu_y)P(i,j)}{\sigma_x \sigma_y}
$$

> **Derivation step**: The numerator is the *cross-covariance* of $i$ and $j$ under the joint distribution $P$. Dividing by the product of standard deviations yields a unitless Pearson-like coefficient in $[-1,1]$. *(2 marks for derivation)*

**Physical meaning**: $f_3$ measures the **linear predictability** of one pixel's intensity from its neighbor's. Strong correlation indicates that gray levels change *smoothly* across the image — typical of healthy, well-perfused tissue. Loss of correlation suggests architectural disruption, a hallmark of malignancy. *(1 mark for physical meaning, 1 mark for presentation)*

**(b) Three generalizations of the GCM (7 marks):**

**Generalization 1 — Multi-Scale Displacement Sets**

The classical GLCM uses a single distance $d$. The GCM permits $\mathcal{S}=\{(d_k\cos\theta_k,\ d_k\sin\theta_k) : d_k\in\{1,2,3,4,5\}\}$, capturing texture at multiple spatial frequencies. *Clinical significance*: Tumors exhibit scale-dependent heterogeneity — fine-scale features (cellular-level) and coarse-scale features (organ-level perfusion) are both diagnostically important. Multi-scale GCM is the basis of the **Laws' texture energy masks** and is used in lung-nodule CADx. *(2.5 marks)*

**Generalization 2 — 3-D Volumetric Displacement**

For a CT or MRI volume $I(x,y,z)$, define the 13-direction cube-neighborhood set $\mathcal{S}_{3D}$. The resulting 3-D GCM is a 3-D second-order statistic. *Clinical significance*: A 2-D slice may miss a tumor's full 3-D heterogeneity (e.g., a "tail" of infiltration perpendicular to the slice plane). 3-D GCM features are now standard in **radiomic signatures** of glioblastoma, prostate cancer, and hepatocellular carcinoma, with demonstrably higher prognostic accuracy. *(2.5 marks)*

**Generalization 3 — Generalized Feature Channels (Non-Intensity)**

The GCM need not be restricted to raw intensity. It can be applied to any scalar map $M(\mathbf{r})$ derived from the image — e.g., gradient magnitude $\vert\nabla I\vert$, Laplacian $\nabla^2 I$, Gabor-filtered response, or wavelet sub-band coefficients. *Clinical significance*: Different feature channels highlight different tissue properties (edges, blobs, textures). Multi-channel GCM dramatically enlarges the radiomic feature space and is the foundation of **deep-radiomics** pipelines that fuse learned and hand-crafted features. *(2 marks)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for GCM (Module 2)**

- **Definition**: GCM = second-order joint-probability table of pixel intensities over an *arbitrary displacement set* $\mathcal{S}$.
- **Classical GLCM ⊂ GCM**: Setting $\mathcal{S}=\{\mathbf{d}_{\text{single}}\}$ recovers the Haralick GLCM.
- **Displacement Set $\mathcal{S}$**: The defining parameter. Can be a single vector, 4 directions, multi-scale, multi-directional, neighborhood-shaped, or 3-D.
- **Standard 3-D Set**: 13 cube-neighbor vectors per the IBSI guideline.
- **Gray-Level Quantization**: Always quantize to $N_g \in [32, 128]$ (typically **64** for medical images) before computing.
- **Symmetrization**: $C_{\text{sym}} = C + C^T$ to make the GCM direction-invariant.
- **Normalization**: $P(i,j) = C_{\text{sym}}(i,j) / Z$ where $Z = \sum_{i,j} C_{\text{sym}}(i,j)$.
- **14 Haralick Features**: Energy, Contrast, Correlation, Variance, IDM (Homogeneity), Sum Average, Sum Variance, Sum Entropy, Entropy, Difference Variance, Difference Entropy, IMC-1, IMC-2, Maximal Correlation.
- **Clinical Signatures**:
  - **Malignant vs. Benign**: ↑ Contrast, ↓ Homogeneity, ↑ Entropy.
  - **Treatment Response**: ↓ Correlation, ↑ Sum Entropy post-therapy.
  - **Tissue Characterization**: ↑ Energy for homogeneous organs (liver), ↓ for heterogeneous (lung).
- **Pitfalls**: Forgetting symmetrization, using too many gray levels, omitting normalization, confusing $\mathcal{S}$ with neighborhood.
- **Pipeline Position**: GCM sits between **pre-processing** and **classifier** in the radiomics workflow.
- **Mathematical Notation Vigilance**: Use $P(i,j;\mathcal{S})$ to emphasize the $\mathcal{S}$-dependence; $P(i,j)$ alone is a (commonly used) shorthand.
- **Energy vs. Entropy** are *complementary* indicators: high energy ⇔ low entropy.
- **Contrast** is a *variance-like* measure on the difference $|i-j|$; **Correlation** is a *covariance-like* measure on the joint distribution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Orientation Histograms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Orientation Histograms # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) An **Orientation Histogram** is a feature descriptor that represents the local image structure by accumulating the **magnitudes of image gradients** as a function of their **gradient orientations**, typically quantized into a fixed number of angular bins. It is fundamentally a 1-D (or 2-D joint) probability distribution $H(\theta)$ that summarizes how edge directions, texture patterns, or local structural cues are distributed within a Region of Interest (ROI) of a medical image. Mathematically, an orientation histogram is constructed by: $$H(\theta_k) = \sum_{x,y \in \text{ROI}} m(x,y) \cdot \delta\bigl(\theta(x,y) - \theta_k\bigr)$$ where $\theta_k$ is the $k$-th discrete bin center, $m(x,y)$ is the gradient magnitude at pixel $(x,y)$, $\theta(x,y)$ is the gradient orientation, and $\delta(\cdot)$ is the Kronecker delta that votes pixels into their respective bins. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (Module 2: Image Segmentation and Feature Extraction), orientation histograms are positioned as a **shape- and texture-based feature descriptor** used after edge detection or gradient computation, bridging the gap between low-level pixel gradients and high-level semantic features (e.g., organ contours, lesion boundaries, fiber tracts in Diffusion Tensor Imaging). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024, PEBMT755, Module 2):** Orientation Histograms are explicitly listed as a regional descriptor for representing local image structure. They appear in classical pipelines such as the **Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG)**, **SIFT descriptors**, and **Shape Context**, all of which are exam-relevant. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are looking at a **clock face placed over a small patch of an X-ray or MRI image**, and the clock has **N = 12 equally spaced hour marks** (i.e., bins of 30° each). Now, every time you see a **little arrow** drawn on the image patch — an arrow that points in the direction of the **local edge or texture gradient** — you **drop a marble** into the bucket corresponding to the arrow's direction. Some arrows are **stronger** (high gradient magnitude — like a sharp bone edge), and they drop **bigger marbles**. After scanning the entire patch, you count the marbles in each bucket. The resulting 12 numbers are the **orientation histogram**. It tells you, at a glance: - **Is the patch dominated by horizontal edges?** → big bucket at 0° and 180°. - **Is the texture isotropic (no preferred direction)?** → all buckets roughly equal. - **Is there a curved boundary?** → smooth spread across adjacent bins. In **medical imaging**, this is enormously useful: - **Mammography**: distinguishing spiculated (radiating) lesions from round, well-circumscribed masses. - **Histopathology**: classifying glandular vs. stromal tissue based on fiber orientation. - **Cardiac MRI**: tracking myocardial wall motion direction across the cardiac cycle. - **DTI (Diffusion Tensor Imaging)**: visualizing the principal direction of water diffusion in white matter tracts. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction to Remember:** > - **Edge map** tells you *where* the boundaries are. > - **Orientation histogram** tells you *what directions* those boundaries prefer. > - **HOG descriptor** = orientation histogram computed on a dense grid of overlapping cells with normalization — the workhorse of modern object/scene recognition. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants The following are the **standardized parameters** commonly cited in literature and expected in KTU answers: | Parameter | Standard Value | Description | |---|---|---| | Number of bins $N$ | **9** (Dalal-Triggs HOG) or **36** (10° bins) | Angular resolution of the histogram | | Bin width $\Delta\theta$ | $2\pi / N$ radians, or **20° / 10°** | Width of each angular bin | | Signed vs. unsigned gradient | **0°–180°** (unsigned) or **0°–360°** (signed) | Whether gradient direction matters or only orientation | | Block normalization | **L2-norm**, L1-norm, L1-sqrt | Reduces sensitivity to illumination/contrast changes | | Cell size (HOG) | **8 × 8 pixels** | Spatial region over which histogram is built | | Block size (HOG) | **2 × 2 cells = 16 × 16 pixels** | Group of cells normalized together | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Dalal-Triggs HOG** (CVPR 2005, the seminal paper) is the de-facto reference for orientation histograms in KTU answers. Always cite it when asked about HOG. --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept: From Pixels to Histogram The construction of an orientation histogram proceeds in **five systematic stages**. Each stage has a clear engineering rationale. ### Stage 1 — Gradient Computation The first-order image gradients $G_x$ and $G_y$ are computed at every pixel $(x,y)$ using a discrete differentiation kernel. The **1-D centered mask** $\left[-1,\ 0,\ +1\right]$ is the most common choice (used in Dalal-Triggs). Optionally, a **Gaussian pre-filtering** with $\sigma = 0.5$ to $1.0$ pixels is applied to suppress noise — a critical step in medical images. ### Stage 2 — Magnitude and Orientation At each pixel, the gradient magnitude and orientation are derived: $$m(x,y) = \sqrt{G_x^2(x,y) + G_y^2(x,y)}$$ $$\theta(x,y) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{G_y(x,y)}{G_x(x,y)}\right)$$ The orientation is wrapped (modulo $\pi$ for unsigned, or $2\pi$ for signed) and discretized into the bin index $k$: $$k = \left\lfloor \frac{\theta(x,y)}{\Delta\theta} \right\rfloor \mod N$$ ### Stage 3 — Weighted Voting (Triangular / Gaussian Interpolation) A "hard" vote (Kronecker delta) causes abrupt boundary effects at bin edges. To soften this, the gradient magnitude is distributed across **neighboring bins** using a **triangular** or **Gaussian spatial+angular interpolation kernel**. The vote of pixel $(x,y)$ into bin $k$ is: $$v_k(x,y) = m(x,y) \cdot \max\!\left(0,\ 1 - \frac{\bigl(\theta(x,y) - \theta_k\bigr)}{\Delta\theta}\right)$$ This **tri-linear interpolation** (spatial + angular) is what makes HOG descriptors robust. ### Stage 4 — Cell Aggregation The image (or ROI) is divided into a grid of **cells** (e.g., 8×8 pixels). Within each cell, the orientation histogram is computed. Cells are then grouped into **blocks** (e.g., 2×2 cells) for normalization. ### Stage 5 — Block Normalization The concatenated cell-histogram vector $\mathbf{v}$ of a block is normalized to unit length. The most common scheme is the **L2-norm**: $$\mathbf{v}_{\text{norm}} = \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\sqrt{\|\mathbf{v}\|_2^2 + \epsilon^2}}$$ where $\epsilon$ is a small constant (e.g., $10^{-5}$) to prevent division by zero. **L2-Hys** (L2 followed by clipping values to a maximum of 0.2 and re-normalizing) is the Dalal-Triggs recommended variant. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $G_x(x,y) = I(x+1,y) - I(x-1,y)$ | Horizontal gradient (centered mask) | Gradient computation | | 2 | $G_y(x,y) = I(x,y+1) - I(x,y-1)$ | Vertical gradient (centered mask) | Gradient computation | | 3 | $m(x,y) = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ | Gradient magnitude | Vote weight | | 4 | $\theta(x,y) = \arctan(G_y / G_x)$ | Gradient orientation (radians) | Bin index | | 5 | $\Delta\theta = \pi / N$ (unsigned) or $2\pi / N$ (signed) | Bin width | Histogram discretization | | 6 | $H(\theta_k) = \sum m \cdot \delta(\theta - \theta_k)$ | Hard-vote histogram | Basic formulation | | 7 | $v_k = m \cdot (1 - \vert \theta - \theta_k \vert / \Delta\theta)$ | Tri-linear vote (0 ≤ v ≤ m) | Smooth voting | | 8 | $\mathbf{v}_{\text{norm}} = \mathbf{v} / \sqrt{\Vert\mathbf{v}\Vert_2^2 + \epsilon^2}$ | L2 normalization | Block normalization | | 9 | $\text{clip}(v_i,\, 0,\, 0.2)$ then re-normalize | L2-Hys normalization | Robust descriptor | | 10 | $H_{\text{final}} = \left[\mathbf{v}_{\text{norm}}^{(1)} \Vert \mathbf{v}_{\text{norm}}^{(2)} \Vert \cdots \Vert \mathbf{v}_{\text{norm}}^{(B)}\right]$ | Concatenated HOG vector | Final descriptor | | 11 | $\chi^2(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}) = \sum_i (a_i - b_i)^2 / (a_i + b_i)$ | Chi-squared distance (SVM kernel) | Classifier input | > [!NOTE] > In the KTU formula sheet, students should remember: **gradient magnitude is the vote weight, gradient orientation determines the bin**, and the descriptor is **always the normalized, concatenated histogram vector**. ## 2.3 Why and How — The Engineering Rationale - **Why gradient orientation?** It is **invariant to uniform brightness changes** (adding a constant to the image does not change $G_x, G_y$). - **Why normalize?** It gives **partial invariance to illumination and contrast variations**, which is essential in MRI (intensity non-uniformity) and ultrasound (speckle). - **Why cells and blocks?** It captures **both local structure (cells) and meso-scale layout (blocks)**, mimicking the human visual cortex's hierarchy of simple-to-complex cells. - **How is it used in medical imaging?** As input to SVMs, Random Forests, or CNNs for tasks like tumor classification, organ segmentation refinement, and pathology slide analysis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Utility in B.Tech Projects (KTU 2024 expects awareness):** > - **Lung nodule detection** in CT — HOG features fed to SVM give state-of-the-art false-positive reduction. > - **Diabetic retinopathy** — HOG features on retinal fundus images classify microaneurysms and exudates. > - **Cell nuclei segmentation** in histopathology — orientation histograms distinguish clustered from isolated nuclei. > - **Brain tumor grading** — HOG features on MRI patches help distinguish low-grade vs. high-grade gliomas. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Continuous Distribution to Discrete Histogram ### Step 1 — Continuous Orientation Distribution Consider an image patch $P \subset \mathbb{R}^2$. Define the **continuous orientation density** $\rho(\theta)$ as the integral of gradient magnitudes whose orientation lies in an infinitesimal angular wedge $[\theta, \theta + d\theta)$: $$\rho(\theta) = \int\!\!\int_{P} m(x,y)\, \delta\!\left(\theta(x,y) - \theta\right) \, dx\, dy$$ where $\delta(\cdot)$ is the Dirac delta (continuous analogue of the Kronecker delta). This represents the *ideal* orientation distribution — a function that is zero everywhere except at orientations where pixels have nonzero gradient. ### Step 2 — Discretization into $N$ Bins To make this usable in a computer, we discretize the continuous angle $\theta \in [0, \pi)$ (unsigned case) into $N$ bins with centers $\theta_k$: $$\theta_k = \frac{\pi}{N}\left(k + \tfrac{1}{2}\right), \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$ The bin width is $\Delta\theta = \pi / N$. The discrete histogram is obtained by integrating $\rho(\theta)$ over each bin: $$H(\theta_k) = \int_{\theta_k - \Delta\theta/2}^{\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2} \rho(\theta)\, d\theta$$ ### Step 3 — Replacing the Integral by a Pixel Sum Since the image is discrete, the integral becomes a sum over pixels. Using the sifting property of the Dirac delta: $$H(\theta_k) = \sum_{(x,y) \in P} m(x,y) \cdot \mathbb{1}\!\left[\theta(x,y) \in \text{bin } k\right]$$ where $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function (1 if condition holds, 0 otherwise). This is the **hard-vote** formulation. ### Step 4 — Soft (Tri-linear) Voting — Smoothed Histogram To avoid hard boundary artifacts, replace the indicator with a **triangular kernel** centered at $\theta_k$: $$H_{\text{soft}}(\theta_k) = \sum_{(x,y) \in P} m(x,y) \cdot \max\!\left(0,\ 1 - \frac{\bigl\vert \theta(x,y) - \theta_k \bigr\vert}{\Delta\theta}\right)$$ The factor $\max(0, \cdot)$ ensures that a pixel only contributes to its **two nearest bin centers**, never negative. This is the standard **Dalal-Triggs** vote. ### Step 5 — Block Normalization (L2) Suppose a block contains $C$ cells, and the concatenated unnormalized vector is $\mathbf{v} = [H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_{NC}]^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{NC}$. The L2-normalized descriptor is: $$\mathbf{v}_{\text{norm}} = \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\sqrt{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{NC} v_i^2 + \epsilon^2}}$$ For Dalal-Triggs, $\epsilon = 10^{-5}$ and $N = 9$ bins/cell, with $2 \times 2$ cells/block, giving a 36-D descriptor per block. ### Step 6 — Full Multi-Block Descriptor For a detection window of $W \times H$ pixels with cell size $c \times c$ and block size $b \times b$ cells, the total number of blocks is: $$N_{\text{blocks}} = \left(\frac{W}{c} - b + 1\right) \times \left(\frac{H}{c} - b + 1\right)$$ and the final HOG descriptor dimension is: $$D_{\text{HOG}} = N_{\text{blocks}} \times b^2 \times N$$ **Worked Example:** For a 64×128 pedestrian detection window with 8×8 cells, 2×2-cell blocks, and 9 bins: - Cells per window: $(64/8) \times (128/8) = 8 \times 16 = 128$. - Blocks per window: $(8-2+1) \times (16-2+1) = 7 \times 15 = 105$. - Descriptor length: $105 \times 4 \times 9 = \mathbf{3780}$ dimensions. This is the canonical Dalal-Triggs number — **memorize it for KTU derivations**. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Building a 4-Bin Orientation Histogram Suppose a 3×3 patch has the following pixel intensities: $$I = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 20 & 30 \\ 40 & 50 & 60 \\ 70 & 80 & 90 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step A — Compute gradients at the center pixel** $(1,1)$ (using 1-D centered mask, ignoring borders): $$G_x = I(1,2) - I(1,0) = 60 - 40 = 20$$ $$G_y = I(2,1) - I(0,1) = 80 - 20 = 60$$ **Step B — Magnitude and orientation:** $$m = \sqrt{20^2 + 60^2} = \sqrt{400 + 3600} = \sqrt{4000} \approx 63.25$$ $$\theta = \arctan(60/20) = \arctan(3) \approx 71.57°$$ **Step C — Bin assignment** with $N = 4$ bins (unsigned, 0°–180°), $\Delta\theta = 45°$: Bin centers: $\theta_0 = 22.5°$, $\theta_1 = 67.5°$, $\theta_2 = 112.5°$, $\theta_3 = 157.5°$. Since $\theta = 71.57°$ is closest to $\theta_1 = 67.5°$ (distance 4.07°) vs. $\theta_2 = 112.5°$ (distance 40.93°), the vote goes to bin 1. **Step D — Tri-linear vote** (split between bins 1 and 2): $$v_1 = 63.25 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{4.07}{45}\right) = 63.25 \cdot 0.9096 \approx 57.53$$ $$v_2 = 63.25 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{40.93}{45}\right) = 63.25 \cdot 0.0904 \approx 5.72$$ **Step E — Aggregating over all interior pixels** (4 pixels in a 3×3 patch), and after normalization, the resulting 4-bin orientation histogram encodes the **diagonal edge** of the synthetic gradient field. > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** KTU often asks for a small numerical example like this. Be ready to (i) compute $G_x, G_y$, (ii) compute $m, \theta$, (iii) identify the bin, and (iv) apply tri-linear weighting. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Orientation Histogram (OH) | HOG (Histogram of Oriented Gradients) | SIFT Descriptor | Shape Context | |---|---|---|---|---| | Spatial aggregation | Single ROI / Whole image | Dense grid of cells | Keypoint neighborhood | Log-polar bins | | Normalization | Optional | **Mandatory** (L2 / L2-Hys) | **Mandatory** (L2) | Optional | | Bin count $N$ | Flexible (4–36) | 9 (Dalal-Triggs) | 8 (4×4 array × 8) | 12 (typical) | | Rotation invariance | No (unless rotated) | No (but can be aligned) | **Yes** (dominant orientation) | **Yes** (tangent angle) | | Scale invariance | No | No (image pyramid) | **Yes** (DoG scale space) | No | | Used in medical imaging | Texture analysis | Lesion detection, classification | Image registration | Cell shape analysis | | Computational cost | Very low | Medium | High (keypoint detection) | Medium | | KTU relevance | **High** (core topic) | **High** (canonical example) | Medium | Medium | --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Orientation Histogram Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Input Medical Image
X-ray or MRI or Histology] --> A1[Step 1 Gaussian Pre-filtering
sigma 0.5 to 1.0] A1 --> A2[Step 2 Gradient Computation
Centered Mask minus 1 0 plus 1] A2 --> A3[Step 3 Magnitude and Orientation
m and theta per pixel] A3 --> A4[Step 4 Cell Partitioning
Divide ROI into NxN cells] A4 --> A5[Step 5 Weighted Voting
Tri linear interpolation into bins] A5 --> A6[Step 6 Cell Histogram Assembly
N bin vector per cell] A6 --> A7[Step 7 Block Grouping
Combine adjacent cells] A7 --> A8[Step 8 Block Normalization
L2 norm or L2 Hys] A8 --> A9[Step 9 Concatenation
Full HOG descriptor vector] A9 --> A10[Step 10 Classifier Input
SVM or Random Forest or CNN] A10 --> A11[Output Prediction
Tumor grade or Lesion class] ``` ## 4.2 Conceptual Schematic — Voting Into Angular Bins ```mermaid flowchart LR P0[Pixel Gradient
magnitude m] --> Q1{Bin Index k
via orientation theta} Q1 --> R0[Bin 0
zero to 22.5 deg] Q1 --> R1[Bin 1
22.5 to 67.5 deg] Q1 --> R2[Bin 2
67.5 to 112.5 deg] Q1 --> R3[Bin 3
112.5 to 157.5 deg] Q1 --> R4[Bin 4
157.5 to 180 deg] R0 --> S0[Histogram H of theta
Length N 4 or 9 or 36] R1 --> S0 R2 --> S0 R3 --> S0 R4 --> S0 S0 --> T0[Normalize L2 norm
Robust to illumination] T0 --> U0[Final Descriptor Vector
Input to classifier] ``` ## 4.3 Multi-Stage Block Architecture for HOG Descriptor ```mermaid flowchart TD X0[Detection Window
WxH pixels] --> Y1[Cell 1
8x8 pixels] X0 --> Y2[Cell 2
8x8 pixels] X0 --> Y3[Cell 3
8x8 pixels] X0 --> Y4[Cell 4
8x8 pixels] Y1 --> Z1[Block 1
2x2 cells] Y2 --> Z1 Y3 --> Z1 Y4 --> Z1 Z1 --> W1[Normalize Block 1
36 D vector] W1 --> V0[Concatenate all
block descriptors] V0 --> V1[Final HOG
3780 D for 64x128] ``` ## 4.4 Application Domain Map — Orientation Histograms in Medical Imaging ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[Orientation Histograms] --> B0[Mammography
Mass classification] A0 --> B1[Brain MRI
Tumor grading] A0 --> B2[Lung CT
Nodule detection] A0 --> B3[Histopathology
Cell and tissue analysis] A0 --> B4[Cardiac Imaging
Wall motion tracking] A0 --> B5[Retinal Imaging
Disease classification] A0 --> B6[DTI Tractography
Fiber orientation] B0 --> C0[Output Diagnosis
Benign vs Malignant] B1 --> C0 B2 --> C0 B3 --> C0 B4 --> C0 B5 --> C0 B6 --> C0 ``` --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper] **Define an Orientation Histogram. List its key parameters and explain how it is used as a feature descriptor in medical image analysis.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **Orientation Histogram** is a 1-D distribution $H(\theta_k)$ that records the cumulative gradient magnitude of an image region as a function of discretized gradient angle $\theta_k$. The key parameters are: **(i) number of bins $N$** (typically 9 in HOG, 4–36 elsewhere), **(ii) bin width $\Delta\theta = \pi/N$** for unsigned gradients, **(iii) signed vs. unsigned orientation** (0–π vs. 0–2π), and **(iv) normalization scheme** (L2, L2-Hys, L1-sqrt). In medical imaging, orientation histograms are used as features for lesion classification (e.g., spiculated vs. round masses in mammography), tissue texture characterization, and cell-nuclei analysis in histopathology. They are **invariant to uniform brightness changes** and capture **local shape and orientation cues**, making them robust for downstream classifiers like SVMs. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for parameters, 1 mark for medical application.)* ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between an Edge Map and an Orientation Histogram. Why is normalization essential in a HOG descriptor?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **edge map** is a binary (or grayscale) image marking the **locations** of intensity discontinuities — it answers *where* the edges are. An **orientation histogram** aggregates gradient orientations over a region, answering *what directions* the edges prefer. Normalization is essential in HOG because raw gradient magnitudes are sensitive to **illumination, contrast, and intensity non-uniformity** (a major issue in MRI). Dividing each block's concatenated histogram by its **L2 norm** (or using L2-Hys) makes the descriptor **partially invariant to local intensity scaling**, improving classifier generalization. *(1.5 marks for difference, 1.5 marks for justification of normalization.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain the complete pipeline of constructing a **Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG)** descriptor for a medical image. Include the role of gradient computation, cell partitioning, weighted voting, block normalization, and concatenation. Mention the Dalal-Triggs parameters. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider a 16 × 16 pixel cell with cell size 8 × 8 and block size 2 × 2 cells, using 9 bins per cell. Compute the dimension of the final HOG descriptor for a 64 × 128 detection window. Justify each step of the calculation. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **(a) HOG Pipeline (7 marks):** 1. **Gradient computation (1 mark):** Apply 1-D centered masks $\left[-1, 0, +1\right]$ and $\left[-1, 0, +1\right]^\top$ to the (optionally Gaussian-smoothed) image to obtain $G_x$ and $G_y$ at every pixel. 2. **Magnitude and orientation (1 mark):** Compute $m = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ and $\theta = \arctan(G_y / G_x)$ at each pixel. 3. **Cell partitioning (1 mark):** Divide the image (or detection window) into a grid of **cells** of size $c \times c$ (Dalal-Triggs uses **8 × 8 pixels**). Each cell maintains its own orientation histogram of $N$ bins. 4. **Weighted voting (1.5 marks):** Each pixel casts a vote equal to its gradient magnitude $m$ into the bin corresponding to its orientation $\theta$. Use **tri-linear (triangular) interpolation** across spatial position and angular bin to avoid boundary effects: $$v_k(x,y) = m(x,y) \cdot \max\!\left(0,\ 1 - \frac{\vert \theta(x,y) - \theta_k \vert}{\Delta\theta}\right)$$ 5. **Block grouping and normalization (1.5 marks):** Group adjacent cells (Dalal-Triggs uses **2 × 2 cells per block**) and normalize the concatenated 36-D vector using the L2 norm (or L2-Hys with clipping at 0.2): $$\mathbf{v}_{\text{norm}} = \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\sqrt{\Vert\mathbf{v}\Vert_2^2 + \epsilon^2}}, \quad \epsilon = 10^{-5}$$ 6. **Concatenation (1 mark):** Concatenate the normalized block descriptors over the entire detection window to form the final HOG vector. This vector is fed to a classifier (typically a linear SVM). **Dalal-Triggs parameters (built into the explanation):** cell = 8×8, block = 2×2 cells, $N = 9$ bins, unsigned gradients in [0°, 180°], L2-Hys normalization, 64×128 detection window, 3780-D descriptor. --- **(b) Descriptor Dimension Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Cells per window (1 mark):** With cell size 8 × 8 and window size 64 × 128: $$\text{Cells} = \frac{64}{8} \times \frac{128}{8} = 8 \times 16 = 128 \text{ cells}$$ **Step 2 — Blocks per window (2 marks):** A block is 2 × 2 cells. The number of blocks along each axis is (cells per axis) − (block size) + 1: $$\text{Blocks per row} = 8 - 2 + 1 = 7$$ $$\text{Blocks per col} = 16 - 2 + 1 = 15$$ $$\text{Total blocks} = 7 \times 15 = 105$$ **Step 3 — Dimensions per block (1.5 marks):** Each block has 2 × 2 = 4 cells, and each cell has 9 bins: $$\text{Dim per block} = 4 \times 9 = 36$$ **Step 4 — Final descriptor dimension (1.5 marks):** $$D_{\text{HOG}} = 105 \times 36 = \mathbf{3780 \text{ dimensions}}$$ **Step 5 — Justification (1 mark):** This is the canonical Dalal-Triggs HOG length for a 64 × 128 pedestrian detection window. It is the sum of all 36-D block vectors concatenated in raster scan order across the detection window. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]** **(a)** Derive the **discrete formulation** of an orientation histogram from its continuous orientation density. Explain the role of the Kronecker delta / Dirac delta in the derivation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the role of orientation histograms in **mammographic mass classification** (spiculated vs. circumscribed) and **diffusion tensor imaging** tractography. For each, explain what specific orientation signature is expected and how it informs the diagnosis. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Define the continuous orientation density: $$\rho(\theta) = \iint_{P} m(x,y)\, \delta\!\left(\theta(x,y) - \theta\right)\, dx\, dy$$ The **Dirac delta** $\delta(\cdot)$ acts as a sifting function: it "selects" only those pixels whose orientation equals $\theta$, weighted by their gradient magnitude. **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Discretize $\theta \in [0, \pi)$ into $N$ bins of width $\Delta\theta = \pi/N$ with centers $\theta_k = (k + 1/2)\Delta\theta$: $$\theta_k = \frac{\pi}{N}\left(k + \tfrac{1}{2}\right), \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$ **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Integrate over each bin to get the bin count: $$H(\theta_k) = \int_{\theta_k - \Delta\theta/2}^{\theta_k + \Delta\theta/2} \rho(\theta)\, d\theta$$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Replace the integral by a pixel sum (since images are discrete), and replace the Dirac delta by the **Kronecker delta** $\delta_{ij}$: $$H(\theta_k) = \sum_{(x,y) \in P} m(x,y) \cdot \delta_{k,\, k(x,y)}, \quad k(x,y) = \left\lfloor \theta(x,y) / \Delta\theta \right\rfloor$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Soft-vote extension using triangular kernel: $$H_{\text{soft}}(\theta_k) = \sum_{(x,y) \in P} m(x,y) \cdot \max\!\left(0,\ 1 - \frac{\vert \theta(x,y) - \theta_k \vert}{\Delta\theta}\right)$$ The Kronecker delta in Step 4 implements the **hard assignment**; the triangular kernel in Step 5 implements **soft assignment** for robustness. --- **(b) Medical Applications (7 marks):** **Mammographic Mass Classification (3.5 marks):** - **Circumscribed masses** (typically benign, e.g., cysts, fibroadenomas) have a **smooth, rounded boundary**. Their local gradients point **radially outward** (or inward) with **no preferred angular concentration** — the orientation histogram is **relatively uniform** across bins. - **Spiculated masses** (highly suspicious for malignancy) have **radiating sharp strands** ("spicules") extending from the central mass. Local gradients near the spicules point in **specific radial directions**, producing a **multi-modal, non-uniform histogram** with several dominant peaks. - A classifier (e.g., SVM with HOG input) can distinguish these signatures quantitatively. The **standard deviation** or **entropy** of the orientation histogram serves as a compact scalar feature: low entropy → uniform → circumscribed; high entropy → multi-modal → spiculated. **Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI) Tractography (3.5 marks):** - In DTI, water diffusion in each voxel is modeled as a **3 × 3 tensor** $\mathbf{D}$. The **principal eigenvector** $\mathbf{e}_1$ (largest eigenvalue) gives the **dominant fiber direction**. - A voxel-wise orientation histogram (a 3-D extension where bins are spheres / unit vectors) aggregates these principal directions to **trace white matter tracts** (e.g., corticospinal tract, arcuate fasciculus). - The orientation histogram at each step of tractography selects the next voxel whose principal direction is **most consistent** with the current trajectory (peak of the local histogram). - Clinically, this enables **pre-surgical planning** (avoiding critical tracts during tumor resection) and diagnosis of **demyelinating diseases** (e.g., multiple sclerosis), where tract integrity is reduced. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (avoid these to score full marks):** > > 1. **Forgetting to specify "unsigned" vs. "signed"** gradients. The bin width $\Delta\theta$ is $\pi/N$ for unsigned and $2\pi/N$ for signed. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for ambiguity. > 2. **Confusing orientation histogram with edge map.** They are NOT the same — an edge map is binary/spatial; an orientation histogram is a feature vector aggregating orientations. > 3. **Skipping the normalization step in HOG answers.** Always explicitly write the L2-norm equation; a HOG answer without normalization is incomplete and loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Wrong descriptor dimension.** Memorize the 3780-D figure for Dalal-Triggs (64×128 window, 8×8 cells, 2×2 blocks, 9 bins). Off-by-one errors in block count are common. > 5. **Omitting the medical context.** KTU examiners reward students who explicitly tie the feature to a clinical application (e.g., "this HOG descriptor is used to detect lung nodules in low-dose CT"). Generic answers without application lose marks. > 6. **Using $|$ inside markdown tables.** This breaks the table render. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX. > 7. **Conflating HOG with SIFT.** HOG is a dense, global descriptor; SIFT is a sparse, keypoint-based descriptor. Examiners test this distinction. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition**: An orientation histogram $H(\theta_k)$ is a 1-D distribution of gradient magnitudes aggregated over discrete angular bins. **(Core concept.)** - **Key equation**: $H(\theta_k) = \sum_{(x,y) \in P} m(x,y) \cdot \delta_{k,\, k(x,y)}$, where $m = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ and $k = \lfloor \theta / \Delta\theta \rfloor$. **(Memorize.)** - **Bin width**: $\Delta\theta = \pi / N$ (unsigned, 0°–180°) or $2\pi / N$ (signed, 0°–360°). **(Memorize.)** - **Dalal-Triggs HOG**: $N = 9$ bins, 8×8 cells, 2×2-cell blocks, L2-Hys normalization, 64×128 window, **3780-D** final descriptor. **(Memorize for derivations.)** - **Tri-linear voting** softens boundary artifacts at bin edges — use a triangular kernel of width $\Delta\theta$. **(Important for KTU derivations.)** - **Block normalization** (L2, L2-Hys, L1, L1-sqrt) gives partial invariance to illumination — **mandatory** in HOG. **(Frequently tested.)** - **Medical applications**: mammography (spiculated vs. circumscribed masses), lung CT (nodule detection), brain MRI (tumor grading), retinal imaging, DTI tractography, histopathology (cell analysis). **(Always cite one in Part B answers.)** - **Difference from edge map**: Edge map = *where*; orientation histogram = *what direction*. **(Distinguish clearly.)** - **HOG vs. SIFT**: HOG = dense, no keypoints, no rotation/scale invariance; SIFT = sparse, keypoint-based, **rotation and scale invariant**. **(Common exam question.)** - **Entropy of the histogram** is a compact scalar feature: high entropy → multi-modal orientations (e.g., spiculated lesion, complex texture); low entropy → uniform orientations (e.g., smooth boundary, isotropic tissue). - **Computational cost** scales linearly with image size and bin count; for a 256×256 image with 9 bins, the histogram has only 9 entries but is computed from 65,536 pixel votes. - **Vote weight** is the gradient magnitude, NOT the gradient squared or the pixel intensity. **(Frequent trap.)** - **In KTU derivations, always end with a clinical application** — it demonstrates **Course Outcome (CO) mapping** and **higher-order Bloom's level (Apply/Analyze)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Top 5 for KTU Exam):** > 1. $\Delta\theta = \pi/N$ for unsigned, $2\pi/N$ for signed. > 2. HOG descriptor = concatenated, **normalized** cell histograms from overlapping blocks. > 3. Dalal-Triggs: 9 bins, 8×8 cells, 2×2 blocks, 3780-D. > 4. Tri-linear vote: $v_k = m \cdot \max(0, 1 - |\theta - \theta_k|/\Delta\theta)$. > 5. Always cite a medical application (mammography, lung CT, brain MRI, DTI). ---

topics.title: Textons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Textons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of advanced medical imaging and texture analysis, a **texton** is formally defined as an elementary, statistically deployable micro-structure (or local image feature) that serves as the fundamental building block of perceived visual texture. The concept was rigorously introduced by Hungarian neuroscientist **Béla Julesz** in **1981** through his pioneering work on pre-attentive texture discrimination. 

In a KTU-aligned sense, a texton is best described as the cluster-center of a collection of local image patch responses obtained by convolving an image with a multi-orientation, multi-scale **filter bank** (typically a set of oriented Gaussian derivative filters or a Gaussian + Laplacian of Gaussian + Gabor filter bank). Each pixel of the image is assigned a discrete *texton label* (its nearest cluster center in filter-response space), and the global image texture is then characterized by the **texton frequency histogram**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Textons are a core concept in texture-based image analysis, which is essential for Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) systems in medical imaging (e.g., classifying malignant vs. benign lesions in mammograms, CT, MRI, and histopathology slides).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus-Aligned Working Definition (Pearson-style):** *"A texton is an image primitive — such as a line segment, a blob, an edge-ending, or a cross — that is detected by a small receptive field (filter) and whose distribution across an image defines its texture."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "Atoms of Texture"

Imagine you are a doctor looking at a histopathology slide under a microscope. You do **not** look at every individual pixel; instead, your visual cortex groups tiny local patterns (small dark blobs, short curved lines, edge endings, crossings) into recognizable *textures* (e.g., "cancerous glandular tissue" vs. "healthy stroma"). 

- The **fundamental micro-patterns** (blobs, lines, endings, crosses) that your eye uses to compose these textures are exactly what Béla Julesz called **textons**.
- They are the *atoms* of texture — just as English is built from a small alphabet of ~26 letters, an image's texture is built from a small "alphabet" of textons (typically **K = 10 to 100** textons in practical medical CAD systems).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *"Texton = Tex + on"* → a small **tex**ture-unit, like a quark inside a texture atom.

## 1.3 The Three Canonical Texton Categories (Julesz, 1981)

| Category | Sub-type | Geometric Description | Medical Imaging Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Elongated** | Bars / line segments | Orientated edge segments of length *l* and width *w* | Collagen fiber bundles, vessel walls, nerve tracts |
| **Elongated** | Edges | Sharp step-like intensity transitions | Tissue boundaries, organ outlines |
| **Non-elongated** | Blobs (terminators) | Gaussian-like intensity peaks of characteristic size *r* | Cell nuclei, micro-calcifications, cysts |
| **Non-elongated** | Crossings | X- or T-junctions of elongated elements | Branching vessels, ductal junctions |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** A texton is *not* a pixel. A texton is a *localized micro-structure* spanning a small receptive field (typically 3×3 to 25×25 pixels) and is defined by its **filter response signature**, not by a single intensity value.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of Texton-Based Texture Analysis

The texton framework for medical image classification (Varma & Zisserman, 2005; reformulated for medical CAD) proceeds in **five sequential, deterministic steps**:

### Step 1 — Filter Bank Selection
Choose a rotationally invariant, multi-scale filter bank **$\mathcal{F} = \{F_1, F_2, \dots, F_N\}$** to probe local image structure. The standard KTU-recommended choice is the **MR8 (Maximum Response 8) filter bank** consisting of:

- 1 Gaussian filter (scale-only, rotation-invariant)
- 1 Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) filter (scale-only)
- 3 oriented edge filters at orientations $\theta \in \{0^\circ, 60^\circ, 120^\circ\}$
- 3 oriented bar filters at the same three orientations

Each oriented filter pair is collapsed to its **maximum response over all 6 orientations**, giving **$N = 8$** filter channels (hence the name MR8). This yields a *rotationally invariant* descriptor.

### Step 2 — Filter Response Extraction
For an input grayscale image $I(x,y)$, the response at each pixel is the vector:

$$\mathbf{R}(x,y) = \left[ F_1 * I, \ F_2 * I, \ \dots, \ F_N * I \right] \in \mathbb{R}^N$$

where $*$ denotes 2D convolution.

### Step 3 — Texton Dictionary Construction (Clustering)
Cluster the set $\{\mathbf{R}(x,y)\}$ of all filter-response vectors (sampled densely from training images) into **$K$** cluster centers using **K-means** (or Gaussian Mixture Models for probabilistic textons). The set of centroids is the **texton dictionary** $\mathcal{T} = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_K\}$.

### Step 4 — Texton Labeling
Assign every pixel a discrete label equal to the index of its nearest texton:

$$\ell(x,y) = \arg\min_{k \in \{1,\dots,K\}} \ \Vert \mathbf{R}(x,y) - t_k \Vert_2$$

### Step 5 — Texton Histogram
The final texture descriptor of an image (or region of interest $\Omega$) is the **normalized texton frequency histogram**:

$$h_k = \frac{1}{\vert \Omega \vert} \sum_{(x,y) \in \Omega} \mathbb{1}\left[\ell(x,y) = k\right], \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, K$$

This $K$-dimensional histogram $\mathbf{h} = (h_1, h_2, \dots, h_K)$ is the **feature vector** fed to a downstream classifier (SVM, k-NN, Random Forest) for lesion/tissue classification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this work for medical imaging?** Pathological tissues (e.g., malignant vs. benign) differ in their *local micro-structural composition* — the relative frequency of cell nuclei (blobs), fiber orientations (bars), and tissue boundaries (edges) shifts systematically. The texton histogram captures exactly this distributional shift.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| # | Concept / Symbol | Mathematical Form | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Filter response vector | $\mathbf{R}(x,y) = \left[ F_1 * I, \dots, F_N * I \right]$ | $N$-dimensional, $N = 8$ for MR8 |
| 2 | Gaussian filter (scale $\sigma$) | $G_\sigma(x,y) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} e^{-(x^2+y^2)/2\sigma^2}$ | Isotropic, unit area |
| 3 | LoG filter | $\nabla^2 G_\sigma = G_{xx} + G_{yy}$ | Detects blobs |
| 4 | Gabor filter (oriented) | $G(x,y;\lambda,\theta,\psi,\sigma) = e^{-(x'^2+\gamma^2 y'^2)/2\sigma^2}\cos(2\pi x'/\lambda + \psi)$ | $\theta$ = orientation |
| 5 | Texton dictionary | $\mathcal{T} = \text{KMeans}\left(\{\mathbf{R}(x,y)\}\right) = \{t_k\}_{k=1}^{K}$ | Typical $K = 10$–$100$ |
| 6 | Pixel texton label | $\ell(x,y) = \arg\min_k \Vert \mathbf{R}(x,y) - t_k \Vert_2$ | Discrete map |
| 7 | Texton histogram bin | $h_k = \frac{N_k}{\sum_j N_j}$ | $\sum_k h_k = 1$ |
| 8 | $\chi^2$ histogram distance | $d(\mathbf{h}_1, \mathbf{h}_2) = \frac{1}{2} \sum_k \frac{(h_{1,k} - h_{2,k})^2}{h_{1,k} + h_{2,k}}$ | Used in classification |
| 9 | Bhattacharyya coefficient | $B(\mathbf{h}_1, \mathbf{h}_2) = \sum_k \sqrt{h_{1,k} \, h_{2,k}}$ | Similarity $\in [0,1]$ |
| 10 | Cluster compactness (K-means obj.) | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \min_{k} \Vert \mathbf{R}_i - t_k \Vert_2^2$ | Minimized iteratively |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical-bar norm $\Vert \cdot \Vert_2$ is the standard Euclidean (L2) norm — represented here as `\Vert … \Vert` to avoid corrupting the markdown table.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Medical Imaging

The texton framework is the *de-facto* texture descriptor in many clinical CAD pipelines:

- **Mammography:** Distinguishing malignant masses (irregular texton distribution) from benign cysts (homogeneous texton distribution).
- **Histopathology:** Grading tumors by texton-histogram dissimilarity from healthy reference tissue.
- **Brain MRI:** Segmenting white-matter lesions (multiple sclerosis plaques) by their distinctive texton signatures.
- **CT lung nodules:** Differentiating solid, part-solid, and ground-glass opacities via texton-frequency analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Convolution to Texton Label Map

We explicitly derive the texton-labeling equation starting from the convolution integral.

**Step 1 — Define the 2D convolution.** The response of a filter $F(x,y)$ to image $I(x,y)$ at pixel $(x_0, y_0)$ is:

$$
(F * I)(x_0, y_0) = \sum_{u} \sum_{v} F(u,v) \cdot I(x_0 - u, \ y_0 - v)
$$

**Step 2 — Stack $N$ filter responses into a vector.** For a bank of $N$ filters $\{F_1, \dots, F_N\}$, the local descriptor at pixel $(x_0, y_0)$ is:

$$
\mathbf{R}(x_0, y_0) = \begin{bmatrix} (F_1 * I)(x_0, y_0) \\ (F_2 * I)(x_0, y_0) \\ \vdots \\ (F_N * I)(x_0, y_0) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the squared Euclidean distance to each texton.** For the $k$-th texton $t_k \in \mathbb{R}^{N}$, the squared distance is:

$$
d_k^2 = \Vert \mathbf{R}(x_0, y_0) - t_k \Vert_2^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{N} \left( R_j(x_0, y_0) - t_{k,j} \right)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Argmin assignment.** The pixel is labeled with the texton index $k^*$ that minimizes $d_k^2$:

$$
\ell(x_0, y_0) = \arg\min_{k \in \{1, 2, \dots, K\}} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \left( R_j(x_0, y_0) - t_{k,j} \right)^2
$$

> This is the **fundamental labeling equation** of the texton framework and is a guaranteed KTU exam topic.

## 3.2 Derivation: Normalized Texton Histogram

Let $\Omega$ be a region of interest containing $M = \vert \Omega \vert$ pixels. Let $N_k$ be the number of pixels in $\Omega$ assigned texton label $k$:

$$
N_k = \sum_{(x,y) \in \Omega} \mathbb{1}\bigl[\ell(x,y) = k\bigr]
$$

The unnormalized histogram bin is $N_k$. Normalization yields a probability mass function:

$$
h_k = \frac{N_k}{M} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{(x,y) \in \Omega} \mathbb{1}\bigl[\ell(x,y) = k\bigr]
$$

**Sanity check (sum-to-unity):**

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{K} h_k = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{k=1}^{K} N_k = \frac{M}{M} = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Texture Descriptors

| Descriptor | Local Operator | Global Signature | Computational Cost | Medical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Texton Histogram** | Filter bank (MR8 / Gabor) | $K$-bin frequency histogram | Medium ($O(MNK)$) | Lesion classification |
| **Gray-Level Co-occurrence (GLCM)** | Pairwise pixel statistics | Co-occurrence matrix → Haralick features | High ($O(L^2)$ per offset) | Tumor heterogeneity |
| **Local Binary Patterns (LBP)** | Thresholded local neighborhood | LBP code histogram | Low ($O(M)$) | Dermoscopy, cell analysis |
| **Gabor Wavelets** | Multi-scale, multi-orientation Gabor | Mean / std of $\vert G * I \vert$ | High | Mammographic masses |
| **MR8 + Texton** | Gaussian + LoG + oriented bars/edges | Texton histogram (above) | Medium | **Most CAD pipelines** |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (for KTU problem-solving practice)

> **Problem:** Consider a $2 \times 2$ grayscale image patch with $N = 2$ filters. Filter responses yield the matrix $\mathbf{R}$ (one column per filter). A 2-texton dictionary is given. Compute the label map and the texton histogram.

$$
\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.2 & 0.5 \\ 0.8 & 1.3 \\ 1.5 & 0.4 \\ 0.6 & 1.1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathcal{T} = \{t_1, t_2\} = \left\{ \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix} 0.7 \\ 1.2 \end{bmatrix} \right\}
$$

**Pixel 1:** $\mathbf{R}_1 = (1.2, 0.5)$. Distances: $d_1^2 = (0.2)^2 + (0)^2 = 0.04$; $d_2^2 = (0.5)^2 + (-0.7)^2 = 0.25 + 0.49 = 0.74$. Minimum = $d_1^2$ → label **1**.

**Pixel 2:** $\mathbf{R}_2 = (0.8, 1.3)$. $d_1^2 = (-0.2)^2 + (0.8)^2 = 0.04 + 0.64 = 0.68$; $d_2^2 = (0.1)^2 + (0.1)^2 = 0.02$. Minimum = $d_2^2$ → label **2**.

**Pixel 3:** $\mathbf{R}_3 = (1.5, 0.4)$. $d_1^2 = (0.5)^2 + (-0.1)^2 = 0.26$; $d_2^2 = (0.8)^2 + (-0.8)^2 = 1.28$. Label **1**.

**Pixel 4:** $\mathbf{R}_4 = (0.6, 1.1)$. $d_1^2 = (-0.4)^2 + (0.6)^2 = 0.52$; $d_2^2 = (-0.1)^2 + (-0.1)^2 = 0.02$. Label **2**.

**Label map (2×2):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Histogram:** $N_1 = 2, \ N_2 = 2, \ M = 4$.

$$
\mathbf{h} = (h_1, h_2) = (0.5, \ 0.5)
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Texton-Based Medical Image Classification Pipeline (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Medical Image I x y] --> B[Preprocessing: Denoising and Intensity Normalization]
    B --> C[Apply MR8 or Gabor Filter Bank with N filters]
    C --> D[Compute N dim Filter Response Vector at every pixel]
    D --> E[Dense Sampling of Response Vectors from Training Set]
    E --> F[K means Clustering to Build Texton Dictionary T]
    F --> G[Argmin Nearest Neighbor Labeling of every pixel]
    G --> H[Construct Normalized K bin Texton Histogram h]
    H --> I[Classifier: SVM or k NN or Random Forest]
    I --> J[Output: Lesion Class, e.g., Benign vs Malignant]
    F --> G
    H --> I
```

## 4.2 The Three Canonical Texton Types (Visual Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1[Elongated Textons]
        A1[Bar / Line Segment of length l, width w, orientation theta]
        A2[Sharp Edge step transition of length l]
    end
    subgraph T2[Non Elongated Textons]
        B1[Blob: Gaussian intensity peak of radius r]
        B2[Terminator: End of elongated element]
        B3[Crossing: X or T junction]
    end
    subgraph F1[Filter Bank F]
        C1[Gaussian]
        C2[LoG]
        C3[Oriented Edge Filters 0 60 120 deg]
        C4[Oriented Bar Filters 0 60 120 deg]
    end
    T1 --> F1
    T2 --> F1
    F1 --> D[Filter Response Vectors R]
    D --> E[K means to Texton Dictionary]
```

## 4.3 Texton Labeling Process (Per-Pixel View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Input Image I] --> P1[For each pixel x0 y0]
    P1 --> P2[Compute N dim Response Vector R x0 y0]
    P2 --> P3[Compute distance d k to every texton t k]
    P3 --> P4[Pick k star = argmin d k]
    P4 --> P5[Assign label ell x0 y0 = k star]
    P5 --> P6[Label Map of same size as I]
    P6 --> P7[Aggregate into K bin Histogram h]
```

> [!TIP]
> When drawing on the KTU answer sheet, sketch the **3 texton categories** (bar, edge, blob), the **filter bank block**, and the **histogram bar chart**. Examiners reward diagrams that show both the local operator and the global descriptor.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**Define a texton. Name and briefly describe the three categories of textons identified by Béla Julesz.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** A *texton* is an elementary, local micro-structure (image primitive) that acts as a fundamental building block of perceived visual texture. In modern image-processing, it is operationally defined as the cluster-centre of local filter responses.
- **(2 marks) Three categories:**
  1. **Elongated textons** — line segments (bars) of specific length, width, and orientation.
  2. **Edge textons** — sharp step-like intensity transitions of specific orientation.
  3. **Non-elongated textons** — blobs (Gaussian-like intensity peaks) of characteristic size, terminators, and crossings.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**What is a texton dictionary? How is it constructed in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
