# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definitions)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

Differentiate between **appearance prior** and **pose prior** in the context of medical image segmentation. Give one real-world medical example for each.

**Model Answer:**

- **Appearance prior** is a probability distribution over intensity or texture features that characterizes *how a target tissue appears* in the imaging modality. It is data-driven and learned from annotated training samples. *Example:* the Hounsfield Unit (HU) distribution of liver parenchyma in non-contrast CT, modeled as a Gaussian with $\mu = 45$ HU and $\sigma = 10$ HU.
- **Pose prior** is a probability distribution over the geometric transformation (translation, rotation, scale) that describes *where and how the object is positioned* in the image. It encodes global spatial context. *Example:* the typical centroid location of the left ventricle in short-axis cardiac MRI, with a prior covariance of $\Sigma_T = \text{diag}(25, 25, 0.01, 0.04)$ mm²·rad².

The two are combined within a Bayesian MAP framework: $P(\mathbf{S}\mid \mathbf{I}) \propto P(\mathbf{I}\mid \mathbf{S}) \cdot P(\mathbf{S})$, where $P(\mathbf{S})$ factorizes into both appearance and pose components.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

What is a **Point Distribution Model (PDM)**? How is Principal Component Analysis (PCA) applied to construct it? State the mode-truncation rule.

**Model Answer:**

A **PDM** is a statistical shape model in which a set of $L$ landmark points placed on the boundary of an anatomical structure is represented as a single vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{2L}$. PCA is applied to a set of $N$ such training shapes after Generalized Procrustes Alignment to remove pose variations.

The construction steps are:
1. Align all $N$ training shapes to a common coordinate frame.
2. Compute mean shape $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$ and covariance $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{N-1} \mathbf{D} \mathbf{D}^T$.
3. Eigen-decompose $\mathbf{C} = \Phi \Lambda \Phi^T$.
4. Retain the first $k$ modes such that $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i \big/ \sum_{i=1}^{2L} \lambda_i \geq 0.98$.

Any plausible instance is then generated as $\mathbf{x} = \bar{\mathbf{x}} + \Phi_k \mathbf{b}$, with shape coefficients bounded by $\vert b_i \vert \leq 3\sqrt{\lambda_i}$.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> *As per KTU 2024 regulations, students answer ONE full question per module. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Derive the **MAP energy function** for medical image segmentation that incorporates both appearance and pose priors, starting from Bayes' theorem. Clearly identify the likelihood term, the appearance prior, and the pose prior. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the construction of a **Statistical Shape Model (Point Distribution Model)** using Principal Component Analysis. Show the steps of generalized Procrustes alignment and derive the shape-coefficient constraint. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) — Derivation of the MAP Energy (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Begin with Bayes' theorem:

$$P(\mathbf{S} \mid \mathbf{I}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{I} \mid \mathbf{S}) P(\mathbf{S})}{P(\mathbf{I})}$$

Step 2 — Drop the evidence $P(\mathbf{I})$ as it is independent of $\mathbf{S}$:

$$\hat{\mathbf{S}} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{S}} \; P(\mathbf{I} \mid \mathbf{S}) P(\mathbf{S})$$

Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm:

$$\hat{\mathbf{S}} = \arg\max_{\mathbf{S}} \; \log P(\mathbf{I} \mid \mathbf{S}) + \log P(\mathbf{S})$$

Step 4 — Negate to obtain a minimization (energy) form:

$$\hat{\mathbf{S}} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{S}} \; \underbrace{-\log P(\mathbf{I} \mid \mathbf{S})}_{E_{app}} - \underbrace{\log P(\mathbf{S})}_{E_{prior}}$$

Step 5 — Expand the prior by assuming conditional independence of pose $\mathbf{T}$ and shape $\mathbf{b}$:

$$E_{prior}(\mathbf{S}) = -\log P(\mathbf{T}) - \log P(\mathbf{b}) = \frac{1}{2}\Delta \mathbf{T}^T \Sigma_T^{-1} \Delta \mathbf{T} + \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{b}^T \Lambda^{-1} \mathbf{b}$$

Step 6 — Assemble the complete MAP energy:

$$\boxed{E(\mathbf{S}, \mathbf{T}, \mathbf{b}) = -\sum_{x \in \Omega} \log P(\mathbf{I}(x) \mid \mathbf{S}(x)) + \frac{1}{2}\Delta \mathbf{T}^T \Sigma_T^{-1} \Delta \mathbf{T} + \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{b}^T \Lambda^{-1} \mathbf{b}}$$

Step 7 — Interpretation: The first term forces pixel intensities to match the expected appearance; the second penalizes geometric configurations far from the typical pose; the third restricts shape deformation to plausible anatomical modes. (Conclusion — 1 mark)

**(b) — Construction of the Point Distribution Model (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Landmark Vector: Each of the $N$ training shapes is represented as:

$$\mathbf{x}_i = (x_{i,1}, y_{i,1}, \ldots, x_{i,L}, y_{i,L})^T \in \mathbb{R}^{2L}$$

Step 2 — Generalized Procrustes Alignment: Iteratively align each $\mathbf{x}_i$ to the evolving mean $\bar{\mathbf{x}}$ by computing the similarity transform $(s_i, \theta_i, t_{i,x}, t_{i,y})$ that minimizes $\Vert \mathbf{T}_i(\mathbf{x}_i) - \bar{\mathbf{x}} \Vert^2$. The translation is the centroid difference, the scale is the RMS-distance ratio, and the rotation comes from the SVD $\mathbf{x}_i \bar{\mathbf{x}}^T = U S V^T \Rightarrow R_i = V U^T$.

Step 3 — Mean Shape: $\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbf{T}_i(\mathbf{x}_i)$

Step 4 — Deviation Matrix and Covariance:

$$\mathbf{D} = [\mathbf{x}_1 - \bar{\mathbf{x}} \mid \cdots \mid \mathbf{x}_N - \bar{\mathbf{x}}] \in \mathbb{R}^{2L \times N}$$

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{N-1} \mathbf{D} \mathbf{D}^T \in \mathbb{R}^{2L \times 2L}$$

Step 5 — Eigendecomposition: $\mathbf{C} \Phi = \Phi \Lambda$, with eigenvalues sorted in descending order $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_{2L}$.

Step 6 — Mode Truncation: Keep the first $k$ modes such that $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i / \sum_{i=1}^{2L} \lambda_i \geq 0.98$.

Step 7 — Shape Reconstruction and Constraint: A valid instance is $\mathbf{x} = \bar{\mathbf{x}} + \Phi_k \mathbf{b}$, with coefficients $\mathbf{b} = \Phi_k^T (\mathbf{x} - \bar{\mathbf{x}})$. The ±3σ plausibility bound is:

$$\vert b_i \vert \leq 3\sqrt{\lambda_i} \quad \text{for} \; i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$$

This bound ensures that the generated shape remains within 99.7% of the training population's variability. (Conclusion — 1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Describe the **Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)** based appearance prior formulation. Derive the EM update equations for fitting a GMM to multi-modal tissue intensity data. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **two-stage segmentation pipeline** that uses a pose prior for global initialization followed by appearance-driven refinement. Discuss the role of multi-resolution processing. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) — GMM Appearance Prior (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Model: For a tissue class $c$, the intensity likelihood is a mixture of $K$ Gaussians:

$$P(\mathbf{I}(x) \mid c) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_{c,k} \cdot \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{I}(x) \mid \mu_{c,k}, \sigma_{c,k}^2)$$

with mixing weights $\pi_{c,k} \geq 0$ and $\sum_k \pi_{c,k} = 1$.

Step 2 — Log-Likelihood of the full image given labels $L$:

$$\log P(\mathbf{I} \mid L, \theta) = \sum_{x \in \Omega} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}[L(x)=c] \log \left[ \pi_{c,k} \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{I}(x) \mid \mu_{c,k}, \sigma_{c,k}^2) \right]$$

Step 3 — E-step: Compute the responsibility of component $k$ for voxel $x$:

$$\gamma_{c,k}(x) = \frac{\pi_{c,k} \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{I}(x) \mid \mu_{c,k}, \sigma_{c,k}^2)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_{c,j} \, \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{I}(x) \mid \mu_{c,j}, \sigma_{c,j}^2)}$$

Step 4 — M-step: Update the parameters using these responsibilities:

$$N_{c,k} = \sum_{x: L(x)=c} \gamma_{c,k}(x)$$

$$\mu_{c,k}^{new} = \frac{1}{N_{c,k}} \sum_{x: L(x)=c} \gamma_{c,k}(x) \, \mathbf{I}(x)$$

$$(\sigma_{c,k}^{new})^2 = \frac{1}{N_{c,k}} \sum_{x: L(x)=c} \gamma_{c,k}(x) \, (\mathbf{I}(x) - \mu_{c,k}^{new})^2$$

$$\pi_{c,k}^{new} = \frac{N_{c,k}}{N_c}$$

Step 5 — Iterate E and M steps until $\vert \log P^{(t+1)} - \log P^{(t)} \vert < \epsilon$.

Step 6 — The fitted GMM is then used in the MAP energy via $E_{app} = -\sum_x \log P(\mathbf{I}(x) \mid c)$, with the chosen $c$ being $\arg\max_k \gamma_{c,k}(x)$. (Conclusion — 1 mark)

**(b) — Two-Stage Pipeline (7 marks):**

**Sketch (textual block diagram):**

```
+---------------------------+
|  Stage 1: Pose Prior      |
|  -----------------------  |
|  Multi-Resolution Pyramid |
|           |               |
|           v               |
|  Sliding Window Search    |
|  (pose hypothesis sampling)|
|           |               |
|           v               |
|  Score by Mahalanobis     |
|  distance to mean pose    |
|           |               |
|           v               |
|  Top-K candidate poses    |
+---------------------------+
            |
            v
+---------------------------+
|  Stage 2: Appearance      |
|  Refinement               |
|  -----------------------  |
|  Warp ROI using T_hat     |
|           |               |
|           v               |
|  Fit GMM to intensities   |
|           |               |
|           v               |
|  Energy minimization via  |
|  level-set or graph-cut   |
|           |               |
|           v               |
|  Convergence? -> Output   |
+---------------------------+
```

Step 1 — **Stage 1 (Pose Initialization):** A multi-resolution image pyramid is built (e.g., 4 scales with $\times 1, \times 1/2, \times 1/4, \times 1/8$ resolutions). At the coarsest level, a sliding window evaluates a similarity transform hypothesis $\mathbf{T} = (t_x, t_y, s, \theta)$ against the pose prior, retaining the top-K candidates by Mahalanobis score.

Step 2 — **Role of Multi-Resolution:** Coarse scales provide robustness and computational efficiency (search over fewer pixels); fine scales provide sub-pixel accuracy. Top-down propagation refines each candidate at the next finer scale.

Step 3 — **Stage 2 (Appearance Refinement):** For each candidate pose $\mathbf{T}_k$, the ROI is warped via $\mathbf{I}_w(x) = \mathbf{I}(\mathbf{T}_k^{-1}(x))$. A GMM is fit to the histogram, and the appearance energy $E_{app}$ is computed.

Step 4 — **Level-Set Evolution:** The contour is evolved under the gradient flow $\partial_t \phi = -\delta(\phi)\left[\log \frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}} + \lambda \cdot \text{pose-force}\right]$.

Step 5 — **Convergence & Output:** Iteration stops when $\Vert \phi^{(t+1)} - \phi^{(t)} \Vert < \tau$. The mask with the lowest total energy $E = E_{app} + E_{pose} + E_{reg}$ is selected as the final segmentation. (Conclusion — 1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the likelihood with the prior.** Many students write $P(\mathbf{S}\mid \mathbf{I})$ when the question asks for $P(\mathbf{S})$ or $P(\mathbf{I}\mid \mathbf{S})$. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign** when converting from log-posterior to energy costs 1 mark.
> 3. **PCA truncation criterion** must be expressed as a *ratio of eigenvalues* — stating only "we take the first few modes" is incomplete.
> 4. **Procrustes alignment** must include all three sub-steps (translation, scale, rotation) — partial answers get only 3/7 marks.
> 5. **EM update equations** must be fully derived — quoting them without derivation yields at most 2/7 marks.
> 6. **Neat sketches earn 1–2 extra marks** — always include block diagrams in pipeline-style questions.
> 7. **Units matter:** Hounsfield Units (HU), mm, mm², radians must be specified where applicable.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Bayes' rule is the foundation** of every prior-based segmentation: $P(\mathbf{S}\mid \mathbf{I}) \propto P(\mathbf{I}\mid \mathbf{S}) P(\mathbf{S})$.
- **Appearance prior** = what the tissue *looks like* (intensity, texture); modeled by Gaussian, GMM, or multivariate feature Gaussians.
- **Pose prior** = where and how the object is *positioned* (translation, rotation, scale); modeled as a multivariate Gaussian on $\mathbf{T}$.
- **Shape prior (PDM/PCA)** captures non-rigid anatomical variability via $\mathbf{x} = \bar{\mathbf{x}} + \Phi_k \mathbf{b}$ with $\vert b_i \vert \leq 3\sqrt{\lambda_i}$.
- **Mode truncation rule:** retain 95–98% of cumulative eigenvalue energy.
- **Generalized Procrustes Alignment** removes pose variation *before* PCA is applied to shape data.
- **MAP energy = − log-posterior** = appearance energy + pose energy + shape energy + regularizer.
- **GMM appearance fitting** uses the EM algorithm: E-step computes responsibilities $\gamma_{c,k}(x)$; M-step updates $\mu, \sigma^2, \pi$.
- **Two-stage pipelines** (pose init → appearance refinement) outperform single-stage methods, especially for low-contrast boundaries.
- **Multi-resolution pyramids** reduce search complexity and improve global-to-local convergence.
- **Level-set methods** naturally incorporate priors via the gradient flow $\partial_t \phi = -\partial E / \partial \phi$.
- **Real-world applications:** radiotherapy planning, surgical navigation, cardiac MRI, Alzheimer's atrophy measurement.
- **Exam mantra:** Always state the *physical interpretation* of every equation; examiners reward contextual clarity.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Atlas-Based Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Atlas-Based Segmentation (ABS)** is a knowledge-driven, registration-based medical image segmentation paradigm in which a pre-constructed anatomical reference template — known as an *atlas* — comprising one (or several) already-labelled intensity image(s) is spatially aligned to an unlabelled target image, and the resulting spatial transformation is then used to **warp (propagate) the label map** of the atlas into the coordinate space of the target image, thereby producing a fully segmented output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Atlas-based segmentation is a registration-driven technique where a reference volume (atlas), in which every voxel has been manually annotated with anatomical structure labels, is non-rigidly registered to the patient's image; the inverse deformation field is applied to the atlas labels so that the anatomical parcellation is transferred to the patient. For robustness, *multi-atlas* approaches employ an ensemble of atlases and combine their propagated labels using a **label fusion** rule (Majority Voting, Weighted Voting, STAPLE, SIMPLE, etc.).

Mathematically, given an atlas pair $(I_A, L_A)$ and a target image $I_T$, the segmentation $L_T$ is obtained as:

$$L_T(\mathbf{x}) = L_A\!\left(\phi^{-1}(\mathbf{x})\right)$$

where $\phi$ is the spatial transformation that aligns $I_A$ to $I_T$, and $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is a voxel coordinate in the target image space.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Map Overlays" Mental Model

Imagine you have a **printed road map of Kerala (the atlas)** with every town, river and district boundary clearly drawn and labelled. Now you place this printed map on top of a *blank satellite photograph* (the patient's MRI) of a slightly different part of the world that *looks similar in structure* (the target). The map is **rubber-sheeted, twisted and stretched** (non-rigid registration) until the rivers in the printed map line up with the dark valleys in the satellite photo. Once alignment is achieved, you *press down* on the map and the labels transfer through the paper onto the photograph below. That is exactly what atlas-based segmentation does — the *labels are propagated* through the estimated deformation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary You MUST Memorise for KTU Viva:**
> - **Atlas** = the pre-labelled reference image (intensity + segmentation)
> - **Registration** = finding the geometric transform $\phi$ that aligns atlas to target
> - **Warping / Deformation** = applying $\phi$ to the atlas labels
> - **Label Fusion** = combining the labels from multiple atlases into a single consensus
> - **STAPLE** = *Simultaneous Truth and Performance Level Estimation* — a probabilistic fusion algorithm

### 1.3 Why Atlas-Based Segmentation Matters in Biomedical Engineering

- It **embeds expert anatomical knowledge** (manually delineated by radiologists) directly into the algorithm — no machine-learning training set required.
- It produces **topologically and anatomically plausible** segmentations, even when image contrast is poor.
- It is the **gold-standard workhorse** for brain parcellation in tools like **FreeSurfer, FSL FIRST, SPM12, ANTs, and BrainSuite**.
- It is fully **unsupervised at inference time** for single-atlas approaches — a single registration step is sufficient.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants Used Throughout

| Symbol / Metric | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) | Spatial overlap of predicted vs. ground-truth | 0.85 – 0.95 (good brain GM/WM) |
| Hausdorff Distance (HD) | Maximum boundary error in mm | 1 – 3 mm (acceptable) |
| Jaccard Index | Intersection-over-Union of label sets | 0.70 – 0.90 |
| Mean Surface Distance (MSD) | Average boundary error in mm | < 1.5 mm (excellent) |
| Number of atlases N | Library size in multi-atlas | 15 – 50 (typical) |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline — The Five Logical Steps

1. **Atlas Construction (offline).** A single expert (or a consensus of experts) manually delineates anatomical structures in a representative image using tools like ITK-SNAP, 3D Slicer or FreeSurfer. The result is the atlas pair $(I_A, L_A)$.
2. **Pre-processing.** Both atlas and target are corrected for **intensity inhomogeneity (bias field)**, spatially normalised to a common orientation, and optionally **skull-stripped** (e.g., using BET in FSL) so that non-brain tissues do not corrupt registration.
3. **Image Registration.** A spatial transformation $\phi : \mathbb{R}^3 \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^3$ is computed that maximises the *similarity* between $I_A \circ \phi$ and $I_T$. This is formulated as:

$$\hat{\phi} = \arg\min_{\phi} \; \mathcal{S}\!\left(I_A \circ \phi,\; I_T\right) + \lambda \cdot \mathcal{R}(\phi)$$

where $\mathcal{S}$ is a *dissimilarity* (cost) function, $\mathcal{R}(\phi)$ is a *regulariser* on the deformation (typically bending energy of a B-spline or L2 norm of the displacement gradient), and $\lambda$ balances data-fidelity vs. smoothness.

4. **Label Propagation (Warping).** The transformation $\hat{\phi}$ is inverted and applied to the atlas label map:

$$L_T(\mathbf{x}) = L_A\!\left(\hat{\phi}^{-1}(\mathbf{x})\right)$$

5. **Label Fusion (multi-atlas only).** For $N$ atlases producing propagated label sets $\{L_T^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N}$, a fusion operator $\mathcal{F}$ produces the final segmentation $L_T^{\text{final}} = \mathcal{F}\!\left(L_T^{(1)}, L_T^{(2)}, \dots, L_T^{(N)}\right)$.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Registration Strategies

| Stage | Transformation Class | Degrees of Freedom | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rigid** (rotation + translation) | 6 | Initial global alignment, intra-subject |
| 2 | **Affine** (rigid + scaling + shearing) | 12 | Compensating scanner geometry differences |
| 3 | **Deformable / Non-rigid** | $10^4$ – $10^6$ | Local anatomical correspondence |

Common non-rigid parametrisations:

- **B-spline Free-Form Deformations (FFD)** — control point grid with cubic B-spline basis
- **Demons algorithm** — diffusion-based, optical-flow analogy
- **SyN (Symmetric Normalisation)** — symmetric, topology-preserving diffeomorphism
- **Diffeomorphic Demons** — preserves invertibility
- **LDDMM** — Large Deformation Diffeomorphic Metric Mapping (most rigorous)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{\phi} = \arg\min_{\phi}\, \mathcal{S}(I_A \circ \phi, I_T) + \lambda \mathcal{R}(\phi)$ | Registration as variational optimisation |
| 2 | $\text{MI}(A,B) = H(A) + H(B) - H(A,B)$ | Mutual Information (information-theoretic similarity) |
| 3 | $\text{NMI}(A,B) = \dfrac{H(A) + H(B)}{H(A,B)}$ | Normalised MI (robust to overlap changes) |
| 4 | $\text{SSD}(A,B) = \sum_{\mathbf{x}} \left[I_A(\mathbf{x}) - I_T(\mathbf{x})\right]^2$ | Sum of Squared Differences (same modality) |
| 5 | $\text{NCC}(A,B) = \dfrac{\sum_{\mathbf{x}}(A-\bar A)(B-\bar B)}{\sqrt{\sum (A-\bar A)^2 \sum (B-\bar B)^2}}$ | Normalised Cross-Correlation |
| 6 | $L_T(\mathbf{x}) = L_A(\phi^{-1}(\mathbf{x}))$ | Label propagation through inverse warp |
| 7 | $\hat{L}(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{k}\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i(\mathbf{x})\,\mathbb{1}\!\left[L_T^{(i)}(\mathbf{x}) = k\right]$ | Weighted / Majority Voting fusion |
| 8 | $\text{DSC}(P,G) = \dfrac{2 \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}$ | Dice Similarity Coefficient (validation) |
| 9 | $d_H(P,G) = \max\!\left\{\sup_{p \in P}\inf_{g \in G} d(p,g),\; \sup_{g \in G}\inf_{p \in P} d(g,p)\right\}$ | Hausdorff Distance (boundary error) |
| 10 | $\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{x} + \sum_{i} \mathbf{c}_i \, \beta_i(\mathbf{x})$ | B-spline FFD parametrisation |
| 11 | $\mathcal{R}_{\text{bend}}(\phi) = \int_{\Omega}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2}\right)^2 + 2\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \partial y}\right)^2 d\mathbf{x}$ | Bending-energy regulariser |
| 12 | $J(\mathbf{x}) = \det(\nabla \phi(\mathbf{x})) > 0$ | Jacobian positivity (diffeomorphism check) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Exam Pearl:** When asked "Which similarity metric is used for *multi-modal* registration?", the answer is **Mutual Information (MI)**. For *same-modality* registration, SSD or NCC is sufficient.

### 2.4 Why the Regulariser $\mathcal{R}(\phi)$ is Non-Negotiable

Without a regulariser, the optimiser will happily fold the atlas inside-out to force every voxel to match the target — a catastrophically *non-physical* deformation. The bending-energy regulariser penalises high-frequency spatial derivatives of $\phi$, forcing the warp to be **smooth and diffeomorphic** (i.e., continuously differentiable with a positive Jacobian determinant everywhere).

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Radiotherapy planning:** Delineate organs-at-risk (OARs) such as the brainstem and optic chiasm.
- **Surgical planning:** Map functional cortex to patient-specific anatomy.
- **Longitudinal studies:** Track atrophy in Alzheimer's disease by repeatedly segmenting follow-up scans.
- **Population studies:** Build statistical atlases (e.g., the MNI152 template built from 152 healthy adults).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mutual Information from First Principles

Mutual Information measures how much knowing one image reduces uncertainty about the other. We start with the Shannon entropy of a discrete random variable:

$$H(A) = -\sum_{a} p_A(a)\,\log p_A(a)$$

The **joint entropy** of two images is:

$$H(A,B) = -\sum_{a,b} p_{A,B}(a,b)\,\log p_{A,B}(a,b)$$

The **conditional entropy** of $A$ given $B$ is:

$$H(A \mid B) = H(A,B) - H(B)$$

By definition, Mutual Information is the reduction in uncertainty of $A$ caused by knowing $B$:

$$\text{MI}(A,B) = H(A) - H(A \mid B)$$

Substituting the conditional-entropy expression:

$$\text{MI}(A,B) = H(A) - \bigl[H(A,B) - H(B)\bigr]$$

Collecting terms yields the symmetric, KTU-board-standard form:

$$\boxed{\;\text{MI}(A,B) = H(A) + H(B) - H(A,B)\;}$$

**Practical estimation in images** (using histogram bin counts $p_{ab} = h(a,b)/N_{\text{voxels}}$):

$$H(A) = -\sum_{a=1}^{N_b} \frac{h(a)}{N_{\text{voxels}}}\,\log\!\left(\frac{h(a)}{N_{\text{voxels}}}\right)$$

$$H(A,B) = -\sum_{a=1}^{N_b}\sum_{b=1}^{N_b} \frac{h(a,b)}{N_{\text{voxels}}}\,\log\!\left(\frac{h(a,b)}{N_{\text{voxels}}}\right)$$

> **Sanity check:** If the two images are statistically independent, $p_{A,B}(a,b) = p_A(a)\,p_B(b)$ so $H(A,B) = H(A) + H(B)$ and $\text{MI} = 0$ — no information is shared. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)

Let $P$ be the predicted binary mask and $G$ the ground-truth mask, with cardinalities $\vert P \vert$ and $\vert G \vert$.

The **Jaccard Index** is defined as:

$$J(P,G) = \frac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \cup G \vert} = \frac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert - \vert P \cap G \vert}$$

The **Dice coefficient** is the harmonic-like mean of precision and recall:

$$\text{DSC} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{precision} \cdot \text{recall}}{\text{precision} + \text{recall}}$$

with $\text{precision} = \dfrac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert}$ and $\text{recall} = \dfrac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert G \vert}$.

Substituting and simplifying:

$$\text{DSC} = \frac{2 \dfrac{\vert P \cap G \vert^2}{\vert P \vert \cdot \vert G \vert}}{\dfrac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert} + \dfrac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert G \vert}} = \frac{2\,\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}$$

So the compact and famous KTU result is:

$$\boxed{\;\text{DSC}(P,G) = \frac{2\,\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}\;}$$

Properties: $\text{DSC} = 1 \Leftrightarrow P = G$ (perfect), $\text{DSC} = 0 \Leftrightarrow P \cap G = \emptyset$ (no overlap).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Weighted Label Fusion (Majority Voting as a Special Case)

Let there be $N$ atlases, each producing a propagated label $L_T^{(i)}(\mathbf{x}) \in \{1,2,\dots,K\}$ at voxel $\mathbf{x}$, and a per-voxel weight $w_i(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ (e.g., local NCC between atlas $i$ and target after registration).

The **weighted vote** for class $k$ is:

$$V_k(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i(\mathbf{x})\,\mathbb{1}\!\bigl[L_T^{(i)}(\mathbf{x}) = k\bigr]$$

The fused label is the *argmax*:

$$\hat{L}(\mathbf{x}) = \arg\max_{k} V_k(\mathbf{x})$$

**Special case — Unweighted Majority Voting:** Set $w_i(\mathbf{x}) = 1$ for all $i,\mathbf{x}$. Then $V_k(\mathbf{x})$ is just the count of atlases voting for class $k$, and the class with the most votes wins. Ties are broken by *random selection* or by the *most similar atlas* in the library.

**Special case — STAPLE (Simultaneous Truth and Performance Level Estimation):** Models each atlas $i$ as having a sensitivity $p_i$ and specificity $q_i$; iteratively estimates $(p_i, q_i)$ and the true label $T$ by EM until convergence. The update equations (omitted in full here for brevity) yield:

$$W_k^{(t+1)}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\prod_i f(L_T^{(i)}(\mathbf{x}) \mid T = k;\,\theta_i^{(t)})}{\sum_{k'} \prod_i f(L_T^{(i)}(\mathbf{x}) \mid T = k';\,\theta_i^{(t)})}$$

where $f$ is the likelihood model parameterised by the current performance estimates $\theta_i^{(t)}$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: B-spline FFD Regularisation Energy

Let the displacement field be $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}) = \phi(\mathbf{x}) - \mathbf{x}$, parametrised as:

$$\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i,j,k} \mathbf{c}_{i,j,k}\,\beta_i(x)\,\beta_j(y)\,\beta_k(z)$$

where $\beta_i$ is the cubic B-spline basis and $\mathbf{c}_{i,j,k}$ are the control-point coefficients. The **bending energy** of the thin-plate analogy is:

$$\mathcal{R}_{\text{bend}} = \int_{\Omega}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 \mathbf{u}}{\partial x^2}\right)^{\!2} + \left(\dfrac{\partial^2 \mathbf{u}}{\partial y^2}\right)^{\!2} + \left(\dfrac{\partial^2 \mathbf{u}}{\partial z^2}\right)^{\!2} + 2\!\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 \mathbf{u}}{\partial x \partial y}\right)^{\!2} + 2\!\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 \mathbf{u}}{\partial y \partial z}\right)^{\!2} + 2\!\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 \mathbf{u}}{\partial x \partial z}\right)^{\!2}\right] d\mathbf{x}$$

In matrix form for a discrete control-point grid of size $n_x \times n_y \times n_z$:

$$\mathcal{R}_{\text{bend}} = \mathbf{c}^{\top}\,\mathbf{B}\,\mathbf{c}$$

where $\mathbf{B}$ is the (sparse) bending-energy matrix. The full registration cost is then:

$$\mathcal{J}(\mathbf{c}) = \mathcal{S}\!\bigl(I_A(\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{u}_{\mathbf{c}}(\mathbf{x})),\, I_T(\mathbf{x})\bigr) + \lambda\,\mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{c}$$

Optimised by gradient descent (or L-BFGS) with respect to the control-point vector $\mathbf{c}$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Component

| Clinical Scenario | Recommended Atlas Count | Registration | Similarity Metric | Fusion Rule |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Healthy adult brain (T1) | 15 – 30 | Affine + SyN | NCC (or MI for multi-modal) | Weighted Voting (NCC) |
| Paediatric brain (high variability) | 30 – 50 | Affine + Demons | NCC | STAPLE |
| Tumour-bearing brain | 1 + pathology exclusion | Affine + FFD | MI (T1 ↔ T2) | Majority Voting |
| Cardiac MRI (4-chamber) | 5 – 10 | Affine + B-spline FFD | MI | Majority Voting |
| Prostate TRUS–MRI fusion | 1 (real-time) | Rigid + B-spline | MI | Manual refinement |
| Whole-body CT organ-atlas | 20 – 40 | Affine + SyN | MI | STAPLE or SIMPLE |
