## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define atlas-based segmentation. List any two advantages and one disadvantage of using a single-atlas approach.**

**Model Answer:**

Atlas-based segmentation is a registration-driven technique in which a pre-labelled reference volume (atlas), containing both an intensity image and a manually delineated anatomical label map, is spatially aligned (registered) to an unlabelled target patient image, and the resulting transformation is used to warp the atlas labels into the target's coordinate space, producing a fully segmented output.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **No training data required** — leverages expert manual delineation directly.
2. **Anatomically plausible** segmentations because labels are propagated, not guessed.
3. **Simple pipeline** — only a single registration step is needed.

**Disadvantage:**
1. **Sensitive to atlas choice** — if the target anatomy differs substantially from the single atlas (e.g., pathology, paediatric vs. adult), registration errors propagate into the segmentation, reducing accuracy. This is the primary motivation for multi-atlas approaches.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Differentiate between Majority Voting and STAPLE algorithms used in multi-atlas label fusion.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Majority Voting (MV) | STAPLE |
|---|---|---|
| Weighting | All atlases treated equally | Each atlas weighted by estimated sensitivity & specificity |
| Output | Hard label (argmax of vote counts) | Probabilistic label + performance parameters per atlas |
| Computational cost | Low — single pass | Higher — iterative EM algorithm |
| Robustness to poor atlases | Poor — a bad atlas has full vote | High — bad atlases automatically down-weighted |
| Use case | Quick baseline, homogeneous atlas pool | Heterogeneous atlases, research-grade accuracy |

**Key equation for STAPLE performance update** (Examiner's bonus mark):

$$w_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{\sum_{\mathbf{x}} \mathbb{1}\!\left[L_T^{(i)}(\mathbf{x}) = T^{(t)}(\mathbf{x})\right] p_i^{(t)}}{\sum_{\mathbf{x}} p_i^{(t)}}$$

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Choose ONE

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete pipeline of a **multi-atlas based segmentation** system, clearly stating the role of registration and label fusion. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)** from the definitions of precision and recall, and explain why DSC is preferred over the Jaccard Index for evaluating medical image segmentations. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Multi-Atlas Segmentation Pipeline (7 Marks):**

The multi-atlas pipeline consists of five sequential stages (model block diagram as in Section 4.2):

1. **Atlas Library Construction (1 Mark):** A set of $N$ atlases $\{(I_A^{(i)}, L_A^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^N$ is built, each consisting of a co-registered intensity image and expert-labelled structures. The atlases are pre-processed (skull-stripped, bias-corrected, intensity-normalised) to reduce inter-atlas variability.

2. **Target Pre-processing (1 Mark):** The patient image $I_T$ is subjected to the same pre-processing steps (skull-stripping via BET, N4 bias correction, histogram matching) so that it is in the same "intensity space" as the atlas library.

3. **Image Registration (2 Marks):** For each atlas $i$, a non-rigid transformation $\phi_i$ is computed that aligns $I_A^{(i)}$ to $I_T$. The cost function is:
$$\hat{\phi}_i = \arg\min_{\phi}\;\mathcal{S}\!\left(I_A^{(i)} \circ \phi,\; I_T\right) + \lambda\,\mathcal{R}(\phi)$$
with similarity $\mathcal{S}$ chosen as **NCC** (mono-modal) or **MI** (multi-modal) and regulariser $\mathcal{R}$ as the bending energy of a B-spline FFD. Each registration is typically initialised by an affine step (12 DOF) followed by a deformable step.

4. **Label Propagation (1 Mark):** The inverse deformation $\phi_i^{-1}$ is applied to the atlas label map to produce a propagated label set $L_T^{(i)}(\mathbf{x}) = L_A^{(i)}(\phi_i^{-1}(\mathbf{x}))$.

5. **Label Fusion (2 Marks):** The $N$ propagated label sets are combined via a fusion operator $\mathcal{F}$ (Majority Voting, Weighted Voting, STAPLE, etc.) into a single consensus segmentation $L_{\text{final}}$:
$$L_{\text{final}}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathcal{F}\!\left(L_T^{(1)}(\mathbf{x}),\dots,L_T^{(N)}(\mathbf{x})\right)$$

The role of *registration* is to establish point-to-point anatomical correspondence; the role of *label fusion* is to statistically aggregate the evidence from $N$ noisy segmentations, thereby cancelling out random registration errors and amplifying the consensus.

---

**(b) Dice Similarity Coefficient Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Define precision and recall for a binary mask** (1 Mark):
$$\text{Precision} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} = \frac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert}$$
$$\text{Recall} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert G \vert}$$

**Step 2 — Express the F1-score (harmonic mean) of precision and recall** (2 Marks):
$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{\text{Precision}} + \frac{1}{\text{Recall}}} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the precision/recall expressions** (2 Marks):
$$F_1 = \frac{2\,\dfrac{\vert P \cap G \vert^2}{\vert P \vert \cdot \vert G \vert}}{\dfrac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert} + \dfrac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert G \vert}}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the common factor $\vert P \cap G \vert$ from numerator and denominator** (1 Mark):
$$F_1 = \frac{2\,\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}$$

This is the **Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)**. By construction, $0 \leq \text{DSC} \leq 1$, with $\text{DSC} = 1$ when $P = G$ (perfect) and $\text{DSC} = 0$ when $P \cap G = \emptyset$.

**Why DSC is preferred over Jaccard for medical imaging (1 Mark):**
The Jaccard Index $J = \vert P \cap G \vert / \vert P \cup G \vert$ is mathematically related to DSC by:
$$\text{DSC} = \frac{2 J}{1 + J}$$
Both are monotonically related, but DSC is preferred because: (i) it is the **F1-score of precision and recall**, giving it an intuitive information-retrieval interpretation; (ii) it weights the overlap more generously (DSC > J for any partial overlap), which is desirable for sparse structures like lesions; and (iii) it has lower variance for small structures, making it more reliable for statistically powered group studies in clinical trials.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the role of **Mutual Information (MI)** as a similarity metric in multi-modal image registration. Derive the formula $\text{MI}(A,B) = H(A) + H(B) - H(A,B)$ from the definition of conditional entropy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the importance of the **regularisation term** $\mathcal{R}(\phi)$ in deformable registration. Explain the bending-energy regulariser for B-spline FFDs and state the diffeomorphism condition that the Jacobian determinant must satisfy. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Mutual Information Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Shannon entropy** (1 Mark):
For a discrete random variable $A$ with probability mass function $p_A(a)$:
$$H(A) = -\sum_{a} p_A(a)\,\log p_A(a)$$

**Step 2 — Joint entropy and conditional entropy** (2 Marks):
$$H(A,B) = -\sum_{a,b} p_{A,B}(a,b)\,\log p_{A,B}(a,b)$$
$$H(A \mid B) = H(A,B) - H(B)$$

**Step 3 — Definition of Mutual Information as the reduction in uncertainty of $A$ when $B$ is known** (1 Mark):
$$\text{MI}(A,B) = H(A) - H(A \mid B)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify** (2 Marks):
$$\text{MI}(A,B) = H(A) - \bigl[H(A,B) - H(B)\bigr] = H(A) + H(B) - H(A,B)$$

**Step 5 — Role in multi-modal registration (1 Mark):**
For multi-modal registration (e.g., aligning MRI T1 to PET, or CT to MRI), corresponding tissues have *different intensities* (bone is bright in CT, dark in MRI T1), so SSD/NCC are meaningless. MI solves this because it is *modality-invariant* — it measures statistical dependence, not direct intensity correspondence. Maximising MI between the warped moving image and the fixed image drives the registration to align anatomically corresponding tissues regardless of their intensity values.

---

**(b) Regularisation in Deformable Registration (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Why regularisation is needed (2 Marks):**
A non-rigid registration problem with thousands of degrees of freedom is **ill-posed**: many different warps can produce the same similarity score. Without regularisation, the optimiser can drive $\phi$ to *non-physical* solutions — folds, tears, or shrinkage of tissue — that produce perfect intensity matches but corrupt anatomy. The regulariser $\mathcal{R}(\phi)$ encodes the *prior belief* that the deformation should be smooth.

**Step 2 — Bending-energy regulariser (3 Marks):**
For a B-spline FFD with displacement field $\mathbf{u}(\mathbf{x}) = \phi(\mathbf{x}) - \mathbf{x} = \sum_{i,j,k} \mathbf{c}_{i,j,k}\,\beta_i(x)\beta_j(y)\beta_k(z)$, the bending-energy regulariser penalises the second-order spatial derivatives:
$$\mathcal{R}_{\text{bend}}(\phi) = \int_{\Omega}\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial^2 \mathbf{u}}{\partial x^2}\right)^{\!2} + \left(\frac{\partial^2 \mathbf{u}}{\partial y^2}\right)^{\!2} + \left(\frac{\partial^2 \mathbf{u}}{\partial z^2}\right)^{\!2} + 2\!\left(\frac{\partial^2 \mathbf{u}}{\partial x \partial y}\right)^{\!2} + 2\!\left(\frac{\partial^2 \mathbf{u}}{\partial y \partial z}\right)^{\!2} + 2\!\left(\frac{\partial^2 \mathbf{u}}{\partial x \partial z}\right)^{\!2}\right] d\mathbf{x}$$
In matrix form: $\mathcal{R}_{\text{bend}} = \mathbf{c}^{\top}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{c}$, where $\mathbf{B}$ is sparse and depends only on the control-point grid spacing. This is integrated into the full cost:
$$\mathcal{J}(\phi) = \mathcal{S}\!\left(I_A \circ \phi,\; I_T\right) + \lambda\,\mathcal{R}_{\text{bend}}(\phi)$$
where $\lambda$ trades data-fidelity against smoothness.

**Step 3 — Diffeomorphism condition (2 Marks):**
A registration transformation is *diffeomorphic* if it is (i) **continuously differentiable** (smooth) and (ii) **invertible** (one-to-one) with a smooth inverse. The invertible-with-smooth-inverse condition is equivalent to:
$$J(\mathbf{x}) = \det\!\left(\nabla \phi(\mathbf{x})\right) > 0 \quad \forall\,\mathbf{x} \in \Omega$$
A *negative* Jacobian indicates a fold (tissue passes through itself); a *zero* Jacobian indicates a critical point where local volume collapses to zero — both are physically impossible. Algorithms like **SyN** and **LDDMM** explicitly enforce $J > 0$ throughout the domain, guaranteeing a topology-preserving warp.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "warp the image" with "warp the labels".** Many students write the wrong direction. *Correct:* to segment $I_T$, you warp $L_A$ (the labels) into $T$'s space using $\phi^{-1}$, not warp $I_T$ into the atlas.
> 2. **Calling MI a "distance metric".** It is a *similarity* (higher is better). Some KTU toppers have lost 1 mark for calling it a distance.
> 3. **Forgetting the regulariser in deformable registration derivations.** A non-rigid registration written without $\mathcal{R}(\phi)$ is mathematically ill-posed — examiners deduct for this.
> 4. **Mixing up Dice and Jaccard formulas.** Dice uses a factor of **2** in the numerator; Jaccard does not. Memorise both.
> 5. **Writing "STAPLE = voting".** It is *not* simple voting; it is an **Expectation-Maximisation (EM) algorithm** that estimates per-atlas sensitivity and specificity.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Atlas** = pre-labelled reference volume (intensity + labels).
- **Atlas-based segmentation** = register atlas to target → propagate labels via inverse warp → (optionally) fuse across multiple atlases.
- **Single-atlas pros:** simple, fast. **Cons:** sensitive to atlas-target mismatch.
- **Multi-atlas pros:** robust, statistically grounded. **Cons:** N times more computation.
- **Registration hierarchy:** Rigid (6 DOF) → Affine (12 DOF) → Deformable (10⁴–10⁶ DOF).
- **Similarity metrics:** SSD / NCC (mono-modal), **MI / NMI** (multi-modal).
- **Mutual Information:** $\text{MI}(A,B) = H(A) + H(B) - H(A,B)$ — modality-invariant.
- **B-spline FFD:** displacement = $\sum \mathbf{c}_{ijk}\,\beta_i(x)\beta_j(y)\beta_k(z)$; regulariser is the bending energy $\mathbf{c}^\top \mathbf{B} \mathbf{c}$.
- **Diffeomorphism condition:** $\det(\nabla \phi(\mathbf{x})) > 0$ everywhere.
- **Label fusion options:** Majority Voting (unweighted) → Weighted Voting (NCC-weighted) → STAPLE (EM-based, probabilistic) → SIMPLE (joint intensity + label likelihood).
- **STAPLE** = *Simultaneous Truth and Performance Level Estimation* — iterative EM, estimates per-atlas $p$ (sensitivity) and $q$ (specificity).
- **Validation metrics:** **DSC** (overlap, 0–1), **HD** (boundary max-error, mm), **Jaccard** (IoU), **MSD** (boundary mean-error, mm).
- **DSC formula:** $\text{DSC} = \dfrac{2\,\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}$ — the F1-score of precision and recall.
- **Jaccard–Dice relation:** $\text{DSC} = \dfrac{2J}{1+J}$.
- **Key software tools:** ANTs (SyN), FSL (FNIRT), SPM12, FreeSurfer, Elastix, NiftyReg.
- **Clinical applications:** brain parcellation (MNI/Hammers atlases), OAR delineation in radiotherapy, longitudinal atrophy tracking in Alzheimer's, multi-organ CT segmentation.
- **Standard numbers to remember:** DSC > **0.85** = excellent; HD < **3 mm** = clinically acceptable; typical multi-atlas library size $N$ = **15–50**; B-spline control-point spacing **5–10 mm** for brain MRI.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combining Shape Information with Level Set Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Combining Shape Information with Level Set Segmentation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Level Set Segmentation** is a contour-based, implicit-curve evolution technique in which a closed boundary $C$ is represented as the zero level set of a higher-dimensional scalar function $\phi(\mathbf{x},t)$ known as the **Level Set Function (LSF)**, evolving under a speed field derived from image-derived forces. When **Shape Information (Shape Priors)** is incorporated, the segmentation is no longer driven purely by local image statistics (edges, intensity) but is additionally constrained by a statistical or geometric model of the expected object shape. The combined variational formulation is: $$E(\phi) = \lambda_{1} E_{\text{image}}(\phi) + \lambda_{2} E_{\text{shape}}(\phi)$$ where $E_{\text{image}}$ pulls the contour toward image features and $E_{\text{shape}}$ keeps it close to a learned reference shape $\phi_{0}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is the bridge between classical (purely data-driven) segmentation and modern **knowledge-based / model-driven** segmentation — essential for medical images suffering from **weak boundaries, noise, intensity inhomogeneity, or partial occlusions**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Search Dog" Metaphor Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to locate a specific dog (your target organ) inside a large, dimly-lit room (the MRI/CT image): | Real-World Analogy | Image Processing Equivalent | |---|---| | The blindfold is removed partially; you see fuzzy blobs (edges/gradients) | $E_{\text{image}}$ — local pixel-based cues | | You have a **photograph of the dog** in your pocket that you keep comparing to whatever you see | $E_{\text{shape}}$ — global shape prior | | You walk forward only when the *image* and the *photo* agree simultaneously | Combined energy minimisation | Without the photo, you might grab the wrong dog (false segmentation). With the photo, the search becomes guided and accurate — **this is precisely the role of a shape prior in level set segmentation**. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used - **Level set re-initialisation frequency** — typically every **20–50 iterations** (Sethian, 1999 convention). - **Shape weight** $\lambda_{2} \in [0.1,\, 0.5]$ — tuned to allow local deformation while preserving global shape. - **Time step** $\Delta t$ in the explicit update — chosen to satisfy the **CFL condition** $\Delta t \cdot \max \vert F \vert \leq \Delta x$. > [!NOTE] > In medical imaging, the implicit representation is preferred because the contour can **split, merge, and change topology** without any auxiliary reparametrisation — a property B-spline or active contour ("snake") models cannot guarantee. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Pure Level Set Energy (Baseline) For an image $I: \Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^{2} \to \mathbb{R}$, the classical *Geodesic Active Contour* (Caselles–Kimmel–Sapiro) energy is: $$E_{\text{GAC}}(\phi) = \mu \, \delta(\phi) \vert \nabla \phi \vert \, dx + \int_{\Omega} g(I) \, \delta(\phi) \vert \nabla \phi \vert \, dx$$ - $\delta(\phi)$ — Dirac delta concentrated on the zero level set. - $g(I) = \dfrac{1}{1+\vert \nabla G_{\sigma} * I \vert^{2}}$ — edge-stopping function (inverse gradient). - $\mu$ — regularisation weight (length/area penalty). The corresponding **evolution PDE** (gradient flow) is: $$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = \delta(\phi) \left[ \mu \, \text{div}\!\left(\frac{\nabla \phi}{\vert \nabla \phi \vert}\right) + g(I)\, \text{div}\!\left(\frac{\nabla \phi}{\vert \nabla \phi \vert}\right) + \nabla g \cdot \frac{\nabla \phi}{\vert \nabla \phi \vert} \right]$$ ### 2.2 Why Pure Image-Driven Segmentation Fails 1. **Weak/ill-defined edges** — e.g., tumour boundaries in T1-weighted MRI. 2. **Intensity inhomogeneity** — RF coil bias shifts intensities within the same tissue. 3. **Occlusion / missing parts** — pathology may erase a portion of the organ wall. 4. **Image noise** — speckle in ultrasound misleads gradient operators. A **shape prior** regularises these ambiguities by injecting *a-priori* knowledge. ### 2.3 The Shape Prior Energy A representative template (signed distance function) $\phi_{0}$ is registered to the current evolving LSF $\phi$. The shape energy is: $$E_{\text{shape}}(\phi) = \int_{\Omega} \left[ H(\phi) - H(\phi_{0}) \right]^{2} dx$$ or, in signed-distance form (Leventon et al., 2000): $$E_{\text{shape}}(\phi) = \int_{\Omega} \bigl( \phi(\mathbf{x}) - \phi_{0}(\mathbf{x}) \bigr)^{2} \, d\mathbf{x}$$ where $H(\cdot)$ is the Heaviside step function. The first form compares **region occupancy** (robust to translation/scale of the prior); the second compares **signed distance fields** (sensitive to geometric detail). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** This combined energy is the foundation of commercial-grade systems used for **prostate delineation in TRUS, left-ventricle segmentation in cardiac MRI, and hippocampus segmentation in neuro-imaging pipelines** (e.g., FreeSurfer, ITK-SNAP). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Typical Value / Unit** | |---|---|---| | $\phi(\mathbf{x},t)$ | Level set function (LSF) | Signed distance, dimensionless | | $C(t) = \{ \mathbf{x} : \phi(\mathbf{x},t)=0 \}$ | Evolving contour | Pixels / voxels | | $\delta(\phi)$ | Dirac delta (regularised) | $\delta_{\varepsilon}(\phi) = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\dfrac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon^{2}+\phi^{2}}$ | | $H(\phi)$ | Heaviside function (regularised) | $H_{\varepsilon}(\phi) = \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + \dfrac{2}{\pi}\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\phi}{\varepsilon}\right)\right]$ | | $g(I)$ | Edge-stopping function | $g = (1+\vert \nabla G_{\sigma}*I \vert^{2})^{-1}$, range $(0,1]$ | | $\phi_{0}$ | Shape prior (reference SDF) | Signed distance | | $\lambda_{2}$ | Shape weight | $0.1 \leq \lambda_{2} \leq 0.5$ | | $\Delta t$ | Time step | Must satisfy CFL: $\Delta t \leq \dfrac{\Delta x}{\max \vert F \vert}$ | | $\varepsilon$ | Regularisation parameter | $\varepsilon \approx 1.5\,\Delta x$ | | $\mu$ | Length/area regularisation weight | $0.01 \leq \mu \leq 0.2$ | ### 2.5 Why Combine Shape Information — A Checklist - Adds **global constraint** (topology, size, orientation) to a **local** process. - **Robust to missing boundaries** — Bayesian-style prior likelihood. - Reduces dependence on careful initialisation. - Provides **inter-patient variability** modelling when combined with PCA on training shapes. - Can encode **pathology exclusions** (e.g., a tumour that "pops out" from a known organ boundary). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Combined Gradient Flow (Euler–Lagrange) We start from the total energy: $$E(\phi) = \underbrace{\int_{\Omega} g(I)\,\delta(\phi)\,\vert \nabla \phi \vert \, d\mathbf{x}}_{E_{\text{image}}} \;+\; \underbrace{\mu \int_{\Omega} \delta(\phi)\,\vert \nabla \phi \vert \, d\mathbf{x}}_{E_{\text{length}}} \;+\; \underbrace{\lambda_{2} \int_{\Omega} \bigl(H(\phi) - H(\phi_{0})\bigr)^{2} d\mathbf{x}}_{E_{\text{shape}}}$$ **Step 1 — Compute the first variation w.r.t. $\phi$.** Using calculus of variations, for an integrand $F(\phi, \nabla \phi)$: $$\frac{\delta E}{\delta \phi} = \frac{\partial F}{\partial \phi} - \text{div}\!\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial \nabla \phi}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Variation of $E_{\text{image}}$.** $$\frac{\delta E_{\text{image}}}{\delta \phi} = \delta(\phi)\,\text{div}\!\left(g(I)\,\frac{\nabla \phi}{\vert \nabla \phi \vert}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Variation of $E_{\text{length}}$.** $$\frac{\delta E_{\text{length}}}{\delta \phi} = \mu\,\delta(\phi)\,\text{div}\!\left(\frac{\nabla \phi}{\vert \nabla \phi \vert}\right) = \mu\,\delta(\phi)\,\kappa$$ where $\kappa = \text{div}\!\left(\dfrac{\nabla \phi}{\vert \nabla \phi \vert}\right)$ is the signed curvature. **Step 4 — Variation of $E_{\text{shape}}$.** Differentiating $\bigl(H(\phi) - H(\phi_{0})\bigr)^{2}$ w.r.t. $\phi$, noting that $\phi_{0}$ is a constant (fixed prior): $$\frac{\delta E_{\text{shape}}}{\delta \phi} = 2\,\lambda_{2}\,\delta(\phi)\,\bigl(H(\phi) - H(\phi_{0})\bigr)$$ This is a **scalar volume-mismatch force** — it pushes the evolving region either to grow or shrink depending on whether the current foreground area is less or more than the prior's. **Step 5 — Assemble the gradient flow.** Setting $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\delta E}{\delta \phi}$: $$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = \delta(\phi)\left[\,\mu\,\kappa + g(I)\,\kappa + \nabla g \cdot \frac{\nabla \phi}{\vert \nabla \phi \vert} \;-\; 2\,\lambda_{2}\,\bigl(H(\phi) - H(\phi_{0})\bigr)\,\right]\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 14-Mark Favourite:** Board examiners love asking students to identify the **physical meaning of each term**. Memorise: > 1. $\mu\,\kappa$ — length/curvature regularisation (smoothing). > 2. $g(I)\,\kappa$ — edge-attraction (image force). > 3. $\nabla g \cdot \hat{n}$ — boundary attraction (gradient of edge map). > 4. $2\lambda_{2}\bigl(H(\phi)-H(\phi_{0})\bigr)$ — **shape force** that fills missing regions or erodes spurious ones. ### 3.2 Numerical Discretisation (Explicit Scheme) Let $\phi_{i,j}^{n}$ denote the LSF at grid point $(i,j)$ and iteration $n$. The explicit Euler update is: $$\phi_{i,j}^{n+1} = \phi_{i,j}^{n} + \Delta t \cdot F_{i,j}^{n}$$ where $$F_{i,j}^{n} = \delta_{\varepsilon}(\phi_{i,j}^{n}) \left[\,\mu\,\kappa_{i,j}^{n} + g(I_{i,j})\,\kappa_{i,j}^{n} + \nabla g_{i,j} \cdot \frac{\nabla \phi_{i,j}^{n}}{\vert \nabla \phi_{i,j}^{n} \vert} - 2\lambda_{2}\bigl(H_{\varepsilon}(\phi_{i,j}^{n}) - H_{\varepsilon}(\phi_{0;i,j})\bigr) \right]$$ **Curvature discretisation** (central differences): $$\kappa_{i,j} = \frac{\phi_{xx}\,\phi_{y}^{2} - 2\phi_{x}\phi_{y}\phi_{xy} + \phi_{yy}\,\phi_{x}^{2}}{(\phi_{x}^{2} + \phi_{y}^{2})^{3/2}}$$ **Re-initialisation** to keep $\phi$ as a signed-distance function: $$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial \tau} = \text{sign}(\phi^{0})\left(1 - \vert \nabla \phi \vert\right)$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Toy 1-D Verification) Consider a 1-D LSF on $[0,1]$ with the prior $\phi_{0}(x) = x - 0.5$ (a half-plane at $x=0.5$). Suppose $H(\phi)$ is evaluated at $x=0.45$ where $\phi = -0.05$ and $\phi_{0} = -0.05$ — fully aligned. The shape force becomes $2\lambda_{2}(H(\phi)-H(\phi_{0})) = 0$, i.e., the prior has no corrective effect — which is the desired behaviour when the contour already matches the prior. Now suppose the contour has drifted to $x=0.6$ ($\phi = +0.1$, $\phi_{0} = +0.1$): again zero force. The shape term only acts when the **region indicator** $H(\phi)$ disagrees with $H(\phi_{0})$, i.e., at points where the contour is misplaced relative to the prior's interior/exterior labelling. ### 3.4 Algorithmic Pseudocode (Algorithm Box) | **Step** | **Operation** | |---|---| | 1 | Initialise $\phi^{0}$ (e.g., signed distance from a user-drawn circle) | | 2 | Compute edge map $g(I)$ once | | 3 | Align prior $\phi_{0}$ to image (affine or non-rigid registration) | | 4 | **Repeat until convergence:** | | 4a | Compute $\kappa$, $\nabla g$, $H(\phi)$, $H(\phi_{0})$ | | 4b | Evaluate $F(\phi)$ using boxed PDE | | 4c | Update $\phi^{n+1} = \phi^{n} + \Delta t \cdot F$ | | 4d | Re-initialise $\phi$ every 20–50 iterations | | 5 | Extract contour as $C = \{ \mathbf{x} : \phi(\mathbf{x}) = 0 \}$ | > [!NOTE] > Although this is an image-processing topic, the engineering rigour demands a *convergence criterion* — typically $\dfrac{\Vert \phi^{n+1} - \phi^{n} \Vert_{2}}{\Vert \phi^{0} \Vert_{2}} < 10^{-4}$ or a fixed iteration budget (e.g., 500 iterations). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Combined Shape-Prior Level Set Segmentation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Medical Image I] --> B[Preprocessing] B --> B1[Gaussian Smoothing] B --> B2[Intensity Normalisation] B1 --> C[Edge Stopping Function gI] B2 --> C C --> D[Curvature and Gradient Compute] D --> E[Image Force Fimg] F[Shape Library Training Set] --> G[PCA Shape Model or Single Template] G --> H[Affine Registration to Image] H --> I[Reference SDF phi0] I --> J[Shape Force Fshape = 2 lambda Hphi - Hphi0] E --> K[Combine Energy Functional] J --> K K --> L[Level Set PDE Solver] L --> M[Re-initialisation every K iterations] M --> L L --> N{Convergence Check} N -->|No| L N -->|Yes| O[Extract Zero Level Set Contour] O --> P[Final Segmentation Mask] ``` ### 4.2 Conceptual Flow — How the Two Energies Co-Operate ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DATA[Data Driven Branch] D1[Image Gradients] --> D2[Edge Stopping gI] D2 --> D3[Image Energy Eimage] end subgraph KNOW[Knowledge Driven Branch] K1[Training Shapes] --> K2[Shape Prior phi0] K2 --> K3[Shape Energy Eshape] end D3 --> SUM[Weighted Sum: lambda1 Eimage plus lambda2 Eshape] K3 --> SUM SUM --> EVO[Gradient Descent on phi] EVO --> CON[Converged Contour C] ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree — When Does the Shape Prior Take Over? ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Is the local gradient strong enough?} Q1 -->|Yes| A1[Image force dominates
Contour follows edges] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Does the region agree with the prior?} Q2 -->|Yes| A2[Contour stays put
Shape force is zero] Q2 -->|No| A3[Shape force fills or erodes
Contour moves toward prior] ``` > [!NOTE] > All diagrams use **Mermaid syntax** for universal rendering in GitHub, VS Code, and KTU's recommended Markdown viewers. Node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B1`, `SUM`) to comply with Mermaid's parser safeguards. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why is a shape prior often combined with level set segmentation in medical image analysis?* **Model Answer:** Level set segmentation relies on local image features (edges, intensities) to drive contour evolution. In medical images, boundaries may be **weak, missing, corrupted by noise, or obscured by pathology**, causing the contour to leak or stall. A **shape prior** encodes a-priori geometric knowledge (size, topology, orientation) of the target organ, obtained from training data. Combining the two yields a **hybrid energy** $E = \lambda_{1}E_{\text{image}} + \lambda_{2}E_{\text{shape}}$ that is robust to local image ambiguities while preserving anatomical plausibility. *(3 marks — 1 mark for the limitation of pure level sets, 1 mark for the role of the prior, 1 mark for the combined formulation.)* **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is the role of the Heaviside function $H(\phi)$ in the shape-energy term $E_{\text{shape}} = \int \bigl(H(\phi) - H(\phi_{0})\bigr)^{2} dx$?* **Model Answer:** $H(\phi)$ acts as a **region indicator** of the evolving contour's interior ($\phi > 0$ ⇒ $H=1$; $\phi < 0$ ⇒ $H=0$). It is used because **comparison of region occupancies** (rather than raw signed distance values) makes the shape term invariant to small translational or scaling differences between the current contour and the prior. The squared difference $\bigl(H(\phi)-H(\phi_{0})\bigr)^{2}$ measures the **area of mismatch** between the two regions. *(3 marks — 1 mark for indicator role, 1 mark for region-comparison logic, 1 mark for the invariance property.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, each Part-B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must attempt **one full question** out of the pair. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` **(a)** *Derive the gradient-flow PDE for a level set segmentation that combines (i) geodesic-active-contour image energy, (ii) length regularisation, and (iii) a region-based shape prior energy.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The total energy functional is: $$E(\phi) = \int_{\Omega} g(I)\,\delta(\phi)\,\vert \nabla \phi \vert \, d\mathbf{x} \;+\; \mu \int_{\Omega} \delta(\phi)\,\vert \nabla \phi \vert \, d\mathbf{x} \;+\; \lambda_{2}\int_{\Omega}\bigl(H(\phi)-H(\phi_{0})\bigr)^{2}d\mathbf{x}$$ **Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply calculus of variations to the first two terms. The Euler–Lagrange first variation gives: $$\frac{\delta}{\delta \phi}\int \delta(\phi)\,\vert \nabla \phi \vert \, d\mathbf{x} = \delta(\phi)\,\text{div}\!\left(\frac{\nabla \phi}{\vert \nabla \phi \vert}\right) = \delta(\phi)\,\kappa$$ Thus the combined image + length contribution is $\delta(\phi)\bigl[g(I)\,\kappa + \mu\,\kappa\bigr]$. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the variation of the shape term. Using $\dfrac{\partial H}{\partial \phi} = \delta(\phi)$: $$\frac{\delta E_{\text{shape}}}{\delta \phi} = 2\lambda_{2}\bigl(H(\phi)-H(\phi_{0})\bigr)\,\delta(\phi)$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Write the gradient descent equation $\partial_{t}\phi = -\delta E / \delta\phi$ and add the boundary-attraction term from $\nabla g$: $$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = \delta(\phi)\Bigl[\bigl(\mu + g(I)\bigr)\kappa + \nabla g\cdot\frac{\nabla \phi}{\vert \nabla \phi \vert} - 2\lambda_{2}\bigl(H(\phi)-H(\phi_{0})\bigr)\Bigr]$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclude by stating that this is the **final evolution PDE** whose steady-state zero level set is the desired segmentation. **(b)** *Explain how the parameters $\mu$, $\lambda_{2}$, and $\Delta t$ affect the segmentation outcome, and discuss the CFL stability condition.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Length regularisation weight $\mu$ (2 marks):** A larger $\mu$ penalises the contour length more strongly, producing **smoother, less wiggly boundaries** but may also cause the contour to under-fit fine image structures. A small $\mu$ preserves detail but is sensitive to noise. 2. **Shape weight $\lambda_{2}$ (2 marks):** Controls the trade-off between image evidence and prior knowledge. A **large $\lambda_{2}$** forces the contour to mimic the prior — robust to missing edges but may **suppress genuine pathology** (false negatives). A **small $\lambda_{2}$** allows free deformation, defeating the purpose of the prior. Typical range $0.1 \le \lambda_{2} \le 0.5$. 3. **Time step $\Delta t$ and CFL (3 marks):** For the explicit scheme to be stable, the numerical domain of dependence must contain the physical one. The **Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy (CFL) condition** is: $$\Delta t \cdot \max_{\mathbf{x}} \vert F(\mathbf{x}) \vert \le \Delta x$$ where $F$ is the speed function in the PDE and $\Delta x$ is the grid spacing. Violating CFL leads to numerical blow-up (the LSF develops spikes and the algorithm diverges). In practice $\Delta t \approx 0.5$ when $\Delta x = 1$ and the speed is of order unity. Re-initialisation of $\phi$ to a signed distance function is also essential every 20–50 iterations to maintain $\vert \nabla \phi \vert \approx 1$ and hence numerical stability. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a)** *With neat expressions, distinguish between the edge-based and region-based shape-prior energies used in level set segmentation. Give one example formulation for each.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | **Criterion** | **Edge-Based Shape Energy** | **Region-Based Shape Energy** | |---|---|---| | What is compared | Signed-distance fields (geometry) | Region indicator functions (occupancy) | | Sensitivity | Sensitive to small geometric shifts | Invariant to small shifts/scalings | | Computational cost | Higher (pointwise SDF comparison) | Lower (binary masks) | | Example | $E_{\text{shape}}^{\text{edge}} = \int (\phi - \phi_{0})^{2} d\mathbf{x}$ | $E_{\text{shape}}^{\text{region}} = \int \bigl(H(\phi) - H(\phi_{0})\bigr)^{2} d\mathbf{x}$ | | Best suited to | Detailed anatomical structures (e.g., cortical folds) | Coarse organs with stable topology (e.g., liver, prostate) | **Mark split:** 3 marks for distinguishing criteria, 2 marks for the two example formulas, 2 marks for the suitability discussion. **(b)** *Describe a complete algorithmic pipeline that combines a PCA-based statistical shape model with a level set segmenter. Mention the role of registration and the re-initialisation step.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** 1. **Training phase (2 marks):** Collect $N$ binary masks of the target organ from expert-annotated datasets. Convert each to a signed distance function $\phi_{k}$. Apply **Procrustes alignment** to remove pose variation. Build a statistical shape model $\phi_{0} = \bar{\phi} + \sum_{i=1}^{M} b_{i}\,\mathbf{v}_{i}$ where $\bar{\phi}$ is the mean shape and $\mathbf{v}_{i}$ are the top-$M$ eigenvectors of the covariance matrix. The coefficients $b_{i}$ parameterise shape variability. 2. **Initialisation & Registration (2 marks):** Place a rough initial contour (e.g., user-clicked bounding circle). Align the mean shape $\bar{\phi}$ to the current image via **affine registration** (translation, rotation, scale) to produce $\phi_{0}$ used in the shape energy. 3. **Evolution (2 marks):** Iterate the combined gradient-flow PDE; at each step the shape force pulls the contour toward the projected $\phi_{0}$. The PCA coefficients $b_{i}$ can be re-estimated to allow anatomically plausible deformations. 4. **Re-initialisation (1 mark):** Every 20–50 iterations, solve $\partial_{\tau}\phi = \text{sign}(\phi^{0})(1 - \vert \nabla \phi \vert)$ to restore $\phi$ to (approximate) signed-distance form, preserving numerical stability and CFL compliance. Conclude with contour extraction at the zero level set. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the Dirac delta $\delta(\phi)$** when writing the evolution PDE — it is essential because forces act *only on the contour*, not throughout the domain. (–1 to –2 marks) > 2. **Confusing the Heaviside $H(\phi)$ with the Dirac delta $\delta(\phi)$** in the shape term — $H$ is the indicator, $\delta$ is its derivative. (–1 mark) > 3. **Omitting the curvature term** $\mu\,\kappa$ — the examiner cannot award full credit if your PDE allows the contour to fragment or develop sharp corners without penalisation. (–1 mark) > 4. **Not mentioning the CFL condition** in any stability-related sub-question — this is a favourite 1–2 mark follow-up. (–1 mark) > 5. **Using unsmoothed $\delta$ and $H$** in implementation discussions — always write $\delta_{\varepsilon}$ and $H_{\varepsilon}$ with $\varepsilon \approx 1.5 \Delta x$. (–½ mark) > 6. **Writing $\vert x \vert$ using the vertical bar `|` inside markdown tables** — this breaks the table; always use `\vert x \vert` in your LaTeX. (formatting penalty) --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Level set representation:** $C(t) = \{ \mathbf{x} : \phi(\mathbf{x},t) = 0 \}$; $\phi$ is a signed distance function. - **Why combine shape priors:** Robust to weak edges, noise, occlusions, and intensity inhomogeneity. - **Combined energy:** $E = E_{\text{image}} + \mu E_{\text{length}} + \lambda_{2} E_{\text{shape}}$. - **Region-based shape energy:** $E_{\text{shape}} = \int \bigl(H(\phi) - H(\phi_{0})\bigr)^{2} dx$ — area mismatch. - **Edge-based shape energy:** $E_{\text{shape}} = \int (\phi - \phi_{0})^{2} dx$ — geometric mismatch. - **Evolution PDE:** $\partial_{t}\phi = \delta(\phi)\bigl[(\mu + g)\kappa + \nabla g \cdot \hat{n} - 2\lambda_{2}(H(\phi) - H(\phi_{0}))\bigr]$. - **Each term's meaning:** $\mu\kappa$ smooths; $g\kappa$ attracts to edges; $\nabla g \cdot \hat{n}$ snaps; shape term fills/erodes. - **Heaviside regularised:** $H_{\varepsilon}(\phi) = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + \tfrac{2}{\pi}\arctan(\phi/\varepsilon)\right]$. - **Dirac regularised:** $\delta_{\varepsilon}(\phi) = \tfrac{1}{\pi}\,\dfrac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon^{2} + \phi^{2}}$. - **CFL condition:** $\Delta t \cdot \max \vert F \vert \le \Delta x$. - **Re-initialisation frequency:** every 20–50 iterations to a signed-distance function. - **Shape weight:** $\lambda_{2} \in [0.1,\, 0.5]$; **length weight:** $\mu \in [0.01,\, 0.2]$. - **PCA shape models:** Mean $\bar{\phi}$ + linear combination of eigenvectors $\mathbf{v}_{i}$ with coefficients $b_{i}$. - **Registration is mandatory** before applying the prior — unaligned priors degrade the shape force. - **Topology freedom:** Level sets handle splitting/merging of contours naturally — a key advantage over parametric snakes. - **Always write $\delta_{\varepsilon}$, $H_{\varepsilon}$ in formal answers** — never the un-smoothed versions. - **Examiner tip:** A 14-mark derivation question always wants (i) the variational derivative, (ii) the gradient flow, and (iii) a physical interpretation of each term. Missing any one of the three caps you at ~10/14.

topics.title: Solutions Based on Graph Cuts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solutions Based on Graph Cuts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Graph Cuts

**Graph Cuts** constitute a combinatorial optimization technique used extensively in computer vision and medical image processing for solving energy minimization problems. Formally, a graph cut represents the partition of a graph $\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$ into two disjoint subsets $\mathcal{S}$ and $\mathcal{T}$ such that the source node $s \in \mathcal{S}$ and the sink node $t \in \mathcal{T}$, achieved by severing a set of edges whose total cost (capacity) is minimum.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, graph cuts are positioned as energy-based segmentation frameworks where the objective is to find an optimal binary labeling $L: \mathcal{P} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$ that minimizes a global energy functional $E(L)$ defined over the image pixels $\mathcal{P}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Graph cuts provide a globally optimal solution to binary segmentation problems under certain energy function constraints, making them invaluable in delineating anatomical structures, tumors, lesions, and organs from surrounding tissues in modalities like CT, MRI, and Ultrasound.

## 1.2 The Graph Cut Energy Function

The total energy to be minimized is given by:

$$E(L) = \sum_{p \in \mathcal{P}} D_p(L_p) + \lambda \sum_{\{p,q\} \in \mathcal{N}} V_{pq}(L_p, L_q) \cdot \delta(L_p \neq L_q)$$

Where:
- $D_p(L_p)$ — **data term** measuring the penalty of assigning label $L_p$ to pixel $p$
- $V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)$ — **smoothness term** penalizing label discontinuity between neighboring pixels $p$ and $q$
- $\lambda \geq 0$ — weighting coefficient controlling the relative influence of smoothness
- $\mathcal{N}$ — set of all neighboring pixel pairs (typically 4 or 8 connected)
- $\delta(L_p \neq L_q)$ — indicator function returning 1 when labels differ, 0 otherwise

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Flood and Dam Analogy**
>
> Imagine a hilly landscape with two large reservoirs — one filled with blue water (source $s$) representing the "object" region, and another filled with red water (sink $t$) representing the "background" region. The terrain between them contains valleys and ridges. When the dams release water, the water flows downhill seeking the easiest path. The "Graph Cut" is essentially the **barrier you would build along the highest ridges** that completely separates the two water bodies, costing the least amount of material.
>
> In image segmentation:
> - The **landscape** is the image with pixel intensities as elevation
> - The **blue water** flows through pixels likely to be the object
> - The **red water** flows through pixels likely to be background
> - The **minimum cut** = the optimal boundary between object and background

## 1.4 Physical & Mathematical Constants in Graph Cuts

The following standard parameters and metrics govern graph cut operations:

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------|
| $\lambda$ (smoothness weight) | $\lambda \in [0.1, 10.0]$ | Controls boundary smoothness |
| Neighborhood size $\mathcal{N}$ | 4 or 8 connected | Pixel adjacency definition |
| Source capacity $C_s$ | $[1, 1000]$ | Penalty for object labeling |
| Sink capacity $C_t$ | $[1, 1000]$ | Penalty for background labeling |
| Number of nodes $\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ | $= N$ (number of pixels) | Computational complexity driver |

> [!TIP]
> The graph cut framework guarantees **global optimality** for binary segmentation under submodular energy conditions, a property rarely found in other segmentation techniques.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem

The cornerstone of graph cut optimization is the **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem** by Ford and Fulkerson (1956), which establishes a duality between two seemingly different optimization problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ford-Fulkerson Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem:** The maximum amount of flow that can be sent from source $s$ to sink $t$ in a capacitated graph equals the minimum total capacity of all edges that, when removed, disconnect $s$ from $t$.

Mathematically, the theorem states:

$$\max_{f} \sum_{v \in \mathcal{V}} f(s, v) = \min_{(S,T)} \sum_{\substack{u \in \mathcal{S}, v \in \mathcal{T} \\ (u,v) \in \mathcal{E}}} c(u, v)$$

Where $f(s, v)$ represents the flow on edge $(s, v)$ and $c(u, v)$ is the capacity of edge $(u, v)$.

## 2.2 Decomposition of the Optimization Process

The graph cut segmentation procedure is executed through the following structured logic steps:

### Step 1: Graph Construction
- Create a node for each pixel $p \in \mathcal{P}$
- Add two terminal nodes: source $s$ (object) and sink $t$ (background)
- Connect $s$ to each pixel via $s$-links with capacity $D_p(1)$ (object penalty)
- Connect each pixel to $t$ via $t$-links with capacity $D_p(0)$ (background penalty)
- Connect neighboring pixels $(p, q)$ via $n$-links with capacity $\lambda \cdot V_{pq}$

### Step 2: Capacity Assignment
The edge capacities are derived from the energy components:
- $s$-link to $p$: $c(s, p) = D_p(\text{obj})$
- $t$-link from $p$: $c(p, t) = D_p(\text{bkg})$
- $n$-link between $p$ and $q$: $c(p, q) = \lambda \cdot V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)$

### Step 3: Max-Flow Computation
Apply augmenting path algorithms (Ford-Fulkerson, Edmonds-Karp, Boykov-Kolmogorov) to find maximum flow from $s$ to $t$.

### Step 4: Min-Cut Extraction
Identify the saturated edges (where flow equals capacity) and cut them. The pixels reachable from $s$ form the object region, while those reachable from $t$ form the background.

### Step 5: Label Assignment
For each pixel $p$:
$$L_p = \begin{cases} 1 \text{ (object)} & \text{if } p \text{ is reachable from } s \\ 0 \text{ (background)} & \text{if } p \text{ is reachable from } t \end{cases}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Expression | Description | Application Context |
|----------|------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Energy Function** | $E(L) = \sum_{p} D_p(L_p) + \lambda \sum_{\{p,q\}} V_{pq} \delta(L_p \neq L_q)$ | Total energy to minimize | All graph cut segmentation |
| **Data Term (Negative Log Likelihood)** | $D_p(L_p) = -\ln P(I_p \mid L_p)$ | Pixel-wise likelihood penalty | Statistical segmentation |
| **Potts Model Smoothness** | $V_{pq} = \exp\left(-\frac{(I_p - I_q)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\text{dist}(p,q)}$ | Intensity-based smoothness | Boundary preservation |
| **Max-Flow Min-Cut** | $\max f = \min C(S,T)$ | Ford-Fulkerson duality | Cut equivalence proof |
| **Flow Conservation** | $\sum_{u} f(u, v) = \sum_{w} f(v, w) \quad \forall v \neq s, t$ | Kirchhoff's law for flows | Network flow algorithms |
| **Residual Capacity** | $r_f(u,v) = c(u,v) - f(u,v) + f(v,u)$ | Available capacity for augmentation | Augmenting path computation |
| **Boykov-Kolmogorov Augment** | $\mathcal{P} = \text{FindPath}(s, t, G_f)$ | Augmenting path search | Modern max-flow solver |
| **Cut Capacity** | $C(S,T) = \sum_{u \in \mathcal{S}, v \in \mathcal{T}} c(u,v)$ | Total cut cost | Min-cut objective |
| **Saturated Edge Condition** | $f(u,v) = c(u,v) \implies \text{edge in cut}$ | Cut identification | Final segmentation |
| **Submodularity Constraint** | $V_{pq}(0,0) + V_{pq}(1,1) \leq V_{pq}(0,1) + V_{pq}(1,0)$ | Energy regularizability | Graph cut applicability |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The energy function $E(L)$ must satisfy the **submodularity condition** for graph cut optimization to be applicable. Violating this condition makes the problem NP-hard and the min-cut approach fails!

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Medical Imaging

Graph cuts find extensive applications in:

1. **Tumor Segmentation**: Delineating glioma, meningioma, and metastatic lesions from MRI and CT volumes
2. **Organ Extraction**: Segmenting liver, kidney, lungs, and heart from abdominal/chest scans
3. **Vessel Tracking**: Extracting vascular structures in MR angiography (MRA) and CT angiography (CTA)
4. **Atrophy Measurement**: Quantifying brain tissue loss in neurodegenerative diseases
5. **Image Restoration**: Denoising, inpainting, and deblurring of medical images
6. **Multi-modal Registration**: Aligning CT-MRI, PET-MRI pairs through graph-based cost functions

> [!NOTE]
> The **Boykov-Kolmogorov (BK) algorithm** is the most widely used max-flow implementation in medical image analysis due to its polynomial time complexity and superior performance on vision graphs with sparse structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of Energy Minimization and Min-Cut

The fundamental theoretical link between the energy functional and the min-cut problem is established below.

### Starting Point
Consider the binary labeling energy:

$$E(L) = \sum_{p \in \mathcal{P}} D_p(L_p) + \lambda \sum_{\{p,q\} \in \mathcal{N}} V_{pq}(L_p, L_q) \cdot \delta(L_p \neq L_q)$$

### Step 1: Decompose the Energy into S and T Components
A cut $(S, T)$ partitions the graph such that $s \in S$ and $t \in T$. The labeling $L$ induced by this cut assigns:
- $L_p = 1$ (object) if $p \in S$
- $L_p = 0$ (background) if $p \in T$

### Step 2: Express Data Term via Cut
The data term contribution of pixel $p$ can be written as:

$$D_p(L_p) = \begin{cases} D_p(1) & \text{if } p \in S \text{ (object)} \\ D_p(0) & \text{if } p \in T \text{ (background)} \end{cases}$$

The cut cost contributed by the $s$-link and $t$-link of pixel $p$ is:
- $c(s, p) = D_p(1)$ (penalty of putting $p$ in object)
- $c(p, t) = D_p(0)$ (penalty of putting $p$ in background)

### Step 3: Identify Which Link is Cut
For pixel $p$:
- If $p \in S$: the $s$-link is internal to $S$ (not cut), the $t$-link crosses the cut (cut)
- If $p \in T$: the $s$-link crosses the cut, the $t$-link is internal to $T$ (not cut)

Therefore, the data term contribution to the cut cost is:

$$D_p^{\text{cut}} = \begin{cases} D_p(0) & \text{if } p \in S \\ D_p(1) & \text{if } p \in T \end{cases}$$

### Step 4: Express Smoothness Term via Cut
For an $n$-link between $p$ and $q$ with capacity $\lambda \cdot V_{pq}$:
- The link is cut only when $p$ and $q$ are on different sides, i.e., $L_p \neq L_q$
- This matches the indicator function $\delta(L_p \neq L_q)$ exactly

### Step 5: Total Cut Capacity Equals Energy
Summing all contributions, the capacity of cut $(S, T)$ is:

$$C(S, T) = \sum_{p \in S} D_p(0) + \sum_{p \in T} D_p(1) + \sum_{\substack{\{p,q\} \\ p \in S, q \in T}} \lambda \cdot V_{pq}$$

By algebraic rearrangement (subtracting the constant $\sum_p D_p(0)$ from both sides):

$$E(L) = C(S, T) + \text{constant}$$

### Step 6: Equivalence Conclusion
Since the constant does not affect the minimization, we conclude:

$$\boxed{\arg\min_{L} E(L) \equiv \arg\min_{(S,T)} C(S, T)}$$

This establishes the **bijection** between energy minimization and minimum s-t cut computation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Augmenting Path and Residual Network

The Ford-Fulkerson method relies on iteratively finding augmenting paths in the residual network.

### Step 1: Define Residual Capacity
For an edge $(u, v)$ with capacity $c(u, v)$ and current flow $f(u, v)$:

$$r_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v)$$

For the reverse edge $(v, u)$:

$$r_f(v, u) = f(u, v)$$

### Step 2: Bottleneck Identification
Given an augmenting path $\mathcal{P}: s \to v_1 \to v_2 \to \cdots \to t$, the bottleneck capacity is:

$$\Delta f = \min_{(u, v) \in \mathcal{P}} r_f(u, v)$$

### Step 3: Flow Augmentation
Update the flow along path $\mathcal{P}$:

$$f'(u, v) = f(u, v) + \Delta f \quad \forall (u, v) \in \mathcal{P}_{forward}$$

$$f'(v, u) = f(v, u) - \Delta f \quad \forall (v, u) \in \mathcal{P}_{reverse}$$

### Step 4: Termination Condition
The algorithm terminates when no augmenting path exists from $s$ to $t$ in the residual network. At this point, by the max-flow min-cut theorem:

$$f^* = \max f = \min_{(S,T)} C(S, T) = C(S^*, T^*)$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

Consider a 3-pixel image with the following energy components:

| Pixel $p$ | $D_p(1)$ (object) | $D_p(0)$ (background) |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------|
| $p_1$ | 3 | 5 |
| $p_2$ | 1 | 4 |
| $p_3$ | 4 | 2 |

With $n$-link between $p_1$ and $p_2$: $V_{12} = 2$, and between $p_2$ and $p_3$: $V_{23} = 3$. Take $\lambda = 1$.

### Step 1: Enumerate All Possible Labelings
There are $2^3 = 8$ possible labelings. We compute $E(L)$ for each.

### Labeling 1: $L = (0, 0, 0)$ — All Background
$$E = (5 + 4 + 2) + 1 \cdot (0 + 0) = 11$$

### Labeling 2: $L = (1, 0, 0)$ — Only $p_1$ is Object
$$E = (3 + 4 + 2) + 1 \cdot (2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 0) = 9 + 2 = 11$$

### Labeling 3: $L = (0, 1, 0)$ — Only $p_2$ is Object
$$E = (5 + 1 + 2) + 1 \cdot (2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1) = 8 + 5 = 13$$

### Labeling 4: $L = (0, 0, 1)$ — Only $p_3$ is Object
$$E = (5 + 4 + 4) + 1 \cdot (2 \cdot 0 + 3 \cdot 1) = 13 + 3 = 16$$

### Labeling 5: $L = (1, 1, 0)$ — $p_1$ and $p_2$ are Object
$$E = (3 + 1 + 2) + 1 \cdot (2 \cdot 0 + 3 \cdot 1) = 6 + 3 = 9$$

### Labeling 6: $L = (1, 0, 1)$ — $p_1$ and $p_3$ are Object
$$E = (3 + 4 + 4) + 1 \cdot (2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 0) = 11 + 2 = 13$$

### Labeling 7: $L = (0, 1, 1)$ — $p_2$ and $p_3$ are Object
$$E = (5 + 1 + 4) + 1 \cdot (2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 0) = 10 + 2 = 12$$

### Labeling 8: $L = (1, 1, 1)$ — All Object
$$E = (3 + 1 + 4) + 1 \cdot (0 + 0) = 8$$

### Step 4: Identify Minimum Energy
$$\min E(L) = 8 \text{ at } L = (1, 1, 1)$$

### Step 5: Verify via Min-Cut
Computing all 8 possible s-t cuts and their capacities yields the same minimum value of 8, confirming the theoretical equivalence.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Graph Cut Algorithms

| Algorithm | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Best Use Case |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Ford-Fulkerson** | $O(E \cdot \max f)$ | $O(V + E)$ | Integer capacities, small graphs |
| **Edmonds-Karp** | $O(V \cdot E^2)$ | $O(V + E)$ | Generic graphs, BFS-based |
| **Dinic's Algorithm** | $O(V^2 \cdot E)$ | $O(V + E)$ | Dense graphs, blocking flows |
| **Push-Relabel** | $O(V^2 \cdot \sqrt{E})$ | $O(V)$ | Large networks |
| **Boykov-Kolmogorov** | $O(V^{1.5} \cdot E \cdot C)$ | $O(V)$ | Vision graphs (best for images) |

Where $V$ is the number of vertices, $E$ is the number of edges, $\max f$ is the maximum flow value, and $C$ is the cost of the min cut.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Graph Construction for a 2D Medical Image

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the step-by-step construction of an s-t graph for medical image segmentation using graph cuts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[Input Medical Image CT or MRI] --> preprocess[Preprocessing: Noise Reduction Intensity Normalization]
    preprocess --> roi[Define Region of Interest ROI]
    roi --> seedobj[User Seeds Object Source s]
    roi --> seedbkg[User Seeds Background Sink t]
    seedobj --> nodelink[Add s Links Object Penalty Dp 1]
    seedbkg --> nodelink2[Add t Links Background Penalty Dp 0]
    nodelink --> nlink[Add n Links Between Neighboring Pixels]
    nodelink2 --> nlink
    nlink --> graphready[S t Graph Construction Complete]
    graphready --> maxflow[Apply Max Flow Algorithm Boykov Kolmogorov]
    maxflow --> saturated[Identify Saturated Edges]
    saturated --> cutextract[Extract Minimum s t Cut]
    cutextract --> label[Assign Labels Object or Background]
    label --> boundary[Generate Segmentation Boundary]
    boundary --> output[Output Segmented Anatomical Structure]
```

## 4.2 s-t Graph Architecture for a 3×3 Image Patch

The following diagram depicts the graph topology for a small image patch, showing pixel nodes, terminal nodes, and the various link types.

```mermaid
graph TD
    srcNode[Source s Object Terminal]
    snkNode[Sink t Background Terminal]
    p1Node[Pixel p1]
    p2Node[Pixel p2]
    p3Node[Pixel p3]
    p4Node[Pixel p4]
    p5Node[Pixel p5]

    srcNode ---|s link cap Dp1 1| p1Node
    srcNode ---|s link cap Dp2 1| p2Node
    srcNode ---|s link cap Dp4 1| p4Node
    p1Node ---|t link cap Dp1 0| snkNode
    p2Node ---|t link cap Dp2 0| snkNode
    p3Node ---|t link cap Dp3 0| snkNode
    p4Node ---|t link cap Dp4 0| snkNode
    p5Node ---|t link cap Dp5 0| snkNode
    srcNode ---|s link cap Dp3 1| p3Node
    srcNode ---|s link cap Dp5 1| p5Node

    p1Node ---|n link cap lambda V12| p2Node
    p1Node ---|n link cap lambda V14| p4Node
    p2Node ---|n link cap lambda V23| p3Node
    p2Node ---|n link cap lambda V25| p5Node
    p3Node ---|n link cap lambda V35| p5Node
    p4Node ---|n link cap lambda V45| p5Node
```

## 4.3 Max-Flow Min-Cut Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initialize Graph G V E with Capacities] --> B[Set All Flows f u v to 0]
    B --> C[Build Residual Network Gf]
    C --> D{Augmenting Path Exists from s to t}
    D -->|Yes| E[Find Shortest Augmenting Path BFS]
    E --> F[Compute Bottleneck Capacity delta f]
    F --> G[Augment Flow Along Path]
    G --> C
    D -->|No| H[No More Augmenting Paths]
    H --> I[Reachable Set S from s in Residual]
    I --> J[Remaining Set T Contains t]
    J --> K[Cut Edges from S to T Identified]
    K --> L[Minimum s t Cut C S T Equals Max Flow]
    L --> M[Output Segmentation Labeling L]
```

## 4.4 Energy Component Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    energy[Total Energy E of L] --> data[Data Term Dp Lp]
    energy --> smooth[Smoothness Term Vpq Lp Lq]

    data --> objpen[Penalty for Object Assignment]
    data --> bkgpen[Penalty for Background Assignment]
    objpen --> neglog[Negative Log Likelihood of Intensity]
    bkgpen --> histmod[Histogram Based Probabilities]

    smooth --> potts[Potts Model Discontinuity Penalty]
    smooth --> gmm[Gaussian Mixture Model Likelihood]
    smooth --> weight[Lambda Weighting Parameter]

    potts --> sgraph[s Link and t Link Capacities]
    gmm --> sgraph
    neglog --> sgraph
    histmod --> sgraph
    weight --> ngraph[n Link Capacities]
```

## 4.5 Medical Image Segmentation Pipeline Using Graph Cuts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    img1[Acquire CT MRI PET Image] --> img2[Preprocess: Bias Correction Denoising]
    img2 --> img3[Intensity Histogram Analysis]
    img3 --> img4[Train Foreground Background GMM]
    img4 --> img5[User Interactive Seeding Hard Constraints]
    img5 --> img6[Construct S T Graph]
    img6 --> img7[Run Boykov Kolmogorov Max Flow]
    img7 --> img8[Extract Min Cut Segmentation]
    img8 --> img9[Postprocess: Hole Filling Smoothing]
    img9 --> img10[Output: 3D Volume Mask of ROI]
    img10 --> img11[Clinical Quantification Volume Statistics]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]

**Define graph cuts in the context of medical image segmentation. State the max-flow min-cut theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

Graph cuts refer to a class of energy minimization algorithms used for image segmentation where a weighted graph is constructed with image pixels as nodes and additional source $s$ and sink $t$ terminal nodes. The segmentation is achieved by finding a minimum cost cut that separates the graph into two disjoint subsets, corresponding to object and background regions.

The **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem** by Ford and Fulkerson states that the maximum value of flow that can be pushed from source $s$ to sink $t$ through a capacitated network is exactly equal to the minimum total capacity of all edges that need to be removed to disconnect $s$ from $t$.

Mathematically: $\max f = \min_{(S,T)} C(S,T)$

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]

**Explain the role of the data term $D_p(L_p)$ and the smoothness term $V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)$ in the graph cut energy function.**

**Model Answer:**

The **data term** $D_p(L_p)$ represents the cost of assigning a particular label $L_p$ (object or background) to pixel $p$, based on the observed image intensity. It is typically derived from a negative log-likelihood model: $D_p(L_p) = -\ln P(I_p \mid L_p)$. This term ensures that pixels with intensities characteristic of the object (e.g., tumor) are labeled accordingly.

The **smoothness term** $V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)$ penalizes the assignment of different labels to neighboring pixels $p$ and $q$. It enforces spatial coherence in the segmentation, preventing isolated misclassifications. A common choice is the Potts model: $V_{pq} = [L_p \neq L_q]$, which penalizes all label discontinuities equally.

The weighting parameter $\lambda$ controls the trade-off between fidelity to image data and boundary smoothness.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Energy Minimization Framework in Graph Cuts

**Derive the equivalence between the energy minimization problem and the minimum s-t cut problem in graph cut segmentation. Clearly state all assumptions and steps involved.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define the Energy Functional**
For a binary labeling $L: \mathcal{P} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$ of image pixels $\mathcal{P}$:
$$E(L) = \sum_{p \in \mathcal{P}} D_p(L_p) + \lambda \sum_{\{p,q\} \in \mathcal{N}} V_{pq}(L_p, L_q) \cdot \delta(L_p \neq L_q)$$

**Step 2: Construct the Corresponding Graph**
- Add a source $s$ and sink $t$ terminal
- For each pixel $p$, add an $s$-link with capacity $D_p(1)$ and a $t$-link with capacity $D_p(0)$
- For each neighboring pair $\{p, q\}$, add an $n$-link with capacity $\lambda \cdot V_{pq}$

**Step 3: Establish Cut-Labeling Correspondence**
A cut $(S, T)$ with $s \in S$, $t \in T$ induces labeling:
- $L_p = 1$ if $p \in S$
- $L_p = 0$ if $p \in T$

**Step 4: Compute Cut Capacity**
$$C(S, T) = \sum_{p \in S} D_p(0) + \sum_{p \in T} D_p(1) + \sum_{\substack{p \in S, q \in T \\ \{p,q\} \in \mathcal{N}}} \lambda \cdot V_{pq}$$

**Step 5: Show Equivalence**
Adding the constant $\sum_p D_p(0)$ to both $C(S,T)$ and $E(L)$:
$$C(S, T) + \text{const} = E(L)$$

Since constants don't affect minimization:
$$\arg\min_{L} E(L) = \arg\min_{(S,T)} C(S, T)$$

**Step 6: Application of Max-Flow Min-Cut**
By Ford-Fulkerson theorem, $\min C(S,T) = \max f$, which is computed in polynomial time.

**Conclusion:** Minimizing the energy is equivalent to finding the minimum s-t cut, solvable efficiently using max-flow algorithms.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Application in Medical Image Segmentation

**Discuss in detail the application of graph cuts in medical image segmentation, with specific examples and a note on clinical relevance.**

**Model Solution:**

Graph cuts have revolutionized medical image segmentation by providing globally optimal, computationally efficient solutions to complex delineation problems.

**Application 1: Brain Tumor Segmentation in MRI**
- Graph nodes represent voxel intensities from T1, T2, FLAIR, and post-contrast T1 MRI sequences
- Multi-modal data term fuses information from multiple MRI contrasts using Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs)
- The smoothness term preserves the smooth tumor boundaries while allowing sharp transitions at tissue interfaces
- Clinical utility: Volume measurement, treatment planning, response assessment in glioma patients

**Application 2: Liver and Lesion Segmentation in CT**
- 3D graph cuts extend naturally to volumetric CT data
- The data term incorporates Hounsfield Unit (HU) statistics for liver parenchyma vs. lesion
- Interactive seeding by radiologists provides hard constraints
- Clinical utility: Pre-operative planning, transplantation assessment, hepatocellular carcinoma quantification

**Application 3: Cardiac Segmentation in Cine MRI**
- Multi-phase graph cuts segment the left ventricle across the cardiac cycle
- Temporal smoothness terms enforce consistency between consecutive frames
- Clinical utility: Ejection fraction calculation, myocardial mass assessment

**Application 4: Vessel Segmentation in MRA/CTA**
- Tubular structures are extracted using directional smoothness terms
- Clinical utility: Aneurysm detection, stenosis grading, surgical planning

**Advantages over Traditional Methods:**
1. **Global optimality** under submodular energy
2. **Polynomial time complexity** (Boykov-Kolmogorov: $O(V^{1.5} \cdot E \cdot C)$)
3. **Interactive hard constraints** via user seeding
4. **Multi-modal fusion** capability
5. **3D native support** without slice-by-slice processing

**Limitations:**
1. Restricted to **binary segmentation** (extensions like $\alpha$-expansion and $\alpha\beta$-swap handle multi-label)
2. Requires **memory** proportional to graph size
3. May produce **shrinking bias** for thin structures

**Clinical Workflow Integration:**
The graph cut output (binary mask) is fed into downstream pipelines for radiotherapy planning (contour generation), CAD systems (lesion characterization), and quantitative biomarkers (RECIST measurements).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Construction of s-t Graph

**Describe in detail the construction of an s-t graph for binary image segmentation using the graph cut technique. Include the types of edges, their capacities, and the role of terminals.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define the Image Domain**
Let $\mathcal{P} = \{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_N\}$ denote the set of $N$ pixels in the image, and let $\mathcal{N}$ represent the set of neighboring pixel pairs (4 or 8 connected neighborhood).

**Step 2: Identify Terminal Nodes**
Two additional nodes are introduced:
- **Source node $s$**: Represents the "object" class (foreground)
- **Sink node $t$**: Represents the "background" class

**Step 3: Construct Pixel Nodes**
Each pixel $p_i$ becomes a node in the graph, resulting in total $|\mathcal{V}| = N + 2$ nodes.

**Step 4: Add s-links and t-links**
For every pixel $p$, two terminal edges are added:
- **s-link** from $s$ to $p$ with capacity $c(s, p) = D_p(\text{object}) = D_p(1)$
- **t-link** from $p$ to $t$ with capacity $c(p, t) = D_p(\text{background}) = D_p(0)$

These edges encode the **data term** of the energy function. A high $s$-link capacity means the pixel strongly prefers object assignment; a high $t$-link capacity means background assignment is preferred.

**Step 5: Add n-links**
For every neighboring pixel pair $\{p, q\} \in \mathcal{N}$:
- **n-link** between $p$ and $q$ with capacity $c(p, q) = \lambda \cdot V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)$

These edges encode the **smoothness term** and are typically undirected (or implemented as two directed edges with equal capacity).

**Step 6: Example Capacity Values**
For a tumor segmentation in MRI:
- $D_p(\text{tumor})$: low for hyperintense pixels, high for normal tissue
- $D_p(\text{normal})$: complementary values
- $V_{pq}$: Potts model or intensity-based penalty

**Step 7: Interactive Seeding (Hard Constraints)**
If a pixel is hard-seeded as object, set $c(p, t) = 0$ and $c(s, p) = \infty$.
If hard-seeded as background, set $c(s, p) = 0$ and $c(p, t) = \infty$.

**Step 8: Graph Properties**
The constructed graph is a **capacitated, directed (or undirected), two-terminal** graph suitable for s-t cut algorithms. Total edges: $2N + |\mathcal{N}|$.

**Conclusion:** A well-constructed s-t graph faithfully represents the segmentation problem as an energy minimization task, and its minimum cut directly yields the optimal binary segmentation.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Boykov-Kolmogorov Algorithm

**Explain the Boykov-Kolmogorov (BK) max-flow algorithm with its three main stages: growth, augmentation, and adoption.**

**Model Solution:**

The **Boykov-Kolmogorov algorithm** (2004) is the most widely used max-flow solver in computer vision and medical image analysis due to its efficiency on sparse graphs with non-uniform capacities.

**Overview:**
BK maintains two search trees — one rooted at $s$ (active set $S$) and one rooted at $t$ (active set $T$) — and grows them simultaneously to find augmenting paths.

**Stage 1: Growth Stage**
- Start with both trees containing only their roots ($s \in S$, $t \in T$)
- Repeatedly select an "active" node from either tree
- Explore its neighbors using **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** within the residual graph
- A neighbor $v$ is added to the same tree as the active node if:
  - There is residual capacity from the active node to $v$
  - $v$ is not yet in either tree
- If a free node is reached, it becomes active; if it belongs to the opposite tree, a path is found

**Stage 2: Augmentation Stage**
- An augmenting path $\mathcal{P}$ connecting $s$ to $t$ has been found
- The **bottleneck capacity** $\Delta f = \min_{(u,v) \in \mathcal{P}} r_f(u, v)$ is computed
- Flow is augmented along $\mathcal{P}$, saturating at least one edge
- Saturated edges become orphans (no longer reachable from their parent tree)

**Stage 3: Adoption Stage**
- Process all orphaned nodes
- Try to "adopt" each orphan to an active parent in the same tree
- If no parent exists, the orphan becomes a **free node** (removed from both trees)
- This stage maintains tree integrity and prevents infinite loops

**Termination:**
The algorithm terminates when $s$ and $t$ become disconnected in the residual network. At this point, the set $S$ (nodes reachable from $s$) defines one side of the min cut, and $T$ (remaining nodes containing $t$) defines the other side.

**Complexity:**
The BK algorithm runs in $O(V^{1.5} \cdot E \cdot C)$ time on vision graphs, where $C$ is the min-cut capacity. This is significantly faster than generic max-flow on typical image segmentation problems.

**Advantages for Medical Imaging:**
1. Excellent performance on **sparse, grid-like graphs** typical of images
2. **Reuses search trees** across iterations for efficiency
3. **Polynomial guarantees** with practical near-linear runtime
4. **Parallel implementations** available for GPU acceleration
5. Memory-efficient with $O(V)$ space requirement

**Application Example:** In a 3D MRI volume with $256 \times 256 \times 200$ voxels, BK can segment a tumor in seconds, making it suitable for clinical workflows.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Graph Cut Questions (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the submodularity condition**: Students often state that graph cuts minimize any energy function. The examiner expects the explicit statement that **$V_{pq}(0,0) + V_{pq}(1,1) \leq V_{pq}(0,1) + V_{pq}(1,0)$ must hold**. Without this, the problem is NP-hard.
>
> 2. **Confusing s-links with n-links**: Many students interchange these terms. Remember: **s-links and t-links connect pixels to terminals**; **n-links connect pixels to each other**.
>
> 3. **Skipping the constant in energy-cut equivalence**: The derivation requires showing $C(S,T) = E(L) - \text{constant}$, and that the constant does not affect minimization. Omitting this loses 2-3 marks.
>
> 4. **Omitting the data term interpretation**: When discussing $D_p(L_p)$, always mention it represents **negative log-likelihood** based on intensity histograms or GMMs.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the indicator function** $\delta(L_p \neq L_q)$ in the smoothness term — losing the connection to graph cut capacity.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning the Ford-Fulkerson attribution** for the max-flow min-cut theorem — examiners allocate marks for historical accuracy.
>
> 7. **Missing clinical relevance**: Application questions require **specific medical examples** (tumor, organ, vessel), not generic statements.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Graph Cuts**

### Core Concepts
- [x] Graph cut = minimum s-t cut in a capacitated graph
- [x] Energy function: $E(L) = \sum D_p(L_p) + \lambda \sum V_{pq} \delta(L_p \neq L_q)$
- [x] Max-flow min-cut theorem: $\max f = \min C(S,T)$ (Ford-Fulkerson)
- [x] Three edge types: $s$-links, $t$-links, $n$-links
- [x] Submodularity required for polynomial solvability
- [x] Global optimality guarantee for binary segmentation
- [x] Boykov-Kolmogorov algorithm: growth → augmentation → adoption

### Key Parameters
- [x] $\lambda$ — smoothness weight (typically 0.1 to 10)
- [x] Neighborhood $\mathcal{N}$ — 4 or 8 connected
- [x] $D_p(L_p)$ — negative log-likelihood
- [x] $V_{pq}$ — Potts model or exponential decay
- [x] Hard seeds — infinite capacity on the undesired link

### Medical Imaging Applications
- [x] Brain tumor segmentation (multi-modal MRI)
- [x] Liver/lesion segmentation (CT)
- [x] Cardiac segmentation (cine MRI)
- [x] Vessel extraction (MRA/CTA)
- [x] Atrophy quantification (neurodegeneration)
- [x] Multi-modal registration via graph cost

### Equations to Memorize
- [x] $E(L) = \sum_p D_p(L_p) + \lambda \sum_{\{p,q\}} V_{pq} \delta(L_p \neq L_q)$
- [x] $\max f = \min C(S,T)$
- [x] $r_f(u,v) = c(u,v) - f(u,v) + f(v,u)$
- [x] $\Delta f = \min_{(u,v) \in \mathcal{P}} r_f(u,v)$
- [x] $V_{pq}(0,0) + V_{pq}(1,1) \leq V_{pq}(0,1) + V_{pq}(1,0)$ (submodularity)

### Algorithms
- [x] Ford-Fulkerson — basic augmenting paths
- [x] Edmonds-Karp — BFS-based, $O(VE^2)$
- [x] Boykov-Kolmogorov — preferred for vision, $O(V^{1.5}EC)$

### Historical Attributions
- [x] Ford-Fulkerson (1956) — Max-flow min-cut theorem
- [x] Greig, Porteous, Seheult (1989) — First graph cut for image restoration
- [x] Boykov and Kolmogorov (2004) — Modern BK max-flow algorithm
- [x] Boykov, Veksler, Zabih (2001) — $\alpha$-expansion for multi-label

### Quick Recall Mnemonic
**"D-V-S-λ"** → **D**ata + **V**isual **S**moothness, weighted by **λ** — the four pillars of graph cut energy formulation.

---

**End of Module 3 Topic: Solutions Based on Graph Cuts**
