topics.title: Graph Cuts on Pre-Segmented Images
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Graph Cuts on Pre-Segmented Images

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Graph Cuts on Pre-Segmented Images** is a region-based, combinatorial optimization technique for image segmentation that operates on an oversegmented image (obtained via watershed, SLIC superpixels, or mean-shift clustering). Instead of treating every pixel as a graph node — which is computationally expensive — the algorithm first merges homogeneous pixels into compact regions (superpixels/segments) and then constructs a **Region Adjacency Graph (RAG)**. In this RAG, every node represents a pre-segmented region, and every edge connects two spatially adjacent regions, weighted by a similarity/affinity measure. The final segmentation is obtained by computing a **minimum s–t cut** on this graph that minimizes a global energy functional composed of a **data fidelity term** (how well a region matches a foreground/background model) and a **boundary regularization term** (how different two adjacent regions are).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PEBMT755, Module 3):** This topic is studied under "Advanced Segmentation Paradigms" and bridges **classical watershed-based oversegmentation** with **energy-minimization frameworks** (Boykov–Kolmogorov, 2004). It is a high-yield topic for Part A (3 marks) and frequently tested as a 14-mark analytical question in ESE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Map of Neighborhoods"

Imagine a city divided into blocks (neighborhoods), where each block is a **pre-segmented region** of similar houses. The city planner needs to decide which blocks belong to "District A" (foreground — e.g., a tumor) and which to "District B" (background — healthy tissue). To do this:

1. The planner writes a **"block census"** — measuring the average brightness, texture, and intensity histogram of every block (the **data term**).
2. The planner then draws a **map** connecting neighboring blocks, with thicker lines between similar blocks and thinner lines between dissimilar blocks (the **boundary/edge weights**).
3. A **fence** must be built around the foreground district such that the **total cost of cutting through the roads is minimum** and the **total census match is maximum**. The set of roads cut forms the segmentation boundary.

This "minimum-cost fence" is precisely what the **min-cut / max-flow algorithm** computes on the Region Adjacency Graph.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The algorithm never reasons about individual pixels; it reasons about *regions*. This is the crucial efficiency leap that makes graph cuts tractable on large 3D medical volumes (CT, MRI) where pixel-wise graph cuts would be infeasible.

### 1.3 Why Pre-Segmentation? The Motivation

| Problem in Pixel-Graph Cuts | How Pre-Segmentation Solves It |
|---|---|
| $O(N^2)$ memory for $N$ pixels (millions in 3D MRI) | Reduces nodes to $\sim 10^3$–$10^4$ regions |
| Noise causes spurious tiny cuts | Regions average out noise via region statistics |
| Slow max-flow convergence | Sparse RAG allows near-real-time cuts |
| Hard to incorporate region-shape priors | Each region carries rich descriptors (mean, variance, histogram) |

> [!TIP]
> **Constant to remember:** A typical brain MRI slice of $256 \times 256 = 65{,}536$ pixels becomes $\sim 200$–$500$ superpixels after watershed oversegmentation — a **100× to 300× reduction** in graph size.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline — Six Logical Steps

The end-to-end pipeline of Graph Cuts on Pre-Segmented Images proceeds as follows:

1. **Pre-segmentation stage** — Apply a watershed transform (or SLIC, mean-shift) to obtain an oversegmented image $\mathcal{I}_{\text{seg}} = \{R_1, R_2, \dots, R_K\}$ where $K \ll N$ (number of pixels).
2. **Feature extraction per region** — For each region $R_i$, compute a descriptor vector $\mathbf{f}_i = (\mu_i, \sigma_i, \mathbf{h}_i)$ where $\mu_i$ is the mean intensity, $\sigma_i$ the variance, and $\mathbf{h}_i$ the normalized intensity histogram.
3. **Region Adjacency Graph (RAG) construction** — Define graph $G = (V, E)$:
   - $V = \{v_1, \dots, v_K\}$ — one node per region.
   - $E = \{(v_i, v_j) \mid R_i \text{ and } R_j \text{ share a boundary}\}$.
4. **Weight assignment** — Each edge $(v_i, v_j)$ gets a boundary weight $w_{ij}$ measuring dissimilarity (high weight = strong boundary = likely to cut).
5. **Energy functional definition** — Define the Gibbs energy $E(L)$ over a labeling $L: V \to \{0, 1\}$ (0 = background, 1 = foreground):
6. **Optimization via min-cut/max-flow** — Compute the global minimum of $E(L)$ using the **Boykov–Kolmogorov (BK) algorithm** or **Boykov–Veksler–Zabih (BVZ) graph cuts**, yielding the optimal segmentation.

### 2.2 The Energy Functional — The Heart of the Method

The energy to be minimized is the standard **Markov Random Field (MRF) energy** in its multi-label/region form:

$$E(L) \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{i \in V} D_i(L_i)}_{\text{Data Term}} \;+\; \underbrace{\lambda \sum_{(i,j)\in E} V_{ij}(L_i, L_j)}_{\text{Smoothness / Boundary Term}}$$

#### 2.2.1 Data Term $D_i(L_i)$

Penalizes assigning label $L_i$ to region $R_i$ if its features disagree with the model of that label:

$$D_i(L_i) \;=\; -\log P\bigl(\mathbf{f}_i \mid L_i\bigr)$$

where $P(\mathbf{f}_i \mid L_i)$ is the likelihood of region $R_i$'s descriptor under the Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) of label $L_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For pre-segmented images, the data term uses **region statistics** (mean, histogram) rather than single-pixel intensity. This is what makes region-based graph cuts robust to noise — a noisy pixel cannot "vote" as strongly as a whole region's average.

#### 2.2.2 Boundary Term $V_{ij}(L_i, L_j)$

The standard **Potts model** assigns a penalty only when adjacent regions receive *different* labels:

$$V_{ij}(L_i, L_j) \;=\; w_{ij} \cdot \delta(L_i \neq L_j) \quad\text{where}\quad \delta(\cdot) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } L_i \neq L_j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The boundary weight $w_{ij}$ is typically:

$$w_{ij} \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(\mu_i - \mu_j)^2}{2\sigma_b^2}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\text{dist}(R_i, R_j)}$$

where $\mu_i, \mu_j$ are the mean intensities of adjacent regions and $\sigma_b$ is a bandwidth parameter. **Strong intensity discontinuity** (a real anatomical boundary) yields **low $w_{ij}$**, encouraging the cut to pass through it.

#### 2.2.3 Regularization Constant $\lambda$

The scalar $\lambda \geq 0$ balances the two terms. As $\lambda \to \infty$, the algorithm prefers the smallest possible boundary (over-smoothing). As $\lambda \to 0$, it labels each region purely by likelihood (over-segmentation into noise-driven fragments).

### 2.3 Min-Cut / Max-Flow Equivalence

By the **Ford–Fulkerson theorem (1956)**, for any $s$–$t$ graph:

$$\min_{C \in \mathcal{C}_{s,t}} \vert C \vert \;=\; \max_{f \in \mathcal{F}} \vert f \vert$$

where $\vert C \vert$ is the capacity of a cut and $\vert f \vert$ is the value of a flow. For the BK augmentation, two search trees ($\mathcal{S}$-tree and $\mathcal{T}$-tree) grow from the source and sink simultaneously, finding augmenting paths in near-linear time on typical RAGs.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Typical Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E(L) = \sum_i D_i(L_i) + \lambda \sum_{(i,j)} V_{ij}(L_i, L_j)$ | Total Gibbs energy to minimize | Joules (normalized, unitless) |
| 2 | $D_i(L_i) = -\log P(\mathbf{f}_i \mid L_i)$ | Negative log-likelihood (data penalty) | $\geq 0$, unitless |
| 3 | $V_{ij} = w_{ij}\cdot\delta(L_i \neq L_j)$ | Potts boundary penalty | $\geq 0$, unitless |
| 4 | $w_{ij} = \exp(-(\mu_i-\mu_j)^2 / 2\sigma_b^2)\cdot 1/d_{ij}$ | Boundary weight between regions $i,j$ | $(0, 1]$ |
| 5 | $\mu_i = (1/\vert R_i\vert)\sum_{p \in R_i} I(p)$ | Mean intensity of region $R_i$ | Hounsfield Units (CT), arbitrary (MRI) |
| 6 | $\sigma_i^2 = (1/\vert R_i\vert)\sum_{p \in R_i}(I(p)-\mu_i)^2$ | Region intensity variance | $(HU)^2$ |
| 7 | $\min \vert C \vert = \max \vert f \vert$ | Min-cut / Max-flow duality | — |
| 8 | $\vert C \vert = \sum_{(i,j)\in C} w_{ij}$ | Capacity of an $s$–$t$ cut | unitless |
| 9 | $K \approx 10^3$–$10^4$ | Typical #regions after oversegmentation | — |
| 10 | $N = M \times M$ (2D) or $M^3$ (3D) | Original pixel/voxel count | — |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Radiation oncology:** Pre-operative delineation of tumors and organs-at-risk in CT/MRI before radiotherapy planning.
- **Surgical planning:** Liver, kidney, and brain tumor segmentation for 3D-printed anatomical models.
- **Cardiac MRI:** Delineating left-ventricle myocardium from blood pool and surrounding tissue.
- **Histopathology:** Segmenting nuclei and glands in H&E-stained microscopy images.
- **Industrial NDE:** Detecting defects in radiographic weld images.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Pixel Energy to Region Energy

We begin with the **pixel-wise** energy functional and integrate over each pre-segmented region to obtain the **region-wise** energy used in graph cuts on pre-segmented images.

**Starting Point (Pixel-Level Energy):**

$$E_{\text{pixel}}(L) = \sum_{p \in \mathcal{P}} D_p(L_p) + \lambda \sum_{(p,q) \in \mathcal{N}} V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)$$

**Step 1 — Partition the pixel set $\mathcal{P}$ into $K$ regions:**
The oversegmentation maps $\mathcal{P} \to \{R_1, \dots, R_K\}$, i.e., $\mathcal{P} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{K} R_i$ and $R_i \cap R_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$. Every pixel $p \in R_i$ inherits the label $L_i$ of its parent region (label-consistency assumption):

$$L_p = L_i \quad \text{for all } p \in R_i$$

**Step 2 — Re-express the data term as a sum over regions:**
The pixel-level data cost for region $R_i$ under label $L_i$ becomes the aggregated negative log-likelihood over all its pixels. Assuming pixels within a region are i.i.d. under the region descriptor model:

$$D_i^{\text{region}}(L_i) = \sum_{p \in R_i} D_p(L_i) = \sum_{p \in R_i} \bigl[-\log P(I(p) \mid L_i)\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Take the leading-order (mean-field) approximation:**
By the law of large numbers, since $\vert R_i \vert \gg 1$, the sum is well approximated by the mean:

$$\begin{aligned} D_i^{\text{region}}(L_i) &= \vert R_i \vert \cdot \left[ -\frac{1}{\vert R_i \vert} \sum_{p \in R_i} \log P(I(p) \mid L_i) \right] \\[4pt] &\approx \vert R_i \vert \cdot H\bigl(P(\cdot \mid L_i) \,\|\, \text{emp}_i\bigr) \end{aligned}$$

where $H$ is the cross-entropy between the label model and the empirical distribution of $R_i$. In practice, this is computed as:

$$D_i^{\text{region}}(L_i) \approx \vert R_i \vert \cdot \left[\frac{(\mu_i - \hat{\mu}_{L_i})^2}{2\hat{\sigma}_{L_i}^2} + \log \hat{\sigma}_{L_i}\right]$$

where $\hat{\mu}_{L_i}$ and $\hat{\sigma}_{L_i}$ are the parameters of the foreground/background Gaussian model.

**Step 4 — Aggregate the smoothness term over region-pair edges:**
The pixel-pair set $\mathcal{N}$ collapses to the region-edge set $E$ in the RAG. The boundary penalty for edge $(i,j)$ in the RAG is the **sum** of pixel-pair penalties along the shared boundary $\partial R_i \cap \partial R_j$:

$$V_{ij}^{\text{region}}(L_i, L_j) = \sum_{\substack{p \in R_i,\, q \in R_j \\ (p,q)\in \mathcal{N}}} w_{pq} \cdot \delta(L_i \neq L_j)$$

**Step 5 — Final region-level energy:**

$$\boxed{\; E_{\text{region}}(L) = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \vert R_i \vert \cdot D_i^{\text{reg}}(L_i) + \lambda \sum_{(i,j)\in E} \left(\sum_{p \in \partial R_{ij}} w_{pq}\right) \delta(L_i \neq L_j) \;}$$

This is the working energy minimized by graph cuts on the pre-segmented image. The $\vert R_i \vert$ factor ensures larger regions contribute proportionally more to the data term, naturally biasing the cut toward segmenting anatomically meaningful (large) structures.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimal Boundary Weight from Fisher's Discriminant

To derive a theoretically grounded choice of $w_{ij}$, consider two adjacent regions $R_i, R_j$ as samples from two distributions. The optimal cut should pass where the distributions differ *most*. The **Fisher ratio** gives this:

$$F_{ij} = \dfrac{(\mu_i - \mu_j)^2}{\sigma_i^2 + \sigma_j^2}$$

**Step 1 — Define a probabilistic cut likelihood:**
If a cut is placed on edge $(i,j)$, the probability that it correctly separates the two classes is:

$$P(\text{cut at } (i,j)) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-\alpha F_{ij})}$$

for some steepness parameter $\alpha > 0$.

**Step 2 — Convert to an energy via the Boltzmann distribution:**
Taking the negative log:

$$\begin{aligned} w_{ij} &= -\log P(\text{cut at } (i,j)) \\ &= \log\bigl(1 + \exp(-\alpha F_{ij})\bigr) \\ &\approx \alpha F_{ij} \quad \text{(for small } F_{ij}\text{, using } \log(1+e^{-x})\approx e^{-x}) \\[4pt] &\approx \alpha \cdot \frac{(\mu_i - \mu_j)^2}{\sigma_i^2 + \sigma_j^2} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
- Large mean-intensity difference $\vert\mu_i - \mu_j\vert$ → strong anatomical boundary → **high $w_{ij}$** → cut is **encouraged** here.
- Large within-region variance (noisy/textured regions) → **low $w_{ij}$** → cut is **discouraged** (the cut would split a homogeneous area).

> [!NOTE]
> This Fisher-derived weight is mathematically equivalent to a **Gaussian-GBMR (Geometric Brownian Motion Ratio)** boundary detector and is the gold standard for medical image graph cuts.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Aspect | Pixel-Graph-Cut (Boykov 2001) | Region-Graph-Cut (Pre-Seg) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of nodes | $N$ (millions) | $K \sim 10^3$–$10^4$ |
| Memory | $O(N)$ (often > 4 GB for 3D) | $O(K)$ (few MB) |
| Speed | Minutes–hours | Seconds |
| Noise robustness | Low (pixel-level) | High (region-averaged) |
| Boundary precision | Sub-pixel | Limited by pre-seg boundaries |
| Best use case | 2D natural images | 3D medical volumes |
| Algorithm | BK / BVZ | BK on RAG |
| Re-segmentation possible? | No (must rebuild graph) | Yes (just relabel regions) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Medical Image I] --> B[Pre-Processing]
    B --> B1[Noise Filtering]
    B --> B2[Intensity Normalization]

    B2 --> C
    C --> C1[Watershed Transform]
    C --> C2[SLIC Superpixels]
    C --> C3[Mean Shift Clustering]

    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Feature Extraction Per Region]
    E --> E1[Mean Intensity]
    E --> E2[Variance]
    E --> E3[Histogram]
    E1 --> F[Region Adjacency Graph Build]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Compute Edge Weights w_ij]
    G --> H[Add Source and Sink Terminals]
    H --> H1[T links for FG likelihood]
    H --> H2[T links for BG likelihood]
    H1 --> I[Construct s-t Flow Network]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[Boykov Kolmogorov Max Flow Solver]
```

> In the diagram above, **t-links** (terminal links) connect every region to the source or sink with capacity equal to the data term $D_i(\cdot)$. **n-links** (neighbor links) connect adjacent regions with capacity equal to the boundary weight $w_{ij}$. The min-cut separates the graph into the source-side (foreground, red) and sink-side (background, green). The "mixed" orange region is a boundary region whose final label is decided by the cut.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a Region Adjacency Graph (RAG) in the context of pre-segmented image analysis, and what do its nodes and edges represent?**

**Model Answer:**
A Region Adjacency Graph (RAG) is a graph structure $G = (V, E)$ built on top of an oversegmented image. The **nodes** $V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_K\}$ correspond to the $K$ homogeneous pre-segmented regions (superpixels/segments) obtained from a watershed or SLIC algorithm. The **edges** $E$ connect pairs of nodes $(v_i, v_j)$ whose corresponding regions share a common spatial boundary. Each edge is assigned a weight $w_{ij}$ that quantifies the dissimilarity (or affinity) between the two regions, typically using intensity, texture, or gradient-based features. The RAG enables region-level energy minimization via graph cuts, reducing the problem from millions of pixels to thousands of regions.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark each for: definition, node meaning, edge meaning; bonus mark for mentioning weight function.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State the Min-Cut / Max-Flow theorem. Why is it central to graph-cut segmentation?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Min-Cut / Max-Flow theorem** (Ford–Fulkerson, 1956) states that in any flow network with a designated source $s$ and sink $t$, the **minimum capacity of an $s$–$t$ cut** equals the **maximum value of a flow** from $s$ to $t$:

$$\min_{C \in \mathcal{C}_{s,t}} \sum_{(i,j) \in C} w_{ij} \;=\; \max_{f \in \mathcal{F}} \sum_{e \in \text{out}(s)} f(e)$$

It is central to graph-cut segmentation because the energy functional $E(L)$ can be **exactly represented** as the capacity of a cut in a suitably constructed $s$–$t$ graph. The data term $D_i$ becomes the capacity of the $t$-links (region-to-terminal edges), and the boundary term $w_{ij}$ becomes the capacity of the $n$-links (inter-region edges). Therefore, finding the optimal segmentation $L^*$ is equivalent to finding the **minimum $s$–$t$ cut**, which is a **polynomial-time solvable** combinatorial optimization problem. This is what makes graph cuts both **globally optimal** (no local minima) and **computationally efficient**.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for equivalence to energy, 1 mark for global optimality.)*

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the energy functional used in graph-cut segmentation of pre-segmented medical images. Clearly define the data term, the boundary (smoothness) term, and the role of the regularization constant $\lambda$. How does each term change when moving from pixel-based to region-based graph cuts?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the Boykov–Kolmogorov (BK) max-flow algorithm used to minimize the graph-cut energy. Explain the concept of augmentation trees and how the algorithm handles repeated search.

---

### **Question A — Model Solution**

#### Part (a) Solution — The Energy Functional

The energy to be minimized over a labeling $L: V \to \{0, 1\}$ is:

$$E(L) = \sum_{i \in V} D_i(L_i) + \lambda \sum_{(i,j) \in E} V_{ij}(L_i, L_j)$$

**Data Term $D_i(L_i)$:**
This term measures the cost of assigning label $L_i$ to region $R_i$ based on how well the region's features match the model for that label. In a pre-segmented image, the data term uses **region-level statistics** (mean intensity $\mu_i$, variance $\sigma_i^2$, histogram $\mathbf{h}_i$) rather than per-pixel intensity:

$$D_i(L_i) = -\log P(\mathbf{f}_i \mid L_i) \quad \text{where } \mathbf{f}_i = (\mu_i, \sigma_i, \mathbf{h}_i)$$

For a Gaussian model with parameters $(\hat{\mu}_{L_i}, \hat{\sigma}_{L_i})$:

$$D_i(L_i) = \frac{(\mu_i - \hat{\mu}_{L_i})^2}{2\hat{\sigma}_{L_i}^2} + \log \hat{\sigma}_{L_i}$$

**Boundary Term $V_{ij}(L_i, L_j)$:**
This term penalizes label discontinuities between adjacent regions. The **Potts model** formulation:

$$V_{ij}(L_i, L_j) = w_{ij} \cdot \delta(L_i \neq L_j)$$

The boundary weight $w_{ij}$ is computed using the Fisher ratio as derived earlier:

$$w_{ij} = \alpha \cdot \frac{(\mu_i - \mu_j)^2}{\sigma_i^2 + \sigma_j^2}$$

**Role of $\lambda$:**
- Small $\lambda$: data term dominates → each region is labeled by its likelihood → may produce noisy/fragmented segments.
- Large $\lambda$: boundary term dominates → the algorithm prefers the smallest possible boundary → may over-smooth the segmentation.
- Typical choice: $\lambda \in [0.1, 10]$; tuned by grid search and Dice-score validation.

**Change from pixel-based to region-based graph cuts:**

| Aspect | Pixel-Based | Region-Based (Pre-Seg) |
|---|---|---|
| Domain of $D_i$ | Single pixel $p$ | Whole region $R_i$ |
| Aggregation | None | $\sum_{p \in R_i} D_p(L_i) = \vert R_i \vert \cdot D_i^{\text{reg}}$ |
| Noise robustness | Low | High (region averaging) |
| Weight $w_{ij}$ | Per-pixel gradient $\nabla I$ | Aggregated over $\partial R_{ij}$ boundary pixels |
| Memory | $O(N)$ | $O(K)$ with $K \ll N$ |

*(7 marks: 2 for data term, 2 for boundary term, 1 for $\lambda$ role, 2 for pixel-vs-region comparison.)*

---

#### Part (b) Solution — Boykov–Kolmogorov Max-Flow Algorithm

The **BK algorithm** (2004) is an augmenting-path max-flow algorithm specifically optimized for computer-vision graphs (sparse, multi-terminal). It maintains **two non-overlapping search trees** rooted at the source $s$ and the sink $t$.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialization:** Set residual capacity $r_e = w_e$ for every edge. Create a forest with $s$ in the $\mathcal{S}$-tree and $t$ in the $\mathcal{T}$-tree. All other nodes are *free*.

2. **Growth Stage:** Repeatedly grow both trees:
   - A *free* node is added to $\mathcal{S}$ (or $\mathcal{T}$) by following a non-saturated edge from an *active* node in the tree.
   - A node becomes *active* when added to a tree, and *passive* when all its neighbors have been explored.
   - Growth stops when an *active* node in $\mathcal{S}$ encounters an *active* node in $\mathcal{T}$ — this is an **augmenting path**.

3. **Augmentation Stage:** Augment the flow along the found path. This may **saturate** some edges, possibly causing tree edges to become orphans (children with no valid parent). These orphans are detected and either re-attached (to a parent in the same tree) or moved to the *free* set.

4. **Adoption Stage:** Re-establish the tree structure by finding new parents for orphan nodes.

5. **Termination:** When $\mathcal{S}$ and $\mathcal{T}$ cannot be connected by any augmenting path, the flow is **maximum** and the min-cut is the set of saturated edges crossing the trees.

**Sketch (Augmentation Trees):**

```
   [s]                              [t]
    |                                |
    v                                v
   R1 (S-active) --w12--> R2 (S-passive)
    |                        |
    v                        v
   R3 (free) <--w34-- R4 (T-active)
                         |
                         v
                        R5 (T-passive)
```

**Repeated Search Handling:**
A naive augmenting-path search can re-explore the same dead-end subgraphs. The BK algorithm solves this with:
- **Tree reuse:** After augmentation, the unsaturated parts of both trees remain valid for the next growth phase.
- **Orphan adoption:** Disconnected subtrees are *adopted* into their nearest valid parent rather than rebuilt from scratch.

This gives BK near-linear average-case time on vision graphs, compared to $O(N^2)$ for the classical Ford–Fulkerson method on dense graphs.

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for growth/augmentation stages, 1 for adoption, 2 for tree-reuse efficiency.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare pixel-based and region-based graph cuts. Why is pre-segmentation preferred for 3D medical volumes? Quantify the complexity reduction achieved when a 3D MRI volume of size $256 \times 256 \times 200$ is pre-segmented into approximately 5000 superpixels.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the optimal boundary weight $w_{ij}$ between two adjacent regions $R_i$ and $R_j$ using the Fisher discriminant ratio. Show that the boundary penalty is high when the regions are statistically distinct and low when they are similar.

---

### **Question B — Model Solution**

#### Part (a) Solution — Complexity Comparison and Quantification

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Pixel-Based GC | Region-Based GC (Pre-Seg) |
|---|---|---|
| Graph size | $N$ (pixels) | $K$ (regions), $K \ll N$ |
| Memory | $O(N)$ | $O(K)$ |
| Time | $O(N^{1.5})$ typical | $O(K^{1.5})$ typical |
| Noise sensitivity | High | Low |
| Boundary precision | Sub-pixel | Limited by RAG |
| 3D feasibility | Often infeasible | Highly feasible |
| Re-segmentation | Rebuild graph | Just relabel |

**Why pre-segmentation is preferred for 3D medical volumes:**
1. **Memory feasibility:** A $256 \times 256 \times 200$ volume has $N = 13{,}107{,}200$ voxels. A pixel-graph needs $\sim 13$ million nodes and $\sim 80$ million edges, requiring > 1 GB RAM.
2. **Time feasibility:** Max-flow on such a graph takes tens of minutes.
3. **Statistical robustness:** Medical images have low SNR; region averaging denoises effectively.
4. **Anatomical priors:** Pre-segments often correspond to tissue sub-units (e.g., gray matter patches), making the labeling problem well-posed.

**Quantification of Complexity Reduction:**
- Pixel-based: $N = 256 \times 256 \times 200 = 13{,}107{,}200$ nodes
- Region-based: $K = 5000$ nodes
- Reduction factor: $N / K = 13{,}107{,}200 / 5000 \approx 2621$
- Memory reduction (edges scale similarly): $2621 \times$ smaller
- Time complexity reduction (assuming $O(N^{1.5})$): $(13{,}107{,}200 / 5000)^{1.5} \approx 134{,}000 \times$ speedup

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Many students forget that the max-flow algorithm is **polynomial but not linear**, so a $2621\times$ reduction in nodes can yield a $> 100{,}000\times$ speedup. Always show the **time-complexity exponent**.

*(7 marks: 3 for comparison, 2 for 3D rationale, 2 for the numerical quantification.)*

---

#### Part (b) Solution — Fisher-Based Boundary Weight Derivation

We want to derive $w_{ij}$ such that it is **large** (encouraging a cut) when regions $R_i$ and $R_j$ are statistically distinct, and **small** (discouraging a cut) when they are similar.

**Step 1 — Define the Fisher ratio.**
For two classes with means $\mu_i, \mu_j$ and variances $\sigma_i^2, \sigma_j^2$:

$$F_{ij} = \frac{(\mu_i - \mu_j)^2}{\sigma_i^2 + \sigma_j^2}$$

This ratio is large when the means are well-separated relative to within-class spread — exactly the situation of a real anatomical boundary.

**Step 2 — Model the cut probability using a sigmoid.**
The probability that the cut is correctly placed at edge $(i,j)$ is:

$$P(\text{cut at }(i,j)) = \sigma(\alpha F_{ij}) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-\alpha F_{ij})}$$

where $\alpha > 0$ is a steepness parameter.

**Step 3 — Convert to an energy.**
Since the BK algorithm minimizes the **sum of cut capacities** (i.e., energy), we set:

$$\begin{aligned} w_{ij} &= -\log P(\text{cut at }(i,j)) \\ &= -\log\!\left(\frac{1}{1 + e^{-\alpha F_{ij}}}\right) \\ &= \log\!\left(1 + e^{-\alpha F_{ij}}\right) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the small-ratio approximation.**
For small $\alpha F_{ij}$ (i.e., regions that are similar), using $\log(1+x) \approx x$:

$$w_{ij} \approx e^{-\alpha F_{ij}} = \exp\!\left(-\alpha \cdot \frac{(\mu_i - \mu_j)^2}{\sigma_i^2 + \sigma_j^2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Verify the desired behavior.**

**Case 1 — Regions are distinct (anatomical boundary):**
- $\vert\mu_i - \mu_j\vert$ is large → $F_{ij}$ is large → $\alpha F_{ij}$ is large → $e^{-\alpha F_{ij}} \approx 0$ → wait, that gives a *small* weight.

Let us re-examine: actually, for a distinct boundary, we want a **HIGH** weight (so the cut is cheap at this edge). The above derivation gives the wrong sign for an energy term because $- \log(\text{large probability}) = \text{small}$. This is correct for **affinity** (preference to keep together), not for **dissimilarity**.

The proper boundary weight for graph-cut **dissimilarity** is:

$$\boxed{\; w_{ij} = \alpha \cdot \frac{(\mu_i - \mu_j)^2}{\sigma_i^2 + \sigma_j^2} \;}$$

This is **directly** proportional to the Fisher ratio, so:
- **Distinct regions** (large $F_{ij}$) → **high $w_{ij}$** → **cut is encouraged** here.
- **Similar regions** (small $F_{ij}$) → **low $w_{ij}$** → **cut is discouraged**.

**Step 6 — Sanity check on units:**
- $(\mu_i - \mu_j)^2$: (Hounsfield Units)$^2$ for CT.
- $\sigma_i^2 + \sigma_j^2$: (Hounsfield Units)$^2$.
- Ratio: dimensionless. ✓
- $w_{ij}$: dimensionless. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** A common mistake is to invert the Fisher ratio when writing the boundary weight. The boundary weight $w_{ij}$ must be **directly** proportional to $(\mu_i - \mu_j)^2$ so that strong intensity gradients yield high cut-encouragement. Always verify the sign of proportionality with a numerical example.

*(7 marks: 2 for Fisher ratio definition, 2 for the sigmoid → energy conversion, 2 for the final boxed formula, 1 for the sanity check.)*

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Definition (must know):** Graph cuts on pre-segmented images = min-cut/max-flow on a Region Adjacency Graph built after watershed/SLIC oversegmentation.
- **Pipeline order:** Image → Pre-segmentation → Region features → RAG → Energy → Min-cut → Mask.
- **Energy formula (high yield):** $E(L) = \sum_i D_i(L_i) + \lambda \sum_{(i,j)} w_{ij} \cdot \delta(L_i \neq L_j)$.
- **Potts model:** $\delta(L_i \neq L_j) = 1$ if labels differ, $0$ otherwise.
- **Min-cut = Max-flow theorem (Ford–Fulkerson, 1956):** central duality that makes the optimization tractable.
- **Fisher-ratio boundary weight:** $w_{ij} \propto (\mu_i - \mu_j)^2 / (\sigma_i^2 + \sigma_j^2)$ — must be **directly** proportional.
- **Complexity reduction:** 3D MRI $256^2 \times 200$ with 5000 superpixels → ~$2600\times$ node reduction, ~$10^5\times$ time speedup.
- **Boykov–Kolmogorov (BK) algorithm:** two non-overlapping search trees (S-tree and T-tree), growth–augmentation–adoption phases, tree reuse for efficiency.
- **Region-vs-pixel trade-off:** Region-based is fast and noise-robust; pixel-based gives sub-pixel boundary precision.
- **Regularization $\lambda$:** controls smoothness — small = fragmented, large = over-smoothed. Tune via Dice score.
- **Real-world applications:** Tumor delineation, organ-at-risk segmentation, cardiac MRI, histopathology.
- **Common exam traps:** (i) Inverting the Fisher ratio sign, (ii) Forgetting the $\vert R_i \vert$ weighting in the data term, (iii) Confusing t-links (region-to-terminal) with n-links (region-to-region).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Rendering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Surface Rendering ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Surface Rendering** is a 3D visualization technique in medical imaging that reconstructs and displays the external geometric boundaries (iso-surfaces) of anatomical structures from volumetric (3D voxel) datasets. It extracts a polygonal mesh representing the boundary between regions of different intensity values (e.g., bone vs. soft tissue) and renders it using illumination and shading models to simulate realistic 3D appearance on a 2D display. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for PEBMT755, Surface Rendering is studied as a **segmentation-driven visualization** paradigm where the 3D data volume $V(x,y,z)$ is sampled, an iso-value $\tau$ (threshold) is chosen, and the surface $S = \{(x,y,z) : V(x,y,z) = \tau\}$ is extracted and rendered. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — The Sand-Castle of Voxels** > > Imagine a stack of transparent ice cubes, each painted with a number representing tissue density. Now, if you pour warm water over the entire stack, only the cubes whose density number equals a chosen value (say, the number **700**, representing bone) begin to melt at exactly the same rate. The boundary that appears between the melting and non-melting cubes forms a **3D shell** — this is your surface. By illuminating this shell with a torch from one side, the parts facing the torch appear bright, the parts in shadow appear dark, and you instantly perceive a 3D shape. That is exactly what surface rendering does to a CT/MRI volume. - **Voxel**: The 3D equivalent of a pixel, with intensity $I(x,y,z)$. - **Iso-surface**: The set of points where intensity equals the chosen threshold $\tau$. - **Polygon mesh**: The triangulated surface that the GPU finally shades and displays. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | **Hounsfield Unit threshold for bone** | $\tau_{bone}$ | **≈ 300 HU** | Threshold above which voxels are classified as bone | | **Hounsfield Unit threshold for soft tissue** | $\tau_{soft}$ | **≈ 50 HU** | Threshold above which voxels are classified as soft tissue | | **Marching Cubes lookup-table size** | $L$ | **256 entries (15 unique by symmetry)** | Possible vertex configurations per cube | | **Polygons per typical anatomical scan** | $N_{tri}$ | **10⁵ – 10⁶** | Order of mesh complexity | | **Refresh rate (clinical workstations)** | $f_r$ | **≥ 30 Hz** | Smooth real-time interaction | > [!NOTE] > **Why these numbers matter for KTU:** Examiners love to test whether students know the threshold values and the lookup-table size. Memorize that Marching Cubes has **15 unique cases** (not 256) due to rotational and inversion symmetry. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of Surface Rendering The surface rendering pipeline is a sequence of well-defined stages: 1. **Volumetric Data Acquisition** — Obtain a 3D scalar field $V(x,y,z)$ from CT, MRI, or Ultrasound. 2. **Pre-processing** — Apply filtering (Gaussian, median) to reduce noise that would otherwise create spurious surfaces. 3. **Segmentation / Thresholding** — Convert continuous intensities to a binary or multi-label field. 4. **Iso-surface Extraction** — Use the Marching Cubes algorithm to find the surface $S$. 5. **Mesh Decimation (optional)** — Reduce the number of triangles $N_{tri}$ for faster rendering. 6. **Normal Vector Computation** — Compute $\mathbf{n}$ at each vertex for shading. 7. **Illumination / Shading** — Apply the Phong, Gouraud, or Flat shading model. 8. **Projection & Hidden-Surface Removal** — Use Z-buffer algorithm. 9. **Display** — Rasterize polygons onto the 2D screen. ### 2.2 The Marching Cubes Algorithm — Detailed Logic The algorithm was proposed by **Lorensen and Cline (1987)** and is the cornerstone of surface rendering. **Logic Steps:** - The volume is divided into a regular grid of small cubes. Each cube has **8 vertices**, each vertex has a value $V_i$ (i = 1..8). - A threshold $\tau$ is chosen. Each vertex is labeled **inside** (if $V_i \geq \tau$) or **outside** (if $V_i < \tau$). - Each vertex thus has 2 possible states → $2^8 = \mathbf{256}$ possible configurations for the cube. - By exploiting **rotational symmetry** and **inversion symmetry**, these reduce to only **15 unique cases**. - For each case, a precomputed lookup table gives the intersection pattern (which edges of the cube the iso-surface crosses). - The intersection point on each crossed edge is computed by **linear interpolation** between the two endpoint intensities. - Triangles are generated to approximate the local patch of iso-surface. ### 2.3 Shading Models (The "Why" and "How") > [!IMPORTANT] > **Shading ≠ Lighting.** Lighting computes how light reflects off a surface. Shading decides **how the lighting value is distributed across a polygon** (flat, interpolated at vertices, or interpolated per pixel). **(a) Flat Shading** — Single normal per polygon; cheapest; gives a faceted look. $$I_{flat} = K_a I_a + K_d I_0 (\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{L})$$ **(b) Gouraud Shading** — Normals are first averaged at each shared vertex, lighting is computed per vertex, then color is bilinearly interpolated across the polygon. $$\mathbf{n}_v = \frac{\sum_{j} \mathbf{n}_j \cdot A_j}{\sum_j A_j}$$ where $A_j$ is the area of polygon $j$ sharing vertex $v$. **(c) Phong Shading** — Normals are interpolated per fragment (pixel) across the polygon, and lighting is computed at every fragment. Most realistic, most expensive. $$\mathbf{n}_{frag} = \frac{\sum_i w_i \mathbf{n}_i}{\left\| \sum_i w_i \mathbf{n}_i \right\|}$$ where $w_i$ are barycentric / distance-based weights. ### 2.4 The Phong Illumination Model (Combined Formula) The complete Phong illumination equation used in surface rendering is: $$I = \underbrace{K_a I_a}_{\text{ambient}} + \underbrace{K_d \, I_0 \, (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})}_{\text{diffuse}} + \underbrace{K_s \, I_0 \, (\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{V})^n}_{\text{specular}}$$ where: - $K_a, K_d, K_s$ = ambient, diffuse, specular reflection coefficients - $I_a$ = ambient light intensity - $I_0$ = point light source intensity - $\mathbf{N}$ = surface normal (unit vector) - $\mathbf{L}$ = light direction (unit vector) - $\mathbf{R}$ = reflection of $\mathbf{L}$ about $\mathbf{N}$ - $\mathbf{V}$ = viewer direction (unit vector) - $n$ = specular exponent (shininess, typically **1 to 200**) The reflection vector is given by: $$\mathbf{R} = 2(\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})\mathbf{N} - \mathbf{L}$$ ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU 2024 Scheme — Surface Rendering** | # | Formula | Meaning | Use in Exam | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $I = K_a I_a + K_d I_0(\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L}) + K_s I_0(\mathbf{R}\cdot\mathbf{V})^n$ | Phong illumination model | **Most asked** 14-mark derivation | | 2 | $\mathbf{R} = 2(\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L})\mathbf{N} - \mathbf{L}$ | Reflection vector | Sub-part of Phong derivation | | 3 | $\mathbf{n}_v = \frac{\sum_j \mathbf{n}_j A_j}{\sum_j A_j}$ | Gouraud vertex normal | Comparison questions | | 4 | $P = P_1 + \frac{\tau - V_1}{V_2 - V_1}(P_2 - P_1)$ | Linear interpolation on MC edge | **Numerical problems** | | 5 | $S = \{(x,y,z) : V(x,y,z) = \tau\}$ | Iso-surface definition | Definition questions | | 6 | $\tau_{bone} \approx 300$ HU, $\tau_{soft} \approx 50$ HU | CT thresholds | Numerical values | | 7 | 256 cases → **15 unique** | Marching Cubes symmetry | Short answer | > **Engineering Utility:** The Phong model is the mathematical foundation of nearly every medical visualization workstation (OsiriX, 3D Slicer, Mimics). Understanding it allows biomedical engineers to optimize rendering pipelines in PACS, surgical simulators, and VR-based training platforms. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Linear Interpolation for Marching Cubes Edge Intersection When an edge of a cube has one vertex **inside** ($V_1 \geq \tau$) and the other **outside** ($V_2 < \tau$), the iso-surface must cross that edge somewhere between $P_1$ and $P_2$. Let $P_1$ be the position of vertex 1 with intensity $V_1$, and $P_2$ be the position of vertex 2 with intensity $V_2$. **Step 1 — Parameterize the edge:** $$P(t) = P_1 + t(P_2 - P_1), \quad 0 \leq t \leq 1$$ **Step 2 — Apply the iso-surface condition $V(P(t)) = \tau$:** Assuming the intensity varies linearly along the edge (a valid assumption for small cubes): $$V(P(t)) = V_1 + t(V_2 - V_1)$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $t$ such that $V(P(t)) = \tau$:** $$V_1 + t(V_2 - V_1) = \tau$$ $$t(V_2 - V_1) = \tau - V_1$$ $$t = \frac{\tau - V_1}{V_2 - V_1}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $t$ back into the parameterization:** $$P = P_1 + \frac{\tau - V_1}{V_2 - V_1}(P_2 - P_1)$$ This is the **exact 3D position** of the iso-surface intersection along the edge. This is the formula examiners will expect for a numerical problem. > **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** Let $P_1 = (0,0,0)$ with $V_1 = 100$, $P_2 = (1,0,0)$ with $V_2 = 500$, and $\tau = 300$. Then: > > $$t = \frac{300 - 100}{500 - 100} = \frac{200}{400} = 0.5$$ > > $$P = (0,0,0) + 0.5 \cdot (1,0,0) = (0.5, 0, 0)$$ > > The iso-surface crosses the edge exactly at the midpoint. This is intuitive because the threshold is exactly halfway between $V_1$ and $V_2$. ### 3.2 Derivation: Surface Normal from Gradient of the Volume To shade the extracted surface realistically, we need the normal $\mathbf{N}$ at each vertex. The mathematical definition is the **normalized gradient of the intensity field**: $$\mathbf{N} = \frac{\nabla V(x,y,z)}{\| \nabla V(x,y,z) \|}$$ **Step 1 — Define the gradient:** $$\nabla V = \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}, \frac{\partial V}{\partial z} \right)$$ **Step 2 — Approximate each partial derivative using central differences:** $$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} \approx \frac{V(x+1,y,z) - V(x-1,y,z)}{2 \Delta x}$$ $$\frac{\partial V}{\partial y} \approx \frac{V(x,y+1,z) - V(x,y-1,z)}{2 \Delta y}$$ $$\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} \approx \frac{V(x,y,z+1) - V(x,y,z-1)}{2 \Delta z}$$ **Step 3 — Normalize to get a unit normal:** $$\mathbf{N} = \frac{\left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}, \frac{\partial V}{\partial z} \right)}{\sqrt{ \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\right)^2 }}$$ This is the **gold-standard method** for computing normals directly from the volume (preferred over face-normal averaging for medical imaging because it is smoother). ### 3.3 Derivation: The Phong Illumination Model (Component by Component) The Phong model is the sum of three physically-motivated terms. **Step 1 — Ambient term.** Models the uniform background light that ensures no surface is ever pitch black: $$I_{amb} = K_a I_a$$ **Step 2 — Diffuse term (Lambert's Cosine Law).** A perfectly matte surface reflects light proportional to the cosine of the angle between the surface normal and the light direction: $$I_{diff} = K_d I_0 (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})$$ Derivation of $(\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})$: By definition, $\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L} = \|N\| \|L\| \cos\theta$. Since both are unit vectors, $\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L} = \cos\theta$. The power per unit area is $I_0 \cos\theta$, hence the formula. **Step 3 — Specular term.** Models the shiny highlight. The reflection vector is: $$\mathbf{R} = 2(\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})\mathbf{N} - \mathbf{L}$$ Derivation: $\mathbf{R}$ is the mirror reflection of $\mathbf{L}$ about $\mathbf{N}$. The component of $\mathbf{L}$ along $\mathbf{N}$ is $(\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})\mathbf{N}$. The perpendicular component is $\mathbf{L} - (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})\mathbf{N}$. Reflecting negates this component: $$\mathbf{R} = (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})\mathbf{N} - [\mathbf{L} - (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})\mathbf{N}] = 2(\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})\mathbf{N} - \mathbf{L}$$ **Step 4 — Specular intensity.** The brightness depends on how close the viewer direction $\mathbf{V}$ is to $\mathbf{R}$: $$I_{spec} = K_s I_0 (\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{V})^n$$ The exponent $n$ controls the **shininess**: small $n$ = broad highlight, large $n$ = sharp pinpoint highlight. **Step 5 — Sum the three terms:** $$I = K_a I_a + K_d I_0 (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L}) + K_s I_0 (\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{V})^n$$ This is the complete Phong illumination equation used in virtually every medical surface renderer. > **Numerical Example:** Given $K_a = 0.2, K_d = 0.6, K_s = 0.4, I_a = 0.3, I_0 = 1.0, \mathbf{N} = (0,0,1), \mathbf{L} = (0.5, 0, 0.866), \mathbf{V} = (0, 0, 1), n = 50$. > > Compute: > $\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L} = 0.866$ > $\mathbf{R} = 2(0.866)(0,0,1) - (0.5, 0, 0.866) = (-0.5, 0, 0.866)$ > $\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0.866$ > $I = 0.2(0.3) + 0.6(1.0)(0.866) + 0.4(1.0)(0.866)^{50}$ > $I = 0.06 + 0.5196 + 0.4 \times (0.866)^{50}$ > $I = 0.06 + 0.5196 + 0.4 \times 0.00075 = 0.06 + 0.5196 + 0.0003 = \mathbf{0.580}$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Shading Models | Property | Flat Shading | Gouraud Shading | Phong Shading | |---|---|---|---| | Normal source | One per face | Averaged at vertices | Interpolated per pixel | | Lighting computed at | Face center | Vertices only | Every pixel | | Realism | Low (faceted) | Medium (smooth) | High (very smooth) | | Computational cost | **Lowest** | Medium | **Highest** | | Mach band artifacts | Severe | Reduced | None | | Typical use | Quick previews | Interactive medical viewers | Diagnostic/surgical planning | ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Surface Rendering vs. Volume Rendering | Property | Surface Rendering | Volume Rendering | |---|---|---| | Data used | Only the iso-surface | Entire volume | | Memory required | **Low (mesh only)** | **High (full volume)** | | Speed | **Fast (real-time)** | Slower (GPU-heavy) | | Internal structures | **Not shown** | **Shown** | | Requires segmentation | Yes | Optional | | Best for | Bones, skin, implants | Tumors, vessels, soft tissue | | KTU exam weightage | **High (this module)** | Module 5 topic | ## 4. Each colored region represents a different processing stage: **blue** (acquisition), **orange** (preparation), **purple** (geometry), **green** (shading), **pink** (illumination/depth), **teal** (display). ### 4.2 Marching Cubes Algorithm — Per-Cube Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Read 8 Voxels of a Cube] --> B[Compare each Voxel with Threshold Tau] B --> C[Build 8-bit Index from Inside Outside States] C --> D{Lookup Table Entry 0 to 255} D -->|Empty Entry| E[Skip: No Surface in this Cube] D -->|Non-empty Entry| F[Identify Crossed Edges from Lookup Table] F --> G[Compute Intersection Points via Linear Interpolation] G --> H[Connect Intersections to form Triangles] H --> I[Compute Normals from Volume Gradient] I --> J[Append Triangles to Global Mesh] J --> K{More Cubes in Volume?} K -->|Yes| A K -->|No| L[Output Complete Polygon Mesh] L --> M[Send to Shading and Lighting Pipeline] style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000 style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00,color:#000 style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#f57c00,color:#000 style D fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000 style E fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#455a64,color:#000 style F fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#7b1fa2,color:#000 style G fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#7b1fa2,color:#000 style H fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#388e3c,color:#000 style I fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#388e3c,color:#000 style J fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#388e3c,color:#000 style K fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000 style L fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000 style M fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00796b,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Phong Illumination Model — Light-Surface Interaction Block ```mermaid flowchart LR LS[Light Source
