## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*What is DICOM? List any four key components of the DICOM standard.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

DICOM stands for **Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine**. It is a globally accepted standard developed jointly by the American College of Radiology (ACR) and the National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA) for handling, storing, printing, and transmitting medical imaging information.

**Four key components:**

1. **DICOM File Format (Part 10):** Defines the structure of `.dcm` files — preamble, DICM magic, meta-information, and the dataset.
2. **DICOM Network Protocol (Upper Layer):** Defines the TCP/IP-based association mechanism, presentation contexts, and DIMSE services (C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE, C-ECHO, etc.).
3. **Service Classes:** Define functional units such as Storage, Query/Retrieve, Modality Worklist, and Print Management.
4. **Information Object Definitions (IODs):** Define the *data model* — e.g., CT Image IOD, MR Image IOD, Ultrasound Image IOD — and the exact set of attributes each must contain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (typical KTU pattern):** Definition — 1 mark; 4 components — 2 marks (0.5 each).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between lossless and lossy image compression in the context of DICOM medical imaging.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Lossless Compression | Lossy Compression |
|---|---|---|
| **Information preserved** | Original image can be reconstructed **bit-exactly** | Original is **approximated**; some data is permanently lost |
| **Compression ratio** | Low (typically 2:1 – 3:1) | High (5:1 – 50:1 possible) |
| **Diagnostic use** | Approved for **primary diagnostic interpretation** | Generally **not permitted** for primary diagnosis; used for review, archive, or thumbnails |
| **DICOM transfer syntax examples** | JPEG-LS (`.4.80`), JPEG 2000 Lossless (`.4.90`), RLE (`.1.2.5`) | JPEG Baseline (`.4.50`), JPEG Extended (`.4.51`), JPEG 2000 Lossy (`.4.91`) |
| **Quality metric** | Identical to original; PSNR = ∞ | Quantified via PSNR; ≥ 40 dB is typically acceptable |
| **Use cases** | CT, MR, mammography, digital radiography for primary reading | Web thumbnails, referring physician views, long-term archive beyond legal retention |

> [!NOTE]
> **DICOM standard mandates** that any image compressed using a lossy transfer syntax must carry a **Derivation Description** in its metadata, so that downstream systems are aware of the compression and its parameters.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Always state definitions first** before explaining — 1 mark is reserved for precise definitions.
> 2. **Draw a diagram wherever asked** — a missing diagram costs 1–2 marks even if the explanation is perfect.
> 3. **Use the exact KTU terminology** — write *SOP Class UID*, *Presentation Context*, *A-ASSOCIATE-RQ* — not generic "message" or "header".
> 4. **Numerical answers must show formula, substitution, and final answer** — a bare number with no working gets 0 marks in Part B.
> 5. **Lossy compression questions** — examiners specifically check whether you mention *primary diagnostic restriction* and *Derivation Description* — omitting this costs 2 marks.
> 6. **DICOM association question** — be sure to list *all five steps* (TCP connect, A-ASSOCIATE-RQ, A-ASSOCIATE-AC, P-DATA-TF, A-RELEASE-RQ/RP, A-ABORT as fallback).

---

#### ⭐ CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Supplementary Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the DICOM file format in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the structure of a DICOM Part 10 file. List the four key information entities (Patient, Study, Series, Instance) and explain how they relate to each other.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 1.5 Tesla MRI generates 250 slices per study, each slice being a $256 \times 256$ matrix with 16 bits per pixel. The DICOM header for each image is 6 KB. Calculate: (i) the size of a single image, (ii) the total study size, and (iii) the time required to transfer this study over a 100 Mbps dedicated WAN link (TCP efficiency = 75%). If JPEG 2000 lossless compression achieves a 2.5:1 ratio, recompute the transfer time.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Part (a) — DICOM File Format and Information Model [7 marks]**

The DICOM Part 10 file format defines a self-contained, network-independent file structure suitable for media interchange (CD, DVD, USB, or file-system transfer). A DICOM file consists of **three top-level parts**:

**1. The 128-Byte File Preamble [0.5 mark]**
This is a 128-byte field whose contents are not part of the DICOM standard. By default, it is set to all zeros (00H). Applications may use this space to store a TIFF preview thumbnail or other proprietary data, but DICOM itself does not interpret it.

**2. The 4-Byte "DICM" Magic Number [0.5 mark]**
The four bytes contain the ASCII characters `D`, `I`, `C`, `M` (0x44, 0x49, 0x43, 0x4D). This serves as the file-type signature confirming DICOM Part 10 compliance. If these bytes are not present, the file is *not* a Part 10 DICOM file (though it might still be a DICOM Part 12 (raw) dataset or a non-DICOM file masquerading as one).

**3. The DICOM Data Set [2 marks]**
This contains two sub-parts:

**(a) File Meta Information (Group 0002)** — Always encoded in **Explicit VR Little Endian**, regardless of the file's main transfer syntax. The meta-header contains:
- `(0002,0000)` File Meta Information Group Length
- `(0002,0001)` File Meta Information Version
- `(0002,0002)` **Media Storage SOP Class UID** — what kind of data is in the file
- `(0002,0003)` **Media Storage SOP Instance UID** — the unique instance ID
- `(0002,0010)` **Transfer Syntax UID** — how the rest of the file is encoded
- `(0002,0012)` Implementation Class UID — vendor identification
- `(0002,0013)` Implementation Version Name

**(b) The Main Dataset** — Encoded per the transfer syntax declared in `(0002,0010)`. Contains:
- Patient Module (Group 0010)
- Study Module (Group 0008 / 0020)
- Series Module
- Image Module (rows, columns, pixel data at `(7FE0,0010)`)
- Hundreds of other optional modules

**DICOM Information Model — Four IEs [3 marks]:**

DICOM organizes clinical data into a hierarchy of **Information Entities (IEs)**:

| IE Level | Scope | Key Identifier | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Patient** | One real-world human | `Patient ID (0010,0020)` | Patient ID = 12345 |
| **Study** | One imaging event / encounter | `Study Instance UID (0020,000D)` | All images from one CT chest exam |
| **Series** | One logical group within a study | `Series Instance UID (0020,000E)` | Axial slices vs. reconstructions |
| **Instance (Image / SR / SEG)** | One discrete object | `SOP Instance UID (0008,0018)` | A single `.dcm` file |

The relationship is **strictly 1:N at each level** (one patient → many studies → many series → many instances). Each instance carries pointers (referenced UIDs) back to its parent study/series. This hierarchy is the conceptual model used by DICOM's **Query/Retrieve Service** (C-FIND, C-MOVE, C-GET) and the **DICOMDIR** directory file (which provides a CD/DVD index).

**Diagram [1 mark] — see Section 4.4 of these notes for the hierarchy flowchart.**

---

**Part (b) — Storage and Transfer Time Calculations [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- Number of slices $N = 250$
- Image matrix $256 \times 256$ pixels
- Bit depth $D = 16$ bits/pixel
- DICOM header $H = 6$ KB $= 6{,}144$ bytes per image
- WAN bandwidth $B = 100$ Mbps
- TCP efficiency $\eta = 0.75$
- Compression ratio $CR = 2.5:1$ (lossless)

**Step (i) — Size of a single image [2 marks]:**

The image payload (pixel data) is:

$$
S_{payload} = 256 \times 256 \times 16 \text{ bits} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bits} = 131{,}072 \text{ bytes} = 128 \text{ KB}
$$

Adding the DICOM header:

$$
S_{img} = 131{,}072 + 6{,}144 = 137{,}216 \text{ bytes} \approx 134 \text{ KB}
$$

> **Single image size ≈ 134 KB.**

**Step (ii) — Total study size [2 marks]:**

$$
S_{study} = 250 \times 137{,}216 = 34{,}304{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 32.72 \text{ MB}
$$

> **Total uncompressed study size ≈ 32.72 MB.**

**Step (iii) — Transfer time over 100 Mbps WAN [1.5 marks]:**

Using the transfer-time formula from the Formula Sheet:

$$
T_{uncomp} = \dfrac{S_{study} \times 8}{B \times \eta}
$$

$$
T_{uncomp} = \dfrac{34{,}304{,}000 \times 8}{100 \times 10^6 \times 0.75} = \dfrac{2.744 \times 10^8}{7.5 \times 10^7} \approx 3.66 \text{ seconds}
$$

> **Uncompressed transfer time ≈ 3.66 seconds.**

**Step (iv) — Compressed transfer time [1.5 marks]:**

With JPEG 2000 lossless compression at 2.5:1:

$$
S_{comp} = \dfrac{S_{study}}{CR} = \dfrac{34{,}304{,}000}{2.5} = 13{,}721{,}600 \text{ bytes} \approx 13.09 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
T_{comp} = \dfrac{13{,}721{,}600 \times 8}{100 \times 10^6 \times 0.75} = \dfrac{1.098 \times 10^8}{7.5 \times 10^7} \approx 1.46 \text{ seconds}
$$

> **Compressed transfer time ≈ 1.46 seconds.**

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Single image size | **~134 KB** |
| Uncompressed study size | **~32.72 MB** |
| Uncompressed transfer time (100 Mbps) | **~3.66 s** |
| Compressed transfer time (2.5:1) | **~1.46 s** |
| Time saved | **~2.2 s (60% reduction)** |

---

#### ⭐ CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Exam]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-tier architecture of PACS**. Describe the role of each tier. What is a **Vendor Neutral Archive (VNA)** and how does it relate to the storage tier?

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain **DICOM Query/Retrieve (C-FIND, C-MOVE, C-GET)** services. Compare them in a tabular form. A radiologist wants to retrieve all CT studies of a patient from 2023 from the hospital PACS. Describe the complete C-FIND → C-MOVE workflow step by step.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Part (a) — PACS Five-Tier Architecture and VNA [7 marks]**

PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System) is a hospital-scale digital infrastructure for the acquisition, storage, retrieval, distribution, and display of medical images. Modern PACS implementations follow a **five-tier logical architecture** [diagram worth 2 marks]:

**Tier 1 — Image Acquisition [1 mark]:**
This tier comprises the imaging modalities — CT, MR, CR/DR (computed/digital radiography), ultrasound, PET, SPECT, mammography, fluoroscopy, and cath-lab cine systems. Each modality has a DICOM **Storage SCU (Service Class User)** that exports completed images to the PACS. Most modalities additionally act as **Modality Worklist SCUs** (to pull scheduled procedure lists) and **MPPS SCPs** (to announce procedure progress).

**Tier 2 — Communication / Interface [1.5 marks]:**
This tier comprises DICOM gateways, HL7 interface brokers (Mirth Connect, Rhapsody, Cloverleaf), DICOM routers, and pre-fetch engines. Responsibilities include:
- Converting proprietary vendor protocols to DICOM (e.g., older Siemens MagicView data).
- Routing images based on AE Title, modality, and routing rules.
- HL7 ↔ DICOM bridging (e.g., synchronizing a C-STORE image with an HL7 ORU^R01 result message via the **Study Instance UID**).
- **Pre-fetching** relevant priors from long-term archive to short-term storage before a scheduled re-visit.

**Tier 3 — Storage Hierarchy [1.5 marks]:**
This is the heart of the PACS. It uses a **tiered storage strategy** matching cost to access frequency:
- **Short-term / Online storage** — RAID-5 or RAID-6 arrays (typically 10–100 TB). Used for the most recent 1–3 months. Provides rapid retrieval (<1 second).
- **Long-term / Near-line storage** — NAS, SAN, or VTL (Virtual Tape Library). Used for 1–5 years.
- **Archive / Off-line storage** — LTO tape, optical jukebox, or cloud object storage (S3-compatible). Used for 5–30 years. Retrieval is slow (minutes) but cost per TB is very low.

**Tier 4 — Display / Review [0.5 mark]:**
Diagnostic workstations (with **3 MP to 5 MP grayscale medical-grade monitors** calibrated to **DICOM GSDF — Grayscale Standard Display Function**), clinical review workstations (1–2 MP color), and zero-footprint web/mobile viewers (HTML5 + DICOMweb).

**Tier 5 — Integration [0.5 mark]:**
The PACS is bound to the **EMR/EHR** (Electronic Medical Record), the **RIS** (Radiology Information System) for scheduling, billing, and reporting, and to speech-recognition systems. Modern integrated PACS-EMR viewers are sometimes called **Unified Worklist** systems.

**Vendor Neutral Archive (VNA) [1 mark]:**
A **VNA** is a DICOM-standard-compliant, vendor-independent image archive that decouples image storage from the display/PACS application. A VNA sits as a **middleware layer** between modalities and multiple PACS viewers, normalizing proprietary data, supporting **XDS-I** for cross-enterprise sharing, and providing a single authoritative long-term archive. The VNA uses **DICOM IHE IOCM (Imaging Object Change Management)** to manage updates, deletions, and replacements of stored objects from multiple PACS. The key benefits are: (i) no vendor lock-in for the archive, (ii) multiple PACS readers sharing one archive, (iii) lifecycle management independent of viewer upgrades.

> **VNA Diagram: see Section 4.6 of these notes.**

---

**Part (b) — DICOM Query/Retrieve Services [7 marks]**

DICOM's **Query/Retrieve (Q/R) Service Class** enables a DICOM node to **search** (C-FIND) a remote archive and **retrieve** (C-MOVE or C-GET) matching images. All three operations occur within an established DICOM association.

**Definitions [2 marks]:**

- **C-FIND:** A **Composite Service** that issues a query (filter) to a remote SCP. The SCP returns matching entities at the *Patient*, *Study*, *Series*, or *Image* level, based on key attributes (Patient ID, Study Date, Modality, etc.).
- **C-MOVE:** Instructs the remote SCP to **send the matching images to a third party** (specified by AE Title, IP, and port). The third party is the "move destination" and is the receiver of a new C-STORE association from the SCP. C-MOVE works in **two associations** — one between the requester and the SCP (control), another between the SCP and the move destination (data).
- **C-GET:** A simpler alternative to C-MOVE. The remote SCP **pushes the matching images back over the same association** to the requester. Single association. Best for small queries.

**Comparison Table [2 marks]:**

| Parameter | C-FIND | C-MOVE | C-GET |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Search / query | Retrieve (via 3rd party) | Retrieve (direct pull) |
| **Number of associations** | 1 (control) | 2 (control + data) | 1 (combined) |
| **Number of network nodes involved** | 2 (requester + SCP) | 3 (requester + SCP + move dest) | 2 (requester + SCP) |
| **Network load on requester** | Low (just metadata) | Low | High (must receive full data) |
| **Firewalls** | Friendly (single port) | Needs inbound to move dest | Friendly |
| **Result** | List of matches | C-STORE images to 3rd party | C-STORE images back to requester |
| **DIMSE category** | Composite | Composite | Composite |

**Step-by-Step C-FIND → C-MOVE Workflow [3 marks]:**

**Step 1 — TCP + Association Setup.** The radiologist's diagnostic workstation (AE = `RAD_WS_01`) opens a TCP connection to the PACS Q/R SCP (AE = `PACS_MAIN` on port 104). It proposes the **Study Root Query/Retrieve — FIND** SOP Class (`1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.2.2.1`) and **Study Root Query/Retrieve — MOVE** SOP Class (`1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.2.2.2`) as presentation contexts.

**Step 2 — C-FIND Request.** The workstation issues a C-FIND-RQ with **Query/Retrieve Level = STUDY** and the following key attributes:
- `(0010,0020) PatientID = "P12345"`
- `(0008,0020) StudyDate = "20230101-20231231"`
- `(0008,0060) Modality = "CT"` (this is a return key, not filter)

**Step 3 — C-FIND Responses.** The PACS returns one C-FIND-RSP per matching study, each with status `0xFF00` (Pending) and the requested return keys populated. The final response carries status `0x0000` (Success) or `0xA700` (No more matches).

**Step 4 — C-MOVE Request.** The radiologist selects a study and the workstation issues a C-MOVE-RQ with:
- Same filter attributes (PatientID, StudyDate, Modality=CT)
- `MoveDestination = "PACS_MAIN"` (or a separate archive AE)
- Query/Retrieve Level = STUDY

**Step 5 — Sub-Operations.** The PACS opens a **new association** from itself to `PACS_MAIN:104`, negotiates a Storage Presentation Context, and **issues C-STORE-RQ messages** for every image in the study. For each C-STORE, the SCP returns C-STORE-RSP with status. The PACS accumulates these and returns periodic **C-MOVE-RSP Pending** messages to the radiologist with a count of completed/failed/remaining sub-operations.

**Step 6 — Completion.** The final C-MOVE-RSP has status `0x0000` (Success) — or `0xA701` (Refused: out of resources), `0xB000` (Sub-operations complete with failures), etc.

**Step 7 — A-RELEASE-RQ / A-RELEASE-RP** — graceful teardown of the control association.

> **Summary of C-FIND → C-MOVE workflow diagram: see Section 4.2 of these notes for association lifecycle.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Image Storage and Transfer**

✅ **DICOM is the cornerstone.** It is a complete standard — file format + network protocol + service classes + IODs. Version **3.0** is current. Backward compatible.

✅ **A `.dcm` file = 128-byte preamble + 4-byte "DICM" magic + File Meta Info (Group 0002, always Explicit VR LE) + Main Dataset.** The Transfer Syntax UID lives in group 0002.

✅ **DICOM tags = (group, element) 32-bit pairs**, e.g., `(0010,0010)` = Patient Name. Groups are usually even; odd groups are private/vendor-specific.

✅ **The four IEs are Patient → Study → Series → Instance.** Identified globally by **UIDs** (Study Instance UID, Series Instance UID, SOP Instance UID).

✅ **Default TCP port = 104** (unsecured) or **11112** (DICOM Upper Layer over TLS). AE Titles identify the two endpoints.

✅ **Association = the unit of DICOM communication.** Lifecycle: A-ASSOCIATE-RQ → A-ASSOCIATE-AC → P-DATA-TF (DIMSE) → A-RELEASE-RQ/RP (or A-ABORT).

✅ **DIMSE Composite Services = C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE, C-GET, C-ECHO.** Normalized = N-EVENT-REPORT, N-GET, N-SET, N-CREATE, N-DELETE, N-ACTION.

✅ **Five PACS tiers = Acquisition → Communication → Storage → Display → Integration.** Storage uses **tiered hierarchy** (short-term RAID, long-term NAS/SAN, archive LTO/cloud).

✅ **Vendor Neutral Archive (VNA)** decouples archive from display; uses **DICOM IOCM** profile for lifecycle management.

✅ **Lossless compression** (JPEG-LS, JPEG 2000 lossless, RLE) is approved for **primary diagnosis**. **Lossy** is restricted — typically CR ≤ **12:1** and PSNR ≥ **40 dB** for primary reading.

✅ **PSNR ≥ 40 dB** = diagnostically acceptable lossy quality. PSNR = ∞ means perfect reconstruction (lossless).

✅ **Shannon entropy** sets the theoretical lower bound on lossless compression: $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ bits/symbol.

✅ **HL7 v2.x** (pipe-delimited) is the dominant clinical messaging standard; **FHIR** (RESTful JSON) is the modern successor. **DICOM** carries images; **HL7** carries clinical context.

✅ **IHE Scheduled Workflow (SWF)** = MWL + MPPS + Storage Commitment + PIR. The standard imaging workflow.

✅ **DICOMweb** = WADO-RS (Retrieve), QIDO-RS (Query), STOW-RS (Store) — RESTful DICOM for modern web/mobile. Uses **DICOM JSON** or **DICOM XML** for metadata.

✅ **Security in DICOM**: TLS for transport (port 11112), application-level de-identification, DICOM **Basic Application Level Confidentiality Profile** (PS 3.15 Annex E) — removes/remaps PHI tags.

✅ **Teleradiology** = WAN PACS access. Uses **DICOM routers** and **lossless compression** to manage bandwidth.

✅ **Common DICOM SOP Class UIDs to memorize**:
- CT Image Storage: `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2`
- MR Image Storage: `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.4`
- CR Image Storage: `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.1`
- Ultrasound Image Storage: `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.6.1`
- Study Root Q/R Find: `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.2.2.1`
- Study Root Q/R Move: `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.2.2.2`
- Modality Worklist Find: `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.2.3.1`
- Verification (Echo): `1.2.840.10008.1.1`

✅ **C-STORE status codes to memorize**: `0x0000` Success, `0xA700`/`0xA701` Refused Out of Resources, `0x0110` Processing Failure, `0x0122` SOP Class Not Supported, `0x0124` Not Authorized.

✅ **File-size formula** to remember: $F = H + W \times H_{px} \times D \times N_f$ bytes.

✅ **Transfer time formula**: $T = \dfrac{F \times 8}{B \times \eta}$ seconds, with $\eta \approx 0.7$–$0.85$ for TCP.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Management: Archiving
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Image Management: Archiving

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Medical Image Archiving

**Medical Image Archiving** refers to the systematic acquisition, storage, organization, retrieval, and long-term preservation of digital medical images and associated metadata within a healthcare enterprise. In the context of the **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** standard, archiving encompasses the entire data lifecycle — from the moment an image is generated by a modality (CT, MRI, US, X-ray) to its eventual migration, backup, or deletion after the legally mandated retention period.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, image archiving is the **central nervous system of a PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System)**. It is governed by the **IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise)** radiology workflow profile and conforms to standards like **DICOM PS3.4 (Storage Service Class)**, **DICOM PS3.10 (Media Storage)**, and **HL7 (Health Level Seven)** messaging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The core components of archiving include the **Archive Server**, **DICOM C-STORE / C-FIND / C-MOVE / C-GET Service Classes**, **Storage Hierarchy (Online / Nearline / Offline)**, **RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks) configurations**, and **compression protocols** (JPEG, JPEG-LS, JPEG 2000, Wavelet).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of a medical image archive as the **"Central Library of a Hospital"**:

- The **modality (CT/MRI scanner)** is the **newspaper printing press** — it generates a huge volume of raw data every day.
- The **PACS server** is the **library's main desk and catalog system** — it receives the "newspapers," stamps them with an ID, and files them in the right section.
- The **short-term storage (Online)** is the **library's reading room shelf** — fast access, recently used books, smaller capacity.
- The **long-term storage (Nearline/Offline)** is the **basement vault** — slower access, huge capacity, used less often but preserved for years.
- The **DICOM standard** is the **universal cataloging rule (like the Dewey Decimal System)** — it ensures any radiologist in any hospital can find and read the image correctly.
- **Compression** is like **microfilming the newspapers** to save space — lossless means you can read every word clearly, lossy means the print is slightly blurry but readable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics Used in Archiving:**
> - Average CT study size: **~150 MB**
> - Average MRI study size: **~300 MB**
> - Average CR/DX X-ray: **~25 MB**
> - Average Mammography (digital): **~60 MB per view**
> - Typical hospital annual data growth: **10–20 TB**
> - Legal retention period (Kerala/India): **minimum 5 years** (radiology), up to **patient lifetime + 5 years** for some records.

### 1.3 Why Image Archiving Matters

| Aspect | Without Proper Archiving | With PACS-Based Archiving |
|--------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Physical Storage** | Tens of thousands of film jackets | Zero physical film |
| **Retrieval Time** | 30 minutes – 2 days | 2–5 seconds |
| **Lost Studies** | 8–15% lost rate | < 0.5% |
| **Duplicate Studies** | 20–30% redundant | < 2% |
| **Cost per Study** | ₹350–₹500 (film + storage) | ₹40–₹60 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PACS Architecture and Archive Components

The **PACS architecture** for archiving follows a **three-tier hierarchical model**:

1. **Tier 1 — Acquisition Tier (Modality Gateway)**
   - Modality Worklist (MWL) server
   - DICOM C-STORE SCP (Service Class Provider)
   - Converts proprietary vendor formats to DICOM

2. **Tier 2 — Archive/Management Tier**
   - **Archive Server** (the heart of archiving)
   - **Database Server** (patient/study/series/image hierarchy)
   - **Storage Infrastructure** (RAID, NAS, SAN, cloud)
   - **DICOM Router / Prefetcher**

3. **Tier 3 — Display/Distribution Tier**
   - Diagnostic Workstations
   - Web-based viewers
   - Mobile/tablet viewers
   - Hanging Protocol engine

### 2.2 DICOM Information Model for Archiving

The DICOM information model is a **4-level hierarchical structure**:

```
Patient (Level 0)
   └── Study (Level 1)        [one examination event]
         └── Series (Level 2)   [one acquisition sequence]
               └── Image (Level 3) [one file / instance]
```

Each level has a unique identifier:
- **Patient ID (0010,0020)**
- **Study Instance UID (0020,000D)**
- **Series Instance UID (0020,000E)**
- **SOP Instance UID (0008,0018)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why UID-based hierarchy?** UIDs are globally unique 64-character strings (e.g., `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2.12345.67890`). This guarantees no two images anywhere in the world can have the same identifier — a critical property for cross-enterprise archive exchange.

### 2.3 DICOM Storage Service Class (The Heart of Archiving)

The **DICOM Storage Service Class (PS3.4 Section B)** defines how images are pushed to an archive:

**C-STORE-RQ (Request) Message Flow:**
1. **Association Request** — SCU (client) proposes SOP Class + Transfer Syntax to SCP (archive).
2. **Association Accept** — SCP agrees.
3. **C-STORE-RQ** — SCU sends a single image with all metadata.
4. **C-STORE-RSP** — SCP returns Status (Success = `0x0000`, Failure codes).
5. **Association Release** — TCP/IP connection closed.

### 2.4 Compression Techniques Used in Archiving

| Compression Type | Standard | Compression Ratio | Diagnostic Use | DICOM Transfer Syntax UID |
|------------------|----------|-------------------|----------------|---------------------------|
| **Uncompressed** | RAW / Explicit VR Little Endian | 1:1 | Diagnostic mammography (some) | 1.2.840.10008.1.2.1 |
| **JPEG Lossless** | JPEG-LS (ISO 14495-1) | ~2:1 | Diagnostic | 1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.70 |
| **JPEG Baseline (Lossy)** | JPEG | 10:1 to 30:1 | Reference/screening | 1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.50 |
| **JPEG 2000 Lossless** | ISO 15444-1 | ~2.5:1 | Diagnostic | 1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.90 |
| **JPEG 2000 Lossy** | ISO 15444-1 | 10:1 to 40:1 | Diagnostic (configurable) | 1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.91 |
| **RLE Lossless** | DICOM RLE | ~2:1 | Diagnostic | 1.2.840.10008.1.2.5 |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|-----------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Compression Ratio (CR)** | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Dimensionless; CR = 10 means 10× smaller |
| **Percentage Saving (S)** | $S = \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{Compressed Size}}{\text{Original Size}}\right) \times 100\%$ | Expressed in % |
| **Bits per Pixel Storage** | $B_{total} = W \times H \times B_{depth}$ | bytes if $B_{depth}$ in bytes |
| **Storage Capacity (RAID 5)** | $C_{usable} = \dfrac{N-1}{N} \times C_{disk} \times N$ | N disks, single parity |
| **RAID 6 Usable Capacity** | $C_{usable} = \dfrac{N-2}{N} \times C_{total}$ | Double parity |
| **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_i}$ | Reliability metric |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\sigma_{signal}^{2}}{\sigma_{noise}^{2}}\right)$ | Quality metric for lossy |
| **PSNR** | $\text{PSNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{L_{max}}{\text{RMSE}}\right)$ | L = max pixel value |
| **RMSE** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{N}[I(i,j) - \hat{I}(i,j)]^2}$ | Reconstruction error |
| **Archive Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{\text{Number of Studies}}{\text{Time Interval}}$ | Studies per hour |
| **Storage Hierarchy Access Time** | $t_{avg} = p_1 t_1 + p_2 t_2 + p_3 t_3$ | $p_1 + p_2 + p_3 = 1$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud-based archiving** (e.g., AWS HealthLake, Google Cloud Healthcare API) allows hospitals to outsource storage.
- **Vendor Neutral Archive (VNA)** decouples the archive from any single PACS vendor, providing long-term interoperability.
- **DICOMweb (PS3.18)** uses RESTful APIs (WADO-RS, STOW-RS, QIDO-RS) for modern web-based archiving.
- **Tiered storage (HSM - Hierarchical Storage Management)** automatically migrates older studies from SSD → HDD → Tape → Cloud.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Effective Storage Capacity for RAID Arrays

**Problem:** A PACS archive uses **6 disks of 2 TB each in a RAID 5 configuration**. Compute the usable storage, the percentage of overhead, and the maximum simultaneous disk failures tolerated.

**Step 1: Identify RAID 5 Parity Configuration**
In RAID 5, **one disk worth of capacity is consumed by distributed parity** for every disk set. For $N$ disks, the parity occupies the equivalent of **1 disk**.

$$
\text{Parity Disks Equivalent} = 1
$$

**Step 2: Compute Usable Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total\_raw} &= N \times C_{disk} \\
&= 6 \times 2 \text{ TB} \\
&= 12 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{usable} &= (N-1) \times C_{disk} \\
&= (6-1) \times 2 \text{ TB} \\
&= 5 \times 2 \text{ TB} \\
&= 10 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Overhead Percentage**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%_{overhead} &= \dfrac{C_{parity}}{C_{total\_raw}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{1}{N} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{1}{6} \times 100 \\
&= 16.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Failure Tolerance**
RAID 5 tolerates **1 simultaneous disk failure** (single parity). If two disks fail simultaneously, data is lost.

$$
\text{MTTDL}_{\text{RAID5}} \approx \dfrac{\text{MTBF}_{disk}^{2}}{N \times (N-1) \times \text{MTTR}^{2}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Compression Ratio and Space Saved

**Problem:** A 16-bit CT slice of size $512 \times 512$ pixels is compressed using JPEG-LS at a ratio of 2.8:1. Calculate the original file size, the compressed size, and the percentage storage saving.

**Step 1: Compute Original (Uncompressed) Size**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{total} &= W \times H \times B_{depth} \\
&= 512 \times 512 \times 2 \text{ bytes} \quad [\text{since 16-bit} = 2 \text{ bytes}] \\
&= 524288 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 512 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Compressed Size**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{compressed} &= \dfrac{C_{original}}{\text{CR}} \\
&= \dfrac{512 \text{ KB}}{2.8} \\
&= 182.86 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Percentage Saving**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \left(1 - \dfrac{C_{compressed}}{C_{original}}\right) \times 100\% \\
&= \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2.8}\right) \times 100\% \\
&= \left(1 - 0.3571\right) \times 100\% \\
&= 64.29\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Annual Storage Impact**
If a hospital performs **40,000 CT studies/year**, with an average of **300 slices per study**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Uncompressed} &= 40000 \times 300 \times 512 \text{ KB} \\
&= 6 \times 10^{9} \text{ KB} \\
&\approx 5.59 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Compressed} &= \dfrac{5.59}{2.8} \\
&\approx 2.0 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of DICOM Storage SOP Class Verification

The **Storage Service Class** uses the following status code logic:

$$
\text{Status Code} = \begin{cases} 0x0000 & \text{Success} \\ 0xA700 & \text{Out of Resources} \\ 0xA701 & \text{Out of Resources (SOP Class)} \\ 0xC000 & \text{Cannot Understand} \\ 0x0110 & \text{Processing Failure} \end{cases}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Average Archive Retrieval Time (3-Tier Hierarchy)

**Problem:** A PACS has:
- **Tier 1 (Online SSD):** $p_1 = 0.20$, $t_1 = 0.5$ s
- **Tier 2 (Nearline HDD):** $p_2 = 0.50$, $t_2 = 4$ s
- **Tier 3 (Offline Tape/Cloud):** $p_3 = 0.30$, $t_3 = 30$ s

Compute average retrieval time $t_{avg}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{avg} &= p_1 t_1 + p_2 t_2 + p_3 t_3 \\
&= (0.20)(0.5) + (0.50)(4) + (0.30)(30) \\
&= 0.10 + 2.0 + 9.0 \\
&= 11.1 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Derivation of PSNR for Lossy Compression Validation

**Problem:** A JPEG 2000 compressed chest X-ray (12-bit, max value = 4095) yields an RMSE of 1.8 against original. Compute PSNR.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PSNR} &= 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{L_{max}}{\text{RMSE}}\right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4095}{1.8}\right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}(2275) \\
&= 20 \times 3.357 \\
&= 67.14 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Benchmark:** Diagnostic-quality compressed images typically require PSNR $\geq$ **50 dB**. Values below **40 dB** are considered unacceptable for primary diagnosis.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Compression Trade-Off

| Method | Ratio | PSNR (dB) | Diagnostic OK? | Archive Use Case |
|--------|-------|-----------|----------------|-------------------|
| JPEG 2000 Lossless | 2.5:1 | $\infty$ | ✅ Yes | Primary diagnostic archive |
| JPEG-LS Lossless | 2.1:1 | $\infty$ | ✅ Yes | CT/MRI primary archive |
| JPEG 2000 (20:1) | 20:1 | 52 | ✅ Yes | Web/screening |
| JPEG Baseline (10:1) | 10:1 | 38 | ⚠️ Limited | Reference images only |
| JPEG Baseline (30:1) | 30:1 | 32 | ❌ No | Thumbnails only |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of PACS Archive

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Modality["Modality Scanner CT MRI CR US"]:::mod
    MWL["Modality Worklist MWL Server HL7 Feed"]:::mod
    QC["DICOM Router and QA Station"]:::core
    Archive["PACS Archive Server DICOM C-STORE SCP"]:::core
    DB["Patient Database Study Series Index"]:::core
    Tier1["Tier 1 Online SSD 0 to 6 months"]:::tier
    Tier2["Tier 2 Nearline HDD 6 months to 3 years"]:::tier
    Tier3["Tier 3 Offline Tape or Cloud 3 plus years"]:::tier
    Backup["Disaster Recovery Site Replication"]:::sec
    Viewer["Diagnostic Workstation Web Viewer Mobile"]:::display
    VNA["Vendor Neutral Archive VNA"]:::core
    classDef mod fill:#ffd166,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tier fill:#118ab2,stroke:#0c5d7a,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef sec fill:#ef476f,stroke:#b91c4c,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef display fill:#c77dff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    Modality --> QC
    MWL --> Modality
    QC --> Archive
    Archive --> DB
    Archive --> Tier1
    Tier1 --> Tier2
    Tier2 --> Tier3
    Archive --> VNA
    VNA --> Viewer
    Tier3 --> Backup
```

### 4.2 DICOM C-STORE Storage Workflow Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Modality as Modality SCU
    participant Archive as PACS Archive SCP
    participant DB as Archive Database
    participant Storage as Tiered Storage
    Modality->>Archive: A-ASSOCIATE-RQ Proposed Presentation Contexts
    Archive-->>Modality: A-ASSOCIATE-AC Accepted
    Modality->>Archive: C-STORE-RQ Image Instance
    Archive->>DB: Validate Patient Study Series UID
    DB-->>Archive: Validation OK
    Archive->>Storage: Write to Tier 1 SSD
    Storage-->>Archive: Write Confirmation
    Archive->>DB: Update Index
    Archive-->>Modality: C-STORE-RSP Status 0x0000 Success
    Archive-->>Modality: A-RELEASE-RQ
    Modality-->>Archive: A-RELEASE-RP
```

### 4.3 Storage Hierarchy and Access-Time Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Newly Acquired Images] --> B["Tier 1 Online SSD Capacity 5 TB Access 0.5 s"]
    B -->|"After 6 months"| C["Tier 2 Nearline HDD Capacity 50 TB Access 4 s"]
    C -->|"After 3 years"| D["Tier 3 Offline Tape or Cloud Capacity Unlimited Access 30 s"]
    D -->|"After 10 years"| E["Secure Deletion or Permanent Archive"]
    classDef hot fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef warm fill:#ffd166,stroke:#b08900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cold fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#1864ab,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef del fill:#dee2e6,stroke:#495057,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class B hot
    class C warm
    class D cold
    class E del
```

### 4.4 DICOM Information Model Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P["Patient Level 0 Patient ID 0010 0020 Patient Name"] --> S["Study Level 1 Study Instance UID 0020 000D Study Date Accession Number"]
    S --> SE["Series Level 2 Series Instance UID 0020 000E Modality Series Number"]
    SE --> I["Image Level 3 SOP Instance UID 0008 0018 Instance Number Pixel Data"]
    classDef lvl0 fill:#ef476f,stroke:#b91c4c,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef lvl1 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#b08900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lvl2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lvl3 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#0c5d7a,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    class P lvl0
    class S lvl1
    class SE lvl2
    class I lvl3
```

### 4.5 Compression Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start["DICOM Image Ready for Archive"] --> Check{"Is it a Mammogram or Primary Diagnostic?"}
    Check -->|"Yes"| Lossless["Use JPEG-LS or JPEG 2000 Lossless"]
    Check -->|"No"| Type{"Use Case?"}
    Type -->|"Diagnostic"| CompLossless["JPEG 2000 Lossy 5 to 10 ratio PSNR above 50 dB"]
    Type -->|"Reference"| CompLossy["JPEG Baseline 20 to 30 ratio"]
    Type -->|"Web Mobile"| Heavy["JPEG 2000 or Wavelet 50 to 100 ratio"]
    Lossless --> Archive1["Archive at original fidelity"]
    CompLossless --> Archive2["Archive compressed diagnostic"]
    CompLossy --> Archive3["Archive reference only"]
    Heavy --> Archive4["Archive display only"]
    classDef q fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#fab005,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class Archive1,Archive2,Archive3,Archive4 s
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define PACS and list its four main components used in archiving.**

**Model Answer:**

**PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System)** is an integrated medical informatics system that acquires, stores, retrieves, distributes, and displays digital medical images using computer networks and standardized protocols (DICOM / HL7).

The **four main components** used in archiving are:

1. **Image Acquisition Modalities** — CT, MRI, CR, US, X-ray machines with DICOM conformance.
2. **Archive Server (Database + Storage)** — Central DICOM C-STORE SCP that stores studies in tiered storage.
3. **Network Infrastructure** — High-speed LAN (typically $\geq$ **1 Gbps**) with secure VPN for WAN archival.
4. **Display/Review Workstations** — Diagnostic and clinical viewing stations with retrieval capability.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for components)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Lossless and Lossy compression in the context of medical image archiving. Give one DICOM example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Lossless Compression | Lossy Compression |
|---------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Definition** | Original image can be **exactly reconstructed** from compressed data | Original image **cannot be exactly reconstructed**; some pixel data lost |
| **Compression Ratio** | Low (typically **2:1 to 3:1**) | High (**10:1 to 100:1**) |
| **Diagnostic Quality** | Always preserved | Depends on ratio; risky at high ratios |
| **File Extension** | Smaller reduction | Significantly smaller |
| **DICOM Example** | **JPEG-LS (1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.70)** | **JPEG Baseline (1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.50)** |
| **Use Case** | Mammography, primary diagnosis | Web viewing, thumbnails, references |

*(3 marks: tabulated differentiation with DICOM UIDs)*

---

### Part B — Full-Question Solutions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a PACS archive system** and the role of **DICOM Storage Service Class** in archiving. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A hospital uses **8 disks of 4 TB each in a RAID 6 configuration** for the PACS archive. Calculate the **usable storage capacity, the percentage of overhead, and the number of simultaneous disk failures tolerated**. Discuss the advantage of RAID 6 over RAID 5 for a PACS archive. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution (a) — PACS Archive Architecture:**

The PACS archive architecture consists of **three tiers**:

**Tier 1 — Acquisition Tier:**
- Modalities (CT, MRI, CR, US) generate DICOM images.
- A **Modality Worklist (MWL)** server feeds patient demographic data via HL7.
- A **DICOM Router** receives C-STORE requests from modalities.

**Tier 2 — Archive/Management Tier:**
- The **Archive Server** is the central SCP (Service Class Provider) running DICOM Storage SOP Class.
- It validates the **Patient → Study → Series → Image hierarchy** (4-level DICOM model).
- It writes images to **Tiered Storage** (Online SSD → Nearline HDD → Offline Tape/Cloud).
- A **Database Server** maintains the indices for retrieval via C-FIND/C-MOVE.

**Tier 3 — Display/Distribution Tier:**
- Diagnostic workstations retrieve images using **DICOM WADO** or **C-MOVE**.

**Role of DICOM Storage Service Class:**
- Defines the **C-STORE operation** as the protocol for pushing images from an SCU (modality) to an SCP (archive).
- Uses **SOP Class UIDs** (e.g., `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2` for CT Image Storage) to identify image type.
- Uses **Transfer Syntax UIDs** to negotiate compression (JPEG, JPEG 2000, RLE).
- Returns **Status Codes** (0x0000 = Success, 0xA700 = Out of Resources) for verification.

*(7 marks: 3 for diagram description, 2 for tier explanation, 2 for DICOM Storage role)*

---

**Solution (b) — RAID 6 Storage Calculation:**

**Given:** $N = 8$ disks, $C_{disk} = 4$ TB, RAID 6.

**Step 1: Total Raw Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total\_raw} &= 8 \times 4 \text{ TB} = 32 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Usable Capacity (RAID 6 reserves 2 disks for double parity)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{usable} &= (N - 2) \times C_{disk} \\
&= (8 - 2) \times 4 \text{ TB} \\
&= 6 \times 4 \text{ TB} \\
&= 24 \text{ TB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Overhead Percentage**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%_{overhead} &= \dfrac{2}{8} \times 100 = 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Failure Tolerance**

$$
\text{Failures tolerated} = 2 \text{ simultaneous disk failures}
$$

**Step 5: Comparison — RAID 6 vs RAID 5 for PACS**

| Feature | RAID 5 | RAID 6 |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Parity disks | 1 | 2 |
| Failure tolerance | 1 disk | 2 disks |
| Overhead (for 8 disks) | 12.5% | **25%** |
| Rebuild risk during single failure | High (URE risk) | **Low (protected)** |
| Suitability for PACS | Acceptable | **Recommended for clinical archive** |

**Conclusion:** For a PACS archive, RAID 6 is preferred because during a **disk rebuild after single-disk failure**, RAID 5 is vulnerable to a second simultaneous failure (a **URE — Unrecoverable Read Error**). RAID 6's double parity protects the archive during this critical rebuild window, which is essential for irreplaceable medical images.

*(7 marks: 2 for calculation of capacity, 1 for overhead, 1 for failure tolerance, 3 for RAID 6 vs RAID 5 discussion)*

---

#### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 14 Marks] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** Explain the **DICOM 4-level information model** used in archiving. Sketch the hierarchy and explain the role of **SOP Instance UID** and **Study Instance UID**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A CT slice of size **$1024 \times 1024$ pixels** with **16-bit depth** is compressed using **JPEG 2000 Lossless at ratio 2.5:1**, then archived. Compute the **(i) original size, (ii) compressed size, (iii) percentage storage saving, and (iv) PSNR** (assume RMSE = 0 for lossless). Comment on why lossless is preferred for primary diagnostic archive. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution (a) — DICOM Information Model:**

The DICOM information model organizes image data in **four hierarchical levels**:

**Level 0 — Patient:**
- Contains demographic information.
- Key tag: **Patient ID (0010,0020)**.

**Level 1 — Study:**
- Represents one imaging examination (e.g., "Chest CT done on 12-May-2024").
- Key tag: **Study Instance UID (0020,000D)** — unique across enterprise.

**Level 2 — Series:**
- A logical grouping of images from one acquisition (e.g., axial slices, or contrast phases).
- Key tag: **Series Instance UID (0020,000E)**.

**Level 3 — Image/Instance:**
- The actual pixel data file.
- Key tag: **SOP Instance UID (0008,0018)** — unique worldwide per image.

**Hierarchy Sketch (textual):**

```
Patient (0010,0020)
   └── Study (0020,000D)
         └── Series (0020,000E)
               └── Image / SOP Instance (0008,0018)
```

**Role of SOP Instance UID (0008,0018):**
- Globally unique identifier for **each individual image**.
- Used to retrieve a single image from the archive via **C-MOVE** or **WADO-RS**.

**Role of Study Instance UID (0020,000D):**
- Uniquely identifies **one imaging study** within the enterprise.
- Used to **retrieve an entire study** (e.g., all 300 slices of a CT chest) from the archive in one operation.

*(7 marks: 3 for 4-level model, 2 for UID roles, 2 for sketch)*

---

**Solution (b) — Compression Calculation:**

**Given:** $W = 1024$, $H = 1024$, $B_{depth} = 16$ bits $= 2$ bytes, CR $= 2.5:1$.

**(i) Original Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{original} &= 1024 \times 1024 \times 2 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 2 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Compressed Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{compressed} &= \dfrac{C_{original}}{\text{CR}} = \dfrac{2 \text{ MB}}{2.5} = 0.8 \text{ MB} = 819.2 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage Saving:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2.5}\right) \times 100\% \\
&= \left(1 - 0.4\right) \times 100\% \\
&= 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) PSNR (Lossless, RMSE = 0):**

For a **lossless compression**, RMSE = 0, which means the reconstruction is mathematically identical to the original.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PSNR} &= 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{L_{max}}{\text{RMSE}}\right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4095}{0}\right) \\
&= \infty \text{ dB (mathematically perfect)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment — Why Lossless is Preferred for Primary Diagnostic Archive:**

1. **Pixel-exact reconstruction** preserves subtle pathology (e.g., microcalcifications in mammography, small pulmonary nodules in CT).
2. **Medico-legal requirement:** Original images must be reproducible for any future audit or expert review.
3. **Regulatory compliance:** Most regulatory bodies (ACR, AERB, IRIA) recommend or mandate lossless archival for **primary diagnostic images**.
4. **No diagnostic degradation** with repeated recompression or post-processing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing PSNR as a finite number for lossless — it is mathematically **infinite** (RMSE = 0).
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **SOP Instance UID** with **Series Instance UID** — SOP = image, Series = collection.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Computing usable RAID capacity incorrectly — students often forget that **RAID 6 reserves 2 disks**, not 1.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Saying "JPEG compression is lossless" — JPEG is **always lossy**; the lossless variant is **JPEG-LS** (a different standard).
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Missing the DICOM UID format — they must be globally unique **64-character alphanumeric strings** starting with the country/organization root (e.g., `1.2.840.10008...`).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PACS** = three-tier architecture (Acquisition → Archive → Display).
- **DICOM** is the lingua franca of medical image archiving — uses 4-level model: **Patient → Study → Series → Image**.
- **Archive Server** runs the **DICOM Storage Service Class (C-STORE)** as an SCP.
- **SOP Class UID** identifies the image type; **Transfer Syntax UID** identifies compression.
- **Storage Hierarchy:** Online (SSD) → Nearline (HDD) → Offline (Tape/Cloud) — driven by access frequency and age.
- **RAID 5** = single parity (1 disk overhead, 1 failure tolerance).
- **RAID 6** = double parity (2 disks overhead, 2 failures tolerated) — **recommended for PACS**.
- **Lossless Compression (JPEG-LS / JPEG 2000 Lossless):** ~2–2.5:1 ratio, infinite PSNR, used for **primary diagnostic archive**.
- **Lossy Compression (JPEG / JPEG 2000 Lossy):** 10:1 to 100:1 ratio, PSNR must be ≥ **50 dB** for diagnostic use.
- **VNA (Vendor Neutral Archive)** ensures long-term interoperability across PACS vendor upgrades.
- **DICOMweb (WADO-RS, STOW-RS, QIDO-RS)** is the modern RESTful API for cloud and web archiving.
- **Legal retention period (India):** minimum **5 years**; pediatric/oncology often **patient lifetime + 5 years**.
- **HIPAA / DISHA (India Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act)** compliance requires encryption at rest and in transit.
- **Disaster Recovery:** every archive must have **offsite replication** (3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Systems in a Hospital
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Information Systems in a Hospital

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hospital Information System (HIS)** is a comprehensive, integrated, computer-based information management system designed to capture, store, manipulate, retrieve, analyze, and communicate all clinical, administrative, financial, and operational data associated with the functioning of a modern hospital. It is engineered to streamline workflows, ensure data integrity, support clinical decision-making, and provide a unified digital backbone that connects every department — from Out-Patient (OP) counters and In-Patient (IP) wards to the Radiology Department, the Pathology Laboratory, the Pharmacy, and the Billing Section.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBMT755 – Module 4)**, the term is broadened to encompass the entire ecosystem of sub-systems, which includes:

- **Hospital Information System (HIS)** — the administrative/clinical master system.
- **Radiology Information System (RIS)** — manages imaging orders, scheduling, and reporting.
- **Picture Archiving and Communication System (PACS)** — archives, retrieves, displays, and distributes medical images.
- **Laboratory Information System (LIS)** — handles pathology, biochemistry, and microbiology data.
- **Electronic Medical Record (EMR) / Electronic Health Record (EHR)** — longitudinal patient records.
- **Pharmacy Information System (PIS)** — drug inventory, dispensing, and interaction checks.
- **Computerized Physician Order Entry (CPOE)** — electronic ordering by physicians.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly positions Hospital Information Systems as the "digital nervous system" of a hospital — it is NOT merely billing software. The examiner expects students to understand it as a *networked, standards-based, multi-modular architecture* that delivers the *right information, to the right person, in the right format, at the right time* — the classic *4R paradigm* of healthcare informatics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart City" of a Hospital

Imagine a **huge smart city** that has to operate 24×7, serve thousands of citizens (patients), coordinate hundreds of service providers (doctors, nurses, technicians), keep flawless financial accounts, and never lose a single citizen's identity record even after 50 years.

In this smart city:
- The **central command center** (city hall) = the **HIS Server / Database**.
- The **postal service** that delivers letters between buildings = the **Hospital Network (LAN/WAN)**.
- The **standardized envelope format** used by every post office = the **HL7 / DICOM / IHE standards**.
- The **traffic signals and GPS** that route ambulances = the **Workflow Engine**.
- The **city archive / library** = the **PACS / Long-term Storage Archive**.
- The **citizen ID card** = the **Unique Patient Identifier (UHID/Patient ID)**.
- The **law that every building must speak a common language** = the **IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise) framework**.

Just as a smart city cannot function if its buildings speak different languages, a hospital cannot function if its Radiology, Pathology, and Pharmacy systems cannot "talk" to each other. The HIS is the **digital constitution, post office, and central registry** rolled into one.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Sub-Systems (KTU Favorite):** "**HARP-LP**" — **H**IS, **A**dministrative, **R**IS, **P**ACS, **L**IS, **P**harmacy. This is frequently asked as a 3-mark definition in KTU ESE.

## 1.3 Core Metrics and Standards (Must be in **bold**)

| Metric / Standard | Value / Specification |
|---|---|
| **DICOM** | **Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine** — global standard for medical images |
| **HL7** | **Health Level Seven** — standard for textual clinical/administrative data exchange |
| **IHE** | **Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise** — integration profile framework |
| **ICD-10/11** | **International Classification of Diseases** — diagnosis coding |
| **LOINC** | **Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes** — lab test coding |
| **SNOMED-CT** | **Systematized Nomenclature of Medicine — Clinical Terms** |
| **HIPAA** | **Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act** — privacy/security (US origin, globally referenced) |
| **UHID** | **Unique Health Identifier** — 14–16 digit patient ID |
| **Typical HIS Downtime Budget** | **< 0.1% (≈ 4 minutes/month)** |
| **Mean uptime requirement** | **99.9% (Three-Nines availability)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Hospital Information System

The HIS is organized as a **multi-tier client–server architecture**, typically realized as a **3-Tier** or **N-Tier** system.

### 2.1.1 The Three Tiers

- **Tier 1 — Presentation Layer (Front-End / Client Tier)**
  - Web-based portals, mobile apps, kiosk terminals, departmental workstations.
  - Functions: data entry, result viewing, order placement.
  - Technologies: HTML5, React/Angular, HL7 FHIR web interfaces.

- **Tier 2 — Application / Logic Layer (Middle Tier)**
  - Business rules engine, workflow manager, integration engine (Mirth/Rhapsody/InterSystems Ensemble).
  - Functions: validates input, enforces protocols, routes HL7/DICOM messages, triggers alerts.
  - Technologies: Java/.NET/Python servers, REST/SOAP APIs.

- **Tier 3 — Data Layer (Database Tier)**
  - Centralized **RDBMS** (Oracle, MS SQL Server, PostgreSQL) + **DICOM Archive** (often a separate vendor-neutral archive — VNA).
  - Functions: persistent storage, ACID transactions, indexing, backup, encryption at rest.
  - Technologies: RAID-6/RAID-10 storage, SAN/NAS, tape-based long-term archive.

### 2.1.2 Functional Modules (The HARP-LP decomposition, expanded)

- **Administrative Module** — Registration, Appointment Scheduling, OP/IP management, Bed Management, Billing & Insurance (TPA) claims.
- **Clinical Module** — EMR, CPOE, Nursing notes, Treatment plans, E-Prescription.
- **Imaging Module — RIS + PACS**
  - *RIS* handles: order entry, modality worklist (MWL), scheduling, report sign-off, billing of imaging procedures.
  - *PACS* handles: DICOM image acquisition, short/long-term archive, diagnostic workstation display (DICOM-conformant), teleradiology.
- **Laboratory Module (LIS)** — Sample accession, analyzer interfacing, QC, delta checks, critical-value alerts.
- **Pharmacy Module** — Stock management, barcode-based dispensing, drug–drug interaction engine, Narcotic register.
- **Support Module** — HR, Payroll, Inventory, Equipment maintenance (CMMS), Biomedical waste tracking.
- **Decision Support Module** — Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS), Drug dosage calculators, Sepsis early-warning scores.

## 2.2 Why the System is Needed — The "Why" behind each Layer

- **Centralized patient ID** — prevents duplicate records, ensures longitudinal continuity (a patient seen in 2010 and 2024 must be recognized as the same person).
- **Standards-based messaging (HL7/DICOM)** — allows heterogeneous vendors (GE, Siemens, Philips, Roche) to interoperate.
- **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** — receptionist cannot read MRI images; a junior doctor cannot sign off a CT report.
- **Audit trail** — every access, modification, and deletion is logged with timestamp and user ID (HIPAA / DISHA / ABDM compliance).
- **Disaster Recovery (DR)** — geographically separated mirror site with RPO ≤ 15 min and RTO ≤ 1 hour (typical KTU/industry standard).

## 2.3 Key Engineering Metrics — The "How" of HIS Performance

- **Throughput:** measured in *Transactions Per Second (TPS)*. A 500-bed hospital typically generates **50–150 HL7 messages per minute** at peak.
- **Latency:** end-to-end message delivery < **2 seconds** for OP queries.
- **Storage Growth:** dominated by imaging — see formula sheet below.
- **Network:** HIS backbone usually **10 Gbps fibre** with **1 Gbps to desk**, dedicated **VLANs** for PACS traffic.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Numerical Questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners often ask 3-mark or 14-mark numericals** based on the following formulas. Memorize the **units** carefully.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Image Storage per Study** | $S_{img} = R \times R \times B \times \frac{N}{8}$ bytes | MB per slice | Estimating PACS storage budget |
| 2 | **Study Storage (Multi-slice)** | $S_{study} = \sum_{k=1}^{N_{slices}} S_{img,k} + H$ | MB / study | Capacity planning |
| 3 | **Annual PACS Storage Growth** | $S_{annual} = N_{studies} \times S_{study} \times 365$ | TB / year | Procurement of NAS/SAN |
| 4 | **Compression Saving** | $S_{compressed} = S_{original} \times (1 - C_{ratio})$ | MB | DICOM JPEG 2000 / JPEG-LS |
| 5 | **Network Transfer Time** | $T_{transfer} = \dfrac{S_{file}}{BW_{effective}}$ | seconds | Teleradiology link sizing |
| 6 | **Compression Ratio (CR)** | $CR = 1 - \dfrac{S_{compressed}}{S_{original}}$ | dimensionless | DICOM lossy vs lossless decision |
| 7 | **Total HIS HL7 Throughput** | $TPS_{HIS} = \dfrac{M_{HL7}}{t_{window}}$ | messages/s | Server sizing |
| 8 | **Availability (Three-Nines)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | % | SLA compliance |
| 9 | **RTO / RPO Math** | $RPO = T_{last\_backup} - T_{disaster}$ | time | DR strategy cost trade-off |
| 10 | **RAID-5 Usable Capacity** | $C_{usable} = (N-1) \times C_{disk}$ | TB | PACS archive sizing |
| 11 | **RAID-6 Usable Capacity** | $C_{usable} = (N-2) \times C_{disk}$ | TB | Higher fault tolerance |
| 12 | **Data Redundancy (3-2-1 Rule)** | 3 copies, 2 media, 1 off-site | — | Backup policy |

### 2.4.1 Variable Definitions (use \mid for absolute value if needed)

- $R$ = Image matrix rows (e.g., 512 for CT, 1024 for CR)
- $R$ = Image matrix columns (same convention; we use $R \times R$ for square matrices)
- $B$ = Bit depth (typically 12 or 16 bits for DICOM)
- $N$ = Number of slices / frames
- $H$ = DICOM header overhead (≈ 1–2 KB per file)
- $C_{ratio}$ = Compression ratio expressed as a fraction (e.g., 0.6 for 60% size reduction)
- $BW_{effective}$ = Effective bandwidth after protocol overhead
- $M_{HL7}$ = Number of HL7 messages in time window $t_{window}$
- $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures; $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair
- $N$ = Number of disks in RAID array
- $C_{disk}$ = Capacity of a single disk

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility Note:** These formulas are the exact quantitative tools a *Biomedical Engineer* uses when (a) designing a new hospital, (b) preparing a PACS procurement tender (RFP), (c) justifying a budget to hospital management, or (d) troubleshooting slow network complaints from the Radiology department.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual PACS Storage Requirement

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-mark style):**
A 500-bed hospital performs, on average, the following imaging studies per day:
- 60 Digital Radiography (DR) studies — each 2 images, matrix 3000 × 3000, 14-bit depth.
- 25 CT scans — each 512 slices, matrix 512 × 512, 16-bit depth.
- 10 MRI scans — each 300 slices, matrix 256 × 256, 16-bit depth.
- 5 Ultrasound (US) studies — each 30 frames, matrix 800 × 600, 8-bit depth.

Assume **5% protocol overhead** and **lossless JPEG compression with CR = 0.40** (i.e., file is reduced to 40% of original). Calculate the **total annual storage in TB** required for the PACS.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute uncompressed size of a single image (in bytes)**

For a square matrix with bit depth B, the raw pixel data size in bytes is:
$$
S_{raw} = \frac{R \times R \times B}{8} \quad \text{[bytes per image]}
$$

The image size is the same in all modalities for a given $R \times R \times B$.

**Step 2 — Compute the total raw size per modality per day**

The general per-modality-per-day formula is:
$$
S_{modality,day} = N_{studies} \times N_{images/study} \times S_{raw} \times (1 + O_{protocol})
$$

where $O_{protocol} = 0.05$ is the 5% DICOM header/overhead factor.

**Step 3 — Apply the formula to each modality**

For **DR** (matrix 3000 × 3000, 14-bit, 2 images/study, 60 studies/day):
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{DR,raw} &= \frac{3000 \times 3000 \times 14}{8} = \frac{126{,}000{,}000}{8} = 15{,}750{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 15.75 \text{ MB per image} \\
S_{DR,day} &= 60 \times 2 \times 15.75 \times 1.05 = 126 \times 15.75 \times 1.05 \\
&= 1984.5 \times 1.05 = 2083.725 \text{ MB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **CT** (matrix 512 × 512, 16-bit, 512 slices, 25 studies/day):
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{CT,raw} &= \frac{512 \times 512 \times 16}{8} = \frac{4{,}194{,}304}{8} = 524{,}288 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 0.5 \text{ MB per slice} \\
S_{CT,day} &= 25 \times 512 \times 0.5 \times 1.05 = 12800 \times 0.5 \times 1.05 \\
&= 6400 \times 1.05 = 6720 \text{ MB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **MRI** (matrix 256 × 256, 16-bit, 300 slices, 10 studies/day):
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{MRI,raw} &= \frac{256 \times 256 \times 16}{8} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{8} = 131{,}072 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 0.125 \text{ MB per slice} \\
S_{MRI,day} &= 10 \times 300 \times 0.125 \times 1.05 = 3000 \times 0.125 \times 1.05 \\
&= 375 \times 1.05 = 393.75 \text{ MB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Ultrasound** (matrix 800 × 600, 8-bit, 30 frames, 5 studies/day):
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{US,raw} &= \frac{800 \times 600 \times 8}{8} = 480{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 0.4578 \text{ MB per frame} \quad (\approx 0.458 \text{ MB}) \\
S_{US,day} &= 5 \times 30 \times 0.458 \times 1.05 = 150 \times 0.458 \times 1.05 \\
&= 68.7 \times 1.05 = 72.135 \text{ MB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the total raw size per day across all modalities**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total,raw,day} &= 2083.725 + 6720 + 393.75 + 72.135 \\
&= 9269.61 \text{ MB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the lossless compression (CR = 0.40 means file is 40% of original)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total,compressed,day} &= 9269.61 \times 0.40 \\
&= 3707.844 \text{ MB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Annualize (multiply by 365 days)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{annual} &= 3707.844 \times 365 \\
&= 1{,}353{,}363.06 \text{ MB/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to Terabytes (1 TB = 1,048,576 MB = 1024 GB)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{annual,TB} &= \frac{1{,}353{,}363.06}{1{,}048{,}576} \\
&\approx 1.291 \text{ TB/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed for KTU answer sheet):**
$$
\boxed{S_{annual} \approx 1.29 \text{ TB/year (compressed)}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation (mandatory 1–2 lines in KTU answer script):**
With a 5-year retention policy and a **3-2-1 backup**, the hospital must provision a **usable primary archive of ~6.5 TB**, **secondary copy of 6.5 TB**, and an **off-site DR copy of 6.5 TB**, totaling **~19.5 TB over 5 years**. A 30 TB RAID-6 NAS would be the typical procurement recommendation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Teleradiology Link Transfer Time

**Problem Statement:**
A CT study of 200 slices (each 512 × 512 × 16-bit, compressed with CR = 0.50) must be transmitted to a remote interpretation centre over a **dedicated 50 Mbps** link with **25% protocol overhead**. How long does the transfer take?

### 3.2.1 Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the raw slice size in bytes**
$$
S_{slice,raw} = \frac{512 \times 512 \times 16}{8} = 524{,}288 \text{ bytes} = 0.5 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute compressed slice size**
$$
S_{slice,compressed} = 0.5 \times 0.50 = 0.25 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total study size**
$$
S_{study} = 200 \times 0.25 = 50 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for protocol overhead (TCP/IP, TLS encryption, DICOM upper-layer)**
Effective bandwidth:
$$
BW_{eff} = 50 \text{ Mbps} \times (1 - 0.25) = 37.5 \text{ Mbps} = 4.6875 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute transfer time**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{transfer} &= \frac{S_{study}}{BW_{eff}} = \frac{50 \text{ MB}}{4.6875 \text{ MB/s}} \\
&\approx 10.67 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$
\boxed{T_{transfer} \approx 10.67 \text{ s} \;\; (\text{well within the 30 s SLA for urgent tele-CT})}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — HIS Availability Calculation

A HIS has $MTBF = 3000$ hours and $MTTR = 4$ hours. Compute the **availability percentage** and the **maximum allowable annual downtime**.

**Step 1 — Plug into the availability formula**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{3000}{3000 + 4} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{3000}{3004} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 99.867\% \quad \text{(≈ "Two-Nines-plus")}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual downtime**
$$
\text{Downtime} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \text{ h} = 0.00133 \times 8760 \approx 11.65 \text{ h/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to the KTU benchmark**
A 99.9% target (Three-Nines) demands downtime < 8.76 h/year — so this HIS is *just below* the KTU "good practice" bar. Recommendation: deploy a **redundant cluster with automatic failover** to push MTTR below 1 hour.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of HIS Sub-Systems

| Sub-System | Primary Data Type | Standard Used | Key Function | Example Vendor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HIS** | Text / Numerical | HL7 v2.x / FHIR | Master patient index, billing, appointments | Epic, Cerner, MEDITECH |
| **EMR/EHR** | Clinical text, codes | HL7 FHIR, SNOMED-CT, ICD-10 | Longitudinal patient record | Allscripts, Athenahealth |
| **RIS** | Imaging orders, reports | HL7, DICOM Modality Worklist (MWL) | Order scheduling, report sign-off | Siemens syngo Workflow |
| **PACS** | DICOM images | DICOM 3.0 | Image archive, display, distribution | GE Centricity, Carestream |
| **LIS** | Lab results | HL7, ASTM E1394 | Sample tracking, analyzer interface | Roche cobas IT, Abbott |
| **PIS** | Drug inventory, scripts | HL7 | Stock, dispensing, interactions | McKesson, PharmNet |
| **CPOE** | Orders | HL7, NCPDP (pharmacy) | Physician electronic ordering | Cerner CPOE |
| **CDSS** | Decision rules | CDS Hooks (FHIR) | Real-time clinical alerts | IBM Watson Health |
