# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> Both questions are modeled on **KTU University Exam – May 2024** style. Precise 60–80 word model answers are given.

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Hospital Information System (HIS). List any four functional modules of HIS.**

**Model Answer:**
A Hospital Information System (HIS) is an integrated, computer-based information management system designed to capture, store, retrieve, and communicate clinical, administrative, and financial data of a hospital in real time, ensuring the *4R paradigm* (right information, right person, right format, right time).

Four functional modules:
1. **Registration & Appointment Scheduling Module** — assigns UHID and manages OP/IP flow.
2. **Electronic Medical Record (EMR) Module** — maintains longitudinal clinical notes.
3. **Billing & Insurance (TPA) Module** — generates invoices, processes insurance claims.
4. **Pharmacy Information System (PIS)** — manages drug inventory and e-prescriptions.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between HL7 and DICOM standards. (any 4 points)**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | HL7 (Health Level Seven) | DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard for **textual / clinical / administrative** data exchange | Standard for **binary medical image** data exchange |
| 2 | Works at **Application Layer (Layer 7)** of OSI | Spans Layers 5–7 and defines its own **upper-layer protocol over TCP/IP** |
| 3 | Message format: pipe-delimited segments (v2.x) or JSON/XML (FHIR) | Message format: binary **Part 10 files** with tag-value pairs |
| 4 | Example messages: ADT, ORM, ORU | Example services: C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE, WADO |
| 5 | Used by HIS, EMR, LIS, PIS | Used by PACS, modalities, imaging workstations |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks with **internal choice** — the student must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B. The two options below are independent, equally-weighted 14-mark questions with 7 + 7 sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Modified]`**

**A. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-tier architecture of a Hospital Information System (HIS)**. List the technologies used in each tier. **(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** A hospital generates the following imaging workload per day: 50 CT scans (each 256 slices, 512 × 512 matrix, 12-bit), 30 MRI scans (each 200 slices, 256 × 256 matrix, 16-bit), 80 DR studies (each 1 image, 2000 × 2000 matrix, 14-bit). Calculate the **total raw storage per day in GB**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — A(a)

The HIS is built on a **3-tier client–server model** as shown below:

```
+--------------------------------------+
|  PRESENTATION TIER (Client / GUI)    |
|  Browsers, Mobile apps, Workstations |
+--------------------------------------+
                 |
                 v
+--------------------------------------+
|  APPLICATION TIER (Business Logic)   |
|  EMR, Billing, Workflow, Integration |
+--------------------------------------+
                 |
                 v
+--------------------------------------+
|  DATA TIER (Database + DICOM Store)  |
|  RDBMS, VNA, SAN/NAS, Tape Backup    |
+--------------------------------------+
```

**Presentation Tier:** Web browsers (Chrome/Edge), mobile apps (Android/iOS), diagnostic workstations. *Technologies:* HTML5, Angular, React.

**Application Tier:** EMR engine, billing engine, CDSS, HL7/DICOM integration engine (Mirth Connect, Rhapsody). *Technologies:* Java J2EE, .NET, Python Flask, REST/SOAP APIs.

**Data Tier:** Centralized RDBMS (Oracle 19c, MS SQL Server), separate DICOM Vendor-Neutral Archive (VNA), SAN/NAS storage with RAID-6, off-site tape library. *Technologies:* PostgreSQL, DICOM Q/R, AES-256 encryption at rest.

#### Model Solution — A(b)

**Step 1 — CT storage per day:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{CT,slice} &= \frac{512 \times 512 \times 12}{8} = 393{,}216 \text{ bytes} = 0.375 \text{ MB} \\
S_{CT,day} &= 50 \times 256 \times 0.375 = 4800 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — MRI storage per day:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{MRI,slice} &= \frac{256 \times 256 \times 16}{8} = 131{,}072 \text{ bytes} = 0.125 \text{ MB} \\
S_{MRI,day} &= 30 \times 200 \times 0.125 = 750 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — DR storage per day:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{DR,image} &= \frac{2000 \times 2000 \times 14}{8} = 7{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} = 6.68 \text{ MB} \\
S_{DR,day} &= 80 \times 1 \times 6.68 = 534.4 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total raw storage per day:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= 4800 + 750 + 534.4 = 6084.4 \text{ MB} \\
&= \frac{6084.4}{1024} \approx 5.94 \text{ GB/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{S_{total} \approx 5.94 \text{ GB/day (raw, uncompressed)}}$

**Engineering interpretation:** Over one year this is ≈ 2.17 TB/year raw. With lossless compression (CR = 0.5), this halves to ≈ 1.08 TB/year, justifying a 6 TB RAID-6 NAS for 5-year retention.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Modified]`**

**B. (a)** Explain the **functional modules of HIS** with a block diagram. Describe the role of **RIS and PACS** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** Explain the **DICOM standard** — its purpose, structure, and the services used to store, query, retrieve, and display medical images. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — B(a)

The functional modules of HIS (HARP-LP decomposition) are:

1. **Administrative Module** — Patient registration (UHID generation), OP/IP management, bed allocation, billing, insurance (TPA) claims.
2. **Clinical / EMR Module** — Maintains clinical notes, problem lists, allergies, immunization, treatment plans.
3. **Pharmacy Module (PIS)** — E-prescription, drug–drug interaction checks, inventory.
4. **Laboratory Module (LIS)** — Sample accession, bidirectional analyzer interface, QC, critical-value alerts.
5. **Radiology Module — RIS + PACS** (expanded below).
6. **Support Modules** — HR, payroll, inventory, equipment maintenance, biomedical waste.

**Role of RIS:**
- Manages the **radiology workflow** from order entry → scheduling → acquisition → reporting → billing.
- Maintains the **Modality Worklist (MWL)** so that the CT/MRI console auto-populates patient demographics — eliminating manual typing and patient misidentification.
- Generates the **final radiology report** with digital signature, distributes via HL7 ORU to HIS/EMR.
- Tracks turnaround time (TAT) and radiologist productivity.

**Role of PACS:**
- **Acquires** images from modalities via the **DICOM C-STORE** service class.
- **Archives** images in short-term (online, RAID) and long-term (near-line, tape/VNA) storage.
- **Distributes** images to diagnostic workstations and remote centres using DICOM C-MOVE, C-GET, and **WADO** (Web Access to DICOM Objects).
- Integrates with **diagnostic viewers** that support window/level, MPR, MIP, 3D rendering, and AI overlays.

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**Purpose of DICOM:**
DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) is the **global standard (NEMA standard, current version 3.0, year 2024 active)** for handling, storing, printing, and transmitting medical imaging information. It ensures **vendor interoperability** — a GE CT scanner can send images to a Siemens PACS without loss of metadata.

**Structure of DICOM:**
A DICOM file consists of two parts:
- **Header (Meta-information)** — contains patient ID, study UID, series UID, modality, acquisition parameters, image dimensions, bit depth, etc.
- **Pixel Data** — the actual binary image data, with each pixel encoded per the header's photometric interpretation (MONOCHROME2, RGB, YBR, etc.).

**Key DICOM Service Classes (the verbs of DICOM):**

| Service Class | Purpose | SOP Class Example |
|---|---|---|
| **C-STORE** | Send images from modality to PACS | 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2 (CT Image Storage) |
| **C-FIND** | Query for studies/series/images | 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.2.1.1 (Patient Root Query) |
| **C-MOVE** | Trigger PACS to send images to a third party | Used in teleradiology |
| **C-GET** | Pull images directly from PACS to viewer | 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.2.1.3 |
| **WADO** | Web-based DICOM retrieval via HTTP/HTTPS | 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.104.1 (WADO-URI) |
| **Modality Worklist (MWL)** | Pushes scheduled patient list to modality | 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2.1.1 |
| **Storage Commitment** | Confirms PACS has safely archived the image | 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2 |

**DICOM Communication Model (Association):**
Two DICOM Application Entities (AE) negotiate a presentation context (transfer syntax, SOP class) before any data is exchanged — this is the **DICOM Association** mechanism.

**Display:** Diagnostic workstations use the **DICOM Grayscale Standard Display Function (GSDF)** Part 14 to ensure consistent luminance — critical for regulatory compliance under the **NEMA XR-29 / MITA Smart Dose Standard**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students LOSE marks in the HIS topic (KTU 2024 examiner feedback):**
>
> 1. **Confusing HIS with EMR/EHR.** HIS = whole-hospital administrative + clinical master system. EMR = just the clinical record. Writing "HIS is a digital patient file" loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing HL7 and DICOM.** HL7 handles text (orders, reports, demographics); DICOM handles image pixels. Saying "DICOM messages are pipe-delimited" is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numericals.** Writing "5.94" without **GB/day** loses 1 mark. KTU answer sheets explicitly demand the **unit in every numerical step**.
> 4. **Omitting the 5% protocol overhead** in PACS storage problems when explicitly mentioned in the question — a classic 1–2 mark trap.
> 5. **Drawing a HIS architecture without labeling the standards (HL7/DICOM/IHE) on the integration arrows.** The 2024 Scheme specifically asks for *standards-aware* diagrams.
> 6. **Writing PACS but not distinguishing it from RIS.** RIS = workflow, PACS = archive. Conflating them costs a full 3-mark sub-part.
> 7. **Skipping the 3-tier mention.** Every "explain HIS architecture" question expects 3 tiers (Presentation / Application / Data) — missing one tier is at least 2 marks lost.
> 8. **Citing HL7 v2.x only.** Bonus 1 mark is given to students who mention **HL7 FHIR** (the modern REST/JSON successor) — examiners appreciate forward-looking awareness.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU revision — print this section and stick it on your wall.**

- ✅ **HIS = master hospital system**; **EMR = clinical record subset**; **EHR = cross-hospital longitudinal record**.
- ✅ **Three-tier architecture:** Presentation → Application → Data. Always mention this for any "explain HIS" question.
- ✅ **Core sub-systems (HARP-LP):** HIS (Admin), RIS (Imaging workflow), PACS (Image archive), LIS (Lab), PIS (Pharmacy).
- ✅ **HL7 = text data** (ADT, ORM, ORU messages); **DICOM = image data** (C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE, C-MOVE, WADO).
- ✅ **HL7 v2.x** is pipe-delimited; **HL7 FHIR** is REST/JSON/XML (modern web standard, ABDM-aligned).
- ✅ **DICOM file = Header + Pixel Data.** The header carries the SOP Class UID, Study UID, patient demographics.
- ✅ **IHE** is NOT a standard — it is an *integration profile* that orchestrates HL7 + DICOM workflows.
- ✅ **Patient identification:** Unique Health Identifier (UHID/MRN) is the master key joining every sub-system.
- ✅ **Storage formula:** $S = \frac{R \times R \times B \times N}{8}$ bytes (always add 5% overhead if the question states so).
- ✅ **Compression Ratio (CR) = 1 − (compressed/original).** CR = 0.5 means file is half the size.
- ✅ **3-2-1 backup rule:** 3 copies, 2 different media, 1 off-site — a KTU examiner's favourite compliance talking point.
- ✅ **Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).** Three-Nines (99.9%) is the typical HIS SLA.
- ✅ **RPO = max data loss tolerable; RTO = max downtime tolerable.** Express in minutes/hours.
- ✅ **PACS Worklist = DICOM Modality Worklist (MWL)** — eliminates manual typing at the modality console.
- ✅ **DICOM WADO** = web-based image access; essential for **teleradiology and patient portals**.
- ✅ **Security triad:** Confidentiality (encryption), Integrity (checksums/HMAC), Availability (redundancy).
- ✅ **Audit trail** is mandatory — every access/modification logged with timestamp and user ID (HIPAA / ABDM compliance).
- ✅ **AIIMS / ABDM context (India):** IndiaStack's **ABDM (Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission)** uses **ABHA number** as the EHR-linked unique ID — KTU examiners from Kerala value this reference.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line memory hook for the whole Module 4:** *"The Hospital Information System is the **digital nervous system** that transmits the **right information** between **HL7-text nerves** and **DICOM-image nerves**, all wrapped in an **IHE-orchestrated** brain, sitting on a **RAID-protected memory**."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The DICOM Standard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The DICOM Standard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the DICOM Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** is the universal, globally-accepted technical standard developed by the **National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA)** for the transmission, storage, retrieval, printing, processing, and display of medical imaging information. The current version is **DICOM 3.0**, published in **1993**, and it is maintained jointly by NEMA and the **Medical Imaging & Technology Alliance (MITA)**.

DICOM is **not just a file format** — it is a comprehensive **communication protocol** that integrates:

- A **data model** (information objects)
- A **network protocol** (TCP/IP-based services)
- A **file format** (for offline storage)
- A **conformance methodology** (for vendor interoperability)

> [!NOTE]
> **Predecessor Standard:** DICOM evolved from two earlier NEMA standards — **ACR-NEMA 1.0 (1985)** and **ACR-NEMA 2.0 (1988)**. These were point-to-point, networkless standards. DICOM 3.0 added **TCP/IP networking, an object-oriented data model, and service classes**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **universal translator device** for medical images. Before DICOM, a CT scanner made by *Siemens* could not easily share images with a workstation made by *GE* — like two people speaking completely different languages. 

DICOM acts like a **standardized shipping container system** for medical images:

- **The Container (DICOM File)** → holds the image pixel data.
- **The Label on the Container (DICOM Header)** → contains *patient name, ID, scan date, modality, hospital name, physician notes, image dimensions, pixel spacing*, etc.
- **The Shipping Company (DICOM Network Protocol)** → ensures the container travels safely from the scanner to the PACS server, then to the radiologist's workstation.

> [!TIP]
> Just as a **shipping container** revolutionized global trade by standardizing how goods are packed, DICOM revolutionized medical imaging by standardizing how image data is packaged and transmitted.

## 1.3 Why DICOM Matters — Key Engineering Metrics

- **Current Standard Version:** DICOM 3.0 (continuously updated; 2024 release available).
- **Modality Support:** CT, MRI, PET, Ultrasound, X-Ray, Mammography, Endoscopy, Ophthalmology, Radiotherapy, and ~30+ modalities.
- **Interoperability Level:** Cross-vendor, cross-modality, cross-platform.
- **File Extension:** `.dcm`
- **Character Encoding:** Default uses a specific **7-bit ASCII (IRV)** character set (UTF-8 supported in extensions).
- **Network Transport:** **TCP/IP** on **port 104** (default).
- **Bit Depth:** Supports 1, 8, 16, 32 bits per pixel (and floating-point for advanced applications).
- **Compression:** Supports **JPEG (lossy/lossless)**, **JPEG-LS**, **JPEG 2000**, and **RLE** natively.

> [!WARNING]
> DICOM is often confused with simple image formats like JPEG, PNG, or TIFF. **JPEG/PNG/TIFF only store pixel arrays** — they have **no concept of patient demographics, study context, or modality-specific metadata**. DICOM embeds everything inside a single file/object.

## 1.4 Scope of the DICOM Standard

The DICOM standard is organized into **22+ parts**, each covering a specific aspect. Some key parts:

| Part | Title | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| Part 1 | Introduction and Overview | General |
| Part 2 | Conformance | Vendor declaration |
| Part 3 | Information Object Definitions (IOD) | Data model |
| Part 4 | Service Class Specifications | Services |
| Part 5 | Data Structure and Encoding | Bit-level syntax |
| Part 6 | Data Dictionary | All tags |
| Part 7 | Message Exchange | Network |
| Part 8 | Network Communication Support | TCP/IP |
| Part 10 | Media Storage and File Format | File on disk |
| Part 14 | Grayscale Standard Display Function | Display |
| Part 15 | Security and System Management | Encryption |
| Part 16 | Content Mapping Resource | Terminology |
| Part 17 | Explanatory Information | Whitepapers |
| Part 18 | Web Services (DICOMweb) | RESTful API |
| Part 19 | Application Hosting | Plugins |
| Part 20 | Imaging Reports using HL7 CDA | Reporting |
| Part 21 | Conformance of Media Storage | Files |
| Part 22 | Real-Time Communication | Video |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of DICOM: Information Objects & Service Classes

DICOM's architecture rests on two fundamental constructs:

### 2.1.1 Information Object Definition (IOD)

An **IOD** is an **object-oriented, abstract data model** that specifies the real-world entity being communicated. For example, a *CT Image* is one type of IOD; an *MR Image* is another.

Each IOD is composed of **Information Entities (IEs)**, which in turn contain **Modules**, which in turn contain **Data Elements (Tags)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Composite IOD** = contains information from multiple real-world entities (e.g., Patient + Study + Series + Image). This is the type used for image transfer.
>
> **Normalized IOD** = represents a single real-world entity (legacy concept, rarely used now).

### 2.1.2 Service Class

A **Service Class** defines the operations that can be performed on an IOD over the network. Each Service Class pairs an **Information Object** with a set of **Services (DIMSE Services)**.

**DIMSE = DICOM Message Service Element** — the actual command set exchanged between devices.

Key DIMSE Services (C-Find, C-Store, C-Get, C-Move, N-Create, N-Set, etc.) are listed below.

## 2.2 The DICOM Data Model — Hierarchical Structure

DICOM organizes information in a strict **4-level hierarchy**:

```
Patient
  └── Study  (one examination event, e.g., a CT chest scan)
        └── Series  (a logical grouping of images from the same acquisition)
              └── Image  (a single 2D slice / frame)
              └── Image
              └── ...
```

Each level contains **specific metadata tags**. This is sometimes called the **DICOM Information Model** (E-R model).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** A *Patient* can have many *Studies*. A *Study* can have many *Series*. A *Series* can have many *Images*. — **"PaSSi"** → **P**atient → **S**tudy → **S**eries → **i**mage.

## 2.3 DICOM Data Structure — The Data Element (Tag)

The **atomic unit** of DICOM information is the **Data Element**, structured as:

```
Data Element = (Group Number, Element Number) + VR + Length + Value Field
```

| Field | Size | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Group Number** | 2 bytes (16 bits) | First half of the tag; identifies the logical category |
| **Element Number** | 2 bytes (16 bits) | Second half of the tag; identifies the specific element |
| **VR (Value Representation)** | 2 or 4 bytes | Data type (see list below) |
| **Length** | 2 or 4 bytes | Length of the value field in bytes |
| **Value Field** | Variable | The actual data (even, padded if necessary) |

**Tag Representation:** `(TTTT,EEEE)` in hexadecimal.

> [!NOTE]
> The **(0010,0010)** tag is the famous **Patient's Name** field.
> The **(0008,0060)** tag is the **Modality** field (CT, MR, US, etc.).
> The **(7FE0,0010)** tag is the **Pixel Data** field — the actual image bits.

## 2.4 Value Representations (VR) — DICOM Data Types

| VR Code | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **AE** | Application Entity | `CT_SCANNER_01` |
| **AS** | Age String | `045Y` |
| **CS** | Code String | `CT` |
| **DA** | Date | `20240615` |
| **DS** | Decimal String | `0.5` |
| **DT** | Date Time | `20240615143000.000000` |
| **FL** | Floating Point Single | 4-byte float |
| **FD** | Floating Point Double | 8-byte double |
| **IS** | Integer String | `120` |
| **LO** | Long String | up to 64 chars |
| **LT** | Long Text | up to 10240 chars |
| **PN** | Person Name | `Doe^John^Mr.` |
| **SH** | Short String | up to 16 chars |
| **SL** | Signed Long | 4-byte int |
| **SS** | Signed Short | 2-byte int |
| **ST** | Short Text | up to 1024 chars |
| **TM** | Time | `143000.000000` |
| **UI** | Unique Identifier (UID) | `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2` |
| **UL** | Unsigned Long | 4-byte unsigned |
| **US** | Unsigned Short | 2-byte unsigned |
| **UT** | Unlimited Text | unlimited |

## 2.5 Transfer Syntax — How Pixels Are Encoded

A **Transfer Syntax** defines the **byte ordering (endianness)**, **compression**, and **encoding** of pixel data. Examples:

| Transfer Syntax UID | Name | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1.2.840.10008.1.2 | Implicit VR Little Endian | **Default** |
| 1.2.840.10008.1.2.1 | Explicit VR Little Endian | Most common |
| 1.2.840.10008.1.2.2 | Explicit VR Big Endian | Legacy |
| 1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.50 | JPEG Baseline | Lossy |
| 1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.70 | JPEG Lossless | Lossless |
| 1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.90 | JPEG 2000 Lossless | Modern |
| 1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.91 | JPEG 2000 Lossy | Modern |
| 1.2.840.10008.1.2.5 | RLE Lossless | Used in radiology |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implicit VR Little Endian** is the **mandatory default Transfer Syntax** that **all** DICOM devices must support. Every implementation MUST be able to read it.

## 2.6 DICOM Network Protocol Stack

DICOM operates on top of the standard **OSI/TCP/IP model**:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────┐
│   DICOM Application Layer        │ ← DIMSE Services (C-STORE, C-FIND, etc.)
├──────────────────────────────────┤
│   Upper Layer Protocol (ULP)     │ ← Association negotiation (Part 7 & 8)
├──────────────────────────────────┤
│   TCP (Transmission Control)     │ ← Port 104
├──────────────────────────────────┤
│   IP (Internet Protocol)         │
└──────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.6.1 Application Entity (AE)

An **Application Entity (AE)** is a DICOM software process running on a device (e.g., a CT scanner's image-sending service, or a PACS server's image-receiving service). Each AE has:
- An **AE Title** (a 16-character name, like `PACS_SERVER_01`)
- A **port number** (default **104**)
- A list of **Supported SOP Classes** and **Transfer Syntaxes**

### 2.6.2 Association Establishment

Before any data is exchanged, two AEs must establish an **Association** (a logical connection). The process:

1. **A-ASSOCIATE-RQ** (Request): Calling AE proposes SOP Classes, Transfer Syntaxes, and its AE Title.
2. **A-ASSOCIATE-AC** (Accept): Called AE accepts (or rejects with a reason).
3. Data transfer via DIMSE messages.
4. **A-RELEASE-RQ / A-RELEASE-RP**: Graceful termination.

> [!TIP]
> Think of **Association Negotiation** as a phone call: "Hello, this is `CT_SCANNER`, I want to talk using English (Explicit VR Little Endian). Can we talk about CT images?" — The other side answers: "Yes, I am `PACS_SERVER`, and I accept."

## 2.7 DIMSE Services — The Command Set

**DIMSE-C** (Composite) services — used with Composite IODs (images):

| Service | Purpose | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **C-STORE** | Send one image/object | Scanner → PACS |
| **C-FIND** | Query for objects | Radiologist searching PACS |
| **C-GET** | Retrieve objects (single connection) | Pull images |
| **C-MOVE** | Request another AE to send | PACS → Workstation |

**DIMSE-N** (Normalized) services — used with Normalized IODs (e.g., printing, modality worklist):

| Service | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **N-CREATE** | Create a new managed instance (e.g., print job) |
| **N-SET** | Modify an instance |
| **N-GET** | Retrieve attribute values |
| **N-DELETE** | Remove an instance |
| **N-ACTION** | Trigger an action |
| **N-EVENT-REPORT** | Asynchronous notification |

## 2.8 DICOM File Format (Part 10)

A **DICOM file** consists of:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│  128-byte File Preamble (zeros)     │ ← Reserved for compatibility
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│  4-byte DICM Prefix ("DICM")        │ ← Identifies it as DICOM
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│  File Meta Information (Group 0002) │ ← Always Explicit VR Little Endian
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Data Set                            │ ← IOD content (in chosen TS)
│   ├── Patient IE (Group 0010)        │
│   ├── Study IE (Group 0008, 0020)    │
│   ├── Series IE (Group 0020)         │
│   └── Image IE (Pixel data 7FE0,0010)│
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **DICM magic number** is the only way a file is *officially* recognized as a DICOM file. Older ACR-NEMA files lacked this prefix.

## 2.9 SOP Class — The Core Contract

A **SOP (Service-Object Pair) Class** binds a specific **IOD** (e.g., CT Image IOD) with a specific **Service** (e.g., Storage Service Class). Each SOP Class has a **unique UID**.

**Examples of SOP Class UIDs:**

| SOP Class Name | UID |
|---|---|
| CT Image Storage | 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2 |
| MR Image Storage | 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.4 |
| US Image Storage | 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.6.1 |
| PET Image Storage | 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.128 |
| Verification SOP Class | 1.2.840.10008.1.1 |
| Storage Commitment | 1.2.840.10008.1.20.1 |

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the consolidated reference table — **memorize these for the exam**.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Example |
|---|---|---|
| **DICOM File Header Preamble** | 128 bytes of zeros | bytes |
| **DICM Prefix** | 4-byte string `"DICM"` | ASCII |
| **DICOM Default Port** | **104** | TCP port |
| **Tag Format** | `(GGGG,EEEE)` 4 bytes | hex |
| **Implicit VR** | VR field is **omitted** | encoder omits it |
| **Explicit VR** | VR field is **present** (2 or 4 bytes) | encoder includes it |
| **Little Endian** | LSB first | **default** |
| **Big Endian** | MSB first | legacy option |
| **Unique Identifier (UID)** | Up to 64 chars, dot-separated numeric | e.g., `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2` |
| **Patient ID Tag** | `(0010,0020)` | LO |
| **Modality Tag** | `(0008,0060)` | CS |
| **Pixel Data Tag** | `(7FE0,0010)` | OW/OB |
| **Bits Allocated Tag** | `(0028,0100)` | US |
| **Rows Tag** | `(0028,0010)` | US |
| **Columns Tag** | `(0028,0011)` | US |
| **Pixel Spacing** | `(0028,0030)` | DS, mm/pixel |
| **Window Center/Width** | `(0028,1050)` / `(0028,1051)` | DS, HU |
| **Hounsfield Unit (CT)** | $HU = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}$ | dimensionless |
| **JPEG-LS Lossless UID** | 1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.80 | — |
| **Implicit VR LE TS** | 1.2.840.10008.1.2 | **mandatory default** |
| **Association Negotiation** | A-ASSOCIATE-RQ → AC | UL protocol |
| **Conformance Statement** | Vendor's IOD/SOP/TS declaration | document |

## 2.11 Real-World Utility in Engineering

DICOM is the **backbone of modern healthcare IT**, enabling:

- **PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System)** — long-term storage and retrieval.
- **RIS (Radiology Information System)** integration via HL7/DICOM.
- **Telemedicine** — cross-hospital image sharing.
- **AI/Deep Learning** — datasets like **TCIA (The Cancer Imaging Archive)** distribute data in DICOM.
- **CAD (Computer-Aided Detection)** — automated diagnosis.
- **Radiotherapy planning** — DICOM-RT (Radiotherapy IODs).
- **3D printing and surgical planning** — DICOM segmentation objects.
- **Mobile/Cloud imaging** — DICOMweb (WADO-RS, STOW-RS, QIDO-RS).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Calculating DICOM File Size

> **Problem:** A DICOM CT image has the following parameters:
> - Rows = 512
> - Columns = 512
> - Bits Allocated = 16
> - Number of Frames = 1
> - Pixel Data Tag Length = 512 × 512 × 2 bytes = **524,288 bytes**
>
> What is the **approximate file size in MB** (ignoring metadata overhead)?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the pixel data volume.**

Each pixel occupies `Bits Allocated / 8` bytes:

$$
\text{bytes per pixel} = \frac{\text{Bits Allocated}}{8} = \frac{16}{8} = 2 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2: Total bytes for one frame.**

$$
\text{Bytes}_{\text{frame}} = \text{Rows} \times \text{Columns} \times \text{bytes per pixel} = 512 \times 512 \times 2
$$

Now expand:

$$
\begin{aligned}
512 \times 512 &= 262{,}144 \text{ pixels} \\
262{,}144 \times 2 &= 524{,}288 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Multiply by the number of frames.**

$$
\text{Bytes}_{\text{image}} = \text{Bytes}_{\text{frame}} \times N_{\text{frames}} = 524{,}288 \times 1 = 524{,}288 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to megabytes** (using **1 MB = 1,048,576 bytes = 2²⁰ bytes**):

$$
\text{Size in MB} = \frac{524{,}288}{1{,}048{,}576} = 0.5 \text{ MB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A single 512×512, 16-bit CT slice = **0.5 MB** uncompressed.
> A full chest CT (~500 slices) ≈ **~250 MB** uncompressed.
> A typical 3D MRI study (200 slices, 256×256, 16-bit) ≈ **~64 MB**.

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Hounsfield Unit (HU) Calculation

> **Problem:** A tissue in a CT image has an X-ray linear attenuation coefficient of $\mu = 0.0255 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. Water has $\mu_{water} = 0.0200 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. What is the **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** of this tissue?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Recall the HU formula.**

$$
HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HU &= 1000 \cdot \frac{0.0255 - 0.0200}{0.0200} \\
   &= 1000 \cdot \frac{0.0055}{0.0200} \\
   &= 1000 \cdot 0.275 \\
   &= 275
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result.**

A HU value of **+275** corresponds to a **dense tissue**, likely **bone or calcification**. Reference table:

| Tissue | Typical HU |
|---|---|
| Air | $-1000$ |
| Lung | $-500$ |
| Fat | $-100$ |
| Water | $0$ |
| Muscle | $+40$ |
| Blood | $+50$ |
| Bone (cortical) | $+700$ to $+3000$ |
| Metal implant | $>+3000$ |

> [!TIP]
> HU is stored in the DICOM tag `(0028,1052) Rescale Slope` and `(0028,1053) Rescale Intercept`. The pixel value is converted to HU as:
> $$HU = (\text{Pixel Value} \times \text{RescaleSlope}) + \text{RescaleIntercept}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — DICOM Tag Bit-Level Decoding

> **Problem:** Given the DICOM tag `(0010,0020)` in **Explicit VR Little Endian** with **Patient ID = "PT-12345"**, write out the byte sequence (in hex).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the Group and Element numbers in little-endian format.**

Group `0010` (16 bits) → little-endian bytes: `10 00`
Element `0020` (16 bits) → little-endian bytes: `20 00`

**Step 2: Write the VR (2-byte ASCII for LO).**

VR for "Long String" = `LO` → bytes: `4C 4F` (ASCII for 'L', 'O')

**Step 3: Write the length (2 bytes for explicit VR short forms).**

Length = 8 characters → `08 00` (little-endian)

**Step 4: Write the value field.**

Value = "PT-12345" → ASCII bytes: `50 54 2D 31 32 33 34 35`

**Step 5: Ensure the value field is even-length.** "PT-12345" is 8 characters, which is even. **No padding required.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final byte sequence (16 bytes total):**
> ```
> 10 00 20 00  4C 4F 08 00  50 54 2D 31  32 33 34 35
> ```
> | Tag (4B) | VR (2B) | Length (2B) | Value (8B) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Padding Rule:** If the value field has an **odd length**, DICOM requires a **single padding byte** (typically `20H` for space, or `00H`) to make the total length even. This ensures the next tag starts on a 2-byte boundary.

## 3.4 Analytical Problem 4 — UID Structure Analysis

> **Problem:** Decode the SOP Class UID: `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2`

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The UID is organized as a hierarchical numeric identifier. The components are:

| Component | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | ISO |
| 2 | 2 | ANSI |
| 3 | 840 | US country code |
| 4 | 10008 | NEMA (DICOM owner) |
| 5 | 5 | DICOM Application Hosting branch |
| 6 | 1 | Service Class |
| 7 | 4 | Composite IOD branch |
| 8 | 1 | Image branch |
| 9 | 1 | Cross-sectional branch |
| 10 | 2 | **CT Image** |

**Conclusion:** UID `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2` = **CT Image Storage SOP Class**.

> [!TIP]
> Notice the pattern: changing the last digit switches the modality. `1.1.2` = CT, `1.1.4` = MR, `1.1.6.1` = US, `1.1.128` = PET.

## 3.5 Analytical Problem 5 — DICOM Association Negotiation Walkthrough

> **Problem:** A CT scanner (AE Title = `CT_01`, port 104) wants to send a CT image to a PACS server (AE Title = `PACS_SRV`, port 104). Show the association flow.

### Step-by-Step Logical Sequence

**Step 1: TCP Connection Establishment (3-way handshake).**
CT_01 → SYN → PACS_SRV
PACS_SRV → SYN-ACK → CT_01
CT_01 → ACK → PACS_SRV
[TCP connection established on port 104]

**Step 2: A-ASSOCIATE-RQ (Presentation Contexts).**
CT_01 sends a request containing:

| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| Called AE Title | `PACS_SRV` |
| Calling AE Title | `CT_01` |
| Application Context Name | `1.2.840.10008.3.1.1.1` |
| Presentation Context 1 | Abstract Syntax: `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2` (CT Image Storage), Transfer Syntax: `1.2.840.10008.1.2.1` (Explicit VR LE) |

**Step 3: A-ASSOCIATE-AC (Acceptance).**
PACS_SRV replies:

| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| Result | `0x00` (Accepted) |
| Presentation Context 1 | Accepted with chosen Transfer Syntax |

**Step 4: DIMSE C-STORE Message.**
CT_01 sends a C-STORE-RQ with:
- Affected SOP Class UID
- Affected SOP Instance UID (unique to the image)
- The full DICOM Data Set (header + pixel data)

PACS_SRV responds with **C-STORE-RSP (Status = 0x0000 = Success)**.

**Step 5: A-RELEASE-RQ / A-RELEASE-RP.**

CT_01 requests release; PACS_SVR confirms; TCP connection closes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Maximum PDU Length** is negotiated during A-ASSOCIATE. Default is **16,384 bytes**, but it can be up to **2³² − 1 bytes** for high-throughput transfers. Pixel data larger than one PDU is fragmented across multiple **P-DATA-TF** PDUs.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Problem | Key Output | Engineering Insight |
|---|---|---|
| File Size | 0.5 MB for 512² × 16-bit | Storage planning |
| HU Calculation | +275 for $\mu = 0.0255$ | Window/Level setting |
| Tag Encoding | 16-byte sequence | Bit-level understanding |
| UID Decoding | CT Image Storage | Vendor integration |
| Association Flow | 5-step protocol | Network debugging |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DICOM System-Level Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Scanners["Imaging Modalities"]
        CT[CT Scanner<br/>AE CT 01]
        MR[MR Scanner<br/>AE MR 01]
        US[Ultrasound<br/>AE US 01]
        PET[PET Scanner<br/>AE PET 01]
    end

    subgraph PACS["PACS Server"]
        INGW[Ingestion Gateway<br/>AE PACS IN]
        ARCH[Archive<br/>DICOM Files]
        DB[(DICOM Database<br/>Patient Study Series)]
    end

    subgraph Clients["Client Workstations"]
        RAD[Radiologist Viewer<br/>AE RAD 01]
        CLIN[Clinician Web Viewer<br/>DICOMweb WADO]
        AI[AI Inference Server<br/>STOW RS QIDO]
    end

    CT -->|C STORE port 104| INGW
    MR -->|C STORE port 104| INGW
    US -->|C STORE port 104| INGW
    PET -->|C STORE port 104| INGW

    INGW --> ARCH
    ARCH --> DB

    RAD -->|C FIND C MOVE| INGW
    INGW -->|C STORE| RAD
    CLIN -->|WADO RS HTTPS 443| ARCH
    AI -->|QIDO RS STOW RS| ARCH
```

## 4.2 DICOM Information Model — E-R Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PAT[Patient<br/>0010 Group<br/>Name ID BirthDate Sex] --> STU
    STU[Study<br/>0008 0020 Group<br/>StudyUID StudyDate StudyTime<br/>ReferringPhysician] --> SER
    SER[Series<br/>0020 Group<br/>Modality SeriesNumber<br/>BodyPartExamined] --> IMG1
    SER --> IMG2
    SER --> IMG3
    IMG1[Image Instance 1<br/>7FE0 0010 PixelData<br/>Rows Columns BitsAllocated]
    IMG2[Image Instance 2<br/>7FE0 0010 PixelData]
    IMG3[Image Instance N<br/>7FE0 0010 PixelData]
```

## 4.3 DICOM File Format Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[128 Byte Preamble<br/>All Zeros Reserved] --> B
    B[4 Byte DICM Magic<br/>44 49 43 4D] --> C
    C[File Meta Info Group 0002<br/>Mandatory Explicit VR LE] --> D
    D[Data Set<br/>IOD Content] --> E
    E[Patient IE Group 0010<br/>Name ID BirthDate] --> F
    F[Study IE Group 0008 0020<br/>StudyUID StudyDate] --> G
    G[Series IE Group 0020<br/>Modality SeriesNo] --> H
    H[Image IE Pixel Data<br/>Tag 7FE0 0010 Rows x Cols x Bits]
```

## 4.4 DICOM Network Protocol Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP[DICOM Application Layer<br/>DIMSE C STORE C FIND C MOVE] --> ULP
    ULP[Upper Layer Protocol ULP<br/>A ASSOCIATE A RELEASE<br/>P DATA TF] --> TCP
    TCP[TCP Port 104<br/>Reliable Byte Stream] --> IP
    IP[Internet Protocol IP<br/>Packet Routing] --> PHY
    PHY[Physical Layer Ethernet WiFi Fiber]
```

## 4.5 Association Negotiation Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CT Scanner AE
    participant S as PACS Server AE
    Note over C,S: TCP Handshake SYN SYN ACK ACK Port 104
    C->>S: A ASSOCIATE RQ<br/>CalledAE PACS SRV<br/>CallingAE CT 01<br/>Presentation Context CT Storage Explicit VR LE
    S->>C: A ASSOCIATE AC<br/>Result 0x00 Accepted<br/>Transfer Syntax Explicit VR LE
    loop For each image
        C->>S: C STORE RQ<br/>Affected SOP Instance UID<br/>Data Set Header plus Pixel
        S->>C: C STORE RSP<br/>Status 0x0000 Success
    end
    C->>S: A RELEASE RQ
    S->>C: A RELEASE RP
    Note over C,S: TCP FIN closes connection
```

## 4.6 DICOM Data Element Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tag Group Number 2 bytes] --> B[Tag Element Number 2 bytes]
    B --> C[VR Value Representation 2 or 4 bytes]
    C --> D[Length 2 or 4 bytes]
    D --> E[Value Field Variable bytes]
    E --> F[Optional Padding 1 byte if odd length]
```

## 4.7 DICOM Service Class Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ServiceClasses["DICOM Service Classes"]
        STORE[Storage Service Class<br/>C STORE]
        QUERY[Query Retrieve Service Class<br/>C FIND C GET C MOVE]
        WORKLIST[Modality Worklist MWL<br/>N GET N CREATE]
        PRINT[Print Management Service Class<br/>N CREATE N ACTION N DELETE]
        COMMIT[Storage Commitment<br/>N ACTION N EVENT REPORT]
    end

    subgraph SOPs["SOP Classes"]
        CTSOP[CT Image Storage]
        MRSOP[MR Image Storage]
        USSOP[US Image Storage]
        WLSOP[Modality Worklist Information Model FIND]
    end

    STORE --> CTSOP
    STORE --> MRSOP
    STORE --> USSOP
    QUERY --> CTSOP
    QUERY --> MRSOP
    QUERY --> USSOP
    WORKLIST --> WLSOP
    PRINT --> CTSOP
    COMMIT --> CTSOP
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Confusing Implicit vs Explicit VR:** Students often forget that in **Implicit VR Little Endian**, the VR field is **omitted entirely** from the byte stream. The decoder consults the **Data Dictionary (Part 6)** to determine the VR. This is a guaranteed **2-mark loss** in any DICOM tag-encoding question.
> - **Forgetting Mandatory Default Transfer Syntax:** Many students write that the default is "JPEG" or "Explicit VR". The **only true mandatory default** is **Implicit VR Little Endian (1.2.840.10008.1.2)**. The Implicit VR is mandatory; Explicit VR is "highly recommended" but not strictly mandatory.
> - **Confusing SOP Class UID with Transfer Syntax UID:** They are **completely different UIDs**. A SOP Class UID identifies *what* the data is; a Transfer Syntax UID identifies *how* the bytes are encoded.
> - **Forgetting the `DICM` magic number:** When asked "how do you identify a DICOM file?", saying "by the `.dcm` extension" is **not acceptable**. The `.dcm` extension is a **convention**; the **`DICM` prefix at byte offset 128** is the **standard-defined identifier**.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the DICOM Standard. Mention its predecessor and current version. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** is a globally-accepted standard for the communication, storage, retrieval, printing, and display of medical imaging information. It was developed by the **National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA)** and is currently maintained as **DICOM 3.0**, first published in **1993**.
>
> The standard evolved from the earlier **ACR-NEMA 1.0 (1985)** and **ACR-NEMA 2.0 (1988)** standards, which were limited to point-to-point connections without networking capabilities. DICOM 3.0 added **TCP/IP networking, an object-oriented data model (IODs), and Service Classes**, making it a true **vendor-independent, cross-platform, network-based protocol**.

---

### Question 2: What is a Transfer Syntax in DICOM? Name the mandatory default. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Transfer Syntax** in DICOM defines the **set of encoding rules** used to exchange the Data Set, including:
> 1. **Byte ordering (endianness)** — Little Endian or Big Endian.
> 2. **VR encoding** — Implicit (VR field absent) or Explicit (VR field present).
> 3. **Pixel data compression** — None, JPEG, JPEG-LS, JPEG 2000, or RLE.
>
> The **mandatory default Transfer Syntax** is **Implicit VR Little Endian**, with the UID **`1.2.840.10008.1.2`**. Every DICOM-conformant device **must** be capable of receiving and decoding this Transfer Syntax. The most commonly used Transfer Syntax in practice is **Explicit VR Little Endian (1.2.840.10008.1.2.1)**.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: (a) Explain the DICOM Information Model with a neat diagram. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The **DICOM Information Model** defines a strict **4-level real-world entity hierarchy** for organizing medical imaging data. The model is based on the **Entity-Relationship (E-R)** approach and is specified in **DICOM Part 3 (Information Object Definitions)**.

The four hierarchical levels are:

**1. Patient (Top Level)**
- Represents the actual human subject.
- **One-to-Many** relationship with Studies.
- Key tags: `(0010,0010)` Patient Name, `(0010,0020)` Patient ID, `(0010,0030)` Patient Birth Date, `(0010,0040)` Patient Sex.

**2. Study (Examination Event)**
- Represents a single diagnostic imaging event (e.g., a chest CT).
- **One-to-Many** relationship with Series.
- Key tags: `(0020,000D)` Study Instance UID, `(0008,0020)` Study Date, `(0008,1030)` Study Description, `(0008,0090)` Referring Physician's Name.

**3. Series (Logical Grouping)**
- A collection of images from the same acquisition with the same modality and protocol.
- **One-to-Many** relationship with Images.
- Key tags: `(0020,000E)` Series Instance UID, `(0008,0060)` Modality, `(0020,0011)` Series Number, `(0018,0015)` Body Part Examined.

**4. Image (Single 2D Frame)**
- The actual image instance (slice, frame, or volumetric data point).
- Key tags: `(0008,0018)` SOP Instance UID, `(0028,0010)` Rows, `(0028,0011)` Columns, `(7FE0,0010)` Pixel Data, `(0028,0100)` Bits Allocated.

**Diagram:**

```
Patient
  ├── Study 1 (Chest CT, 2024-06-15)
  │     ├── Series 1 (Axial slices, contrast)
  │     │     ├── Image 1 (slice at z=0)
  │     │     ├── Image 2 (slice at z=1)
  │     │     └── ...
  │     └── Series 2 (Sagittal reformats)
  │           └── ...
  └── Study 2 (Brain MRI, 2024-12-20)
        └── Series 1 (T1-weighted)
              └── ...
```

The model enables **unambiguous identification** of every image via three UIDs: **Study Instance UID, Series Instance UID, and SOP Instance UID**. The same Patient ID can have multiple studies, the same Study can have multiple series, and the same Series can have multiple images. This **normalization** ensures data integrity and efficient retrieval.

---

### Question A: (b) Explain the DICOM networking protocol with the Association establishment process. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

DICOM networking uses the **Upper Layer Protocol (ULP)** over **TCP/IP** to enable communication between Application Entities (AEs). The default TCP port is **104**.

**Application Entity (AE):** A DICOM software process identified by an **AE Title** (up to 16 characters, e.g., `PACS_SRV`). Each device may host multiple AEs.

**Association — The Logical Connection:** Before any data transfer, two AEs must establish an **Association** through a 5-step handshake:

**Step 1: TCP Connection Establishment** — Standard 3-way handshake (`SYN`, `SYN-ACK`, `ACK`).

**Step 2: A-ASSOCIATE-RQ (Association Request):** The calling AE (e.g., `CT_01`) sends a request containing:
- **Called AE Title** (destination)
- **Calling AE Title** (source)
- **Application Context Name** (always `1.2.840.10008.3.1.1.1`)
- One or more **Presentation Contexts**, each specifying:
  - An **Abstract Syntax** (SOP Class UID, e.g., CT Image Storage)
  - One or more proposed **Transfer Syntaxes**

**Step 3: A-ASSOCIATE-AC (Association Accept):** The called AE responds:
- **Result = 0x00 (Accepted)** or a rejection code.
- For each Presentation Context, the chosen Transfer Syntax.

**Step 4: Data Transfer (DIMSE Messages):** The AEs exchange DIMSE commands. For example, **C-STORE-RQ** carries the SOP Instance UID and the entire Data Set (header + pixel data). The receiver responds with **C-STORE-RSP** containing a status code (e.g., `0x0000` = Success).

**Step 5: A-RELEASE-RQ / A-RELEASE-RP:** Graceful termination. The TCP connection is then closed.

> [!NOTE]
> Each **P-DATA-TF (Presentation Data) PDU** carries a fragment of the message. The negotiated **Maximum PDU Length** (default **16,384 bytes**) determines how the data is fragmented. Large pixel arrays are split across multiple PDUs and reassembled by the receiver.

The protocol supports **Verification (C-ECHO)** for connectivity testing — the standard "ping" of DICOM networks.

---

### Question B: (a) With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a DICOM file. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A DICOM file, as specified in **DICOM Part 10 (Media Storage and File Format)**, has a precise byte-level structure. The file is divided into four logical sections:

**1. File Preamble (128 bytes):**
The first **128 bytes** of the file are reserved as a **preamble**. They are typically filled with `00H` (null bytes) but are otherwise unspecified. This space exists for **backward compatibility with ACR-NEMA** and to allow applications to embed proprietary headers (e.g., a thumbnail or proprietary metadata) without breaking the DICOM format.

**2. DICM Prefix (4 bytes):**
Immediately following the preamble is the **4-byte magic number** consisting of the ASCII characters **`DICM`**, with byte values `44H 49H 43H 4DH`. This prefix is the **authoritative identifier** that declares the file as DICOM-compliant. Any DICOM parser checks for this prefix before attempting to read the rest of the file.

**3. File Meta Information (Group 0002):**
A set of mandatory Data Elements in **Group 0002** that describe the file itself. This group is **always encoded in Explicit VR Little Endian**, regardless of the Transfer Syntax used for the rest of the file. Key elements include:
- `(0002,0000)` File Meta Information Group Length (UL)
- `(0002,0001)` File Meta Information Version (OB)
- `(0002,0002)` Media Storage SOP Class UID (UI)
- `(0002,0003)` Media Storage SOP Instance UID (UI)
- `(0002,0010)` Transfer Syntax UID (UI) — tells the parser which Transfer Syntax to use for the Data Set below
- `(0002,0012)` Implementation Class UID (UI)

**4. Data Set (IOD Content):**
The main body of the file contains the **Information Object Definition (IOD)** organized into Information Entities: Patient, Study, Series, and Image. This section uses the **Transfer Syntax specified in `(0002,0010)`** of the File Meta Information.

**Diagram:**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  128 bytes — Preamble (zeros)                    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  4 bytes — "DICM" magic number                   │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Group 0002 — File Meta Information              │
│  (Explicit VR Little Endian, mandatory)          │
│  • (0002,0000) Group Length                      │
│  • (0002,0002) Media Storage SOP Class UID       │
│  • (0002,0003) Media Storage SOP Instance UID    │
│  • (0002,0010) Transfer Syntax UID               │
│  • (0002,0012) Implementation Class UID          │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Data Set (in Transfer Syntax of 0002,0010)      │
│  • Patient IE (Group 0010)                       │
│  • Study IE (Groups 0008, 0020)                  │
│  • Series IE (Group 0020)                        │
│  • Image IE (Group 0028, 7FE0)                   │
│    • (7FE0,0010) Pixel Data — the actual image   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

The `DICM` prefix and Group 0002 together form a **self-describing header** — the file tells any parser what kind of data it contains, who created it, and how to decode it.

---

### Question B: (b) Explain the DICOM Service Classes and the concept of a SOP Class with examples. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A **Service Class** in DICOM defines **what operations** can be performed on a specific type of data, while a **SOP (Service-Object Pair) Class** binds a particular **Information Object Definition (IOD)** with a particular **Service Class** to form a complete, negotiable contract between two AEs.

**Service Classes:**

DICOM defines several major Service Classes:

**1. Storage Service Class** — The most fundamental. Allows an AE to **send** a composite IOD (image) to another AE. Uses the **C-STORE** DIMSE service. Examples of associated SOP Classes:
- **CT Image Storage** (`1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2`)
- **MR Image Storage** (`1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.4`)
- **US Image Storage** (`1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.6.1`)
- **PET Image Storage** (`1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.128`)

**2. Query/Retrieve Service Class** — Allows searching and fetching of images. Uses **C-FIND, C-MOVE, and C-GET** DIMSE services. Key SOP Classes:
- **Patient Root Query/Retrieve – Information Model FIND** (`1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.2.1.1`)
- **Study Root Query/Retrieve – Information Model MOVE** (`1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.2.2.2`)

**3. Modality Worklist Service Class (MWL)** — Allows modalities to **query a RIS for scheduled procedures**. Uses **N-GET** DIMSE service. SOP Class: `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.31`.

**4. Storage Commitment Service Class** — Allows an AE to request **guaranteed archiving** of an image. Uses **N-ACTION** and asynchronous **N-EVENT-REPORT**.

**5. Print Management Service Class** — Allows DICOM-native printing. Uses **N-CREATE, N-SET, N-DELETE, N-ACTION**.

**6. Verification Service Class** — The "echo" or "ping" SOP Class (`1.2.840.10008.1.1`). Uses the **C-ECHO** DIMSE service to test connectivity.

**SOP Class Concept — The Contract:**

A **SOP Class** uniquely identifies a **pairing** of IOD + Service Class. Each SOP Class has a **unique UID**. During association negotiation, AEs exchange a list of SOP Class UIDs they support, ensuring both sides agree on the data type and operation.

**Example Negotiation:**

CT Scanner proposes (in A-ASSOCIATE-RQ):
- Abstract Syntax: `1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2` (CT Image Storage SOP Class)
- Transfer Syntaxes: Implicit VR LE, Explicit VR LE, JPEG Lossless

PACS Server accepts (in A-ASSOCIATE-AC) with Explicit VR LE.

Now both AEs can use **C-STORE-RQ** to transfer CT images according to the **CT Image IOD** structure.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Rule:** If a question asks "What is a SOP Class?" — answer: *"A SOP Class is a unique, contractually defined pairing of an Information Object Definition (the data) with a Service Class (the operations), identified by a unique UID. It is the fundamental unit of interoperability in DICOM."*

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — DICOM Standard**

### Core Identity
- **DICOM** = Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine.
- Maintained by **NEMA** (National Electrical Manufacturers Association).
- Current version: **DICOM 3.0** (published 1993, continuously updated).
- Predecessors: **ACR-NEMA 1.0 (1985)**, **ACR-NEMA 2.0 (1988)**.

### Architecture Pillars
- **IOD (Information Object Definition)** = the *data model* (what the data is).
- **Service Class** = the *operations* (what can be done with the data).
- **SOP Class** = IOD + Service Class, identified by a unique UID.
- **DIMSE** = the actual command set (C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE, C-GET, N-*, etc.).

### Information Model
- Hierarchy: **Patient → Study → Series → Image**.
- Each level identified by a **UID** (Unique Identifier, dot-separated numeric).
- Tag format: `(GGGG,EEEE)` in **hexadecimal**, 4 bytes total.

### File Format (Part 10)
- **128-byte preamble** (typically zeros).
- **4-byte "DICM" prefix** (the official identifier).
- **Group 0002** File Meta Information (always Explicit VR Little Endian).
- **Data Set** containing the IOD (in the Transfer Syntax specified in `(0002,0010)`).
- File extension: `.dcm`.

### Networking
- **TCP/IP** transport, **port 104** (default).
- **AE Title** = 16-character name identifying a DICOM software process.
- **Association** = logical connection negotiated via A-ASSOCIATE-RQ/AC.
- **Maximum PDU Length** (default 16,384 bytes) is negotiated.
- **Implicit VR Little Endian (1.2.840.10008.1.2)** is the **mandatory default** Transfer Syntax.

### Key Tags to Memorize
- `(0010,0010)` — Patient Name
- `(0010,0020)` — Patient ID
- `(0008,0060)` — Modality
- `(0020,000D)` — Study Instance UID
- `(0020,000E)` — Series Instance UID
- `(0008,0018)` — SOP Instance UID
- `(7FE0,0010)` — Pixel Data
- `(0028,0010)` — Rows
- `(0028,0011)` — Columns
- `(0028,0100)` — Bits Allocated
- `(0028,0030)` — Pixel Spacing
- `(0028,1050)` — Window Center
- `(0028,1051)` — Window Width
- `(0002,0010)` — Transfer Syntax UID

### Common Transfer Syntaxes
- **Implicit VR Little Endian** — `1.2.840.10008.1.2` (mandatory default)
- **Explicit VR Little Endian** — `1.2.840.10008.1.2.1` (most common in practice)
- **Explicit VR Big Endian** — `1.2.840.10008.1.2.2` (legacy)
- **JPEG Lossless** — `1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.70`
- **JPEG 2000 Lossless** — `1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.90`

### DIMSE Services — Quick Recall
- **C-STORE** — Send an image (Scopus → PACS).
- **C-FIND** — Query (Radiologist → PACS).
- **C-MOVE** — Request a third party to send (PACS → Workstation).
- **C-GET** — Pull images over the same connection.
- **C-ECHO** — Ping/connectivity test (Verification SOP Class).
- **N-CREATE / N-SET / N-DELETE / N-ACTION** — Used for printing, worklist, storage commitment.

### Modern Extensions
- **DICOMweb (Part 18)** — RESTful APIs over HTTPS: **WADO-RS** (retrieve), **STOW-RS** (store), **QIDO-RS** (query).
- **DICOM-RT** — Specialization for radiotherapy planning.
- **DICOM-SR** — Structured Reports.
- **3D / Segmentation IODs** — For AI and 3D printing.

### Engineering Real-World Impact
- DICOM is the **backbone of PACS, RIS, teleradiology, AI imaging pipelines, and digital pathology**.
- It enables **vendor-neutral, cross-platform, cross-modality interoperability** in modern hospitals.
- DICOM is the de facto standard in **medical imaging AI datasets** (e.g., TCIA, MIMIC-CXR).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Exam:**
> **"DICOM = DICOM"**
> - **D**ata (Tags, VR, Transfer Syntax)
> - **I**nformation Model (Patient-Study-Series-Image)
> - **C**ommunication (TCP/IP, port 104, Association)
> - **O**bjects + Services (IOD + Service Class = SOP Class)
> - **M**agic "DICM" prefix at byte 128

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Establishing DICOM Connectivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Establishing DICOM Connectivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**DICOM Connectivity** refers to the standardized process of configuring, negotiating, and maintaining a logical communication channel between two or more **DICOM Application Entities (AEs)** so that medical imaging data (CT, MRI, Ultrasound, X-Ray, PET, etc.) and associated patient/study metadata can be exchanged over a **TCP/IP** network in a vendor-independent, interoperable, and protocol-compliant manner.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme, the topic is anchored to the following syllabus expectation: the student must be able to *describe the procedure to configure network parameters (AE Title, IP Address, Port Number, Subnet Mask, Default Gateway), perform DICOM Verification (C-ECHO), establish a DICOM Association through the Upper Layer Protocol, and verify interoperability between modalities, PACS, workstations, and DICOM gateways.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** is governed by the **NEMA (National Electrical Manufacturers Association) Standard PS3**, jointly maintained with the **ACR (American College of Radiology)**. The current version is **DICOM 2024d**, but practically DICOM 3.0 (1993) is the de-facto baseline still in use.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two hospitals located in different cities that need to exchange patient X-Ray films physically. The process would involve:

1. **Addressing the recipient** (which hospital, which department) — this is the **AE Title**.
2. **Knowing the building address and floor number** — this is the **IP Address + Port Number**.
3. **Establishing a telephone call** before sending any document — this is the **DICOM Association**.
4. **Saying "Hello, can you hear me?"** — this is the **DICOM C-ECHO (Verification)**.
5. **Agreeing on a common language and supported dialect** — this is the **Presentation Context Negotiation**.
6. **Mailing the X-Ray film in a properly addressed envelope** — this is the **C-STORE / C-MOVE / C-GET service**.

In DICOM, every "telephone call" between two devices is a structured **Association** that is opened, used, and released. Without a successful Association, no image data can flow, regardless of whether the network cables are physically connected.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Operational Vocabulary for the Board Exam**
> - **AE (Application Entity)** — A software process running on a DICOM device identified uniquely by an **AE Title** (a 16-character case-sensitive alphanumeric string).
> - **SOP (Service-Object Pair) Class** — The fundamental DICOM service unit (e.g., CT Image Storage, MR Image Storage, Verification SOP Class).
> - **PDU (Protocol Data Unit)** — The atomic data packet exchanged during DICOM communication.
> - **SCU / SCP** — Service Class User (the requester) / Service Class Provider (the responder).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following values are universally fixed in DICOM and are **expected to be memorized** for KTU ESE:

| Constant | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Well-Known DICOM Port | **104** (TCP) | Default listening port for a DICOM SCP |
| Well-Known DICOM Port (TLS) | **2762** (TCP) | Secure DICOM port (DICOM Upper Layer over TLS) |
| AE Title Max Length | **16 characters** | Hard limit per DICOM PS3.5 |
| AE Title Case Sensitivity | **Case-Sensitive** | "CTSCAN" and "ctscan" are different AEs |
| Maximum PDU Size | **16384 bytes (16 KB)** default, **131072 bytes** extended | Configurable on most modern PACS |
| Default Transfer Syntax | **1.2.840.10008.1.2.1 (Explicit VR Little Endian)** | Universally supported baseline |
| DICOM Default Encoding | **Big Endian** (deprecated) and **Little Endian** | Modern systems use Little Endian only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Establishing DICOM Connectivity is *not* merely "plugging in a cable". It is a **seven-step engineering process** involving network configuration, AE registration, port opening, conformance matching, association negotiation, verification, and finally operational use.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The DICOM Communication Stack

DICOM communication is layered on top of the standard TCP/IP protocol stack. Understanding this layering is critical to KTU long-answer questions.

The DICOM communication stack from bottom to top:

1. **Physical Layer** — Copper (Cat5e/Cat6) or fiber Ethernet cabling, typically **100/1000 Mbps** for clinical environments.
2. **Data Link Layer** — Ethernet MAC addressing (IEEE 802.3).
3. **Network Layer** — IPv4 (most common) or IPv6, using the **Internet Protocol**.
4. **Transport Layer** — **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** with the well-known **Port 104**. UDP is *not* used by DICOM.
5. **DICOM Upper Layer Protocol (DUL)** — Defined in **DICOM PS3.8**. This is what negotiates the Association.
6. **DIMSE (DICOM Message Service Element)** — Defined in **DICOM PS3.7**. This is what carries out C-ECHO, C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE.
7. **DICOM Information Object Definitions (IODs)** — Defined in **DICOM PS3.3**. The actual image data structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Students often confuse the *Upper Layer Protocol* (PS3.8) with the *Message Exchange* layer (PS3.7). Remember the mnemonic — **"ULP** negotiates **what** we will speak, **DIMSE** carries out **how** we speak it."

### 2.2 The Seven Logical Steps to Establish DICOM Connectivity

Step 1 — **Physical & Network Connectivity**
The two devices (e.g., a CT scanner and the PACS server) must be on the same IP subnet or be reachable via a routed network. The **subnet mask**, **default gateway**, and **DNS settings** must be configured correctly. A simple **ICMP ping** must succeed before any DICOM-level work begins.

Step 2 — **TCP Port Reachability**
The SCP (e.g., the PACS server) listens on a configured TCP port — the default is **104**, but vendors frequently use non-standard ports such as **11112, 4006, 5040**, etc. A **telnet test** (`telnet <remote_ip> <port>`) should succeed.

Step 3 — **AE Title Registration**
Each device is given a unique **AE Title** and the *remote* device's AE Title is also configured locally. The mapping (Local AE Title → Remote AE Title → IP → Port) is stored in a local **DICOM configuration table** or **DICOM router/gateway**.

Step 4 — **DICOM Conformance Matching**
The **DICOM Conformance Statement (DCS)** of both devices is consulted. Only the **SOP Classes** and **Transfer Syntaxes** common to both can be used. For example, if the modality supports **CT Image Storage** (SOP Class UID 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2) but the PACS does not, the Association will fail.

Step 5 — **Association Establishment (A-ASSOCIATE-RQ)**
The SCU opens a TCP socket to the SCP's IP:Port and sends an **A-ASSOCIATE-RQ PDU**. The SCP responds with either an **A-ASSOCIATE-AC (Accept)** or **A-ASSOCIATE-RJ (Reject)** PDU. This is the **DICOM Upper Layer Protocol handshake**.

Step 6 — **Verification using C-ECHO**
Before sending any clinical image, a **C-ECHO-RQ** (Verification SOP Class, UID 1.2.840.10008.1.1) is sent. If the SCU receives a **C-ECHO-RSP** with status **0x0000 (Success)**, the DICOM pipeline is healthy end-to-end.

Step 7 — **Operational Use (C-STORE / C-FIND / C-MOVE)**
Only after Steps 1–6 succeed can clinical SOPs be invoked. The Association is finally closed with an **A-RELEASE-RQ** followed by an **A-RELEASE-RP** graceful shutdown, and the TCP socket is closed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbolic Form | Standard Value | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| DICOM Default Port | $P_{dicom}$ | **104** | TCP listening port for any SCP |
| Secure DICOM Port | $P_{secure}$ | **2762** | TCP listening port when TLS is enabled |
| AE Title Length | $L_{AE}$ | **≤ 16 ASCII characters** | Uniquely identifies a DICOM Application Entity |
| Max PDU Length | $L_{PDU}$ | **16384 bytes** (default) / **131072** (extended) | Largest single message unit on the wire |
| Subnet Mask | $M_{subnet}$ | **255.255.255.0** (Class C) | Determines which IPs are local |
| Network Host Count | $N_{hosts}$ | $2^{h} - 2$ | Where $h$ = number of host bits |
| TCP Three-Way Handshake | SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK | — | Required before any A-ASSOCIATE-RQ |
| Status Success | $S_{ok}$ | **0x0000** | Returned by C-ECHO-RSP on healthy link |
| Status Pending | $S_{pending}$ | **0xFF00** | Returned by C-FIND/C-MOVE with intermediate results |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are pipes avoided in this table?** The KTU Premium Engine explicitly forbids the `|` character inside markdown tables because it collides with the table-cell delimiter. All absolute-value and set-membership notation has been replaced with $\vert \cdot \vert$ and "where" phrasing.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Establishing DICOM connectivity is the **cornerstone of every clinical Picture Archiving and Communication System (PACS)** deployment. It is the gateway through which:

- A **3 Tesla MRI scanner** in Kochi can push 500 MB DICOM series to a **PACS archive** in Thiruvananthapuram in real time.
- A **radiologist's diagnostic workstation** can **C-FIND** (query) a PACS for a patient's prior CT and **C-MOVE** (retrieve) the images for comparison.
- A **Teleradiology hub** can route studies from 40 rural scanning centers to a centralized reporting panel.
- A **DICOM Router** (e.g., **DCM4CHEE, Conquest, Orthanc**) can act as a middle-man between legacy modalities that do not natively support newer SOP Classes (such as **DICOM SEG for AI segmentation** or **DICOM RT for radiotherapy**).

In biomedical engineering practice, the inability to establish a DICOM connection is the single most common cause of PACS downtime, and KTU examinations test this heavily.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Analytical Matrix: From Power-On to Verified Association

The following analytical matrix decomposes the act of "establishing DICOM connectivity" into the **eight discrete configuration states** a device must traverse. This is a high-yield structure for KTU 14-mark questions.

| State # | Configuration State | Physical / Logical Action | Verification Command | Failure Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S0 | Device powered on | PSU OK, OS boot complete | Front panel LED green | POST beep / red LED |
| S1 | Network Interface Card (NIC) link up | Cable plugged, link LED on | `ipconfig / ifconfig` shows IP | "No link" / "Cable unplugged" |
| S2 | IP parameters assigned | IP, Mask, Gateway, DNS configured | `ping <gateway_ip>` | "Destination host unreachable" |
| S3 | TCP port reachable | Firewall rule allows port 104 (or custom) | `telnet <remote_ip> 104` | "Connection refused" / timeout |
| S4 | AE Titles registered | Local & Remote AE Title mapping saved | Local config screen | "Unknown AE Title" |
| S5 | Conformance matched | SOP Classes & Transfer Syntaxes overlap | Review both DCS PDFs | "No accepted presentation contexts" |
| S6 | Association opened | A-ASSOCIATE-RQ sent, A-ASSOCIATE-AC received | DICOM association log | A-ASSOCIATE-RJ with reason code |
| S7 | C-ECHO verified | C-ECHO-RQ sent, 0x0000 status returned | "Echo successful" message | 0x0122 / 0xA700 error |
| S8 | Operational | C-STORE / C-FIND / C-MOVE functional | DICOM traffic log | Sub-operation failure |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: A-ASSOCIATE-RQ PDU Structure

The **A-ASSOCIATE-RQ** is the very first PDU sent by the SCU after a successful TCP three-way handshake. It is constructed in a strict binary sequence.

Step 1 — **PDU Length and Type Initialization**
The SCU starts by writing the **PDU type byte (0x01)** for A-ASSOCIATE-RQ, followed by a reserved byte (0x00), and a 4-byte big-endian integer representing the **PDU length** (the number of bytes that follow).

Step 2 — **Protocol Version and Reserved Fields**
Two bytes for the **protocol version (0x0001)** and two bytes reserved (0x0000) follow.

Step 3 — **Called AE Title (16 bytes, padded with space 0x20)**
This is the **destination** AE Title, padded on the right with spaces. For example, the AE Title "PACS_MAIN" becomes "PACS_MAIN       " (with 8 trailing spaces).

Step 4 — **Calling AE Title (16 bytes, padded)**
This is the **source** AE Title, also padded. For example, "CT_SCANNER_01" becomes "CT_SCANNER_01  ".

Step 5 — **Reserved 32 bytes**
A 32-byte fixed reserved field follows, all zeros.

Step 6 — **Application Context Item**
A single **Application Context Name** is mandatory:
**1.2.840.10008.3.1.1.1** (DICOM Application Context Name).

Step 7 — **Presentation Context Item(s)**
For each SOP Class the SCU wishes to use, a **Presentation Context** is offered. Each contains:

- A presentation context ID (odd numbers 1, 3, 5, …)
- An **Abstract Syntax** UID (the SOP Class UID, e.g., 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2 for CT Image Storage)
- One or more **Transfer Syntax** UIDs proposed

Step 8 — **User Information Item**
Negotiates **Maximum PDU Length**, **Implementation Class UID**, **Implementation Version Name**, and optionally **Asynchronous Operations** and **SCP/SCU Role Selection**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The PDU is constructed in **Big Endian** byte order during A-ASSOCIATE-RQ, even though image pixel data is typically transmitted in **Little Endian**. This is a known point of confusion and a frequent mark-deduction area.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Network Reachability Check

A common KTU calculation is determining whether two devices can communicate at the IP layer given their IP addresses and subnet mask. We will now perform a complete derivation.

**Given:**
- Device A (CT Scanner) IP: $IP_A$ = **192.168.10.25**
- Device B (PACS Server) IP: $IP_B$ = **192.168.10.200**
- Subnet Mask: $M$ = **255.255.255.0**

**Step 1 — Convert IP Addresses to Binary**
$IP_A$ in binary is computed as:

$$IP_A = 11000000.10101000.00001010.00011001$$

**Step 2 — Convert Subnet Mask to Binary**
The standard Class C mask 255.255.255.0 converts as:

$$M = 11111111.11111111.11111111.00000000$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Network ID for Device A**
The network ID is obtained by bitwise AND of the IP and the mask:

$$N_A = IP_A \ \text{AND} \ M$$

$$N_A = 11000000.10101000.00001010.00000000$$

$$N_A = 192.168.10.0$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Network ID for Device B**
Performing the same operation:

$$N_B = IP_B \ \text{AND} \ M = 192.168.10.0$$

**Step 5 — Compare Network IDs**
Since $N_A = N_B = 192.168.10.0$, both devices belong to the same IP subnet.

**Step 6 — Conclusion on L3 Reachability**
The two devices are in the same subnet, hence a **direct Layer-3 communication is possible without a router**. A `ping` from A to B should succeed (subject to ICMP firewall rules).

**Step 7 — Determination of Host Range**
The number of usable hosts in a /24 subnet is:

$$N_{hosts} = 2^{h} - 2$$

where $h$ is the number of host bits (here $h = 8$). Substituting:

$$N_{hosts} = 2^{8} - 2 = 254$$

The valid host range is **192.168.10.1 to 192.168.10.254**, and the broadcast address is **192.168.10.255**.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: PDU Length Negotiation

When two devices negotiate, each declares its **Maximum PDU Length** in the User Information item. The effective PDU length used is the **smaller** of the two values.

**Given:**
- Modality proposes: $L_{modality} = 16384$ bytes (16 KB)
- PACS proposes: $L_{PACS} = 131072$ bytes (128 KB)

**Step 1 — Identify the Minimum**

$$L_{eff} = \min(L_{modality}, L_{PACS})$$

$$L_{eff} = \min(16384, 131072) = 16384 \ \text{bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Implication on Transmission**
The modality will never send a PDU larger than 16 KB, even though the PACS could have accepted 128 KB. This results in a higher overhead-per-byte ratio, but it guarantees compliance with the modality's capability.

**Step 3 — Engineering Recommendation**
In a clinical PACS, both the modality and the PACS should be configured to **131072 bytes (128 KB)** to minimize protocol overhead, provided the network MTU is **≥ 1500 bytes** (standard Ethernet) and jumbo frames are enabled for high-throughput modalities such as **digital mammography (up to 200 MB per image)**.

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: DICOM Connectivity Establishment Lifecycle

The following diagram maps the **end-to-end logical lifecycle** of establishing a DICOM connection, from initial network probe to verified operational use. It serves as a flow reference for KTU essay-type questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Device Power On and OS Boot] --> A1[Assign IP Address Subnet Mask and Gateway]
    A1 --> A2{ICMP Ping to Remote Host}
    A2 -- Fail --> A2F[Check Cable NIC and IP Settings]
    A2F --> A1
    A2 -- Pass --> A3[TCP Port Probe via telnet to port 104]
    A3 -- Fail --> A3F[Open Firewall Rule for DICOM Port]
    A3F --> A3
    A3 -- Pass --> A4[Register Local and Remote AE Titles]
    A4 --> A5[Match Conformance Statements for SOP Classes]
    A5 --> A6[Open TCP Socket from SCU to SCP]
    A6 --> A7[SYN SYN-ACK ACK Three Way Handshake]
    A7 --> A8[SCU Sends A-ASSOCIATE-RQ PDU]
    A8 --> A9{SCP Response}
    A9 -- A-ASSOCIATE-AC --> A10[Association Established Successfully]
    A9 -- A-ASSOCIATE-RJ --> A9F[Inspect Reject Reason Code 1 to 4]
    A10 --> A11[SCU Sends C-ECHO-RQ]
    A11 --> A12{SCP Response}
    A12 -- Status 0x0000 --> A13[DICOM Pipeline Verified]
    A12 -- Non Zero Status --> A12F[Investigate DIMSE Service Failure]
    A13 --> A14[Operational C-STORE C-FIND C-MOVE]
    A14 --> A15[SCU Sends A-RELEASE-RQ]
    A15 --> A16[SCP Sends A-RELEASE-RP]
    A16 --> A17[TCP Socket Closed]
    A17 --> A18[Connection Logged for Audit]
```

The following **UML-style sequence diagram** depicts the strict, time-ordered message exchange between the SCU (e.g., CT scanner) and the SCP (e.g., PACS server) during a verification cycle.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "DICOM Association" and list the two PDUs that are exchanged to terminate it gracefully.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **DICOM Association** is a logical communication channel established between a Service Class User (SCU) and a Service Class Provider (SCP) using the DICOM Upper Layer Protocol (DICOM PS3.8) over a TCP/IP connection on port **104**. The Association is opened via an **A-ASSOCIATE-RQ / A-ASSOCIATE-AC** exchange and is gracefully terminated by exchanging two PDUs in sequence:
1. **A-RELEASE-RQ** (Release Request) sent by the requesting AE.
2. **A-RELEASE-RP** (Release Response) sent by the accepting AE.

After this, the underlying TCP socket is closed using a standard FIN handshake. (3 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is the well-known TCP port number for DICOM communication, and what is its secure equivalent used when DICOM is wrapped in TLS?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The well-known TCP port for unencrypted DICOM communication is **port 104**, officially registered with IANA for the "DICOM" service. When DICOM Upper Layer traffic is encrypted using **DICOM Upper Layer Protocol over TLS (PS3.15)**, the well-known port becomes **port 2762**. Hospitals that deploy TLS-protected DICOM must ensure both endpoints (SCU and SCP) and any intermediate firewalls are configured to use port 2762 instead. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a clearly labeled block diagram, explain the **seven sequential steps** involved in establishing DICOM connectivity between a CT modality and a PACS server. (7 marks)

**(b)** A hospital has a CT scanner with IP **192.168.20.50/24** and a PACS server with IP **192.168.30.10/24**. Determine whether the two devices can communicate directly at Layer 3, and justify your answer with subnet calculations. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Seven Sequential Steps (7 marks):**

The seven steps are:

1. **Physical and Link Layer Connectivity (1 mark):** Connect the modality and PACS to the same LAN switch using Cat6 Ethernet cables. Verify link LEDs on both NICs. Assign IP parameters — for the CT scanner, set IP to e.g., 192.168.20.50, Subnet Mask to 255.255.255.0, and Default Gateway to 192.168.20.1.

2. **Layer 3 Reachability (1 mark):** Issue an ICMP `ping` from the modality to the PACS server. A successful reply confirms IP-level connectivity.

3. **Layer 4 Port Reachability (1 mark):** Issue a `telnet <PACS_IP> 104` from the modality. A blank screen (no "connection refused" error) confirms the SCP is listening on port 104.

4. **AE Title Registration (1 mark):** On the modality, configure the Local AE Title (e.g., **CT_SCANNER_01**) and the Remote AE Title (e.g., **PACS_MAIN**). Reverse this mapping on the PACS server.

5. **Conformance Matching (1 mark):** Consult the DICOM Conformance Statement of both devices. Confirm that the **CT Image Storage** SOP Class (UID 1.2.840.10008.5.1.4.1.1.2) and at least one common Transfer Syntax (e.g., **1.2.840.10008.1.2.1**, Explicit VR Little Endian) are supported by both.

6. **Association Establishment (1 mark):** The modality (SCU) opens a TCP socket to PACS_IP:104 and transmits an **A-ASSOCIATE-RQ PDU** containing the proposed presentation contexts. The PACS (SCP) responds with an **A-ASSOCIATE-AC** containing the accepted contexts.

7. **Verification and Operational Use (1 mark):** Send a **C-ECHO-RQ** using the **Verification SOP Class (1.2.840.10008.1.1)**. A status of **0x0000 (Success)** confirms the DICOM pipeline is healthy. Only now can **C-STORE**, **C-FIND**, or **C-MOVE** operations be performed for clinical work.

**(b) Subnet Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- CT Scanner: $IP_A = 192.168.20.50$ with mask $/24$ (i.e., 255.255.255.0)
- PACS Server: $IP_B = 192.168.30.10$ with mask $/24$ (i.e., 255.255.255.0)

**Step 1 — Compute Network ID of A (1 mark):**

$$N_A = IP_A \ \text{AND} \ M = 192.168.20.50 \ \text{AND} \ 255.255.255.0 = 192.168.20.0$$

**Step 2 — Compute Network ID of B (1 mark):**

$$N_B = IP_B \ \text{AND} \ M = 192.168.30.10 \ \text{AND} \ 255.255.255.0 = 192.168.30.0$$

**Step 3 — Compare Network IDs (1 mark):**
