## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
> *Differentiate between lossless and lossy image compression in the context of medical imaging. Provide one DICOM-compliant standard for each type.*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Aspect | Lossless | Lossy |
|---|---|---|
| **Reconstruction** | Bit-exact; original image is recovered exactly | Approximation; original cannot be recovered |
| **Compression Ratio** | 2:1 to 3:1 | 10:1 to 30:1 |
| **Diagnostic Use** | Primary diagnosis, mammography, pediatric imaging | Archival, teaching files, screening |
| **DICOM Standard** | JPEG-LS (1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.80) | JPEG 2000 (1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.91) |
| **Regulatory Status** | Always acceptable | Restricted by jurisdiction and ratio |

#### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
> *Define "compression ratio" and "PSNR" for a medical image. Why is PSNR alone insufficient to guarantee diagnostic fidelity?*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

- **Compression Ratio (CR)** is the ratio of the original uncompressed file size to the compressed file size: $CR = S_{\text{orig}} / S_{\text{comp}}$.
- **PSNR** measures pixel-level fidelity between original and reconstructed images: $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}(L^2 / \text{MSE})$ dB.
- **Why PSNR is insufficient for diagnosis**: PSNR is a global, pixel-by-pixel metric. It does not capture *local* diagnostically-relevant features such as microcalcifications, lesion margins, or fine textures. A compressed image may have a high PSNR yet blur a critical 2-mm pulmonary nodule because that nodule occupied only a few pixels and contributed negligibly to MSE. Hence, **task-specific metrics** (detection sensitivity, ROC-AUC of lesion detection) are used alongside PSNR to validate clinical safety.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 4 Style]

**(a)** Describe the JPEG 2000 image compression pipeline in detail. Explain the role of the Discrete Wavelet Transform, the EBCOT tier-1 coding, and the MQ arithmetic coder. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A 12-bit grayscale CT slice of size $512 \times 512$ pixels is compressed using a lossy scheme. The compressed file size is 1.05 MB. Compute (i) the compression ratio, (ii) the bits-per-pixel, and (iii) the percentage storage saving. Comment on whether this CR is acceptable for primary diagnosis. **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Solution to A(a) — JPEG 2000 Pipeline (7 marks)**:

JPEG 2000 (ISO/IEC 15444-1) is a wavelet-based, scalable compression standard widely adopted in DICOM for medical imaging.

**Step 1 — Tiling (1 mark)**: The input image is partitioned into non-overlapping rectangular tiles. Each tile is encoded independently, allowing parallel encoding and random access to image sub-regions.

**Step 2 — DC Level Shift (1 mark)**: For a $k$-bit image, $2^{k-1}$ is subtracted from every sample to convert unsigned integers to signed integers, centering the dynamic range symmetrically around zero. For a 12-bit medical image, the shift value is $2^{11} = 2048$.

**Step 3 — Discrete Wavelet Transform (2 marks)**: Each tile undergoes a 2-D DWT, producing a multi-resolution pyramid of sub-bands (LL, LH, HL, HH) at each level. The 5/3 wavelet is reversible (lossless) and the 9/7 wavelet is non-reversible (lossy). The LL sub-band contains the low-frequency approximation; LH, HL, HH contain horizontal, vertical, and diagonal details, respectively. Energy compaction means most of the signal energy concentrates in the LL sub-band.

**Step 4 — Quantization (1 mark)**: In lossy mode, each sub-band is quantized with a step size $1/\Delta_b$ where $\Delta_b$ is the sub-band exponent from the standard. This is the **only lossy step**. In lossless mode, $\Delta_b = 1$ for all sub-bands and quantization is bypassed.

**Step 5 — EBCOT Tier-1 Coding (1 mark)**: Each sub-band is divided into small code-blocks (e.g., $64 \times 64$ pixels). Each code-block is encoded independently using **bit-plane coding with three coding passes** (significance propagation, magnitude refinement, cleanup). This produces an embedded bit-stream that can be **truncated at any point** to trade off bit-rate for quality (the foundation of rate-distortion scalability).

**Step 6 — MQ Arithmetic Coder (1 mark)**: The symbols from each bit-plane pass are entropy-coded using the **MQ coder**, a binary adaptive arithmetic coder derived from the Q-coder. It models symbol probabilities adaptively and achieves a coding efficiency very close to the entropy.

**Step 7 — Packet Assembly and Codestream**: The compressed code-blocks are grouped into **quality layers** and **resolution levels**, and then packed into a JPEG 2000 codestream. This is what gets encapsulated inside the DICOM file using the appropriate Transfer Syntax UID.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key differentiator from JPEG**: JPEG uses an 8×8 block DCT, which produces **blocking artifacts** at low bit-rates. JPEG 2000 uses a global wavelet, eliminating blocking artifacts and providing **multi-resolution, ROI, and SNR scalability** in a single codestream.

---

**Solution to A(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**:

**Given**:
- Image size = $512 \times 512$ pixels, 12 bits/pixel.
- Compressed file size = 1.05 MB = $1.05 \times 8 \times 10^6$ bits = $8.4 \times 10^6$ bits.

**Step 1 — Uncompressed file size (1 mark)**:

$$S_{\text{orig}} = 512 \times 512 \times 12 = 3{,}145{,}728 \text{ bits} = 0.375 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 2 — Compression ratio (2 marks)**:

$$CR = \frac{S_{\text{orig}}}{S_{\text{comp}}} = \frac{0.375 \text{ MB}}{1.05 \text{ MB}} = 0.357$$

**This is less than 1, which is impossible for compression!** The student must immediately notice a unit mistake — the compressed file (1.05 MB) is *larger* than the original (0.375 MB). This means either (i) the file size was specified in **bits**, not bytes, or (ii) the problem is using decimal vs. binary MB.

**Re-interpreting: Let compressed file = 1.05 Mbits = 0.13125 MB (1 mark)**:

$$CR = \frac{0.375}{0.13125} = 2.857 \approx 2.86 : 1$$

This is a plausible lossless-to-lightly-lossy ratio.

**Step 3 — Bits per pixel (2 marks)**:

$$\text{bpp} = \frac{S_{\text{comp}} \text{ (bits)}}{M \times N} = \frac{1.05 \times 10^6}{512 \times 512} = \frac{1{,}050{,}000}{262{,}144} = 4.005 \text{ bits/pixel}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage saving (1 mark)**:

$$S = \left( 1 - \frac{1}{CR} \right) \times 100\% = \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2.857} \right) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.350) \times 100\% = 65.0\%$$

**Step 5 — Clinical acceptability (1 mark)**:

A CR of 2.86:1 is in the **lossless / visually lossless** range. This is fully acceptable for primary diagnosis under all major radiology regulatory frameworks (ACR, ESR, RSNA). It is *not* a high-ratio lossy compression; the radiologist can read the images with full diagnostic confidence.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**:
> - Marks are deducted if the student uses $L = 255$ in PSNR for a 12-bit image. **Always use $L = 4095$ for 12-bit DICOM**.
> - Marks are deducted if the student forgets to multiply file size by 8 to convert bytes to bits.
> - Marks are deducted for writing "1 MB = $2^{20}$ bytes" when the problem statement uses the decimal definition ($1 \text{ MB} = 10^6$ bytes). Match the problem's convention.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 4 Style]

**(a)** Explain the Shannon entropy-based justification for lossless compression. Define entropy, average codeword length, and coding efficiency. Using the probabilities $p_1 = 0.5$, $p_2 = 0.25$, $p_3 = 0.125$, $p_4 = 0.125$, construct a Huffman code and verify its efficiency. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the PACS architecture. Explain the role of DICOM in image communication and storage. List any four DICOM transfer syntaxes used in medical image compression. **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Solution to B(a) — Entropy and Huffman Code (7 marks)**:

**Step 1 — Shannon Entropy Concept (1 mark)**: For a discrete memoryless source $X$ with symbol probabilities $\{p_i\}$, the entropy is the average information per symbol:

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \log_2 p_i \text{ bits/symbol}$$

This is the *theoretical lower bound* on the lossless bit-rate. No code can losslessly compress below $H(X)$ bits per symbol.

**Step 2 — Average Codeword Length (1 mark)**: For a code with codeword lengths $l_i$:

$$L = \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \, l_i \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Step 3 — Coding Efficiency (1 mark)**:

$$\eta = \frac{H(X)}{L} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Build the Huffman Tree (2 marks)**:

Given: $p_1 = 0.5$, $p_2 = 0.25$, $p_3 = 0.125$, $p_4 = 0.125$.

- Merge $p_3 = 0.125$ and $p_4 = 0.125$ → $N_1$ with $p = 0.25$.
- Merge $p_2 = 0.25$ and $N_1 = 0.25$ → $N_2$ with $p = 0.50$.
- Merge $p_1 = 0.5$ and $N_2 = 0.5$ → Root with $p = 1.00$.

**Codewords** (0 = left, 1 = right):
- $s_1$ → `0` (length 1)
- $s_2$ → `10` (length 2)
- $s_3$ → `110` (length 3)
- $s_4$ → `111` (length 3)

**Step 5 — Compute Entropy (1 mark)**:

$$H(X) = -(0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125)$$

$$= -(0.5 \times (-1) + 0.25 \times (-2) + 0.125 \times (-3) + 0.125 \times (-3))$$

$$= -(-0.5 - 0.5 - 0.375 - 0.375) = 1.75 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Average Codeword Length (0.5 mark)**:

$$L = (0.5)(1) + (0.25)(2) + (0.125)(3) + (0.125)(3) = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.75 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Step 7 — Coding Efficiency (0.5 mark)**:

$$\eta = \frac{1.75}{1.75} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

This is an **optimal Huffman code** — the source probabilities are exact powers of $(1/2)$, which always yields a fully efficient code.

---

**Solution to B(b) — PACS and DICOM (7 marks)**:

**Step 1 — PACS Architecture (2 marks)**: PACS consists of four major components connected by a high-speed hospital network:

1. **Image Acquisition Modalities** (CT, MR, X-Ray, US, Mammography).
2. **DICOM Gateway / Router** that accepts images from modalities using DICOM C-STORE.
3. **PACS Archive Server** with database, image storage, and tiered storage management.
4. **Display Workstations** (diagnostic and review) for radiologists to read and report.

**Step 2 — Role of DICOM (2 marks)**: DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) is the universal protocol standard for medical imaging. It:

- Defines the **information model** (Patient → Study → Series → Instance).
- Standardizes **network services** (C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE, C-GET, WADO-RS).
- Specifies **Transfer Syntaxes** that describe how pixel data is encoded (uncompressed, JPEG, JPEG-LS, JPEG 2000, RLE).
- Embeds **metadata tags** (patient name, modality, study date, acquisition parameters) within the same file as the pixel data, making the image self-describing.
- Enables **interoperability** between equipment from different vendors.

**Step 3 — Four DICOM Transfer Syntaxes for Compression (2 marks)**:

| # | UID | Description |
|---|-----|-------------|
| 1 | `1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.70` | **Lossless JPEG** (Process 14, Selection Value 1) |
| 2 | `1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.80` | **JPEG-LS Lossless** |
| 3 | `1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.90` | **JPEG 2000 Lossless** |
| 4 | `1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.91` | **JPEG 2000 Lossy** |

**Step 4 — Storage and Retrieval Workflow (1 mark)**: A CT scan completes → the modality C-STOREs the image to the PACS gateway (using the agreed Transfer Syntax) → the PACS archives the image in compressed form on RAID-6 storage → the radiologist's workstation issues a C-FIND/C-MOVE query to retrieve the study → the image is decompressed in memory → radiologist reads and signs off the report → the report is sent to the Hospital Information System via HL7.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (PACS Questions)**:
> - Marks are deducted for confusing **DICOM and HL7** (DICOM handles *imaging*; HL7 handles *textual* clinical data like reports, demographics, orders). Both run over TCP/IP but serve different functions.
> - Marks are deducted for omitting the **Transfer Syntax UID** when describing compressed DICOM — it is mandatory for the receiving system to know which decoder to invoke.
> - Marks are deducted for stating "PACS uses JPEG compression" without specifying which JPEG variant (JPEG, JPEG-LS, JPEG 2000 — they are *different* standards with different DICOM UIDs).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Principles of Image Compression for Archiving and Communication (Module 4)**

- ☐ **Three forms of redundancy** attacked by compression: coding, inter-pixel (spatial), psychovisual.
- ☐ **Lossless vs Lossy**: lossless is bit-exact (CR = 2–3:1); lossy is approximation (CR = 10–30:1, irreversible).
- ☐ **Shannon entropy** $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ is the **theoretical lower bound** on lossless bit-rate; no code can do better on average.
- ☐ **Huffman coding** is a prefix-free variable-length code built by iteratively merging the two lowest-probability nodes; efficiency $\eta = H/L \times 100\%$.
- ☐ **JPEG pipeline** = block partitioning → DC shift → 8×8 DCT → quantization (lossy step) → zig-zag scan → Huffman/Arithmetic coding. Suffers from **blocking artifacts**.
- ☐ **JPEG 2000 pipeline** = tiling → DC shift → DWT (5/3 or 9/7) → quantization → EBCOT → MQ arithmetic coder. **Multi-resolution, ROI, no blocking artifacts.**
- ☐ **JPEG-LS (LOCO-I)** uses the MED predictor and Golomb-Rice coding; it is the **DICOM-recommended lossless** standard for medical images.
- ☐ **PSNR formula** uses $L = 2^k - 1$ where $k$ = bit-depth; for 12-bit DICOM, $L = 4095$.
- ☐ **PSNR alone is insufficient** for clinical validation — task-specific metrics (lesion-detection AUC) are also required.
- ☐ **DICOM Transfer Syntax UID** must be specified in every compressed image; receiving systems use it to invoke the correct decoder.
- ☐ **Common DICOM Transfer Syntax UIDs**: 1.2.840.10008.1.2.4.70 (Lossless JPEG), .4.80 (JPEG-LS), .4.90 (JPEG 2000 Lossless), .4.91 (JPEG 2000 Lossy).
- ☐ **PACS architecture** = modalities + DICOM gateway + archive server + display workstations, all on a high-speed network.
- ☐ **Tiered storage**: SSD (hot) → HDD RAID-6 (warm) → NAS/Tape (cold) → Cloud (DR).
- ☐ **3-2-1 backup rule** is the industry standard for clinical archives.
- ☐ **VNA** decouples storage from the PACS application, enabling multi-vendor interoperability.
- ☐ **DICOMweb** (WADO-RS, STOW-RS, QIDO-RS) is the cloud-native RESTful evolution of DICOM.
- ☐ **High-ratio lossy compression is generally NOT acceptable for primary diagnosis** in mammography, pediatric imaging, or any study where microcalcifications or subtle lesions may be present; lossless or visually-lossless (CR ≤ 5:1) is preferred.
- ☐ **Bits-per-pixel (bpp)** is the universal currency for comparing image codecs.
- ☐ **Hospital storage sizing** example: $\sim$16 TB/year uncompressed → $\sim$6.5 TB/year with 2.5:1 lossless → $\sim$45–80 TB provisioned for a 7-year archive with redundancy.
- ☐ **No compression is "free"** — every lossy step is a potential legal and clinical risk; every lossless step adds CPU latency to the workflow.
- ☐ **Visually lossless** compression (PSNR > 50 dB, error < 0.5% of dynamic range) is the practical sweet spot for primary diagnosis in modern PACS.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Principles of Image Compression for Archiving and Communication (PEBMT755)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Philosophy of Image Compression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Philosophy of Image Compression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Image Compression** is the art and science of reducing the amount of data required to represent an image, while preserving the essential information content for a given application. In the context of medical imaging, it is the process of encoding an original digital image using fewer bits than the original representation, exploiting statistical, spatial, spectral, and perceptual redundancies inherent in image data.

Mathematically, image compression can be expressed as a mapping from an original image space $\mathcal{I}$ to a compressed bitstream $\mathcal{B}$, such that an inverse (or approximate inverse) mapping recovers an image $\hat{I}$ from $\mathcal{B}$:

$$f: \mathcal{I} \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}, \quad g: \mathcal{B} \longrightarrow \hat{\mathcal{I}}$$

where $\hat{I} \approx I$ for lossy schemes, or $\hat{I} = I$ exactly for lossless schemes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The *philosophy* of image compression is concerned with the **"why"** — the fundamental motivations, redundancy models, and trade-offs — rather than the algorithmic "how." It forms the theoretical bedrock on which all practical codecs (JPEG, JPEG 2000, PNG, DICOM-JPEG) are built.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Compression Philosophy

The philosophy of image compression rests on three fundamental pillars, each addressing a different type of redundancy present in image data:

| Pillar | Type of Redundancy | Question Answered |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Statistical** | Coding Redundcy | Are the symbols coded optimally? |
| **Spatial** | Interpixel Redundancy | Are neighboring pixels correlated? |
| **Perceptual** | Psychovisual Redundancy | Can the human eye/brain not perceive certain details? |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Packed Suitcase

Imagine you are going on a **two-week vacation** and must fit everything into a **single suitcase**. You naturally:

1. **Roll clothes tightly** (removes "air gaps" → *interpixel redundancy*).
2. **Convert bulky items into multi-purpose ones** (removes duplicated utility → *coding redundancy*).
3. **Leave behind items you "won't really miss"** (sacrifices minor detail → *psychovisual redundancy*).

**Image compression does the same to a digital image:** it removes redundant "air," repacks intelligently, and discards what your eye will not detect. The suitcase is smaller, lighter, and easier to ship — but the *essence* of your trip is preserved.

## 1.4 Why Image Compression is Indispensable in Medical Imaging

A single modern CT slice may be **512 × 512 pixels × 16 bits ≈ 0.5 MB**. A full chest CT volume of 500 slices = **250 MB per patient**. A hospital generating thousands of studies per day needs **terabytes of storage and gigabit bandwidth** for transmission.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Benchmark Metrics (used universally in compression):**
> - **Compression Ratio (CR):** Typically **2:1 to 50:1** depending on modality and loss tolerance.
> - **Bits per Pixel (bpp):** Reduced from 8/12/16 bpp to as low as **0.1–2 bpp**.
> - **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio):** Diagnostic-quality threshold ≈ **40 dB**.

Without compression, modern PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication Systems) and **telemedicine** workflows would be economically and technically infeasible.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental Data Redundancies

### 2.1.1 Coding Redundancy (Statistical)
- Code: Assignment of codewords to symbols (e.g., gray levels).
- Redundancy arises when codeword lengths are **not matched to symbol probabilities**.
- **Fix:** Use variable-length coding (e.g., Huffman, Arithmetic, Shannon-Fano) so that more probable symbols receive **shorter codes**.

$$L_{avg} = \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} l(r_k) \cdot p(r_k)$$

where $l(r_k)$ is the number of bits in the codeword for gray level $r_k$ and $p(r_k)$ is its probability.

### 2.1.2 Interpixel Redundancy (Spatial / Structural)
- Caused by **statistical dependencies** between neighboring pixels.
- Adjacent pixels in natural and medical images are **highly correlated** — a smooth organ region rarely has a sudden jump to black.
- **Fix:** Predictive coding, transform coding (DCT, DWT), run-length encoding.

The 2-D autocorrelation function:

$$\rho(\Delta x, \Delta y) = \frac{\sum_{x,y} [I(x,y) - \mu_{I}][I(x+\Delta x, y+\Delta y) - \mu_{I}]}{\sum_{x,y} [I(x,y) - \mu_{I}]^2}$$

### 2.1.3 Psychovisual Redundancy (Perceptual)
- The Human Visual System (HVS) cannot distinguish all 65,536 gray levels of a 16-bit image.
- **Fix:** Quantization, chroma subsampling, perceptual weighting in transform coefficients.

## 2.2 Information-Theoretic Foundation

The **Information Content** of a symbol $r_k$ with probability $p(r_k)$ is:

$$I(r_k) = -\log_2 p(r_k) \quad \text{(bits)}$$

The **Entropy** $H$ of an image source is the **average information per symbol**:

$$H = -\sum_{k=0}^{L-1} p(r_k) \log_2 p(r_k) \quad \text{(bits/symbol)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shannon's First Theorem (Noiseless Coding Theorem):** The minimum average codeword length achievable for a source with entropy $H$ is bounded below by $H$ itself. That is, $L_{avg} \geq H$. Equality holds only for perfect coding.

## 2.3 Compression Performance Metrics

### Compression Ratio
$$C_R = \frac{n_1}{n_2}$$

where $n_1$ = number of bits in original, $n_2$ = number of bits in compressed representation.

### Relative Data Redundancy
$$R_D = 1 - \frac{1}{C_R} = 1 - \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

### Coding Efficiency
$$\eta = \frac{H}{L_{avg}} \times 100\%$$

A perfectly efficient coder has $\eta = 100\%$, i.e., $L_{avg} = H$.

## 2.4 Objective Fidelity Criteria (Reconstruction Quality)

For two $M \times N$ images $I(x,y)$ (original) and $\hat{I}(x,y)$ (reconstructed):

### Mean Squared Error (MSE)
$$e_{MSE} = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} [I(x,y) - \hat{I}(x,y)]^2$$

### Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)
$$e_{RMSE} = \sqrt{e_{MSE}}$$

### Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR)
$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \cdot \log_{10} \left( \frac{(\text{peak value})^2}{e_{MSE}} \right) = 20 \cdot \log_{10} \left( \frac{\text{peak value}}{e_{RMSE}} \right)$$

For images with $k$ bits per pixel, the peak value is $2^k - 1$ (e.g., **255 for 8-bit**).

## 2.5 Subjective Fidelity Criteria

| Rating | Quality | Impairment |
|--------|---------|------------|
| 1 | Excellent | Imperceptible |
| 2 | Good | Perceptible, not annoying |
| 3 | Fair | Slightly annoying |
| 4 | Poor | Annoying |
| 5 | Bad | Very annoying |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Symbol | Meaning | Units |
|---------|--------|---------|-------|
| $I(r_k) = -\log_2 p(r_k)$ | Information | Bits per symbol | bits |
| $H = -\sum p_k \log_2 p_k$ | Entropy | Avg. info per symbol | bits/symbol |
| $L_{avg} = \sum l_k p_k$ | Avg. codeword length | Mean bits per symbol | bits/symbol |
| $\eta = H / L_{avg}$ | Coding efficiency | Quality of code | % |
| $C_R = n_1 / n_2$ | Compression ratio | Size reduction factor | dimensionless |
| $R_D = 1 - 1/C_R$ | Relative redundancy | Excess data fraction | dimensionless |
| $e_{MSE}$ | Mean squared error | Mean pixel error | intensity² |
| $\text{PSNR}$ | Peak SNR | Reconstruction quality | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In medical imaging, PSNR > 40 dB and SSIM > 0.95 are typical clinical acceptance thresholds. Teleradiology (e.g., between Kottayam and a remote hospital) relies entirely on compression to be viable over constrained-bandwidth links.

## 2.7 A Generic Compression System Model

The compression pipeline consists of three sequential stages:

1. **Mapper (Transformer):** Converts image data into a form that exploits interpixel redundancy (e.g., DCT, DWT, predictive).
2. **Quantizer:** Reduces psychovisual redundancy by selectively discarding less-important data. (Present only in lossy schemes.)
3. **Symbol Encoder:** Generates fixed- or variable-length codes for the quantized output, exploiting coding redundancy (e.g., Huffman, Arithmetic, RLE).

The decoder applies **inverse symbol encoding → dequantization → inverse mapping**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Compression Ratio from First Principles

**Goal:** Show how $C_R$ and $R_D$ are related starting from bit counts.

### Step 1 — Define Original Bit Count

For an $M \times N$ image with $k$ bits per pixel, the total number of bits is:

$$n_1 = M \cdot N \cdot k$$

### Step 2 — Define Compressed Bit Count

After applying the compression algorithm, the compressed bit count is $n_2$.

### Step 3 — Form the Compression Ratio

By definition, the compression ratio is the ratio of original to compressed bit count:

$$C_R = \frac{n_1}{n_2} = \frac{M \cdot N \cdot k}{n_2}$$

### Step 4 — Derive the Relative Redundancy

The fraction of data that is redundant (i.e., could be removed) relative to the original is:

$$R_D = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = 1 - \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

### Step 5 — Connect to $C_R$

Substituting $\frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1}{C_R}$:

$$R_D = 1 - \frac{1}{C_R}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $C_R = 1$ → no compression, $R_D = 0$.
- $C_R = 10$ → compressed to one-tenth size, $R_D = 0.9$ (90% redundancy removed).
- $C_R \to \infty$ → $R_D \to 1$ (theoretical maximum).

## 3.2 Worked Example: Computing $C_R$ and $R_D$

**Problem:** A 1024 × 1024 12-bit mammogram is compressed to 1.5 MB. Find $C_R$ and $R_D$.

**Step 1 — Original size in bits:**

$$n_1 = 1024 \times 1024 \times 12 = 12{,}582{,}912 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to bytes:**

$$n_1 = \frac{12{,}582{,}912}{8} = 1{,}572{,}864 \text{ bytes} \approx 1.5 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 3 — Compressed size in bits:**

$$n_2 = 1.5 \text{ MB} \times 8 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 12{,}582{,}912 \text{ bits} \approx 12.58 \text{ Mb}$$

**Step 4 — Compression ratio:**

$$C_R = \frac{n_1}{n_2} = \frac{12{,}582{,}912}{12{,}582{,}912} = 1$$

**Conclusion:** No compression achieved. To reach a clinically useful $C_R = 20$, the compressed file must be **75 KB**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Entropy of a Toy Image

**Source Image (2×2 pixels, 3 gray levels {0, 1, 2}):**
$$I = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Count symbol frequencies.**

- $r_0 = 0$: 2 occurrences → $p(0) = 2/4 = 0.5$
- $r_1 = 1$: 1 occurrence → $p(1) = 1/4 = 0.25$
- $r_2 = 2$: 1 occurrence → $p(2) = 1/4 = 0.25$

**Step 2 — Compute per-symbol information.**

$$I(0) = -\log_2 0.5 = 1 \text{ bit}$$

$$I(1) = -\log_2 0.25 = 2 \text{ bits}$$

$$I(2) = -\log_2 0.25 = 2 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Compute entropy.**

$$H = \sum p_k I(r_k) = 0.5(1) + 0.25(2) + 0.25(2) = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

Theoretically, this 2-bit-per-symbol image can be losslessly compressed to **1.5 bits/symbol on average** — saving 25% of the data with no loss whatsoever.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Huffman Coding vs Fixed-Length Coding

Using the same image as above:

**Step 1 — Fixed-length code (FLC):** Each of 3 symbols needs $\lceil \log_2 3 \rceil = 2$ bits.

$$L_{avg}^{FLC} = 2 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Step 2 — Huffman code (probability-ordered):**

| Symbol | Probability | Huffman Code | Length |
|--------|-------------|--------------|--------|
| 0 | 0.50 | 0 | 1 bit |
| 1 | 0.25 | 10 | 2 bits |
| 2 | 0.25 | 11 | 2 bits |

**Step 3 — Average length with Huffman:**

$$L_{avg}^{H} = 0.5(1) + 0.25(2) + 0.25(2) = 1.5 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Step 4 — Coding efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{H}{L_{avg}} = \frac{1.5}{1.5} = 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Compression ratio (FLC vs Huffman):**

$$C_R = \frac{L_{avg}^{FLC}}{L_{avg}^{H}} = \frac{2}{1.5} = 1.33$$

A modest 33% gain — illustrating that **Huffman saturates to entropy** for sources with probabilities that are exact powers of $1/2$.

## 3.5 Derivation: PSNR from MSE

**Step 1 — Start with the mean squared error** as defined earlier.

**Step 2 — Define the peak signal power.**

For a $k$-bit image, the maximum possible signal value (peak) is $V_{max} = 2^k - 1$, and its squared value is $(2^k - 1)^2$.

**Step 3 — Form the signal-to-noise ratio.**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{V_{max}^2}{e_{MSE}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to decibels.**

By definition, $\text{PSNR (dB)} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(\text{SNR})$:

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \cdot \log_{10} \left( \frac{(2^k - 1)^2}{e_{MSE}} \right) = 20 \cdot \log_{10} \left( \frac{2^k - 1}{e_{RMSE}} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.**

Let $k=8$ (so $2^k - 1 = 255$) and $e_{MSE} = 100$.

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{255^2}{100} \right) = 10 \log_{10}(650.25) \approx 28.13 \text{ dB}$$

This is **below the clinical diagnostic threshold of 40 dB**, indicating unacceptable quality loss for a primary diagnostic interpretation.

## 3.6 Worked Example: PSNR Comparison of Two Compression Schemes

| Scheme | MSE | PSNR (8-bit) | Diagnostic? |
|--------|-----|--------------|-------------|
| Lossless (PNG) | 0 | $\infty$ dB | Yes |
| JPEG Q=95 | 5.2 | 40.97 dB | Yes |
| JPEG Q=75 | 38.7 | 32.25 dB | Limited |
| JPEG Q=50 | 95.4 | 28.34 dB | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Takeaway:** Higher PSNR (closer to ∞) ⇒ better fidelity. Diagnostic medical imaging almost always demands PSNR > 40 dB or **strictly lossless** archiving.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Compression System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["Source Image I(x,y)"] --> map["Mapper / Transformer (DCT DWT Predictive)"]
    map --> quant["Quantizer (Lossy only)"]
    quant --> symEnc["Symbol Encoder (Huffman Arithmetic RLE)"]
    symEnc --> chan["Channel Storage Network"]
    chan --> symDec["Symbol Decoder"]
    symDec --> dequant["Dequantizer"]
    dequant --> invMap["Inverse Mapper IDCT IDWT"]
    invMap --> out["Reconstructed Image Ihat(x,y)"]

    classDef lossy fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef lossless fill:#d6f5ff,stroke:#2c3e50,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000

    class quant,dequant lossy
    class src,out,chan data
    class map,symEnc,symDec,invMap lossless
```

## 4.2 Compression Workflow — Lossy vs Lossless

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Image"] --> B{"Compression Mode?"}
    B -->|Lossless| C1["Predictive / Transform"]
    C1 --> D1["Entropy Coding (Huffman)"]
    D1 --> E1["Compressed Bitstream"]
    E1 --> F1["Reconstruction: 100% identical"]

    B -->|Lossy| C2["Transform (DCT / DWT)"]
    C2 --> D2["Quantization (lossy step)"]
    D2 --> E2["Entropy Coding"]
    E2 --> F2["Compressed Bitstream"]
    F2 --> G2["Reconstruction: Approximate"]

    F1 --> H["Use case: Diagnostic archiving, regulatory compliance"]
    G2 --> I["Use case: Teleradiology, screening, preview"]

    classDef lossy fill:#fde0e0,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef lossless fill:#dff5d8,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000

    class C2,D2,G2,I lossy
    class C1,D1,F1,H lossless
    class B decision
```

## 4.3 Redundancy Reduction Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Data Redundancy in Images"] --> R1["Coding Redundancy"]
    R --> R2["Interpixel Redundancy"]
    R --> R3["Psychovisual Redundancy"]

    R1 --> F1["Fix: Variable-Length Coding (Huffman, Arithmetic)"]
    R2 --> F2["Fix: Predictive Coding, Transform Coding DCT DWT"]
    R3 --> F3["Fix: Quantization, Perceptual Weighting"]

    F1 --> OUT["Compressed Image"]
    F2 --> OUT
    F3 --> OUT

    classDef root fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef fix fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006633,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#996600,color:#000

    class R,R1,R2,R3 root
    class F1,F2,F3 fix
    class OUT out
```

## 4.4 Rate–Distortion Trade-off Curve (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["High Bitrate (Lossless)"] -->|Increase CR| B["PSNR drops slowly"]
    B -->|Further compression| C["Knee Region: rapid quality loss"]
    C -->|Extreme compression| D["PSNR floor: unusable"]
    D --> E["Visual artifacts, blocking, ringing"]

    classDef hi fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef lo fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000

    class A,B hi
    class C mid
    class D,E lo
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Reading of the Curve:** Medical imaging systems always operate on the **left plateau (high bitrate, high PSNR)** to preserve diagnostic content. Consumer imaging (smartphones, streaming) tolerates the **knee region** in exchange for storage savings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define *image compression*. List and briefly explain the three fundamental redundancies exploited by image compression systems. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Image compression is the process of reducing the number of bits required to store or transmit a digital image, while preserving the information needed for its intended use. It exploits three redundancies:

1. **Coding Redundancy** — Non-optimal codeword assignment to symbols. Fixed-length codes assign equal bits to all gray levels regardless of their frequency, wasting bits on rare symbols.
2. **Interpixel Redundancy** — Statistical correlation between adjacent pixels. Natural and medical images exhibit strong spatial correlation.
3. **Psychovisual Redundancy** — The Human Visual System (HVS) cannot perceive certain fine details; these can be discarded without perceived loss.

### **Q2.** Define **entropy** of an image source. What is its significance in image compression? **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The entropy $H$ of an image source with gray-level probabilities $p(r_k)$ is the **average information content per symbol**:

$$H = -\sum_{k=0}^{L-1} p(r_k) \log_2 p(r_k) \quad \text{bits/symbol}$$

**Significance:**
- It represents the **theoretical lower bound** on the average number of bits per symbol for any lossless compression scheme (Shannon's First Theorem).
- It quantifies the **inherent randomness** of the image.
- A perfectly efficient coder achieves $L_{avg} = H$ (coding efficiency $\eta = 100\%$).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the three fundamental data redundancies present in digital images. Describe how each type of redundancy is exploited by compression algorithms, with suitable medical-imaging examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A medical image source has the following gray-level probability distribution. Compute (i) the entropy $H$, (ii) the average codeword length $L_{avg}$ for the given Huffman code, and (iii) the coding efficiency $\eta$. **[7 Marks]**

| Gray level $r_k$ | Probability $p(r_k)$ | Huffman codeword $c_k$ | Length $l_k$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.40 | 0 | 1 |
| 1 | 0.25 | 10 | 2 |
| 2 | 0.15 | 110 | 3 |
| 3 | 0.10 | 1110 | 4 |
| 4 | 0.07 | 11110 | 5 |
| 5 | 0.03 | 11111 | 5 |

---

#### **Model Solution to A(a):**

The three fundamental data redundancies are:

**1. Coding Redundancy:**
- Definition: Arises when the codewords assigned to gray levels are not matched to the probability of occurrence.
- Example: Using a **fixed 8-bit code** for a 16-bit mammogram that predominantly contains only 256 distinct gray levels.
- Exploitation: **Variable-length codes** such as Huffman, Shannon-Fano, and Arithmetic codes assign shorter codes to more probable symbols.
- Medical example: CT lung-window images have most pixels clustered around -500 HU; Huffman coding of the histogram reduces bits per pixel dramatically.

**2. Interpixel (Spatial / Structural) Redundancy:**
- Definition: Statistical dependence between adjacent pixels.
- Mathematical measure: 2-D autocorrelation function $\rho(\Delta x, \Delta y)$, which is high for natural and medical images.
- Exploitation: Predictive coding (pixel-DPCM), transform coding (DCT, DWT).
- Medical example: In an MRI brain slice, the white matter regions show pixel values that change very slowly across space — a perfect target for differential pulse code modulation (DPCM).

**3. Psychovisual (Perceptual) Redundancy:**
- Definition: Information that the human visual system ignores or cannot resolve.
- Examples: Imperceptible high-frequency details, slight intensity changes, certain texture patterns.
- Exploitation: **Quantization** (especially coarse quantization of high-frequency transform coefficients), chroma subsampling, perceptual weighting.
- Medical example: Subtle quantization of high-frequency wavelet coefficients in a JPEG 2000 medical stream; radiologists do not perceive the loss.

The **overall compression strategy** = Remove all three redundancies sequentially through the *Mapper → Quantizer → Symbol Encoder* pipeline.

---

#### **Model Solution to A(b):**

**(i) Entropy $H$:**

$$H = -\sum p_k \log_2 p_k$$

Computing term-by-term:

- $r=0$: $-0.40 \log_2 0.40 = -0.40 \times (-1.3219) = 0.5288$
- $r=1$: $-0.25 \log_2 0.25 = -0.25 \times (-2) = 0.5000$
- $r=2$: $-0.15 \log_2 0.15 = -0.15 \times (-2.7370) = 0.4105$
- $r=3$: $-0.10 \log_2 0.10 = -0.10 \times (-3.3219) = 0.3322$
- $r=4$: $-0.07 \log_2 0.07 = -0.07 \times (-3.8365) = 0.2686$
- $r=5$: $-0.03 \log_2 0.03 = -0.03 \times (-5.0589) = 0.1518$

Summing:

$$H = 0.5288 + 0.5000 + 0.4105 + 0.3322 + 0.2686 + 0.1518 = 2.1919 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**(ii) Average codeword length $L_{avg}$:**

$$L_{avg} = \sum p_k l_k$$

$$L_{avg} = (0.40)(1) + (0.25)(2) + (0.15)(3) + (0.10)(4) + (0.07)(5) + (0.03)(5)$$

$$L_{avg} = 0.40 + 0.50 + 0.45 + 0.40 + 0.35 + 0.15 = 2.25 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**(iii) Coding efficiency $\eta$:**

$$\eta = \frac{H}{L_{avg}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.1919}{2.25} \times 100\% \approx 97.42\%$$

**Conclusion:** The Huffman code is **97.42% efficient**, very close to the entropy bound. The redundancy $1 - \eta \approx 2.58\%$ is due to the constraint that codeword lengths must be integers.

---

### **Question B (Option 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the **block diagram of a generic image compression system**. Clearly distinguish between lossless and lossy compression paths. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 2048 × 2048, 12-bit digital radiograph is compressed to 1.5 MB. Calculate (i) the compression ratio $C_R$, (ii) the relative data redundancy $R_D$, and (iii) the approximate bits per pixel (bpp) of the compressed image. If a radiologist requires PSNR ≥ 40 dB for diagnostic interpretation, and the compression algorithm produces MSE = 80, determine whether the compressed image meets the diagnostic threshold. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to B(a):**

A generic image compression system consists of **two functional units** — an **encoder** and a **decoder** — connected by a storage or transmission channel.

**Encoder (Compression side):**
1. **Mapper (Transformer):** Converts spatial-domain pixels into a different domain (frequency, wavelet, predictive residuals) to expose interpixel redundancy. Examples: DCT, DWT, KLT.
2. **Quantizer:** *(Lossy only)* Reduces the precision of the transformed coefficients, exploiting psychovisual redundancy. This step is **irreversible** and is the source of all information loss in lossy schemes.
3. **Symbol Encoder:** Produces a compact binary stream using variable-length coding (Huffman, Arithmetic, RLE), exploiting coding redundancy.

**Channel:** The compressed bitstream is stored on disk (PACS) or transmitted over a network (teleradiology).

**Decoder (Reconstruction side):**
1. **Symbol Decoder:** Reverses the entropy coding.
2. **Dequantizer:** *(Lossy only)* Approximate inverse of the quantizer.
3. **Inverse Mapper:** Applies IDCT, IDWT, or inverse prediction to recover the spatial image $\hat{I}(x,y)$.

**Distinction:**
- **Lossless path:** Mapper → Symbol Encoder → Symbol Decoder → Inverse Mapper. No quantizer. $I = \hat{I}$ exactly.
- **Lossy path:** Includes the quantizer/dequantizer. $I \neq \hat{I}$ but $I \approx \hat{I}$ within a controlled error bound.

(Students are expected to draw a clear block diagram with arrows and label the channel/quantizer prominently.)

---

#### **Model Solution to B(b):**

**(i) Compression Ratio $C_R$:**

Original bits:

$$n_1 = 2048 \times 2048 \times 12 = 50{,}331{,}648 \text{ bits}$$

Compressed bits:

$$n_2 = 1.5 \text{ MB} = 1.5 \times 8 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 12{,}582{,}912 \text{ bits}$$

$$C_R = \frac{n_1}{n_2} = \frac{50{,}331{,}648}{12{,}582{,}912} = 4$$

**(ii) Relative Redundancy $R_D$:**

$$R_D = 1 - \frac{1}{C_R} = 1 - \frac{1}{4} = 0.75 \text{ (or 75\%)}$$

**(iii) Bits per Pixel of Compressed Image:**

$$\text{bpp} = \frac{n_2}{M \cdot N} = \frac{12{,}582{,}912}{2048 \times 2048} = \frac{12{,}582{,}912}{4{,}194{,}304} = 3 \text{ bpp}$$

**PSNR check:**

For a 12-bit image, peak value $= 2^{12} - 1 = 4095$.

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{4095^2}{80} \right) = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{16{,}769{,}025}{80} \right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}(209{,}612.8) = 10 \times 5.3215 = 53.21 \text{ dB}$$

**Diagnostic verdict:** $\text{PSNR} = 53.21 \text{ dB} > 40 \text{ dB}$ ✓

The compressed image **meets the diagnostic threshold** with a comfortable margin of 13.21 dB.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $n_1$ and $n_2$:** Convert MB to bits using $8 \times 1024 \times 1024$ — never approximate as $8 \times 10^6$.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\log_2$ in entropy:** Using natural log gives wrong units (nats, not bits) and wrong numerical answers.
> 3. **Confusing PSNR formula variants:** Use $20 \log_{10}$ when the ratio is in linear amplitude, $10 \log_{10}$ when in power.
> 4. **Peak value error:** For $k$-bit images, peak $= 2^k - 1$, NOT $2^k$.
> 5. **Compression ratio direction:** $C_R = n_1 / n_2$, **not** the reverse. A $C_R = 4$ means 4× smaller, not 4× larger.
> 6. **Huffman length must be integer:** If a derivation gives $L_{avg} < H$, the student has made a calculation error — Huffman can only approach but never beat entropy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Image compression** = reduction in bits needed to represent an image, exploiting **statistical, interpixel, and psychovisual** redundancies.
- **Three redundancies:** Coding (Huffman fixes), Interpixel (DCT/DWT fixes), Psychovisual (Quantization fixes).
- **Information content** of symbol $r_k$: $I(r_k) = -\log_2 p(r_k)$ bits.
- **Entropy** $H$ = average information per symbol; theoretical lower bound for lossless $L_{avg}$.
- **Shannon's First Theorem:** $L_{avg} \geq H$, with equality for ideal coding.
- **Compression ratio:** $C_R = n_1/n_2$; **Redundancy:** $R_D = 1 - 1/C_R$.
- **Coding efficiency:** $\eta = H/L_{avg} \times 100\%$.
- **Fidelity criteria:**
  - *Objective:* MSE, RMSE, PSNR, SSIM.
  - *Subjective:* 5-point absolute rating scale.
- **PSNR formula:** $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{(2^k-1)^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$ dB.
- **Diagnostic threshold:** PSNR ≥ **40 dB** for medical-grade lossy; lossless mandatory for primary diagnostic archiving in many jurisdictions.
- **Generic pipeline:** Mapper → Quantizer (lossy only) → Symbol Encoder → Channel → Symbol Decoder → Dequantizer → Inverse Mapper.
- **Medical relevance:** Teleradiology, PACS storage, and cloud-based AI diagnostics all depend fundamentally on compression; the philosophy of compression is the *bedrock* on which these technologies rest.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generic Still-Image Compression System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT755 - ADVANCED MEDICAL IMAGING AND IMAGE PROCESSING TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Generic Still-Image Compression System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generic Still-Image Compression System (GSICS):** A generalized, modular framework that describes the entire process of converting a raw digital still image into a compact bitstream for storage/transmission and subsequently reconstructing it back. It is composed of two parallel pipelines — the **Source Encoder/Decoder** pair and the **Channel Encoder/Decoder** pair — operating in tandem through a noisy communication channel.

The architecture was formalized by **Khalid Sayood** in the canonical textbook *Introduction to Data Compression* and has been adopted as the universal reference model in KTU's image processing pedagogy because it cleanly separates the **compression objectives** (removing redundancy and irrelevancy) from the **communication objectives** (protecting the bitstream against channel errors).

The compression model has two main hierarchies:

| Level | Components | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **System Level** | Source Encoder → Channel Encoder → Channel → Channel Decoder → Source Decoder | Full transmit-receive chain |
| **Source Level** | Mapper → Quantizer → Symbol Encoder | Pure compression pipeline |

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Source Encoding:**
> 1. **Mapper (Transformer):** Decorrelates pixel data (e.g., DCT, DWT, KLT).
> 2. **Quantizer:** Maps continuous/representative values to a finite set — this is the **only lossy** stage in generic compression.
> 3. **Symbol Encoder:** Assigns efficient binary codewords (e.g., Huffman, Arithmetic, LZW).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of the generic still-image compression system as **packing a fragile, oversized family photograph into a reinforced shipping parcel for postal delivery**.

- The **photograph** itself is the raw uncompressed image (millions of pixel bytes).
- The **Mapper** is the *tailor* who cuts and rearranges the photo onto a smaller piece of fabric (removes spatial redundancy).
- The **Quantizer** is the *puzzle assembler* who groups similar shades together and discards details invisible to the eye (irrelevancy removal — lossy step).
- The **Symbol Encoder** is the *postage stamper* who writes the most frequent pixel patterns using short stamps and rare patterns using long stamps (entropy coding).
- The **Channel Encoder** is the *waterproof bubble wrap and rigid box* that protects the parcel from damage during shipping.
- The **Channel** is the *delivery truck and road network* — prone to accidents (noise).
- At the destination, the **Channel Decoder** unwraps the parcel, and the **Source Decoder** reverses tailor → puzzle → stamp operations to reconstruct a near-original photograph.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Compression Ratio (CR):** Defined as $CR = \frac{n_1}{n_2}$ where $n_1$ is the original bit count and $n_2$ is the compressed bit count.
- **Bits per Pixel (bpp):** Average bits required per image pixel after compression, typically $\mathbf{0.1 \le bpp \le 8}$ for 8-bit grayscale images.
- **Image Fidelity Metrics:** PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio), MSE (Mean Squared Error), SSIM (Structural Similarity Index).
- **Standard Reference Text:** Khalid Sayood, *Introduction to Data Compression*, Morgan Kaufmann — adopted in KTU 2024 references.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of the Generic Pipeline

The system is mathematically modelled as a series of **stochastic transformations** applied to a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude image signal $I(x, y)$ of size $M \times N$ pixels.

### Stage 1 — Source Encoder (Compression Side)

**A. Mapper $\rightarrow f_{map}$ :**
- Operates on raw image pixels.
- Goal: Exploit **inter-pixel (spatial) redundancy** by transforming correlated pixel data into a representation where coefficients are largely decorrelated.
- Examples: Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT), Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT), Karhunen-Loève Transform (KLT), Predictive Coding (DPCM).
- Output: A set of transform coefficients $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$.

**B. Quantizer $\rightarrow Q$ :**
- The *only* stage responsible for **irrelevancy reduction**.
- Maps a continuous or finely-quantized input range into a discrete set of *reconstruction levels*.
- Mathematically: $\hat{c} = Q(c) = \text{round}\left(\frac{c}{\Delta}\right)\cdot\Delta$ where $\Delta$ is the **quantization step size**.
- **Uniform vs. Non-uniform** quantizers exist; Lloyd-Max quantizer is optimal for MSE.
- **Lossless schemes (e.g., PNG) simply SKIP this stage.**

**C. Symbol Encoder $\rightarrow E$ :**
- Maps quantized symbols to binary codewords.
- Exploits **statistical redundancy** by assigning shorter codes to more probable symbols.
- Algorithms: **Huffman Coding** (optimal for symbol-by-symbol), **Arithmetic Coding** (near-optimal for joint distributions), **LZW** (dictionary-based, used in GIF/TIFF).

### Stage 2 — Channel Encoder
- Adds structured redundancy for **error detection/correction** (FEC).
- Techniques: Reed-Solomon codes, Convolutional codes, Turbo codes, LDPC codes.
- Bit-error rate (BER) drops from $\sim 10^{-3}$ (unprotected) to $\sim 10^{-9}$ after channel coding in DVB and medical PACS systems.

### Stage 3 — Channel
- Models physical transmission medium: storage media, copper/fiber cable, RF link, intranet.
- Introduces **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** plus burst errors.

### Stage 4 — Channel Decoder
- Inverse of channel encoder; attempts error correction using the redundant parity symbols.
- Outputs a *best estimate* $\hat{B}$ of the original compressed bitstream $B$.

### Stage 5 — Source Decoder (Decompression Side)
- **Symbol Decoder $E^{-1}$:** Converts binary codes back to quantized symbols.
- **Inverse Mapper $f_{map}^{-1}$:** Reconstructs pixel values from coefficients (IDCT, IDWT).
- **Output:** Reconstructed image $\hat{I}(x,y)$ — note the hat, indicating an approximation.

## 2.2 Why These Stages Exist — The "Why" Reasoning

| Stage | Redundancy Type Removed | Why It Matters |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mapper | Coding + Inter-pixel redundancy | Concentrates energy; compressible representation |
| Quantizer | Irrelevancy (psycho-visual) | Human eye cannot perceive fine details |
| Symbol Encoder | Statistical redundancy | Frequent patterns stored efficiently |
| Channel Encoder | (Adds redundancy for robustness) | Protects against noise |

> [!NOTE]
> **Redundancy vs. Irrelevancy:** *Redundancy* is information that can be reconstructed exactly from surrounding data (removable without loss). *Irrelevancy* is information imperceptible to the human observer (removable only with loss).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $CR = \frac{n_1}{n_2}$ | Compression Ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $bpp = \frac{n_2}{M \cdot N}$ | Bits per Pixel | bits/pixel |
| 3 | $\eta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} \times 100\%$ | Compression Efficiency / Space Savings | percent |
| 4 | $R = \sum_i p_i \log_2 \frac{1}{p_i}$ | Shannon's Source Entropy (lower bound on average code length) | bits/symbol |
| 5 | $\bar{L} = \sum_i p_i \cdot l_i$ | Average Codeword Length | bits/symbol |
| 6 | $\eta_{coding} = \frac{H(S)}{\bar{L}} \le 1$ | Coding Efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta = \frac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{2^B - 1}$ | Quantization Step Size (B bits) | intensity unit |
| 8 | $PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{MAX_I^2}{MSE}\right)$ | Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 9 | $MSE = \frac{1}{MN}\sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1}\left[I(x,y) - \hat{I}(x,y)\right]^2$ | Mean Squared Error | intensity$^2$ |
| 10 | $SSIM(x,y) = \frac{(2\mu_x\mu_y + C_1)(2\sigma_{xy} + C_2)}{(\mu_x^2 + \mu_y^2 + C_1)(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + C_2)}$ | Structural Similarity Index | $-1$ to $1$ |
| 11 | $R(D) \le R(0) - H(D)$ | Rate-Distortion Function (Shannon) | bits/sample |
| 12 | $\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$ | Uniform Quantizer Distortion Variance | signal power$^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` notation in the original LaTeX (e.g., $\vert I - \hat{I} \vert$) when writing the difference magnitude, never the bare pipe `|` inside Markdown table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging (DICOM/JPEG 2000 / JPIP):** Lossless path (no quantizer) for diagnostic-grade MRI/CT; lossy path for teleradiology bandwidth reduction.
- **Satellite Imaging:** JPEG-LS for onboard compression; DCT/JPEG2000 for downlink bandwidth.
- **Archival Storage:** Lossless PNG/FLIF for cultural heritage, fingerprint biometrics.
- **Streaming & Web:** JPEG, WebP, AVIF for low-latency image delivery — all derive from the Sayood generic model.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Code Length and Entropy Bound

Theoretical foundation of the symbol encoder stage.

**Step 1 — Define symbol probabilities.**
Let an alphabet $S = \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n\}$ have occurrence probabilities $p_i = P(s_i)$.

**Step 2 — Define codeword lengths.**
Assign a binary codeword of length $l_i$ bits to symbol $s_i$.

**Step 3 — Compute the expected code length.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{L} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot l_i \\
        &= p_1 l_1 + p_2 l_2 + \ldots + p_n l_n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Shannon's noiseless coding theorem.**
For a memoryless source, the **expected codeword length cannot be less than the source entropy** $H(S)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i \\
     &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 \frac{1}{p_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Concatenate via Kraft's inequality.**
A uniquely decodable prefix code exists *iff*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \le 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final bound on $\bar{L}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) \le \bar{L} \le H(S) + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The symbol encoder can approach, but never beat, the entropy bound. Huffman coding achieves $\bar{L} \le H(S) + 1$, and arithmetic coding pushes $\bar{L}$ arbitrarily close to $H(S)$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean Squared Error and PSNR

This is the quality metric of the reconstructed image at the source decoder output.

**Step 1 — Define the pixel-wise error signal.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e(x,y) = I(x,y) - \hat{I}(x,y)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Average the squared error over the entire image.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MSE = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} \left[I(x,y) - \hat{I}(x,y)\right]^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the peak possible signal power.**
For an 8-bit grayscale image, $MAX_I = 2^B - 1 = 255$.

**Step 4 — PSNR is signal-power-to-noise-power ratio in decibels.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PSNR &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{MAX_I^2}{MSE}\right) \quad \text{dB} \\
     &= 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{MAX_I}{\sqrt{MSE}}\right) \quad \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.**
For a $512 \times 512$, 8-bit image with $MSE = 100$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PSNR &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{255^2}{100}\right) \\
     &= 10 \log_{10}(650.25) \\
     &= 10 \times 2.8130 \\
     &= 28.13 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** PSNR $\ge 40$ dB implies near-imperceptible distortion; $30$–$40$ dB is acceptable; $< 20$ dB is unacceptable for diagnostic imaging.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example: Compression Ratio & Efficiency

A $256 \times 256$, 8-bit grayscale image is compressed from $65{,}536$ bytes to $12{,}288$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Original size.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_1 &= M \cdot N \cdot B = 256 \times 256 \times 8 \text{ bits} \\
    &= 524{,}288 \text{ bits} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compressed size.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_2 &= 12{,}288 \text{ bytes} = 98{,}304 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compression Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{n_1}{n_2} = \frac{524{,}288}{98{,}304} \approx 5.33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Bits per pixel.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
bpp = \frac{n_2}{M \cdot N} = \frac{98{,}304}{65{,}536} = 1.5 \text{ bits/pixel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compression Efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} \times 100\% = \frac{524{,}288 - 98{,}304}{524{,}288}\times 100\% \approx 81.25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** An original 8 bpp image is compressed to **1.5 bpp**, saving ~81% storage — a typical performance for JPEG quality factor 75.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

The Mermaid block diagram below renders the complete Sayood generic still-image compression system, end-to-end, including both the compression (left) and reconstruction (right) pipelines with the channel in the middle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Image I x y] --> B{Mapper}
    B --> C[Transform Coefficients C]
    C --> D{Quantizer Q}
    D --> E[Quantized Symbols S]
    E --> F{Symbol Encoder E}
    F --> G[Compressed Bitstream B]
    G --> H{Channel Encoder}
    H --> I[Channel Coded Bitstream]
    I --> J[AWGN Channel Noise]
    J --> K[Received Bitstream B hat]
    K --> L{Channel Decoder}
    L --> M[Decoded Symbols S hat]
    M --> N{Symbol Decoder E inv}
    N --> O[Reconstructed Coeff C hat]
    O --> P{Inverse Mapper}
    P --> Q[Reconstructed Image I hat x y]

    classDef srcEnc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000;
    classDef chnEnc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000;
    classDef chnDec fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000;
    classDef srcDec fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000;
    classDef src fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000;

    class A,Q src;
    class B,C,D,E,F,G srcEnc;
    class H,I chnEnc;
    class J src;
    class K,L,M chnDec;
    class N,O,P srcDec;
```

## 4.2 Expanded Source Encoder Sub-Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Pixel Block 8x8] --> T1[Forward DCT]
    T1 --> Q1[Uniform Quantization Table]
    Q1 --> Z1[Zig Zag Scan]
    Z1 --> R1[Run Length Encoding RLE]
    R1 --> H1[Huffman or Arithmetic Encoder]
    H1 --> BS1[Entropy Coded Bitstream]

    classDef stage fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000;
    classDef out fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000;
    class P1,T1,Q1,Z1,R1,H1 stage;
    class BS1 out;
```

## 4.3 Information Flow Through the Generic System

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Src as Source Image
    participant Map as Mapper
    participant Q as Quantizer
    participant Enc as Symbol Encoder
    participant ChEnc as Channel Encoder
    participant Ch as Noisy Channel
    participant ChDec as Channel Decoder
    participant Dec as Symbol Decoder
    participant Inv as Inverse Mapper
    participant Dst as Reconstructed Image

    Src->>Map: Pixel data I(x,y)
    Note over Map: Decorrelation via DCT/DWT
    Map->>Q: Coefficients C
    Note over Q: Quantization step Delta
    Q->>Enc: Symbols S
    Note over Enc: Huffman / Arithmetic
    Enc->>ChEnc: Bitstream B
    ChEnc->>Ch: Channel-coded bits
    Note over Ch: AWGN + Burst Errors
    Ch->>ChDec: Noisy bits B-hat
    ChDec->>Dec: Corrected bits
    Dec->>Inv: Symbols S-hat
    Inv->>Dst: Image I-hat(x,y)
```

## 4.4 Trade-Off Triangle — Rate, Distortion, Complexity

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Rate R bits/pixel] --- D[Distortion D MSE]
    D --- C[Complexity C FLOPs]
    C --- R
    R --> R1[Lower R = Higher D]
    D --> D1[Lower D = Higher R]
    C --> C1[Lower C = Higher R or D]
```

> **KTU Insight:** The Rate-Distortion function $R(D)$ is the theoretical lower bound on the number of bits required to achieve a target distortion $D$. Every practical compression algorithm operates on or above this curve.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Generic Still-Image Compression System. List its three main components in the source encoder and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Generic Still-Image Compression System is a modular framework that converts a raw digital image into a compact binary representation by sequentially removing redundancy and irrelevancy, optionally protecting the bitstream through channel coding. The three main components of the source encoder are:
1. **Mapper (1 mark):** Transforms image data into a domain where pixels are decorrelated (e.g., DCT/DWT).
2. **Quantizer (1 mark):** Reduces the number of distinct symbols by mapping ranges to reconstruction levels — the only lossy stage.
3. **Symbol Encoder (1 mark):** Assigns variable-length binary codes to symbols based on their probability (Huffman / Arithmetic).

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Lossy and Lossless image compression. Give one example standard for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Lossless | Lossy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Data recovery | Exact (bit-perfect) | Approximate |
| Quantizer used | No (skipped) | Yes |
| Compression ratio | Low (typically 2:1 to 3:1) | High (10:1 to 100:1) |
| Example standard (1 mark) | PNG, JPEG-LS, FLIF | JPEG, JPEG 2000, WebP |
| Medical use (1 mark) | Diagnostic PACS archive | Teleradiology / preview |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generic still-image compression system. Describe the function of each block in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (3 marks)**

The full block diagram was drawn above in Section 4.1. The student should reproduce:
Source Image → Mapper → Quantizer → Symbol Encoder → Channel Encoder → Channel → Channel Decoder → Symbol Decoder → Inverse Mapper → Reconstructed Image.

**Step 2 — Function of each block (4 marks):**

| Block | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| Mapper | Performs invertible transform (DCT/DWT) to decorrelate pixels; concentrates energy in few coefficients. |
| Quantizer | Replaces each coefficient by a value from a finite set; exploits human perceptual limits. |
| Symbol Encoder | Generates compact binary codewords using entropy coding. |
| Channel Encoder | Adds redundancy (parity bits) for error detection/correction. |
| Channel | Physical medium; introduces AWGN. |
| Channel Decoder | Detects and corrects errors using redundant parity. |
| Symbol Decoder | Converts binary codes back to quantized symbol values. |
| Inverse Mapper | Applies inverse transform (IDCT/IDWT) to reconstruct pixel intensities. |

**Step 3 — Why both encoders exist (1 mark):**
Separation allows independent optimization of compression and reliability.

**Step 4 — Distinguish lossless vs lossy (1 mark):**
Lossless system *removes* the quantizer; lossy retains it.

#### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**A $1024 \times 1024$ 8-bit grayscale image is compressed to 512 KB. Compute (i) Compression Ratio, (ii) Bits per Pixel, and (iii) Percentage Space Saving.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Original size (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_1 &= 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 \text{ bits} \\
    &= 8{,}388{,}608 \text{ bits} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} = 1024 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compressed size (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_2 = 512 \text{ KB} = 524{,}288 \text{ bytes} = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compression Ratio (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR = \frac{n_1}{n_2} = \frac{1024 \text{ KB}}{512 \text{ KB}} = 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Bits per Pixel (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
bpp = \frac{n_2}{M \times N} = \frac{4{,}194{,}304 \text{ bits}}{1{,}048{,}576 \text{ pixels}} = 4.0 \text{ bpp}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Space Saving (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} \times 100\% = \frac{1024 - 512}{1024} \times 100\% = 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Explain the role of the Mapper and Quantizer stages. How do they differ in terms of reversibility and information content?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Role of Mapper (3 marks):**
- Converts pixels to a transform domain.
- Exploits **inter-pixel (spatial) redundancy**.
- Examples: DCT (JPEG), DWT (JPEG 2000), KLT (optimal but rarely used), DPCM (predictive).
- Mapper output: set of *real-valued* coefficients concentrated in low-frequency region.

**Step 2 — Role of Quantizer (3 marks):**
- Maps the continuous range of mapper outputs to a finite set of reconstruction levels.
- Exploits **perceptual irrelevancy** — human eye is less sensitive to high-frequency coefficients.
- Lossy: **non-invertible**, $I(x,y) \ne \hat{I}(x,y)$ in general.
- Quantizer design: uniform (simple), Lloyd-Max (MSE-optimal), embedded (JPEG 2000 EBCOT).

**Step 3 — Reversibility & Information Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Property | Mapper | Quantizer |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reversibility | **Fully reversible** (IDCT/IDWT exist) | **Irreversible** |
| Information loss | None (lossless) | Yes (lossy) |
| Output domain | Continuous | Discrete |
| KTU example | DCT, DWT, KLT | Uniform, Lloyd-Max, Vector |

#### (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2022]
**Define PSNR. An image of size $256 \times 256$ has MSE $= 250$. Find PSNR. Comment on the visual quality.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio) measures the ratio between the maximum possible signal power and the corrupting noise power that affects the fidelity of its representation, expressed in decibels.

**Step 2 — Formula (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{MAX_I^2}{MSE}\right) \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting values (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PSNR &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{255^2}{250}\right) \\
     &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{65025}{250}\right) \\
     &= 10 \log_{10}(260.1) \\
     &= 10 \times 2.4150 \\
     &= 24.15 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Visual quality comment (2 marks):**
A PSNR of **24.15 dB** is *moderately low* — noticeable distortion is present, although the image is still recognisable. According to KTU conventions, PSNR $\ge 30$ dB is generally acceptable for most natural images; diagnostic medical imaging requires $\ge 40$ dB. The compression scheme used here is too aggressive; either quantization step $\Delta$ must be reduced or a more efficient mapper should be employed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in GSICS Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong diagram direction:** Students frequently draw the decoder pipeline *before* the encoder in the answer. Always show **left-to-right: encoder → channel → decoder**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Channel Encoder/Decoder:** Marks are reserved specifically for the channel protection block. Omitting it costs ~2 marks in Part B.
> 3. **Confusion between Mapper and Quantizer reversibility:** Mapper is **reversible**; Quantizer is **NOT**. Mixing this up costs up to 3 marks.
> 4. **PSNR unit:** Always quote PSNR in **dB**. Writing "PSNR = 24.15" without the unit loses 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Compression Ratio trap:** If the question states "compressed to 512 KB", students sometimes take $n_1 = 512$ KB. **Always identify $n_1$ as the original (uncompressed) size** — convert units carefully (KB vs. Kb vs. bytes vs. bits).
> 6. **Lossless = No Quantizer:** If asked to "design a lossless system", you must explicitly mention that the **quantizer is omitted**.
> 7. **MSE dimension:** MSE has units of *intensity squared*. Examiners often ask for verification; mention the squared unit to score the "units" mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Generic Still-Image Compression System**

- ✅ **Architecture:** Source Encoder → Channel Encoder → Channel → Channel Decoder → Source Decoder (Khalid Sayood Model).
- ✅ **Source Encoder sub-blocks:** Mapper (lossless) → Quantizer (lossy) → Symbol Encoder (lossless).
- ✅ **Mapper examples:** DCT (JPEG), DWT (JPEG 2000), KLT (theoretical optimum), DPCM (predictive).
- ✅ **Quantizer types:** Uniform (simple), Lloyd-Max (optimal MSE), Vector Quantization (VQ), Embedded (EBCOT in JPEG 2000).
- ✅ **Symbol Encoder algorithms:** Huffman, Arithmetic, Run-Length, LZW, BWT + MTF.
- ✅ **Channel Encoder examples:** Reed-Solomon, Convolutional, Turbo, LDPC.
- ✅ **Key formulas (memorize):**
   - $CR = \dfrac{n_1}{n_2}$
   - $bpp = \dfrac{n_2}{M \cdot N}$
   - $H(S) \le \bar{L} \le H(S) + 1$
   - $PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{MAX_I^2}{MSE}\right) \text{ dB}$
- ✅ **Image Quality Benchmarks:** PSNR $\ge 40$ dB (excellent), 30–40 dB (good), 20–30 dB (acceptable for non-diagnostic), $< 20$ dB (unacceptable).
- ✅ **Lossless vs Lossy:** Lossless = no quantizer; Lossy retains quantizer.
- ✅ **Redundancy types:** Coding, Inter-pixel (spatial), Psycho-visual (irrelevancy).
- ✅ **Medical application:** DICOM lossless for diagnostic; JPEG/JPEG 2000 lossy for teleradiology and bandwidth-limited transmission.
- ✅ **Common unit conversion:** $1$ byte $= 8$ bits; $1$ KB $= 1024$ bytes $= 8192$ bits.
